In Psalm 136, the writer repeats this phrase twenty six times, tethering every act of God, from the creation of the stars to the deliverance of Israel from their enemies, to His enduring mercy. This tells us that everything God does for His people is an expression of His faithful love. In Jeremiah 33:11, the phrase appears in a promise of restoration. Even when God’s people faced the consequences of their sin through exile, God promised that the "voice of joy" would return because His mercy does not come to an end (Lamentations 3:22-23).

The ultimate proof that God's mercy endures forever is found in the person and work of Jesus Christ. While God is perfectly holy and must judge sin, He is also "rich in mercy" (Ephesians 2:4). Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God and deserve His judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, God demonstrated His love by sending His Son, Jesus, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

This mercy is a gift. We are justified before God by His grace alone, through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, and not by any works of our own (Titus 3:5). Because His mercy endures forever, the salvation He provides is secure, and He will never leave nor forsake those who belong to Him (Hebrews 13:5; Romans 8:38-39).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in His enduring mercy today?
Is the Church of God in Christ (COGIC) a good biblical church?

The Church of God in Christ (COGIC) is a prominent Pentecostal denomination that holds to the core, essential doctrines of the historic Christian faith. They believe in the Trinity, affirming that God exists eternally in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). They also affirm the deity of Jesus Christ, His virgin birth, His sinless life, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, His bodily resurrection, and His future return (John 1:1; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

In terms of salvation, COGIC teaches that human beings are born in sin and need reconciliation with God. They believe that salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). This alignment on the "essentials" means that COGIC is considered a part of the orthodox Christian community.

There are, however, secondary doctrines that distinguish COGIC from other denominations. As a Pentecostal-Holiness body, they emphasize:

1. The Baptism of the Holy Spirit: They believe this is an experience distinct from and subsequent to the new birth, typically evidenced by speaking in other tongues as the Spirit gives utterance (Acts 2:4).
2. Holiness and Sanctification: They place a strong emphasis on "entire sanctification," believing that God calls believers to live a life of practical holiness and separation from the world (1 Peter 1:15-16; Hebrews 12:14).
3. Divine Healing: They believe that the redemptive work of Christ provides for the healing of the human body in answer to believing prayer (James 5:14-15).

While Christians hold varying views on the continuation of miraculous gifts or the specific mechanics of sanctification, these differences are generally viewed as matters of "liberty" rather than "essentials" of the faith.

When evaluating any specific local congregation, it is wise to ensure that the preaching remains centered on the Bible as the final authority for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16-17). The most important question for any church is whether it clearly proclaims the Gospel. The Gospel is the good news that although we are separated from a holy God by our sin, Jesus Christ took our punishment upon Himself. By His death and resurrection, He secured forgiveness and eternal life for all who believe.

If you are seeking peace with God, you can turn to Him now. Repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life (Romans 10:9-10; Acts 3:19). Have you ever placed your trust in Christ's work on your behalf, or is this something you are currently considering?

If you choose to visit a COGIC congregation, I encourage you to look for a community where the Word of God is honored, where Christ is exalted, and where the believers show genuine love for one another (John 13:35). Seeking the counsel of a faithful pastor or an elder in a local church is also a helpful way to grow in your understanding of these biblical truths.
What is the voice of one calling in the wilderness (Isaiah 40:3; John 1:23)?

The "voice of one calling in the wilderness" is a prophetic figure described in the Old Testament and identified in the New Testament as John the Baptist.

In the book of Isaiah, this voice is part of a message of comfort to God’s people. The prophet writes, "A voice cries: 'In the wilderness prepare the way of the Lord; make straight in the desert a highway for our God'" (Isaiah 40:3). Historically, this imagery suggested the ancient practice of clearing and leveling a road for a visiting monarch. Spiritually, it signaled that God was coming to dwell with and rescue His people, requiring them to prepare their hearts for His arrival (Isaiah 40:4-5).

Centuries later, when the Jewish leaders questioned John the Baptist about his identity, he quoted this prophecy to describe his own ministry. He stated, "I am the voice of one crying out in the wilderness, 'Make straight the way of the Lord,' as the prophet Isaiah said" (John 1:23). John saw himself not as the light or the Messiah, but as the herald sent to announce the arrival of Jesus Christ. 

John’s "calling" involved preaching repentance and baptizing those who confessed their sins (Matthew 3:1-6). By calling people to repent, John was "making straight the way," removing the obstacles of pride and sin so that people could recognize and receive Jesus. He eventually pointed his own disciples toward Jesus, identifying Him as "the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world" (John 1:29).

This preparation is necessary because God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him (Isaiah 59:2). The Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to bridge that gap. He lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are justified before God by His grace alone, through faith in Christ alone, rather than by our own efforts or works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Just as the voice in the wilderness urged people to prepare for the Lord's coming, the message of the Bible calls everyone to respond to what Jesus has done. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is neonomianism?

Neonomianism is a theological term that literally means "new law" (from the Greek words "neos" for new and "nomos" for law). It refers to the teaching that the Gospel is a new, simplified law that God has established in place of the Old Testament law. According to this view, since humans are unable to fulfill the requirement of perfect obedience, God has graciously lowered the standard. In neonomianism, the condition for justification is not the perfect righteousness of Christ alone, but rather "sincere obedience" or faith coupled with evangelical works.

This perspective gained significant attention during the 17th century in debates within Reformed and Puritan circles. Proponents of neonomianism argued that while we cannot keep the moral law perfectly, God accepts our faith and our sincere efforts to obey the commands of the Gospel as our qualifying righteousness.

However, the historic Christian faith, grounded in the Scriptures, rejects neonomianism because it undermines the doctrine of justification by faith alone. The Bible teaches that the law of God remains a standard of absolute holiness that no fallen human can meet (Romans 3:19-20). If our acceptance before God depended even partially on the sincerity or quality of our own obedience, we would remain under a burden of guilt and uncertainty.

The true Gospel is not a "new law" that we must fulfill to be saved, but the good news of what Jesus Christ has already fulfilled on our behalf. God is perfectly holy and just (Habakkuk 1:13), and because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him and deserve His judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life of obedience that we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the wrath of God against our sin (2 Corinthians 5:21). He was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, conquering sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

We are justified (declared righteous) before God not by any works or "sincere obedience" of our own, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; Galatians 2:16). When we trust in Christ, His perfect righteousness is credited to us, and our sins are forgiven because they were laid on Him. Good works and obedience are the necessary fruits and evidence of a living faith, but they are never the ground or cause of our salvation (James 2:17-18).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What is your current understanding of how a person is made right with God?
What does it mean that neither height nor depth separates us from the love of God (Romans 8:39)?

In Romans 8:39, the phrase "neither height nor depth" serves as a powerful expression of the total security believers have in the love of God. To understand this, it is helpful to look at how the Apostle Paul uses these terms within the context of the entire passage.

In the ancient world, "height" and "depth" often referred to the extremes of the visible universe or the highest and lowest points of the heavens and the earth. By using these two opposites, Paul is employing a literary device called a merism, where two contrasting parts represent the whole. He is saying that nothing in the highest reaches of heaven or the deepest parts of the abyss, and nothing in between them, has the power to pull a believer away from God.

Some scholars also suggest these terms may have referred to spiritual forces or astrological powers that people in the first century feared. By stating that height and depth cannot separate us from God, Paul is assuring us that no spiritual power, planetary alignment, or supernatural force is sovereign over God’s redeeming grace (Romans 8:38; Ephesians 1:20-22).

It is vital to notice that this unbreakable love is specifically "in Christ Jesus our Lord" (Romans 8:39). This love is not a vague or generic sentiment, but a covenantal commitment rooted in the person and work of Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and just, our sin once separated us from Him (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). However, God demonstrated His love for us by sending His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (Romans 5:8). He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

For those who have placed their faith in Him, this means that salvation is not maintained by our own strength or efforts, but by God’s sovereign power. If you are in Christ, there is no place you can go and no power you can encounter that can sever your relationship with the Father (John 10:28-29). This provides immense comfort during times of suffering or spiritual struggle, reminding us that God’s hold on us is stronger than our hold on Him.

If you have not yet experienced this security, the Bible invites you to receive it as a gift. Salvation is not something we earn by being good, but something we receive by grace alone through faith alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the doctrine of preterition?

The doctrine of preterition refers to the sovereign act of God in which He chooses to pass over those who are not among the elect, leaving them in their natural state of sin and subject to His just judgment. This concept is a specific aspect of the broader doctrine of predestination. While election describes God's active and gracious choice to save certain individuals by His mercy, preterition describes His decision to withhold that saving grace from others, allowing them to follow their own rebellious wills.

Scripture teaches that God is the sovereign Potter who has authority over the clay, as seen in Romans 9:21. He has the right to make from the same lump one vessel for honorable use and another for dishonorable use. This decision is not based on any merit or lack thereof in the individual, but on the secret counsel of God's own will. Jesus expressed this reality when He thanked the Father for hiding certain truths from the wise and understanding while revealing them to little children in Matthew 11:25-26.

It is important to distinguish preterition from the active work of God in salvation. In election, God sovereignly intervenes to change a person's heart, but in preterition, He simply leaves the person to their own chosen path of disobedience. Therefore, those who are passed over are not treated unjustly, because all people have sinned and fallen short of the glory of God, as stated in Romans 3:23. Their final condemnation is a just consequence of their own sin and rejection of God. Jude 1:4 speaks of those who were long ago designated for this condemnation because of their ungodliness.

While these truths are profound and can be difficult to process, they point back to the holiness of God and the depth of our need for Him. Every human being is naturally separated from God by sin, but God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to Himself. He was buried and rose again on the third day, confirming His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works.

I invite you to turn from your sin today and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What thoughts or questions come to mind as you reflect on God's sovereignty and His invitation to trust Him?
Are there supposed to be any rituals in Christianity?

In Christianity, the term ritual is often understood through the biblical concept of ordinances or sacraments. These are specific outward practices that Jesus Christ commanded His followers to observe as signs of their faith and as means of spiritual encouragement. While Christianity is fundamentally a relationship with God through Jesus Christ rather than a system of merit-based ceremonies, there are two primary practices that the New Testament establishes for the church.

The first is baptism. Jesus commanded His disciples to baptize new believers in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). This practice involves immersing a believer in water as a public testimony of their faith. It symbolizes the believer's union with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4). It is an outward sign of the inward change that has already occurred through the work of the Holy Spirit.

The second practice is the Lord's Supper, also known as Communion. During His last meal with His disciples, Jesus took bread and wine and instructed them to eat and drink in remembrance of Him (Luke 22:19-20). This practice serves as a regular memorial of Christ's sacrificial death on the cross for the forgiveness of sins. It also allows believers to reflect on their ongoing dependence on Him and look forward to His return (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

It is important to distinguish these practices from rituals in some other religions where the act itself is believed to earn God's favor. In the Christian faith, these ordinances do not save a person. The Bible teaches that we are saved by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or ceremonies we perform (Ephesians 2:8-9). The rituals are responses of obedience and love for God, meant to strengthen the community of believers and point our hearts back to the Gospel.

The core of the Christian message is that God is perfectly holy, yet all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was bodily resurrected on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord and will one day return to judge the world and make all things new.

Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12). These church practices are beautiful because they point to this historical reality of what Jesus has done for us.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA), and what do they believe?

The Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) is a Protestant denomination that was formed in 1973. It is characterized by its commitment to being faithful to the Scriptures, true to the Reformed faith, and obedient to the Great Commission of Jesus Christ. It is one of the largest Reformed denominations in the United States and is known for its emphasis on the sovereignty of God and the authority of the Bible.

The beliefs of the PCA are rooted in the historic Christian faith and are specifically summarized in the Westminster Confession of Faith, along with the Westminster Larger and Shorter Catechisms.

**The Authority of Scripture**
The PCA believes that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God. It is the only perfect rule for what we are to believe and how we are to live (2 Timothy 3:16-17; 2 Peter 1:21).

**The Sovereignty of God**
A central theme in PCA theology is the absolute sovereignty of God over all creation, providence, and salvation. They believe that God works all things according to the counsel of His own will (Ephesians 1:11).

**Reformed Theology**
The PCA holds to what is often called "the doctrines of grace." This includes the belief that humanity is spiritually dead in sin and completely unable to save themselves (Ephesians 2:1-3). Salvation is seen as an act of God's free grace alone. They emphasize that God chooses to save people based on His mercy rather than any merit found in the individual (Romans 9:15-16).

**The Gospel and Justification**
At the heart of their teaching is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. They believe that Jesus, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for the sins of His people. He rose bodily from the dead on the third day and ascended into heaven (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). The PCA teaches that sinners are justified (declared righteous) by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone. This righteousness is not earned by our works but is credited to us when we trust in Jesus (Galatians 2:16; Ephesians 2:8-9).

**Church Governance**
The word "Presbyterian" refers to their form of government. The church is governed by "presbyters," or elders, who are elected by the congregation. This includes teaching elders (pastors) and ruling elders (lay leaders). These elders look after the spiritual health of the local church and participate in regional gatherings called presbyteries and an annual national gathering called the General Assembly (Acts 14:23; Titus 1:5).

**Sacraments and Covenant Theology**
The PCA practices two sacraments: Baptism and the Lord’s Supper. Following covenant theology, they believe that baptism is a sign and seal of God's covenant of grace and is administered to believers and their children (Acts 2:38-39). They view the Lord's Supper as a means of grace where believers spiritually feed upon Christ by faith (1 Corinthians 10:16).

**The Christian Life**
The PCA encourages believers to grow in holiness through the "means of grace," which include the preaching of the Word, prayer, and the sacraments. They emphasize the importance of the local church and the responsibility of every believer to share the Gospel with others (Matthew 28:18-20).

This message of grace is the most important truth any person can encounter. Because of our sin, we are separated from a holy God, but through the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ, there is full forgiveness and eternal life for all who believe. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is your current understanding of who Jesus is and what He has done?
Where is God now? Where is God when it hurts?

It is deeply painful when life feels overwhelming and God seems silent or distant. In those moments, it is natural to ask where He is and why He allows such heartaches. The Bible teaches that God is both high and lifted up in His holiness, yet intimately close to those who are suffering.

Scripture tells us that God is omnipresent, meaning there is nowhere you can go where He is not already present. Psalm 139:7-10 describes this beautifully, noting that whether we ascend to heaven or make our bed in the depths, God is there. He is the sovereign Creator who sustains all things by the word of His power, as noted in Hebrews 1:3.

When it hurts, God is not a distant observer. He is described as being "near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit" in Psalm 34:18. He does not watch your pain from a distance; rather, He entered into human suffering through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus is called a "man of sorrows and acquainted with grief" in Isaiah 53:3. Because of the incarnation, we have a Savior who can sympathize with our weaknesses and has been tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, as described in Hebrews 4:15.

The ultimate answer to where God is when it hurts is found at the Cross. God is holy and just, and our sin created a separation between us and Him. Yet, because of His great love, God the Father sent Jesus Christ to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. On the Cross, Jesus took the weight of human sin and the abandonment we deserved so that we would never have to be truly alone. 

God uses our trials to refine us and draw us closer to Himself, promising that "for those who love God all things work together for good," as written in Romans 8:28. He is with you in the fire and the deep waters, promising in Isaiah 43:2 that they will not consume you.

If you are feeling the weight of the world and the distance of God, the Gospel is the bridge that brings you back into His presence. Salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts or by being "good enough," but it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. Jesus died for your sins and rose to give you eternal life and a restored relationship with the Father.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What is it about your current situation that makes God feel the most distant to you right now?
What is the just war theory?

Just war theory is a tradition of ethical reflection that seeks to answer two fundamental questions: when is it morally right to go to war, and how should war be conducted? For centuries, Christian theologians and philosophers have used this framework to balance the biblical call to peace with the responsibility to protect the innocent and uphold justice in a fallen world.

The theory is generally divided into two categories. The first is "justice in entering war," which provides criteria for whether a conflict is morally sanctioned. These criteria include having a just cause, such as defending the weak or resisting an unprovoked attack; being declared by a legitimate authority, as God has ordained governments to maintain order; having a right intention focused on restoring peace rather than seeking vengeance; and ensuring that war is a last resort after all peaceful options have been exhausted. These principles are rooted in the understanding that while God loves peace, He also cares deeply for justice and the protection of life as seen in Genesis 9:6 and Romans 13:1-4.

The second category is "justice in conducting war." This focuses on how a military should behave during a conflict. Key principles include discrimination, which means distinguishing between combatants and innocent civilians, and proportionality, which requires that the amount of force used is not greater than what is necessary to achieve the just objective. These guidelines reflect the biblical command to value all human life and to act with restraint even in the midst of judgment.

While many Christians hold to just war theory, others believe in pacifism, pointing to Jesus' call to "turn the other cheek" in Matthew 5:39 and to be peacemakers in Matthew 5:9. However, proponents of just war theory argue that the government has a specific duty to "bear the sword" to restrain evil and protect the vulnerable, as described in Romans 13:4. Scripture encourages us to live peaceably with all if possible, as stated in Romans 12:18, but it also recognizes the necessity of force to curb the effects of sin in a fallen society.

Ultimately, even the most "just" war is a tragic reminder that we live in a world broken by sin and separated from God. This reality points us to our need for Jesus Christ, the Prince of Peace. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Through Him, God offers reconciliation and the hope of a future world where war will be no more. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What are your thoughts on how a believer should balance the command to love their enemies with the duty to protect the innocent?
What does it mean that women should have a gentle and quiet spirit (1 Peter 3:4)?

In 1 Peter 3:4, the apostle Peter describes the "imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit," noting that it is "very precious in God's sight." To understand this, we must look at the character of the heart rather than just outward behavior.

A gentle spirit refers to a disposition of meekness or humility. In the original language, this word describes strength under control. It is not a sign of weakness or a lack of conviction, but rather a refusal to be vindictive or demanding. This is the same word Jesus used to describe Himself in Matthew 11:29, when He said He is "gentle and lowly in heart." A person with a gentle spirit does not feel the need to push their own way through pride or manipulation, but instead trusts in God's sovereign care.

A quiet spirit refers to an inner tranquility and peace. This is not necessarily about the volume of one's voice or the amount of talking one does, but about the stillness of the soul. It describes a heart that is not easily ruffled, agitated, or prone to fearful outbursts. As Peter mentions in the surrounding verses, this spirit is rooted in a deep trust in God (1 Peter 3:5). When a person rests in the fact that God is in control, they can maintain a calm and peaceful demeanor even in difficult circumstances.

Peter contrasts this inner beauty with external adornments like braided hair or gold jewelry (1 Peter 3:3). While he is not forbidding these things, he is teaching that true, lasting beauty comes from the "hidden person of the heart" (1 Peter 3:4). This spirit is considered imperishable because, unlike physical beauty which fades with time, the character formed by the Holy Spirit grows more beautiful as we walk with Christ.

Ultimately, this gentle and quiet spirit is a reflection of Jesus Christ Himself. While we all struggle with pride, anxiety, and a desire for control, the Gospel offers us a way to be transformed. God is perfectly holy and just (Psalm 145:17), yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from Him and deserve His judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His resurrection, He demonstrated His victory over sin and death and is now Lord of all.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or efforts (Ephesians 2:8-9). When we trust in Him, the Holy Spirit begins to work in our hearts, producing the fruit of gentleness and peace (Galatians 5:22-23).

I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the gift of new life. Have you ever considered how trusting in Jesus' finished work might change the way you view your own inner life?
What does it mean to be free from sin?

To be free from sin means that through Jesus Christ, the power that sin once held over your life has been broken. In the Bible, freedom from sin is described in three distinct ways: freedom from its penalty, freedom from its power, and eventually, freedom from its very presence.

God is perfectly holy and just (Habakkuk 1:13). Because of our sin, every human being is naturally separated from God and faces the just penalty of death (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life that we could not live. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). He is now exalted as Lord over all. This salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we do (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

**Freedom from the Penalty of Sin**
When you trust in Christ, you are justified, which means God declares you righteous. The legal debt you owed for your sins is paid in full. There is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1). You are reconciled to God, moving from a state of spiritual death to eternal life (John 5:24).

**Freedom from the Power of Sin**
In this life, being free from sin means you are no longer a slave to it. Before knowing Christ, we were unable to truly please God because sin was our master (John 8:34). Now, the Holy Spirit lives within believers, giving them the desire and the strength to resist temptation. While Christians still struggle with sin and are not yet perfect, sin no longer has the final word or the rightful authority over their lives (Romans 6:6; Romans 6:7; Romans 6:14). We are free to serve God in the "new way of the Spirit" (Romans 7:6).

**Freedom from the Presence of Sin**
Christians also hold a future hope. When Christ returns, He will renew all things. Believers will receive glorified bodies that are no longer subject to temptation, decay, or death. In that day, we will be free from the very presence of sin forever (Revelation 21:3; Revelation 21:4).

You can turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive this forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is God man-made? Did man create the idea of God?

The historic Christian faith, grounded in the Bible, teaches that God is not a product of human imagination. Instead, God is the eternal, self-existent Creator of all things, including humanity. In the very first verse of the Scriptures, we are told, "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth" (Genesis 1:1). Rather than man creating the idea of God, the Bible explains that God created man in His own image (Genesis 1:26-27).

If God were merely a human invention, we might expect Him to be a reflection of our own desires, comforts, or limitations. However, the God revealed in the Bible is holy, supreme, and sovereign, possessing a nature that is often far beyond human comprehension. He is described as the "everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth," who "does not faint or grow weary" (Isaiah 40:28). The Bible suggests that every person has an innate awareness of God because He has made His existence evident through the things He has created (Romans 1:19-20). This internal "sense of the divine" is not an invention of the mind, but a response to the reality of a Creator who is actually there.

This Creator is not distant or silent. Because God is holy and we have all sinned by turning away from His good design, we find ourselves separated from Him. Yet, out of His great love, God did not leave us in our separation. He entered into human history in the person of Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming that He is Lord over all.

Salvation and a relationship with this eternal Creator are not earned by our own efforts or "goodness," but are received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What makes you wonder if the idea of God originated with humans rather than with God Himself?
What is the significance of the statement “God is within her; she will not fall” in Psalm 46:5?

The statement "God is within her; she will not fall" from Psalm 46:5 carries a profound message of security and divine protection. In its original biblical context, the "her" refers to the city of God, which is Zion or Jerusalem. The psalmist is describing the city as the dwelling place of the Most High, providing a stark contrast to the chaos and instability of the surrounding world described in the opening of the psalm (Psalm 46:2; Psalm 46:3).

The significance of this verse lies in the source of the city's stability. It is not the strength of the fortifications or the size of the army that prevents the city from falling, but the personal presence of God Himself. Because God is "within her," the city remains unmoved even when the earth gives way or the mountains tremble. This verse emphasizes that God’s presence is a constant, immediate source of help, and that His deliverance is certain, as He helps "at break of day" (Psalm 46:5).

For believers today, this passage remains deeply encouraging because it points to the security we have in God through Jesus Christ. While the original text speaks of a physical city, the New Testament reveals that God now dwells within His people collectively as the church and individually through the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 3:16; 1 Corinthians 6:19). Just as the city of God was secure because of His presence, the believer is kept by God's power and will not finally fall away (1 Peter 1:5; Jude 1:24). This does not mean that Christians are exempt from suffering or trials, but it does mean that in the midst of them, we are not overcome because God is our refuge and strength (Psalm 46:1).

Ultimately, this security is found only in the person and work of Jesus Christ. Scripture teaches that we are all separated from God by our sin (Romans 3:23), but Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Peter 2:24). He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death and providing the only way for us to be reconciled to God (1 Corinthians 15:4; Romans 5:1).

Through faith in Christ alone, you can experience the peace of God's presence and the assurance that He will never leave you. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about worship?

The Bible presents worship as the proper response of a created being to the glory, holiness, and love of the Creator. While many people today associate worship primarily with music or singing, the Scriptures describe it as a comprehensive lifestyle of honoring God in all that we do.

At its core, worship is about acknowledging the "worth-ship" of God. This begins with recognizing who He is. The Bible teaches that there is only one God, existing eternally as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, and He alone is worthy of our devotion. Jesus affirmed this when He told His followers that they must worship the Lord their God and serve Him only (Matthew 4:10).

In the Old Testament, worship often involved specific rituals, sacrifices, and a central location like the Temple. However, Jesus introduced a significant shift in how we approach God. He explained that a time was coming, and has now come, when true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth (John 4:23-24). This means that worship is no longer tied to a physical mountain or building, but is a matter of the heart, directed by the truth of God's Word and empowered by the Holy Spirit.

The Apostle Paul further explains that worship involves our entire existence. He urges believers to present their bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God, noting that this is our true and proper worship (Romans 12:1). This implies that every area of life, including our work, our relationships, and our private thoughts, can be an act of worship when done for God’s glory (1 Corinthians 10:31).

Furthermore, the Bible emphasizes the importance of corporate worship, where believers gather together as the church. We are encouraged to sing to one another with psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, making music from our hearts to the Lord (Ephesians 5:19). These gatherings are meant to stir up love and good works, ensuring that we do not neglect meeting together (Hebrews 10:24-25).

True worship is only possible through the work of Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and we are fallen, our sin separates us from Him. We cannot offer acceptable worship on our own merits. However, the Gospel tells us that God loved us so much that He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave. By His sacrifice, He paid the debt for our sins and reconciled us to God. 

When we place our faith in Christ, we are justified by grace and granted access to the Father. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him and entering into a life of worship today?
What is more important, the death of Christ or His resurrection?

In the wisdom of God, the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ are two inseparable parts of a single work of redemption. One cannot be more important than the other because they are functionally and doctrinally united in the Gospel. To remove either is to lose the whole of the Christian faith.

The death of Christ is the means by which our sins are paid for and the justice of God is satisfied. Because God is perfectly holy and just, He cannot simply overlook sin. On the cross, Jesus Christ took the wrath of God that we deserved, acting as our substitute so that we could be forgiven (Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 2:24). Scripture teaches that without the shedding of blood, there is no forgiveness of sins (Hebrews 9:22). In His death, Jesus finished the work of atonement, paying the debt we could never pay.

The resurrection of Christ is the victory that proves His sacrifice was accepted by the Father and that He is truly who He claimed to be. It is the physical, bodily demonstration that He has conquered sin, Satan, and death. The Apostle Paul emphasizes the absolute necessity of the resurrection when he writes that if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is in vain and our faith is futile, and we are still in our sins (1 Corinthians 15:14; 1 Corinthians 15:17). The resurrection justifies the believer, serving as the divine receipt that the price for sin has been paid in full (Romans 4:25).

This is the core of the Gospel message: God is holy and supreme, yet humanity is separated from Him by sin. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is not earned by our works or moral efforts, but is a gift secured by Christ and received by grace alone through faith alone in Him.

You can turn from sin today and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What is your current understanding of who Jesus is and what He has done for you?
What is the emerging / emergent church movement?

The emerging or emergent church movement refers to a diverse trend that arose in the late 1990s and early 2000s, primarily in Western cultures. It began as a reaction among Christians who felt that the traditional and modern church models, especially the "seeker-sensitive" megachurch approach, were failing to reach a postmodern generation. 

At its core, the movement sought to engage a culture that had become skeptical of absolute truth and traditional institutions. Those involved often emphasized "conversation" over "declaration" and "authenticity" over "performance." While the movement was broad and difficult to define, it generally split into two distinct paths.

The first path, often called the "emerging" church, focused primarily on style and mission. These believers wanted to remain faithful to historic Christian doctrines while changing how the church looked and functioned. They often met in coffee shops or homes, used art and liturgy in new ways, and focused heavily on social justice and community involvement. Their goal was to be "missional," living out the Gospel in their local contexts as described in Matthew 28:18-20 and Acts 1:8.

The second path, often referred to as the "emergent" church, went further by questioning the traditional interpretations of Christian doctrine. This branch of the movement was heavily influenced by postmodern philosophy, which views truth as something shaped by community and personal experience rather than objective, universal facts. In this circle, some began to deconstruct or doubt essential biblical truths, such as the authority of Scripture, the necessity of Christ’s substitutionary atonement, and the reality of final judgment.

While the movement encouraged a helpful emphasis on humility and serving the poor, many of its more radical ideas came into conflict with the Word of God. The Bible warns believers to "contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints" in Jude 1:3. It also instructs us in 2 Timothy 4:2-4 to "preach the word" and remain "sober-minded" because there will be times when people will not endure sound teaching but will instead look for teachers who suit their own passions.

In the historic Christian faith, we believe that truth is not something we create or discover within ourselves, but something God has revealed through His Son and His Word. The authority of Scripture is the foundation for our faith and practice, as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16-17. 

The heart of that truth is the Gospel: that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, as noted in Romans 3:23. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a sacrifice for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, which is described in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Salvation is not something we can earn through our own works or cultural relevance, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, as taught in Ephesians 2:8-9.

If you find yourself searching for truth or feeling disillusioned by religious traditions, I encourage you to look to Jesus Christ Himself. You can turn to Him now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Him for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most challenging about the idea of absolute truth in today’s world?
What does the Bible say about underage drinking?

The Bible addresses the topic of alcohol and the conduct of young people through several principles involving obedience to authority, self-control, and the stewardship of one's body.

First, Scripture commands believers to obey the laws of their government. Romans 13:1 states, "Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God." Because the law in many places sets a minimum age for consuming alcohol, a Christian honors God by following these regulations. Similarly, 1 Peter 2:13–14 encourages believers to "Be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution."

Second, for those who are still under the care of their parents, the Bible is clear about the role of a child's obedience. Ephesians 6:1 says, "Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right." If a parent forbids drinking, a child is biblically obligated to respect that boundary.

Third, the Bible consistently warns against the loss of self-control associated with alcohol. Ephesians 5:18 commands, "And do not get drunk with wine, for that is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit." Proverbs 20:1 adds, "Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise." Since young people are often still developing in judgment and maturity, the risks of being led astray by alcohol are significant.

Furthermore, the Bible teaches that a believer’s body is a gift to be used for God's glory. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20 explains, "Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own, for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body." This means making choices that promote health and spiritual clarity rather than engaging in behaviors that could lead to addiction, impaired judgment, or legal consequences.

While we strive to live uprightly, we must recognize that our moral efforts cannot reconcile us to a holy God. We have all fallen short of His standards and are separated from Him by our sin (Romans 3:23). However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day. Through His victory over sin and death, salvation is offered as a free gift. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or rule-keeping.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What are your thoughts on how following God's Word might change the way you view these types of choices?
Will there be a great apostasy / falling away during the end times?

Scripture indicates that a significant falling away or apostasy will occur as the return of Christ approaches. The Apostle Paul specifically mentions this in 2 Thessalonians 2:3, stating that the day of the Lord will not come unless the rebellion, or falling away, comes first. This suggests a period where many who once associated with the faith or the church will turn away from the truth of the Gospel.

Jesus also spoke of this in Matthew 24:10-12, warning that many will turn away from the faith, betray one another, and that because of the increase of wickedness, the love of most will grow cold. Similarly, Paul warned Timothy that in later times some will abandon the faith to follow deceiving spirits and things taught by demons in 1 Timothy 4:1. This apostasy involves a rejection of sound doctrine in favor of teachings that suit personal desires as described in 2 Timothy 4:3-4.

While these warnings are sobering, they serve to encourage believers to remain watchful and rooted in the Word of God. The Bible also provides great comfort for those who truly belong to Christ. Jesus promised that His sheep hear His voice and that no one can snatch them out of His hand in John 10:27-28. For the believer, the falling away of others is a call to persevere in grace, resting in the power of the Holy Spirit to keep us until the end.

Ultimately, our hope is not in our own ability to stay faithful, but in the finished work of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy and just, yet because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers salvation to all who believe.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by our own works or merits. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Should all pronouns referring to God be capitalized?

The question of whether to capitalize pronouns referring to God, such as He, Him, or His, is a matter of personal conviction and stylistic choice rather than a direct biblical command. In the history of the English language and Bible translation, there have been different approaches to this practice.

Many believers choose to capitalize these pronouns as a way to show deep reverence and to clearly distinguish God from human characters in a text. This practice is seen in translations like the New King James Version or the New American Standard Bible. For these Christians, it is an act of worship and a way to highlight the supremacy and holiness of God, who is described as being high and lifted up in Isaiah 6:1.

On the other hand, many other translations, such as the English Standard Version, the King James Version, and the New International Version, do not capitalize these pronouns. This is because the original biblical languages, Hebrew and Koine Greek, did not have a system of capitalization that distinguished between divine and human pronouns. Those who follow this approach often feel that it stays truest to the original text and avoids adding emphasis that the inspired authors did not explicitly include.

Because the Bible does not give a specific rule on this, it falls into the category of Christian liberty. The Apostle Paul writes in Romans 14:5 that each person should be fully convinced in their own mind. Whether you choose to capitalize pronouns or not, the most important thing is the posture of your heart toward God. Colossians 3:17 encourages believers that whatever they do, in word or deed, they should do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through Him.

Ultimately, our reverence for God is expressed most clearly through our faith and obedience. While we honor Him with our writing, we are called to honor Him primarily by recognizing His holiness and our need for His grace. God is perfectly holy, and because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him. However, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything specific about God's character that makes you want to show Him this kind of special honor?
What is Kabbalah?

Kabbalah is an esoteric school of thought and a form of mysticism rooted in Jewish tradition. It seeks to define the relationship between the eternal, mysterious, and infinite God (referred to as Ein Sof) and the finite, mortal universe that He created. While it draws from the Hebrew Scriptures, it interprets them through a lens of hidden meanings and symbolic structures that are not found in the plain text of the Bible.

Central to Kabbalistic thought is the concept of the "Sephirot," which are ten attributes or emanations through which God reveals Himself and continuously creates the universe. Practitioners often focus on the Zohar, a collection of commentaries written in the 13th century, which serves as a primary text for understanding these mystical interpretations. Much of Kabbalah involves the idea that human actions, specifically through rituals and spiritual focus, can help "repair" the world and influence the divine realm.

From a biblical Christian perspective, there are fundamental differences between Kabbalistic teachings and the truth revealed in Scripture. The Bible emphasizes that God is a personal, triune Being (Father, Son, and Holy Spirit) who has made Himself known clearly to us. We do not need secret or hidden knowledge to find Him, for God has spoken finally and fully through His Son, Jesus Christ, as stated in Hebrews 1:1-2.

Furthermore, while Kabbalah often focuses on human effort or mystical experiences to achieve spiritual elevation, the Bible teaches that our primary problem is not a lack of knowledge, but our separation from God due to sin (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). God is perfectly holy and just, and His wrath against sin is real. However, because God is also merciful and loving, He provided the only way for us to be reconciled to Himself.

The heart of the Gospel is that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, not by any works or hidden wisdom we might possess (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a sacrificial, substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This finished work is sufficient to reconcile any person to God.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. In Him, all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden, and we find everything we need for a restored relationship with our Creator (Colossians 2:3). What questions does this raise for you about how we can truly know God?
Should a Christian be a prepper or in any way involved with doomsday prepping?

The question of whether a Christian should be a prepper is largely a matter of Christian liberty and wisdom. Scripture does not give a direct command for or against storing supplies for future hardships, but it does provide several principles to guide our hearts and actions.

On one hand, the Bible commends the wisdom of foresight and stewardship. Proverbs 6:6-8 points to the ant as an example of wisdom because it gathers its food in the harvest and prepares for the winter. Similarly, Proverbs 22:3 says that a prudent person sees danger and hides himself, but the simple go on and suffer for it. Taking reasonable steps to provide for the physical needs of your family is described as a good and necessary thing in 1 Timothy 5:8. When done with the right heart, being prepared allows a believer to be a resource for others during a crisis, following the exhortation in Galatians 6:10 to do good to everyone as we have opportunity.

On the other hand, the primary danger in "doomsday prepping" is the temptation to shift our trust from God to our own resources. Jesus warns His followers in Matthew 6:25-34 not to be anxious about tomorrow or what they will eat or wear, reminding them that their heavenly Father knows their needs. If prepping is driven by a spirit of fear or a desire for total self-sufficiency, it can become a form of idolatry. Psalm 20:7 reminds us that while some trust in chariots and some in horses, we trust in the name of the Lord our God. We must also avoid the hoarding mentality seen in the parable of the rich fool in Luke 12:16-21, who stored up treasures for himself but was not rich toward God.

A Christian's approach to preparation should be characterized by trust in God's sovereignty, a lack of fear, and a heart ready to share with those in need. Whether you have a week's worth of food or a year's worth, your ultimate security must be found in Christ.

The most important preparation any person can make is not for a temporal social collapse, but for eternity. We are all sinners who have fallen short of the holiness of God and are separated from Him. However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to earth. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we can do.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What is it that makes you feel most concerned about the future?
What is the church?

The church is not a building or a physical location, but rather the assembly of people who have been called out by God and joined to Jesus Christ through faith. The Bible uses the Greek word *ekklesia*, which means "assembly" or "congregation," to describe this community of believers.

The church is often understood in two ways. First, there is the universal church, which consists of every person throughout history and across the globe who truly belongs to Christ (Ephesians 1:22-23; Hebrews 12:23). Second, there is the local church, which is a visible, specific gathering of believers in a particular place who meet regularly to worship God, hear the preaching of His Word, and observe the ordinances of baptism and the Lord's Supper (Acts 2:42; Hebrews 10:24-25).

Scripture provides several beautiful metaphors to help us understand the nature and role of the church:

The Body of Christ: This highlights the unity and diversity of the church. Just as a human body has many parts with different functions, every believer has a unique role and gift to use for the good of the whole, while Christ remains the head who directs and sustains the body (1 Corinthians 12:12-27; Colossians 1:18).

The Bride of Christ: This illustrates the deep, sacrificial love Christ has for His people and the purity and devotion the church is called to maintain for Him (Ephesians 5:25-27; Revelation 19:7-9).

The Temple of the Holy Spirit: Under the New Covenant, God does not dwell in a building made by hands but lives within His people. Together, believers are being built into a spiritual house where God's presence dwells (1 Corinthians 3:16; 1 Peter 2:5).

The primary purpose of the church is to glorify God. We do this by building one another up in the faith through fellowship and teaching, and by fulfilling the Great Commission, which is the call to go into all the world to make disciples and share the good news of salvation (Matthew 28:19-20; Ephesians 4:11-13).

At its heart, the church is a family of redeemed sinners. Though God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin (Isaiah 6:3; Romans 3:23), Jesus Christ took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Now, anyone who turns to Him in faith is justified by grace alone and brought into this spiritual family.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is your current understanding of who Jesus is?
Does a cashless society have anything to do with the end times?

The Bible does not use the specific term "cashless society," but many Christians look toward the book of Revelation when considering how modern economic shifts might relate to the end times. The most relevant passage is found in Revelation 13:16-17, which describes a future period where a global leader, often called the Antichrist, exerts total control over the economy. The text states that he causes everyone to receive a mark on their right hand or their forehead, so that no one can buy or sell unless they have the mark (Revelation 13:16-17).

Because a cashless system allows for every transaction to be tracked, restricted, or blocked by a central authority, many believers see the transition away from physical currency as a possible preparation for the economic environment described in Scripture. In a society where digital transactions are the only option, it becomes technologically possible for a government to prevent individuals from participating in the economy if they do not comply with certain requirements.

However, Christians hold a variety of views on this topic. Some see digital technology as the literal fulfillment of these prophecies, while others view the descriptions in Revelation as symbolic of a deeper spiritual allegiance. It is important to remember that throughout history, different generations have identified various technologies or political systems as signs of the end. While we should be discerning and aware of the times (Matthew 16:3), our primary focus should not be on fear or speculation, but on the sovereignty of God.

God remains in control of history, and He calls His people to live with wisdom and trust rather than anxiety. Whether the world uses coins, paper, or digital numbers, the underlying spiritual reality remains the same: we are called to be faithful to Christ regardless of the pressures of the world (Proverbs 3:5-6).

Discussions about the end of the age often lead us to reflect on our own standing before God. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3), yet all of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from Him. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works or efforts (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider these things, what holds you back from trusting Him today? If you are feeling concerned about the future, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John to learn more about the peace that Jesus offers, or to connect with a local church where you can study the Bible with other believers. Seeking the counsel of a pastor can also provide great comfort and clarity during times of uncertainty.
I am a former Catholic. Should I continue to go to Catholic Church so I can reach people for Christ?

It is a beautiful and godly thing to have a deep burden for the people you have known and loved within the Catholic Church. Your desire to see them come to a clear understanding of the Gospel reflects the heart of the Apostle Paul, who expressed a great sorrow and unceasing anguish for his own people, desiring that they might be saved (Romans 10:1).

When considering whether to remain in a specific church for the sake of outreach, there are several biblical principles to weigh. First, your primary responsibility as a believer is to be part of a local body of Christ where the Word of God is rightly preached and the Gospel is clearly proclaimed. We all need the consistent "pure milk of the word" to grow in our salvation (1 Peter 2:2). If a church’s official teachings or liturgy add human works, merit, or additional mediators to the finished work of Christ, it can be spiritually draining and confusing for you to remain there as your primary place of worship.

There is also the matter of your testimony. If you continue to participate in the rituals of a system you have doctrinally left, you may inadvertently communicate to your friends and family that the differences in the Gospel are not significant. Clarity is often the greatest kindness in evangelism. By joining a church that firmly holds to the authority of Scripture alone and justification by grace alone through faith alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; Galatians 2:16), you provide a visible markers of the change God has worked in your heart.

You can be an effective witness to your Catholic friends without being a member of their parish. In fact, being firmly planted in a healthy, Gospel-centered church provides you with the spiritual nourishment and community support necessary to sustain long-term outreach. You can reach them through hospitality, personal conversations, and by sharing how the assurance of Christ’s finished work has changed your life.

The heart of the message we share is that God is perfectly holy, and we are all separated from Him by our sin (Isaiah 59:2). No amount of religious ritual or human effort can bridge that gap. However, God, in His mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the full penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Jesus is the only way to the Father, and there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved (John 14:6; Acts 4:12).

Salvation is a free gift received not by our own doing, but through faith in what Christ has already accomplished. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you navigate these relationships, you might find it helpful to seek the counsel of a pastor at a local Gospel-preaching church who can walk with you through the transition. What specific fears or hesitations do you feel when you think about fully committing to a new church community?
What does the Bible say about world missions?

The Bible reveals that world missions is not merely a human program, but the central heartbeat of God’s plan to redeem a people for Himself from every corner of the earth. This mission begins in the Old Testament and finds its fulfillment in the person and work of Jesus Christ.

**God’s Heart for All Nations**
From the beginning, God intended for His blessing to extend beyond a single group of people. When God called Abraham, He promised that through him all the families of the earth would be blessed (Genesis 12:3). The Psalms echo this global vision, calling for God’s way to be known on earth and His saving power among all nations (Psalm 67:2). The prophets also spoke of a day when the light of God’s salvation would reach the ends of the earth (Isaiah 49:6).

**The Command of Jesus**
World missions is rooted in the authority and command of Jesus Christ. After His resurrection, Jesus gave what is known as the Great Commission. He commanded His followers to go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:18–20). This work is not done in human strength, but by the power of the Holy Spirit, who empowers believers to be witnesses from their local communities to the remotest parts of the earth (Acts 1:8).

**The Message of the Mission**
The core of world missions is the proclamation of the Gospel. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, yet all humanity has sinned and fallen short of His glory, resulting in spiritual separation and judgment. Because of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and cannot be earned by human effort or good works.

**A Gentle Invitation**
Because of what Jesus has done, you can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. Is there anything specific that makes you hesitant to trust Him today?

**The Final Vision**
The ultimate goal of missions is the glory of God. The Bible provides a glimpse into the future, showing a great multitude that no one can number, from every nation, tribe, people, and language, standing before the throne of God and worshiping Him (Revelation 7:9). Missions will continue until every person God has called has heard the good news and the end comes (Matthew 24:14).

**The Role of the Church**
For the believer, missions involves both going and sending. Some are called to cross cultural and geographic boundaries to preach where Christ is not known (Romans 15:20), while others support the work through prayer, financial giving, and local evangelism (Philippians 4:15–17; 3 John 1:5–8). Every believer is called to play a part in the global spread of the Gospel, resting in the promise that Jesus is with His people always, even to the end of the age (Matthew 28:20).
How should we live our lives in light of our identity in Christ?

Living in light of our identity in Christ begins with the understanding that we are no longer defined by our past, our failures, or our own efforts. Because of God's great mercy, those who trust in Jesus are adopted into His family and given a completely new standing. As 2 Corinthians 5:17 states, "Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come."

To live out this identity, we must first rest in our justification. We live from a place of acceptance rather than for acceptance. Romans 5:1 teaches, "Therefore, since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." When we understand that our standing before God is secure because of Christ's perfect work, our daily obedience becomes a joyful response of love and gratitude rather than a fearful attempt to earn His favor.

Living in our new identity also involves a process of transformation called sanctification. Scripture instructs us to "put off" our old self and "put on" the new self. Colossians 3:1-4 encourages believers by saying, "If then you have been raised with Christ, seek the things that are above, where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God. Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth. For you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God." This means we actively turn away from sin and toward righteousness, trusting the Holy Spirit to conform us to the image of Jesus. Romans 6:11 reminds us to "consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus."

Furthermore, our identity gives us a specific purpose. We are not just saved from sin; we are saved for good works and for God's glory. Ephesians 2:10 explains, "For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them." Our lives should reflect the character of our Father through acts of service, kindness, and sharing the truth of the Word with others.

This identity is only possible because of the Gospel. God is holy and just, but because of our sin, we were once separated from Him. However, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation and this new identity are gifts of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him and finding your identity in Him today?
How do I know the Bible is not just mythology?

Distinguishing the Bible from mythology is a profound journey that involves looking at history, archaeology, and the internal consistency of the Scriptures. While myths often describe events in a vague or "timeless" past with characters who lack historical context, the Bible is deeply rooted in real-world history, naming specific people, places, and political rulers that have been verified by external sources.

One of the primary ways we see this is through the eyewitness nature of the writings. For example, the apostle Peter wrote that the believers did not follow "cleverly devised myths" but were instead "eyewitnesses of his majesty" in 2 Peter 1:16. Similarly, the Gospel of Luke begins by stating that the author carefully investigated everything from the beginning to write an orderly account based on those who were eyewitnesses from the start in Luke 1:1-4.

Archaeology also provides significant support for the biblical narrative. For many years, critics doubted the existence of certain people groups or leaders mentioned in the Bible, such as the Hittites or King David. However, archaeological discoveries, like the Tel Dan Stele which mentions the "House of David," have repeatedly confirmed the historical setting of the biblical text. The Bible contains thousands of manuscripts that are far more numerous and closer to the original events than any other ancient piece of literature, which gives us great confidence that the text we have today is what was originally written.

Most importantly, the Bible tells a unified story of God’s redemptive work through Jesus Christ. It describes God as perfectly holy, supreme, and just, as seen in Isaiah 6:3 and Psalm 89:14. It also honestly describes the human condition, noting that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God in Romans 3:23. Because of this sin, we are separated from a holy God, but God in His great love did not leave us without hope.

He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life that we could not live. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins and was buried, but on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death, according to 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. This resurrection is not a mythological symbol; it is a historical claim supported by hundreds of witnesses as noted in 1 Corinthians 15:5-8.

Salvation is not earned by our own good works or moral efforts, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, as explained in Ephesians 2:8-9. This message of the Gospel is the heartbeat of the Bible, showing that God is both a just judge and a merciful Savior.

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him or believing that these historical accounts are true?
What does it mean that the Bible is infallible? What is biblical infallibility?

Biblical infallibility is the belief that the Bible is completely trustworthy and incapable of failing in its purpose. When we say the Scriptures are infallible, we mean that they are a perfect and certain guide in all matters of faith and practice. This doctrine rests on the character of God Himself, who is the ultimate author of the Bible. Because God is holy, true, and cannot lie, the Word He has breathed out is necessarily reliable and true.

While the term is often used interchangeably with "inerrancy," there is a helpful distinction between the two. Inerrancy specifically refers to the fact that the original manuscripts of the Bible do not contain any errors or falsehoods in what they affirm. Infallibility goes a step further by emphasizing that the Bible cannot fail or mislead those who follow its teachings. It is the solid foundation upon which a believer can build their entire life, knowing that God's promises will never prove false.

The Bible makes these claims for itself in several places. The apostle Paul wrote that all Scripture is breathed out by God and is profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness (2 Timothy 3:16). Because it originates with God, it possesses His authority. The psalmist declares that the sum of God's word is truth and every one of His righteous rules endures forever (Psalm 119:160). Jesus Himself affirmed the absolute reliability of the Scriptures when He said that until heaven and earth pass away, not an iota, not a dot, will pass from the Law until all is accomplished (Matthew 5:18).

This reliability is not a result of human wisdom, but of the Holy Spirit's work. The apostle Peter explained that no prophecy of Scripture comes from someone's own interpretation, for no prophecy was ever produced by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit (2 Peter 1:20-21). This divine oversight ensures that the message of God reached us exactly as He intended.

Understanding the Bible as infallible is vital because it points us to the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Scriptures are not merely a collection of historical facts or moral codes, they are the testimony of God's plan to rescue humanity from sin. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, but because of our sin, we are separated from Him. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Because the Bible is infallible, we can have complete confidence in the Gospel it proclaims. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9). If the Bible could fail in its message, our hope for eternity would be uncertain, but since God’s Word is sure, we can rest in His promises.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Have you ever considered what it might mean for your life if everything the Bible says about Jesus is true?
What does the Bible mean when it says something is unclean?
