What do you think about the Bible codes? Is there any validity to them?

The topic of "Bible codes," often referred to as Equidistant Letter Sequencing (ELS), has fascinated many people over the years. This method involves using computers to find words or phrases by skipping a fixed number of letters in the Hebrew text of the Old Testament. While the idea of hidden messages can seem intriguing, there are several important biblical and practical reasons to approach this concept with a high degree of caution and skepticism.

The primary concern many Christians and biblical scholars share is that the Bible is intended to be a clear revelation from God to humanity. This is sometimes called the "perspicuity" or clarity of Scripture. God has spoken to us through the plain meaning of the text, historical events, and most fully through His Son, Jesus Christ. When we focus on finding hidden codes, we risk ignoring the clear and life-changing message that God has already provided on the surface of the pages. Deuteronomy 29:29 tells us that the secret things belong to the Lord our God, but the things revealed belong to us and to our children forever.

From a practical standpoint, many mathematicians have demonstrated that similar "codes" can be found in almost any large work of literature, such as "Moby Dick" or "War and Peace," if one looks long enough using complex computer algorithms. Furthermore, the "codes" are highly sensitive to the specific manuscript used. Because there are minor variations in spelling and letter counts across different ancient Hebrew manuscripts, a code that appears in one version of the text might completely disappear in another.

True spiritual depth is not found in uncovering secret puzzles, but in knowing the Person to whom the whole Bible points. The Scriptures are not a book of magic or a cryptic riddle to be solved, but the living Word of God meant to lead us to salvation and godliness. As Jesus said in John 5:39, the Scriptures testify about Him.

The most important "code" or message in the Bible is not hidden at all, it is the Gospel, which is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes as stated in Romans 1:16. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory as seen in Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we are separated from God. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death according to 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Salvation is a gift given by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone. It is not earned by our own good works or by finding secret knowledge. You can turn to God right now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

Rather than searching for hidden codes, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the plain teaching of the Bible, which is profitable for teaching, rebuking, correcting, and training in righteousness as written in 2 Timothy 3:16. What holds you back from trusting in the clear promises of Christ found in His Word?
How can I see the hand of God moving in my life?

It is a beautiful and biblical desire to want to see the hand of God moving in your life. While God is always present and active, our ability to recognize His work often depends on where we are looking and the "lens" through which we view our circumstances.

One of the most consistent ways to see God's hand is through the lens of His Word. The Bible is the primary way God reveals His character and His purposes to us. When you spend time in Scripture, you begin to recognize His "voice" and His "fingerprints" in the world around you. Psalm 119:105 describes the Word as a lamp to our feet and a light to our path, meaning it illuminates the steps God is asking us to take.

You can also see God's movement by observing the internal changes in your own heart. If you find yourself growing in love, joy, peace, or a desire for righteousness, that is not a product of human effort alone; it is the work of the Holy Spirit. Philippians 2:13 says that it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for His good pleasure. When your desires begin to align with His, you are seeing His hand transforming you from the inside out.

God also moves through His providence, which is His sovereign guidance over all circumstances. Often, we see this most clearly in hindsight. You might look back at a closed door that felt like a disappointment at the time, only to realize later that God was protecting you or redirecting you toward something better. Romans 8:28 promises that for those who love God and are called according to His purpose, He works all things together for good. This does not mean every circumstance is good in itself, but that God is capable of weaving every thread of our lives into a tapestry that reflects His glory and our ultimate spiritual growth.

Furthermore, God often moves through the community of believers. When a friend offers a word of encouragement at just the right moment, or when the church meets a specific need you have, you are seeing God’s grace expressed through His people. 1 Peter 4:10 reminds us that we are stewards of God’s varied grace when we serve one another.

Ultimately, the most significant way to see God's hand is through the person of Jesus Christ. If you are searching for God's presence, the Gospel tells us that He has already reached out to us. Because of our sin, we were separated from God, but in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus, to live a perfect life, die on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rise from the dead to conquer death. 

If you find yourself searching for meaning or feeling far from Him, I invite you to look to Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What specific area of your life feels the most uncertain or quiet right now?
Are we to love the sinner but hate the sin?

While the specific phrase "love the sinner, hate the sin" does not appear word-for-word in the Bible, the principle it describes is deeply rooted in the character of God and the teachings of Jesus. Scripture teaches that God is holy and cannot look upon evil with approval, yet He is also the definition of love and has demonstrated that love toward people who were in rebellion against Him.

God calls His people to have a sincere hatred for what is evil. We are told in Romans 12:9 to "Abhor what is evil; hold fast to what is good." Jude 1:23 even speaks of "hating even the garment stained by the flesh," emphasizing that sin is destructive and contrary to God’s nature. Sin is not a victimless or neutral thing, it is a direct rejection of God’s lordship and leads to spiritual death as noted in Romans 6:23.

At the same time, we are commanded to love our neighbors as ourselves in Matthew 22:39, and Jesus extends this command even to our enemies in Matthew 5:44. This love is not a feeling of approval for a person's choices, but a sacrificial commitment to their highest good. Every person is made in the image of God and possesses inherent dignity, regardless of their behavior.

The ultimate example of this balance is found in the person of Jesus Christ. He was called a "friend of tax collectors and sinners" in Matthew 11:19 because He spent time with those the religious elite rejected. However, His friendship was not an endorsement of their sin. He consistently called people to "go, and from now on sin no more" as seen in John 8:11.

This brings us to the heart of the Gospel. The Bible tells us that "God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us" in Romans 5:8. God’s holiness required that sin be punished, but His love provided the sacrifice. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. Through His resurrection, He defeated sin and death, offering reconciliation to all who believe.

We are all born in sin and separated from God's holiness. Because of this, we cannot save ourselves through good works or moral effort. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. When we trust in Him, God forgives our sin and begins a process of transformation in our lives through the Holy Spirit.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. Is there anything in particular that makes it difficult for you to see how God can love a person while still hating their sin?
Is the Buddhist concept of Zen compatible with the Christian faith?

The question of whether Zen is compatible with Christianity is a significant one, as both traditions value silence, reflection, and a desire for inner peace. However, when we look closely at the core doctrines of the Christian faith and the foundational teachings of Zen Buddhism, we find that they rest on very different understandings of God, humanity, and the nature of reality.

At the heart of the Christian faith is a relationship with a personal, sovereign, and holy Creator. The Bible teaches that God is one, eternally existing in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, as seen in Matthew 28:19 and 2 Corinthians 13:14. Zen, by contrast, typically focuses on non-dualism and the concept of "emptiness" (sunyata). While Christianity emphasizes the distinctness between the Creator and the creation, Zen often seeks to dissolve these distinctions to reach a state of enlightenment or satori.

The practice of meditation also differs in its purpose. In Zen, meditation often involves emptying the mind or moving beyond rational thought to experience a sense of oneness. Biblical meditation, however, is the act of filling the mind with the Word of God and reflecting on His character and works. Psalm 1:2 speaks of the blessed person whose delight is in the law of the Lord and who meditates on it day and night. Similarly, Joshua 1:8 encourages us to keep the Word of God on our lips and meditate on it so that we may be careful to do everything written in it.

The most critical point of divergence concerns the problem of human suffering and the solution for it. Zen teaches that suffering arises from attachment and ignorance, and that liberation comes through self-discipline and sudden insight. The Bible teaches that our fundamental problem is sin, which is a rebellion against God that separates us from Him, as noted in Romans 3:23 and Isaiah 59:2. We cannot save ourselves through any amount of meditation or personal effort. 

The Gospel, or "good news," is that God, in His great love and mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus is truly God and truly man, as described in John 1:1 and John 1:14. He lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day confirms His victory over sin and death, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3–4 and Romans 1:4. 

Salvation is not achieved through enlightenment, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, as stated in Ephesians 2:8–9. While a Christian may practice mindfulness or appreciate silence, these are always directed toward the person of Jesus Christ, who is the only mediator between God and man, according to 1 Timothy 2:5 and John 14:6.

Because the goals of Zen (self-attainment and non-dualism) and Christianity (reconciliation with a personal God through Christ) are so different, they are not ultimately compatible as complete systems of belief.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What aspects of Zen or mindfulness have you found yourself drawn to recently?
What is the Wailing Wall / Western Wall?

The Western Wall, also known as the Kotel in Hebrew or the Wailing Wall, is an ancient limestone wall located in the Old City of Jerusalem. It originally served as a retaining wall for the massive expansion of the Second Temple enclosure started by Herod the Great. While the wall itself was not part of the Temple building, it supported the platform where the Temple stood. When the Roman Empire destroyed Jerusalem and the Temple in 70 AD, this section of the outer wall remained, and it has stood for centuries as a place of deep historical and spiritual significance.

For many centuries, Jewish people have gathered at this wall to pray and to mourn the destruction of the Temple, which was the center of their worship and the place where God’s presence dwelt among His people. The term "Wailing Wall" was coined by European observers who witnessed the Jewish people weeping there as they lamented the loss of their holy house. For the Jewish community, it remains the holiest site where they are permitted to pray because of its proximity to the site of the original Holy of Holies.

As Christians, we view the Western Wall with great respect for its history and its connection to the life of Jesus. We know from the Gospels that Jesus walked in these Temple courts, taught the crowds, and even cleansed the Temple of those who were dishonoring His Father's house in John 2:13-16. Jesus also famously predicted the destruction of these very structures. In Matthew 24:1-2, He told His disciples that the day would come when not one stone would be left upon another, a prophecy that was literally fulfilled just decades later.

While the wall is a significant historical landmark, the Bible points us toward a new and greater reality through Jesus Christ. In John 2:19-21, Jesus referred to His own body as the true Temple. When He died on the cross, the veil of the Temple was torn in two from top to bottom, as recorded in Matthew 27:51. This signified that through His sacrifice, the barrier between God and man was removed. We no longer need to travel to a specific physical location or a wall to meet with God. Instead, those who are in Christ become part of a spiritual building. Ephesians 2:19-22 describes believers as "members of the household of God" being built into "a holy temple in the Lord."

The reality of the Temple and its walls reminds us of our need for reconciliation with God. The Bible teaches that God is holy and supreme, but our sin has created a separation that no human effort or physical monument can bridge. However, God in His mercy sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers salvation as a gift of grace. This salvation is received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our works or religious rituals.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As Hebrews 10:19-22 encourages us, we can now enter the most holy place with confidence by the blood of Jesus.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is Taizé worship, and is it biblical?

Taizé worship is a style of Christian prayer and music that originated in the Taizé Community, an ecumenical monastic order in France founded by Brother Roger in 1940. This style is characterized by its meditative and reflective nature, centering on simple, repetitive songs (often called chants), long periods of silence, and the reading of Scripture. The songs usually consist of a single sentence or two from the Psalms or other biblical texts, set to music and repeated many times to help the worshiper move from the head to the heart.

When evaluating whether Taizé worship is biblical, it is helpful to look at its primary components through the lens of Scripture.

The repetitive nature of the music is often a point of discussion. Some express concern regarding Jesus' warning against "vain repetitions" in Matthew 6:7. However, the Bible also contains examples of repetitive praise. In Psalm 136, the phrase "His love endures forever" is repeated twenty six times. Similarly, the heavenly creatures in Revelation 4:8 unceasingly repeat, "Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty." The biblical distinction rests on the heart's intent and the truth of the words. If repetition is used to dwell deeply on a biblical truth, it can be a form of meditation, which is encouraged in Psalm 1:2.

Silence is another core element of Taizé. Scripture affirms the value of quietness before the Lord. Psalm 46:10 says, "Be still, and know that I am God." In a world full of noise, taking time for silent reflection on God's Word can be a powerful way to hear from the Holy Spirit and cultivate a "gentle and quiet spirit" (1 Peter 3:4).

The biblical faithfulness of Taizé worship in any specific setting often depends on the content of the songs and the theology of the community leading it. Because it is ecumenical, it focuses on shared elements of faith. For a believer, the most critical standard is whether the worship points clearly to the person and work of Jesus Christ. True worship must be "in spirit and in truth" (John 4:24), grounded in the reality of who God is as revealed in His Word.

The foundation of all true worship is the Gospel, the good news of what God has done for us. God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3), and His justice requires that sin be punished. All of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), leaving us separated from Him. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, and then He died a substitutionary, atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (Romans 5:8; 1 John 4:10). He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all (Philippians 2:9-11).

Salvation is not something we can earn through religious rituals or good works; it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?

In practicing or attending Taizé worship, you should use discernment to ensure that the focus remains on the sufficiency of Christ and the authority of the Bible. While the style can be a beautiful tool for reflection, it is the truth of the Gospel that saves and sanctifies us. If you are looking for a way to deepen your prayer life, you might also consider daily Scripture reading and seeking fellowship in a local church where the Word is faithfully preached.
Does the Old Testament truly predict a second advent of the Messiah?

The Old Testament presents a magnificent and complex portrait of the Messiah that many scholars and believers describe as having two distinct horizons. When we look at the prophetic scriptures, we see two seemingly contradictory roles for the promised Deliverer: a Suffering Servant who would be rejected and killed, and a Triumphant King who would rule the nations in peace and righteousness.

The reason Christians believe the Old Testament predicts a second advent is that Jesus Christ perfectly fulfilled the first set of prophecies during His earthly ministry, yet the prophecies of His total, visible, and global reign are still awaiting their final completion. The prophets often saw these two events like distant mountain peaks. From a distance, two peaks can look like one, but as you approach, you realize there is a valley of time between them.

For example, Isaiah 53:5 describes a Messiah who was pierced for our transgressions and crushed for our iniquities. This was fulfilled when Jesus died on the cross as a sacrifice for sin. However, other passages like Daniel 7:13-14 describe the Son of Man coming with the clouds of heaven to be given an everlasting dominion and a kingdom that shall not be destroyed. Since Jesus has already come in humility to deal with sin, we look forward to His return in glory to establish His kingdom fully.

Another striking example is found in the book of Zechariah. In Zechariah 9:9, the Messiah is described as coming humbly, riding on a donkey, which Jesus did during His triumphal entry into Jerusalem. Yet later, in Zechariah 14:3-4, the scripture describes the Lord going out to fight against the nations and His feet standing on the Mount of Olives to bring judgment and restoration. Because God is faithful and His Word is inerrant (Numbers 23:19; 2 Timothy 3:16), we can be certain that just as He fulfilled the prophecies of His first coming, He will fulfill the prophecies of His return.

This second advent is necessary because God is holy and just. While His first coming provided the way for us to be reconciled to Him through His blood, His second coming will bring the final judgment of sin and the renewal of all things (Acts 17:31; Revelation 21:1-5).

At the heart of these two advents is the Gospel. God created us for His glory, but our sin has separated us from Him. Because God is gracious and merciful, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die in our place (Romans 5:8). He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Understanding the Messiah's return gives us great hope in the midst of a broken world. Have you ever wondered how the promise of Christ's return might change the way you view the challenges you face today?
Contemporary Christian music - is it honoring to God? Should it be used in church services?

The question of whether contemporary Christian music honors God and belongs in the church is one that many believers have considered with great care. When we look to the Bible for guidance on worship, we find that God is less concerned with the specific musical style or the era in which a song was written and more concerned with the truth of the lyrics and the condition of the heart.

The Scriptures encourage us to lift our voices in a variety of ways. In Colossians 3:16, we are told to let the word of Christ dwell in us richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom, singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, with thankfulness in our hearts to God. Similarly, Ephesians 5:19 encourages believers to speak to one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody to the Lord with the heart. This suggests a diversity of musical expressions was present even in the early church.

Contemporary music can certainly be honoring to God when it meets a few biblical criteria. First, the content must be biblically sound. Worship is a way we preach the Gospel to one another, so the lyrics should accurately reflect the character of God and the finished work of Jesus Christ. Second, the purpose of the music should be to exalt God rather than to entertain the congregation or draw attention to the performers. John 4:23-24 reminds us that the Father is seeking those who will worship Him in spirit and in truth.

There is also a biblical precedent for "new songs." Psalm 33:3 says to sing to him a new song, play skillfully on the strings, with loud shouts. Every hymn that we now consider traditional was once a contemporary song in its own day. The church has a long history of expressing the timeless truths of God through the musical language of the current generation.

Regarding its use in church services, this is often a matter of Christian liberty and pastoral wisdom. While some prefer the rich theological heritage of older hymns, others find that contemporary styles help them engage emotionally and spiritually with the Lord. In these matters, the church is called to maintain unity and love. Romans 14 and Romans 15:1-7 teach us that we should not live to please ourselves but to build up our neighbors. Whether a church chooses to use hymns, contemporary songs, or a mix of both, the goal should be the edification of the body and the glory of God.

Ultimately, our worship is a response to the wonderful Gospel of Jesus Christ. We were once separated from God by our sin, but because God is rich in mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise again on the third day, conquering death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or even the quality of our worship music.

If you find yourself focusing more on the style of music than the Savior the music points to, it may be helpful to reflect on the depth of His love for you. I invite you to turn from any self reliance and trust fully in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it about the nature of worship that feels most challenging or most life giving to you right now?
What does it mean to test the spirits?

Testing the spirits is the biblical command for believers to carefully evaluate every teaching, prophecy, or spiritual influence to determine if it truly originates from God. This instruction is primarily found in the writings of the apostle John, who wrote, "Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God, for many false prophets have gone out into the world" (1 John 4:1).

The primary test is a doctrinal one, specifically concerning the person and work of Jesus Christ. John explains that every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God, while every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God (1 John 4:2–3). To test a spirit, we must ask if the teaching affirms that Jesus is truly God and truly man, the eternal Word who took on human nature to save sinners.

A second way we test spirits is by comparing every claim to the standard of the Holy Scriptures. The Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and final authority for our faith. We are encouraged to follow the example of the Bereans, who received the word with all eagerness but examined the Scriptures daily to see if what they were being taught was actually true (Acts 17:11). If a message or a teacher contradicts the clear teaching of the Bible, it is not from the Holy Spirit (Galatians 1:8–9).

Furthermore, we can look at the "fruit" or the character resulting from the teaching. Jesus warned that false prophets might appear like sheep but are inwardly ravenous wolves, and He taught that we would recognize them by their fruits (Matthew 7:15–20). A spirit that is truly from God will always exalt Jesus Christ, lead people toward holiness, and align with the fruit of the Spirit, such as love, joy, and peace (Galatians 5:22–23).

This discernment is vital because the true Gospel is the only way we can be reconciled to God. We are all separated from God's holiness by our sin, but in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. This salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we do.

You can turn to God even now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there a specific teaching or situation you are currently trying to discern?
Why should I get married?

Marriage is a profound gift and a divine institution established by God at the very beginning of creation. While not every person is called to marriage, the Bible provides several beautiful reasons why one might seek this union.

First, God designed marriage for companionship and partnership. In the garden of Eden, God observed that it was not good for man to be alone, so He created a helper fit for him (Genesis 2:18). Marriage provides a unique, lifelong friendship where two people can support, encourage, and care for one another through all the seasons of life.

Second, marriage is the foundational building block of the family and the primary context for procreation. God blessed the first couple and told them to be fruitful and multiply (Genesis 1:28). Within the security of a committed marriage covenant, children can be raised and nurtured in the fear and admonition of the Lord (Ephesians 6:4).

Third, marriage is a means of sanctification. Living in such a close, permanent relationship with another person acts like iron sharpening iron (Proverbs 27:17). It provides a daily opportunity to practice selflessness, forgiveness, and patience. As you seek to love your spouse as yourself, the Holy Spirit uses that relationship to refine your character and help you grow in holiness.

Fourth, marriage provides a God ordained context for sexual intimacy. The Bible teaches that the marriage bed is to be kept pure and that sexual expression is a gift to be enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of marriage (Hebrews 13:4; 1 Corinthians 7:2-5). This intimacy serves to strengthen the bond between a husband and wife and protects them from temptation.

Most importantly, marriage is intended to be a living parable of the Gospel. The apostle Paul explains that the union between a husband and wife is a great mystery that actually refers to Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:31-32). A husband is called to love his wife sacrificially, just as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her, while a wife is called to respect and support her husband (Ephesians 5:22-33).

This beautiful picture of marriage points us to our ultimate need for a relationship with God. God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3; Psalm 89:14), yet every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from God, but because of His immense love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to earth. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Salvation is not something we can earn through our own efforts or by being a good spouse, it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your current perspective on the purpose of relationships?
Is Sunday the Christian Sabbath?

The question of whether Sunday is the Christian Sabbath is one that believers have explored with great care throughout church history. While the word Sabbath specifically refers to the seventh day of the week (Saturday) in the Old Testament, most Christians recognize Sunday as the "Lord's Day," the day set apart for worship and rest under the New Covenant.

In the beginning, God established a pattern of work and rest by resting on the seventh day after the work of creation (Genesis 2:2-3). This principle was later codified in the Ten Commandments as a day of holy rest for Israel (Exodus 20:8-11). However, the New Testament records a significant shift following the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Because Jesus rose from the dead on the first day of the week (Matthew 28:1; Mark 16:2), the early church began meeting on Sunday to celebrate His victory over sin and death.

The scriptures show the early believers gathering on the first day of the week to break bread and listen to apostolic teaching (Acts 20:7). Paul also instructed the churches to set aside their offerings on the first day of the week (1 Corinthians 16:2). By the time the book of Revelation was written, this day was commonly referred to as the Lord's Day (Revelation 1:10).

Many Christians, particularly in the Reformed tradition, view Sunday as the Christian Sabbath, believing that the moral principle of "one day in seven" remains while the specific day shifted from the last day of the week to the first to honor the new creation begun in Christ. Others emphasize Christian liberty, noting that the specific regulations of the Old Testament Sabbath were a shadow pointing to the reality found in Jesus (Colossians 2:16-17). In this view, every day is lived for God, but Sunday remains the primary day for corporate worship. Paul also encourages believers to be fully convinced in their own minds regarding the observance of specific days (Romans 14:5).

Ultimately, the Sabbath points us to a deeper spiritual reality. We are unable to save ourselves through our own works or moral efforts because God is perfectly holy and we have all fallen short through sin. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over death and His authority as Lord. 

The Sabbath rest is a beautiful picture of the Gospel: we cease from our own labors to earn God's favor and instead rest entirely in the finished work of Jesus. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What has been your experience with finding rest in your own life?
What does it mean that those who humble themselves will be exalted (Matthew 23:12)?

In Matthew 23:12, Jesus speaks to a crowd and His disciples, offering a sharp contrast to the religious leaders of His day. The scribes and Pharisees sought public honor, prestigious seats, and titles of respect to exalt themselves in the eyes of men (Matthew 23:5-7). Jesus explains that the values of the kingdom of heaven are the opposite of the world's values. To humble oneself means to adopt a heart-posture of lowliness, recognizing that we are not the center of the universe and that we are entirely dependent on God.

Biblical humility is not about self-loathing or denying the gifts God has given us. Rather, it is a right estimation of ourselves in light of God’s holiness (Isaiah 6:5). It involves a willingness to serve others without seeking recognition or reward (Matthew 23:11). The promise that God will exalt the humble means that He notices those who do not promote themselves. While the world might overlook the servant, God promises to bestow His favor, grace, and ultimate honor upon those who walk in lowliness (James 4:6; 1 Peter 5:6).

The ultimate example of this principle is found in the person of Jesus Christ. Although He is God, He did not cling to His rights but humbled Himself by taking on human nature and becoming a servant. He went so far as to submit to a shameful death on a cross for the sake of sinners (Philippians 2:5-8). Because of His perfect humility and obedience, God the Father highly exalted Him, raising Him from the dead and seating Him at His right hand (Philippians 2:9-11).

This truth points us directly to the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just (Psalm 18:30), but every human being has sinned and turned away from Him (Romans 3:23). Our sin creates a debt we cannot pay and a separation we cannot bridge through our own efforts or prideful "good works." However, because of His great mercy, God sent Jesus to live the perfect life we could not live and to die as a substitute in our place. His bodily resurrection proves that the debt for sin is paid and that He is Lord over all (Acts 2:32-36). We are not saved by exalting ourselves or by our own merit, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What stands in the way of you placing your full trust in Him today?
How should poetry in the Bible be interpreted?

Interpreting biblical poetry requires an understanding that God uses beautiful, figurative language to reveal His truth and His heart to us. While historical books provide the narrative of God's work, and the epistles provide logical doctrine, the poetic books (such as Psalms, Job, and Proverbs) use imagery and emotion to engage our whole being.

The primary feature of Hebrew poetry is parallelism, which is a "rhyme of thought" rather than a rhyme of sound. In synonymous parallelism, the second line repeats the thought of the first line using different words to add emphasis or clarity, as seen in Psalm 19:1. In antithetic parallelism, the second line contrasts with the first to highlight a specific truth, which is common in Proverbs 10:1. Recognizing these patterns helps you understand the central point the author is making.

It is also important to recognize that poetry frequently uses metaphors and imagery. When Psalm 91:4 says God will cover you with His feathers, it is not suggesting that God has literal wings, but is using the image of a protective bird to describe His tender care. We should interpret these figures of speech according to their intended meaning rather than insisting on a strict literalism that the author did not intend.

Furthermore, biblical poetry is deeply emotional. The writers of the Psalms often express intense joy, deep sorrow, or even frustration. These passages show us how to bring our honest emotions before God in prayer and worship. However, we must interpret these feelings in light of the rest of Scripture, remembering that while our emotions are real, God’s character remains the unchanging foundation of our faith.

Most importantly, much of the poetry in the Bible points forward to Jesus Christ. Jesus Himself told His disciples that the Psalms spoke of Him in Luke 24:44. Whether through the suffering described in Psalm 22 or the kingly glory in Psalm 2, these poems prepare our hearts to recognize the Messiah.

The beauty of biblical poetry ultimately reveals the holiness of God and the depth of human need. While the poets often cried out in distress because of their sin, as seen in Psalm 51:1-5, the Gospel provides the ultimate answer to those cries. God is perfectly holy, yet we are separated from Him by our sin. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. Through His victory over death, salvation is offered by grace alone through faith in Him alone. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What is the significance of the statement in Romans 1:22 that “professing themselves to be wise, they became fools”?

Romans 1:22 serves as a pivotal point in the Apostle Paul's description of the spiritual condition of humanity. This statement highlights the tragic irony of human pride, showing that when people intentionally turn away from the knowledge of God, they do not become more enlightened. Instead, they lose the very foundation of true wisdom.

The context of this passage explains that God's eternal power and divine nature are clearly seen through the things He has made in Romans 1:20. However, instead of honoring God, humanity suppresses this truth. By claiming to be wise based on their own reasoning and independence from their Creator, they become fools because they have rejected the ultimate source of truth and reality.

This folly is not necessarily a lack of academic intelligence, but a profound spiritual blindness. It is demonstrated most clearly in the exchange mentioned in the following verse, where people trade the glory of the incorruptible God for the worship of created things in Romans 1:23. Scripture teaches that the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom in Psalm 111:10; Proverbs 9:10, so to abandon God is to abandon the starting point of true understanding.

This passage reminds us that our hearts are naturally prone to wander and seek autonomy from God, which leads to spiritual darkness and separation from Him. Because God is perfectly holy and just, He must judge sin. Yet, in His great love, He provided a way for us to be reconciled. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His identity as Lord of all.

True wisdom is found in recognizing our need for Him. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or intellect. You can turn to God today, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Does it help to see how Paul connects our intellectual perspectives with our spiritual relationship with God?
What does it mean that sin is lawlessness?

The statement that "sin is lawlessness" comes directly from the Bible in 1 John 3:4. To understand this, it is helpful to look at both the outward action and the inward heart. Lawlessness describes a state of being where a person lives as though there is no law, or more specifically, as though they are not subject to God's authority. It is not merely a mistake or an accident, but a rejection of God's right to rule over His creation.

In the original language of the New Testament, the word for lawlessness implies a contempt for the law. When we sin, we are not just breaking a rule, we are disregarding the Lawgiver Himself. This rebellion began in the garden when humanity chose to define good and evil for themselves rather than trusting God's Word, as seen in Genesis 3:1-6. Because God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just, any violation of His character or His commands is a serious matter that carries the penalty of spiritual death, according to Romans 6:23.

The Bible teaches that every person has fallen short of God's glory and practiced this lawlessness, as noted in Romans 3:23. However, the Gospel is the good news that God did not leave us in our rebellion. Because of His great love and mercy, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life of complete obedience, never once practicing lawlessness. He then took our sin upon Himself and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Through Christ, we are offered forgiveness and reconciliation. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or efforts to keep the law, as explained in Ephesians 2:8-9. By trusting in Him, our lawlessness is forgiven, and we are given a new heart that desires to follow Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and lawlessness, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As you reflect on this, what do you find most challenging about the idea of God having ultimate authority over your life?
Has anyone ever seen God?

The question of whether anyone has ever seen God is one that the Bible answers with both a "no" regarding God’s full, unveiled essence, and a "yes" regarding His manifestations and His appearance in the person of Jesus Christ.

Scripture teaches that God is spirit (John 4:24) and dwells in unapproachable light, whom no person has ever seen or can see in His full, divine glory (1 Timothy 6:16). When Moses asked to see God’s glory, God told him, "you cannot see my face, for man shall not see me and live" (Exodus 33:20). Because God is holy and we are fallen, sinful creatures, the fullness of His presence would be overwhelming to us.

However, the Bible also records instances where people encountered God in physical forms or "theophanies." For example, Jacob wrestled with a man and later said, "I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered" (Genesis 32:30). Moses and the elders of Israel are described as seeing the God of Israel (Exodus 24:9-11). In these moments, God revealed Himself in a way that human beings could perceive without being consumed by His full majesty.

The ultimate answer to this question is found in Jesus Christ. John 1:18 explains this tension clearly: "No one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known." While no one has seen the invisible essence of God the Father, Jesus is the "image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15) and the "exact imprint of his nature" (Hebrews 1:3). When Jesus was asked by His disciple Philip to show them the Father, Jesus replied, "Whoever has seen me has seen the Father" (John 14:9).

Through the incarnation, God became man. People saw Jesus, touched Him, and heard Him speak. In seeing Jesus, they were truly seeing God in the flesh (John 1:14).

Our sin creates a barrier between us and the holy presence of God, but the Gospel is the good news that this barrier has been removed. God is so holy that He cannot overlook sin, yet He is so loving that He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the dead on the third day. Through His sacrifice, those who trust in Him are forgiven and reconciled to God. Because of what Jesus has done, we have the promise that one day we will see Him face to face in the renewal of all things (1 John 3:2; Revelation 22:4).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the gift of eternal life.

What is it about the idea of seeing God that is most important to you right now?
Are books such as 90 minutes in Heaven and 23 minutes in Hell biblically based?

When considering books like 90 Minutes in Heaven or 23 Minutes in Hell, it is helpful to approach them with a heart that loves the truth and a mind grounded in the Word of God. These accounts are often called near death experiences or heavenly visitation narratives. While they can be emotionally moving and may contain elements that align with certain biblical descriptions, they are not on the same level as Holy Scripture.

The Bible is our final and sufficient authority for faith and practice. The Apostle Paul wrote that all Scripture is breathed out by God and is profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, so that the person of God may be complete and equipped for every good work (2 Timothy 3:16-17). Because the Bible is sufficient, we do not need extra-biblical accounts to give us a "clearer" or "better" picture of the afterlife than what God has already revealed.

There are a few biblical principles to keep in mind when evaluating these books:

First, the Bible often records that those who had genuine visions of God or heaven were overwhelmed and frequently found it difficult or even unlawful to describe what they saw. In 2 Corinthians 12:2-4, Paul speaks of a man who was caught up into paradise and heard things that cannot be told, which man may not utter. Unlike modern bestsellers that provide vivid and cinematic details, the biblical writers often focused more on the holiness of God than the furniture of heaven.

Second, our faith is to be based on the promises of God's Word rather than human experience. We are told in 2 Corinthians 5:7 that we walk by faith, not by sight. While these stories may provide a sense of comfort or wonder, they are subjective personal experiences. They can be influenced by many factors, including physical trauma, medication, or the person's own imagination and cultural expectations.

Third, we must test everything by the Scriptures. The believers in Berea were commended because they received the word with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see if these things were so (Acts 17:11). If an account of heaven or hell contradicts the Bible, it must be rejected. Even if an account seems to align with the Bible, it remains a human testimony and should never be used to establish doctrine or to supplement the Gospel.

The most important truth about the afterlife is not found in the descriptions of the landscape, but in the person of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy and just, but because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Salvation and the hope of eternal life are found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or through special experiences (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Is there anything in particular about these accounts that you find confusing or that draws you to them? Seeking clarity is a wonderful way to grow in your understanding of God's Word.
Why does God not seem to care about the millions of starving children in the world?

The sight of a child suffering is one of the most heartbreaking realities of our world, and it is natural to ask where God is in the midst of such pain. It is important to begin by recognizing that God is not indifferent to this agony. The Scriptures tell us that God is "close to the brokenhearted" (Psalm 34:18) and that He has a profound love for children (Matthew 19:14).

The reality of starvation and suffering is a result of living in a world that is broken and fallen. When sin entered the world, it brought a curse that affected all of creation, leading to decay, injustice, and scarcity (Genesis 3:17; Romans 8:20–22). Much of the hunger we see today is not caused by a lack of resources, as God has provided an earth that produces enough for all, but is instead the result of human greed, war, and the misuse of our roles as stewards. God has given humanity the responsibility to care for one another, and the persistence of such suffering is a reflection of our collective rebellion against His commands to love our neighbors as ourselves (Matthew 22:39; James 4:1–2).

God's ultimate response to suffering was not to remain distant, but to enter into it. Because God is perfectly holy and righteous, He cannot ignore the sin and injustice that cause such pain. Out of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He took upon Himself the weight of human sin and the consequences of our broken world. He then rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and promising a future where "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore" (Revelation 21:4).

This restoration is offered to all who turn to Him. We are reconciled to God and justified in His sight by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, and not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him with the brokenness you see in the world?
What is postmodern therapy, and is it biblical?

Postmodern therapy is an umbrella term for several counseling approaches, such as Narrative Therapy and Solution-Focused Brief Therapy, that are built upon the philosophical framework of postmodernism. At its core, postmodern therapy rejects the idea that there is one objective, universal truth that applies to everyone. Instead, it suggests that "reality" is socially constructed through our language, culture, and personal interactions. In this model, the therapist does not act as an expert who uncovers a hidden problem; rather, they act as a collaborator who helps the client "re-author" their life story to find more helpful or empowering meanings.

When evaluating whether postmodern therapy is biblical, we must look at how it aligns with the teachings of Scripture. There are several significant points of tension to consider:

1. The Nature of Truth: Postmodernism views truth as subjective and relative to the individual. However, the Bible presents truth as objective and grounded in the character of God. Jesus declared, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). Christianity is based on the conviction that God has revealed absolute truth to us through His Word, which is "breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness" (2 Timothy 3:16).

2. The Human Condition: Postmodern therapy often avoids terms like "sin" or "objective morality," preferring to view problems as external narratives or cultural constructs. In contrast, the Bible teaches that our primary problem is not just a "story" we have been told, but a fallen nature. Scripture tells us that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). Because our hearts are prone to self-deception, we cannot simply re-author our lives apart from the transforming power of God.

3. The Source of Authority: In postmodern therapy, the individual's experience and perspective are the ultimate authority. In a biblical worldview, God is the ultimate authority. While our personal stories are important, they must be brought into submission to the "Grand Narrative" of God's redemptive work in history. True renewal of the mind comes from being "transformed by the renewal of your mind" through God's truth, rather than conforming to the patterns of this world (Romans 12:2).

The Gospel provides the ultimate narrative for every human life. It begins with the holiness of God, who created us for His glory. Because of our sin, we are separated from Him and deserve His just judgment. However, in His great mercy, God sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfectly sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not something we construct for ourselves; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes you hesitate to trust Him with your story today?

While some postmodern techniques, such as being a good listener or recognizing how culture influences us, can be helpful, the underlying philosophy often contradicts the essential claims of the Christian faith. If you are seeking guidance, I encourage you to look for a counselor who views the Bible as the final authority for faith and practice. Seeking wise counsel from a local pastor or a certified biblical counselor can ensure that the help you receive is grounded in the unchanging truth of God's Word, which provides the only foundation that truly lasts (Matthew 7:24–27).
I am a Christian who is addicted to smoking. How can I quit?

It is clear that you have a sincere desire to honor God with your life, and I want to encourage you that there is great hope for freedom in Jesus Christ. Many believers face struggles with habits and addictions that feel overwhelming, but God’s grace is sufficient for you, and His power is made perfect in your weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9).

In the Bible, we are reminded that our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit, who lives in us, and that we have been bought with a price (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). This truth is not meant to bring condemnation, but to give you a higher purpose for quitting. You are not just trying to stop a bad habit; you are seeking to steward the gift of your body for His glory.

One of the most important steps in this journey is to rest in your identity as a child of God. Your struggle with smoking does not define who you are in Christ. Because of His death and resurrection, you are forgiven and empowered by the Holy Spirit to walk in newness of life (Romans 6:4).

Practically, I encourage you to bring this struggle into the light. Confess this to a trusted brother or sister in your local church or to your elders, for there is great strength in the fellowship of the saints (James 5:16). Ask them to pray with you and hold you accountable with love and gentleness.

Additionally, try to replace the moments you would usually spend smoking with prayer or reading the Word. When a craving hits, use it as a prompt to talk to the Lord, asking Him for the strength that only He can provide (Philippians 4:13). Surrender each specific moment to Him, knowing that He is faithful to provide a way of escape (1 Corinthians 10:13).

Please keep in mind that nicotine is a physically addictive substance. While I cannot provide medical advice, it is often very helpful to consult a doctor or a medical professional. They can provide guidance on managing the physical withdrawal symptoms as you seek spiritual and emotional healing. Scripture encourages us to seek wise counsel, and medical professionals can be part of God's common grace in your recovery.

How has this struggle affected your relationship with God lately? Remembering that He is a compassionate Father who welcomes you to His throne of grace in your time of need (Hebrews 4:16) can be a great source of peace as you take these next steps.
What is mortification of sin / the flesh?

Mortification of sin is the lifelong process by which a believer, through the power of the Holy Spirit, seeks to "put to death" the sinful desires and habits of the "flesh" or the fallen human nature. The term comes from the Latin word *mortificatio*, which literally means a killing or putting to death.

This practice is rooted in the instruction found in Romans 8:13, which says, "For if you live according to the flesh you will die, but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live." It is also commanded in Colossians 3:5, which tells believers, "Put to death therefore what is earthly in you: sexual immorality, impurity, passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry."

Mortification is not an attempt to earn God's favor or pay for one's own sins through self-punishment. Instead, it is a response to the grace already received. Because a Christian has been united with Christ in His death and resurrection, they are called to actively fight against the remnants of sin that still dwell within them. This involves identifying specific sins, grieving over them because they displease God, and using the "means of grace," such as prayer, Scripture, and the fellowship of the church, to weaken the influence of those sins.

It is vital to remember that mortification is impossible in our own strength. It is only "by the Spirit" that we can successfully resist sin (Romans 8:13). While we are active in the fight, the power to change comes from God's work within us as He conforms us to the image of His Son (Romans 8:29).

This struggle against sin is only possible for those who have first been reconciled to God. Scripture teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23), and the wages of that sin is death (Romans 6:23). However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we do (Ephesians 2:8-9).

If you feel the weight of your sin, know that there is full forgiveness and new life found in Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that makes you hesitate to trust Him today?
Is God’s love reckless?

The word reckless usually describes someone who acts without thinking, lacks a plan, or disregards the consequences of their actions. In that literal sense, God’s love is not reckless. God is omniscient, meaning He knows all things from beginning to end, and He is perfectly wise. Scripture tells us that God works all things according to the counsel of His will (Ephesians 1:11) and that His wisdom is deep and unsearchable (Romans 11:33). Nothing God does is accidental, thoughtless, or a gamble.

When people use the term reckless to describe God's love, they are often trying to express how extravagant, overwhelming, and "foolish" His love appears to a world that values merit and self-preservation. We see this radical nature of His love in the parables Jesus told. For example, a shepherd leaves ninety nine sheep in the open country to pursue one that is lost (Luke 15:4). From a human business perspective, that might seem like an unnecessary risk, but to the shepherd, the individual sheep is worth the pursuit.

The most profound example of this love is found in the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, but every human being has sinned and is separated from Him (Romans 3:23). We cannot earn our way back to Him through good works or religious effort. In what seems like a scandalous act of mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to become a man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and then willingly died on the cross, taking the punishment for our sins as our substitute (Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 2:24). 

Christ did not stay in the grave. He rose bodily on the third day, defeating death and proving that His sacrifice was sufficient (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). This love is not reckless because it was a deliberate, sovereign plan formed before the foundation of the world to redeem a people for Himself (Ephesians 1:4–5). It is a love that is certain, secure, and costly.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does the Bible teach that there is a gift of celibacy?

Yes, the Bible teaches that there is a gift of celibacy, which is often referred to as the gift of singleness. This concept is most clearly presented by the Apostle Paul and by Jesus Himself.

In 1 Corinthians 7:7, Paul writes, "I wish that all were as I myself am. But each has his own gift from God, one of one kind and one of another." In this context, Paul is speaking about his own state of singleness and his ability to remain devoted to the Lord without the cares of marriage. He uses the Greek word *charisma*, which means a gift of grace, to describe this ability. Paul explains further in 1 Corinthians 7:32-35 that the primary purpose of this gift is to allow a believer to have "undivided devotion to the Lord," free from the worldly anxieties and responsibilities that naturally come with caring for a spouse and family.

Jesus also speaks of this in Matthew 19:11-12. After the disciples suggest that it might be better not to marry, Jesus responds, "Not everyone can receive this saying, but only those to whom it is given." He goes on to mention those who have made themselves "eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom of heaven," concluding with, "Let the one who is able to receive this receive it." This indicates that the ability to live a fulfilled, celibate life for the sake of God’s kingdom is a specific calling or enablement granted by God to certain individuals.

It is important to understand that this gift is not necessarily a permanent lack of sexual desire or a lack of interest in marriage. Rather, it is a God given grace that enables a person to be content and fruitful in their service to Christ while remaining single. Both marriage and singleness are viewed in Scripture as gifts from God, and neither state makes a believer more or less holy than the other.

Whether one is called to marriage or to celibacy, our ultimate identity and hope are found in Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation and the forgiveness of sins are found by God's grace alone, through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or relationship status.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What did it mean that Satan wanted to be like the Most High in Isaiah 14:14?

In the book of Isaiah 14:14, the declaration "I will make myself like the Most High" represents the ultimate expression of pride and the origin of rebellion against God. While this passage is part of a "taunt" or proverb directed at the King of Babylon in its immediate historical context, Christians have long recognized within these verses a description of the spiritual fall of Satan.

To understand what it meant for Satan to want to be like the Most High, we must look at the nature of God's unique position and how pride seeks to usurp it.

**Usurping God's Authority and Sovereignty**
The title "Most High" (Elyon) refers to God's supreme status as the possessor of heaven and earth, as seen in Genesis 14:19. When Satan said he would be like the Most High, he was not seeking to mirror God’s holiness, love, or goodness. Instead, he was seeking God's power, authority, and the right to rule. He desired to be autonomous, meaning he wanted to be a law unto himself, beholden to no one. This is the essence of sin, which is a rejection of God's rightful Lordship in favor of self-rule.

**Seeking the Worship Due to God Alone**
Being "like the Most High" also involved a desire for the glory and worship that belong only to the Creator. In the verses preceding Isaiah 14:14, the speaker says, "I will ascend to heaven; above the stars of God I will set my throne on high" (Isaiah 14:13). This reflects a desire to be the center of the universe’s adoration. We see this same ambition later when Satan tempted Jesus in the wilderness, offering Him the kingdoms of the world if Jesus would only fall down and worship him, as recorded in Matthew 4:9.

**The Contrast of Pride and Humility**
The pride of Satan stands in direct contrast to the humility of Jesus Christ. While Satan, a creature, attempted to ascend and make himself equal with God, Jesus, who is truly God, "emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men" (Philippians 2:7). Scripture tells us that because Christ humbled Himself to the point of death on a cross, God has highly exalted Him (Philippians 2:8-9). Satan’s pride led to his fall from heaven (Luke 10:18), while Christ’s humility led to our salvation.

**The Pattern of Temptation**
This desire to be "like God" is the same trap Satan used to deceive humanity in the Garden of Eden. He told Eve that if she ate the forbidden fruit, her eyes would be opened and she would be "like God, knowing good and evil" (Genesis 3:5). This lie suggests that we can find fulfillment by stepping out from under God’s authority and trying to become our own "Most High."

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, and His wrath against such prideful rebellion is real. Because of our sin and our own attempts to live independently of God, we are separated from Him. However, the Gospel is the good news that God, in His mercy and love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is not something we earn by our own works or by trying to make ourselves "god-like" through effort. It is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about the hornet?

In the Bible, the hornet is mentioned primarily as an instrument of God's sovereign power used to assist the Israelites during their conquest of the Promised Land. There are three specific instances where the hornet appears in the Old Testament, each highlighting God's promise to fight on behalf of His people.

In Exodus 23:27, 28, God says, "I will send My fear before you, I will cause confusion among all the people to whom you come, and I will make all your enemies turn their backs to you. And I will send hornets before you, which shall drive out the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite from before you." Here, the hornet is presented as a means to clear the way for Israel's advance.

Later, in Deuteronomy 7:20, Moses encourages the people by saying, "Moreover the LORD your God will send the hornet among them, until those who are left and hide themselves from you are destroyed." This verse suggests that the hornet would reach even those who tried to remain in hiding, ensuring that God's judgment and the clearing of the land were thorough.

Finally, in Joshua 24:11, 12, as Joshua reviews the history of God's faithfulness, he notes, "And I sent the hornet before you, which drove them out from before you, even the two kings of the Amorites, but not with your sword, nor with your bow." This confirms that God fulfilled His promise and emphasizes that the victory belonged to Him, not to the military might or weapons of the Israelites.

Biblical scholars have debated whether these "hornets" were literal stinging insects sent in miraculous swarms to cause panic, or if the term was a metaphor for a "stinging" terror or the "sharp" dread that God placed in the hearts of Israel's enemies. Some also suggest it could represent historical events, such as Egyptian military incursions that weakened the region before the Israelites arrived. Regardless of the physical form it took, the theological point remains clear: God is the one who delivers His people and secures the victory.

This theme of God doing for us what we cannot do for ourselves is central to the entire Bible. Just as the Israelites did not win the land by their own sword or bow, we cannot find peace with God or overcome the power of sin through our own efforts or good works. God is perfectly holy, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the dead on the third day. Through His victory over sin and death, Jesus has cleared the way for us to be reconciled to God.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. Is there anything in particular that makes it difficult for you to trust Him with your life today?
What were the key events in the life of Jesus Christ?

The life of Jesus Christ is the central turning point of human history, revealing the character of God and His plan to reconcile humanity to Himself.

His earthly journey began with the **Incarnation**, where the eternal Son of God took on human nature, being conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the Virgin Mary (Matthew 1:18–25; John 1:14). This event demonstrates that Jesus is truly God and truly man, coming to dwell among His people.

As an adult, His public ministry was inaugurated by His **Baptism** by John the Baptist, where the Father and the Holy Spirit uniquely affirmed His identity (Matthew 3:13–17). Following this, He was led into the wilderness for His **Temptation**, where He remained sinless and perfectly obedient to God, proving His worthiness as our representative (Matthew 4:1–11).

During His **Ministry**, Jesus traveled throughout Galilee and Judea, preaching the Gospel of the Kingdom, performing miracles to demonstrate His authority, and teaching with divine wisdom (Mark 1:14–15; John 20:30–31). A pivotal moment occurred at the **Transfiguration**, where His divine glory was briefly revealed to His closest disciples (Matthew 17:1–8).

The climax of His mission took place in Jerusalem during what is often called Passion Week. This included His **Triumphal Entry** into the city (Matthew 21:1–11), the institution of the **Lord's Supper** (Luke 22:14–20), and His agonizing prayer in **Gethsemane** before His arrest (Matthew 26:36–46).

The most significant events for the Christian faith are His **Crucifixion**, **Burial**, and **Resurrection**. On the cross, Jesus died a substitutionary death, taking the punishment for our sins to satisfy God’s justice and demonstrate His love (John 19:16–30; Romans 5:8). He was buried in a tomb, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, confirming His victory over sin and death (Matthew 28:1–10; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Finally, after appearing to many witnesses, Jesus' **Ascension** marked His return to the Father's right hand, where He currently reigns as Lord and intercedes for His people until His promised return (Acts 1:9–11; Hebrews 7:25).

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, and our sin has created a separation between us and Him. However, because of His great mercy, Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works. You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
Do Christians need a day of rest?

Yes, Christians do need a day of rest, both for their physical well being and their spiritual health. This need is rooted in the way God designed the world and the way He created human beings.

From the very beginning, God established a pattern of work and rest. Although God never grows weary, He rested on the seventh day after the work of creation, setting an example for us (Genesis 2:2-3). This principle was later formalized in the Ten Commandments, where God told His people to "Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy" (Exodus 20:8). This commandment was not meant to be a burden, but a gift. Jesus emphasized this when He said, "The Sabbath was made for man, and not man for the Sabbath" (Mark 2:27).

In the New Testament, we see that the early church began to meet on the first day of the week, Sunday, to celebrate the resurrection of Jesus (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2). While Christians may differ on the specific rules for observing a day of rest, the underlying need remains. We are finite creatures who need to pause from our labors to acknowledge that we are dependent on God. Taking a day for worship and rest helps us to refocus our hearts on Him and find refreshment in His presence.

However, the Bible also speaks of a deeper, spiritual rest that goes beyond just one day of the week. Because of our sin and rebellion against a holy God, we often experience a restlessness of the soul. We try to find peace through our own efforts or works, but these always fall short. The good news of the Gospel is that true rest is found in Jesus Christ alone. Jesus, the Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Jesus offers this rest to everyone who comes to Him. He says, "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). This is a rest from trying to earn God’s favor and a rest from the guilt of sin. We are justified before God by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive this eternal rest.

By setting aside a day for rest and worship, we regularly remind ourselves of the "Sabbath rest" that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9). It is a time to stop relying on our own strength and to trust in the sovereign care of the Lord, who "will not slumber" (Psalm 121:3). What is it that makes it difficult for you to set aside time for rest?
How can I be a good Christian?

It is a wonderful thing to desire to follow Christ, but it is important to understand that being a Christian is not primarily about our own "goodness" or moral effort. Instead, it begins with recognizing our need for God's grace.

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, but all of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and cannot earn our way back to Him through good deeds. However, God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to the world. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, defeating sin and death forever (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

To be a Christian is to be reconciled to God through Jesus. This happens by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8–9). You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

Once you have placed your trust in Christ, "being a good Christian" is the process of growing in your relationship with Him and becoming more like Him through the power of the Holy Spirit. Here are several biblical ways to grow in your faith:

**Abide in Christ**
Jesus said that He is the vine and we are the branches (John 15:5). Apart from Him, we can do nothing. Growing as a Christian is not about trying harder in your own strength, but about staying connected to Jesus through faith and reliance on His Spirit.

**Read and Study the Bible**
The Bible is God’s inspired and authoritative Word. It is described as a lamp to our feet and a light to our path (Psalm 119:105). By reading it daily, you learn about God’s character, His promises, and how He desires for you to live.

**Commit to Prayer**
Prayer is your direct line of communication with your Heavenly Father. The Bible encourages us to pray continually and to bring all our worries and requests to God (1 Thessalonians 5:17; Philippians 4:6). You do not need special words; simply speak honestly to Him from your heart.

**Join a Local Church**
We were not meant to follow Jesus alone. Being part of a local, Bible-preaching church provides you with fellowship, accountability, and the opportunity to hear the Word taught (Hebrews 10:24–25). It is also where you can participate in the sacraments, such as baptism and the Lord’s Supper, which are outward signs of God’s inward grace.

**Walk in Obedience and Love**
As God changes your heart, you will find a new desire to obey His commands and love others.
