Jesus said that the greatest commandments are to love God with all your heart and to love your neighbor as yourself (Matthew 22:37–39). This includes serving those in need, forgiving those who hurt you, and sharing the hope of the Gospel with others.

If you are just beginning this journey, remember that God is patient and faithful. He promises to complete the good work He has started in you (Philippians 1:6). What is your current understanding of who Jesus is and what He has done for you?
What does the Bible say about lawsuits / suing?

The Bible provides guidance on legal disputes by emphasizing reconciliation, the integrity of the Christian witness, and the priority of God’s kingdom over personal rights. The teachings vary slightly depending on whether the dispute is between fellow believers or involves the secular world, but the underlying themes of peace and trust in God remain constant.

When Christians have disagreements with one another, the New Testament strongly discourages taking those matters to secular courts. In 1 Corinthians 6:1-8, the Apostle Paul rebukes believers for suing each other before unbelievers. He argues that since the saints will one day judge the world and even angels, they should be capable of appointing wise members within the church to settle internal disputes. Paul goes so far as to say that it is better to be wronged or defrauded than to bring a lawsuit that damages the reputation of the Gospel. The goal among believers is always restoration and a testimony of love that reflects Christ to the world.

Jesus also spoke about the wisdom of avoiding the courtroom whenever possible. In Matthew 5:25-26, He instructs His followers to settle matters quickly with their accuser while they are still on the way to court. This teaching emphasizes humility and the desire for peace, warning that legal processes can lead to severe and unforgiving consequences. Furthermore, Romans 12:18-19 encourages us to live at peace with everyone as far as it depends on us, leaving room for God’s justice rather than seeking our own vengeance.

The heart behind a lawsuit is often a concern for justice, which is a reflection of God’s own holy and just character. However, because of human sin, our pursuit of justice can easily turn into greed or a desire for revenge. The Bible teaches that we were all once debtors to God’s holy law because of our sin, and we were unable to pay what we owed. In His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord over all.

Salvation is not something we can earn through our own legal standing or good works, but it is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone. When we understand how much God has forgiven us, it changes how we view our rights and our disputes with others. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

If you are currently facing a legal situation, it is wise to seek counsel from your local church elders. They can help provide biblical perspective and mediation, ensuring that your actions honor God and seek the well-being of everyone involved. What is the specific situation you are considering, or what concerns do you have about seeking justice in this way?
What is kingdom now teaching?

Kingdom Now theology, often referred to as Dominion Theology, is a belief system within some charismatic and Pentecostal circles that emphasizes the Church's role in establishing God’s physical kingdom on earth before Jesus Christ returns. Proponents of this view believe that through the fall of man in Genesis, Satan took control of the world, and it is now the responsibility of believers to take back that authority.

The core of this teaching suggests that God has given the Church the power and mandate to influence or take control over various spheres of society, such as government, education, and the arts. This is sometimes called the Seven Mountain Mandate. In this view, Christ will not return until the Church has sufficiently Christianized the world and prepared it for His arrival.

Historically and biblically, many Christians find points of tension with this teaching for several reasons:

1. The Nature of the Kingdom: While Jesus did say that the kingdom of God is in your midst (Luke 17:21), He also told Pilate that My kingdom is not of this world (John 18:36). Historic Christianity generally teaches an already, but not yet reality. We see the kingdom’s influence now through the spiritual transformation of hearts, but the full, physical reign of Christ awaits His personal and bodily return.

2. Human Effort vs. Divine Intervention: Kingdom Now theology can sometimes place a heavy burden on human effort and social activism. However, Scripture teaches that it is Jesus Himself who will put all His enemies under His feet (1 Corinthians 15:25) and that the renewal of all things happens at His coming (Revelation 21:1-5).

3. The Reality of Suffering and Sin: The Bible suggests that until Christ returns, the world will experience significant spiritual struggle and that the Church will often be a persecuted minority rather than a dominant political force (2 Timothy 3:12; John 16:33).

The primary mission of the Church is not to seize earthly power, but to proclaim the Gospel of Jesus Christ. The true Kingdom begins when a person recognizes their sin and separation from a holy God and turns to Jesus in faith. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His Lordship over all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not through our own works or our ability to reform society (Ephesians 2:8-9).

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the hope of His coming Kingdom.

What is your understanding of how the Church should engage with the world today?
What is The Infinite Way?

The Infinite Way is a spiritual teaching and mystical movement founded by Joel S. Goldsmith in 1947. It is generally associated with the New Thought movement, though Goldsmith often sought to distinguish his teachings as a unique form of Christian mysticism. The movement emphasizes the "at-one-ment" of the individual with God and suggests that the physical world, including suffering and evil, is largely an illusion created by a false sense of reality.

While The Infinite Way uses Christian terminology, its core doctrines differ significantly from the historic, biblical Christian faith in several key areas.

First, The Infinite Way teaches that "the Christ" is a state of consciousness or a divine principle that resides within every person. In contrast, the Bible teaches that Jesus Christ is a specific historical Person, the eternal Son of God who is truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14; Colossians 2:9). He is the unique Mediator between God and man, not a level of consciousness that humans can achieve on their own (1 Timothy 2:5).

Second, the movement often views God as an impersonal, infinite divine principle or "Infinite Intelligence." Scripture, however, reveals God as a personal, holy, and sovereign Being who exists eternally in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). He is the Creator who is distinct from His creation, rather than being the substance of all things (Genesis 1:1; Romans 1:25).

Third, The Infinite Way treats sin, disease, and death as "errors of the mind" or illusions that can be overcome by realizing one's oneness with God. Biblical Christianity teaches that sin is a very real rebellion against a holy God, resulting in a literal separation and the reality of death (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because sin is a real spiritual condition, it cannot be corrected through a change in perception; it requires a real sacrifice for atonement.

This leads to the heart of the Gospel. Because God is perfectly holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we cannot save ourselves through meditation or mystical realization. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). He is now exalted as Lord over all. 

Salvation is not found by looking within ourselves to find a divine spark, but by looking outward to Jesus Christ. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, apart from any works or mental exercises (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive true forgiveness and new life.

What are your thoughts on the difference between seeking a divine spirit within versus trusting in the historical Person of Jesus Christ?
Does God have favorites?

The Bible provides a beautiful and balanced answer to this question. In one sense, the Scriptures are very clear that God does not show partiality or favoritism based on human status, wealth, or ethnicity. In another sense, God does choose to set His special love upon people and call them into a relationship with Him, though this is based on His grace rather than anything we have done to earn it.

First, God is perfectly just and does not have favorites in the way humans often do. He does not prefer one person over another because of their outward appearance, their social standing, or their nationality. This is emphasized in Acts 10:34–35, where it says, "So Peter opened his mouth and said: 'Truly I understand that God shows no partiality, but in every nation anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him.'" This truth is echoed in Romans 2:11, which simply states, "For God shows no partiality."

At the same time, we see throughout the Bible that God chooses individuals and groups for specific purposes and relationships. For example, He chose Abraham to be the father of a great nation, and He chose the people of Israel to be His prized possession. However, He explicitly told the Israelites that He did not choose them because they were better or more numerous than others, but simply because He loved them and was keeping His promise (Deuteronomy 7:7–8).

In the New Testament, we see that God expresses a deep, personal favor toward those who are in Christ. This is not because those individuals are "favorites" due to their own merit, but because they have been covered by the perfection of Jesus. Ephesians 1:4–5 explains that God "chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him. In love he predestined us for adoption to himself as sons through Jesus Christ, according to the purpose of his will."

The most important thing to understand about God's favor is that it is rooted in His character of grace. We have all sinned and fallen short of His holiness (Romans 3:23), which means none of us deserve His favor. Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross to pay for our sins, and rise again to defeat death. This gift of salvation and right standing with God is available to everyone who believes.

God's "favor" is not a reward for the worthy, but a gift for the broken who recognize their need for Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What makes you wonder about whether God has favorites? Is it something you are feeling in your own walk with Him?
How can I live a holy life?

Living a holy life is a beautiful and lifelong calling that begins not with what you do for God, but with what God has already done for you in Jesus Christ. To be holy means to be set apart for God's purposes and to reflect His character in your daily life (1 Peter 1:15-16).

The foundation of holiness is understanding that you cannot make yourself holy through your own strength or moral effort. True holiness starts with the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him. In His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature and lived the perfect, sinless life we could not live. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now the exalted Lord over all. We are justified, or made right with God, by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?

Once you have placed your trust in Christ, the process of living a holy life, which the Bible calls sanctification, begins. This is a cooperative work where the Holy Spirit dwells within you to change your desires and empower your actions (Philippians 2:12-13).

First, immerse yourself in the Word of God. The Bible is our ultimate authority and the primary tool God uses to renew our minds and show us how to live in a way that pleases Him (Psalm 119:11; Romans 12:2). As you read, ask the Holy Spirit to give you understanding and the will to obey.

Second, cultivate a life of prayer. Holiness requires a constant dependence on God's grace. You are encouraged to draw near to God's throne of grace with confidence to find help in your time of need (Hebrews 4:16). Prayer keeps your heart aligned with His.

Third, commit to a local church. We were never meant to walk the path of holiness alone. We need the encouragement, accountability, and teaching found within a community of believers (Hebrews 10:24-25). Local elders and mature Christians can provide guidance as you navigate the challenges of life.

Finally, practice daily repentance. Even as we grow in holiness, we will still struggle with sin. When you fail, do not hide from God or fall into despair. Instead, confess your sins to Him, knowing that He is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9). 

Trust that the God who began this good work in you will be faithful to bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ (Philippians 1:6). If you are currently struggling with a specific area of your life, I encourage you to seek the counsel of your local pastor or a trusted believer who can pray with you and offer biblical support.
What is the sinner's prayer?

The "sinner's prayer" is a term often used to describe a prayer of repentance and faith spoken by someone who desires to receive God's forgiveness and begin a relationship with Him through Jesus Christ. While the Bible does not provide one specific set of words that must be said, the concept is rooted in the biblical call to confess our need for a Savior and to believe in the Gospel.

The content of such a prayer usually reflects the essential truths of the Gospel. It begins with an acknowledgment of God's perfect holiness and our own sinfulness. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). The prayer then focuses on the person and work of Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

A sinner's prayer typically expresses three things:
1. Repentance: An admission of sin and a desire to turn away from it.
2. Faith: A belief that Jesus Christ's death and resurrection are the only basis for forgiveness.
3. Lordship: A commitment to follow Jesus as the authority over one's life.

Scripture emphasizes that salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone, not by our own efforts or good works (Ephesians 2:8–9). The prayer itself is not a magical formula, but an outward expression of a heart that God has changed. As Romans 10:9 says, "If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved." This confession and belief are the heart of what it means to call upon the Lord (Romans 10:13).

If you feel the weight of your sin and a desire to know God, I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can a Christian avoid being a doormat for other people?

The Bible calls Christians to a life of sacrificial love and humility, but it does not call us to be "doormats" who enable the sin of others or lose our identity in the demands of people. True biblical service is a choice made out of love for God, whereas being a "doormat" is often driven by the fear of man.

To avoid this, it is helpful to first distinguish between Christian humility and the fear of man. Proverbs 29:25 warns that the fear of man lays a snare, but whoever trusts in the Lord is safe. When we find ourselves unable to say "no" or constantly crushed by the expectations of others, we may be seeking their approval more than God’s. The apostle Paul addressed this heart posture when he wrote, "For am I now seeking the approval of man, or of God? Or am I trying to please man? If I were still trying to please man, I would not be a servant of Christ" (Galatians 1:10). Our primary loyalty is to Christ, and sometimes serving Him means setting boundaries that others may not like.

Jesus provides the perfect example of someone who was deeply servant-hearted yet never a doormat. He frequently withdrew from the crowds to pray and rest, even when there were still many people left to be healed (Luke 5:16; Mark 1:35-38). He was "gentle and lowly in heart" (Matthew 11:29), yet He was also "wise as serpents and innocent as doves" (Matthew 10:16). He spoke the truth directly, even when it offended those in power, and He did not allow the agendas of others to dictate His mission.

We must also recognize that love is not synonymous with unconditional compliance. Ephesians 4:15 instructs us to speak the truth in love. Truly loving someone often involves saying "no" to their unhealthy or sinful requests. If we allow someone to mistreat us or others without consequence, we are not actually loving them in a biblical sense; instead, we may be enabling their sin.

Stewardship is another vital principle. You are a steward of the time, energy, and gifts God has given you (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). If you allow others to deplete your resources through unreasonable demands, you may become unable to fulfill the specific callings God has placed on your life, such as caring for your family or serving your local church. Saying "no" to one thing is often how we say "yes" to what God has prioritized for us.

Ultimately, the strength to set healthy boundaries comes from knowing your identity is secure in Christ. We often allow ourselves to be used by others because we are hungry for their validation or afraid of their rejection. However, the Gospel tells us that we are already fully known and fully loved by God. Because of our sin, we were separated from a holy God, but He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die an atoning death on the cross, and rise bodily on the third day to reconcile us to Himself. When we trust in Christ’s finished work rather than our own performance, we are freed from the need to please everyone else.

If you have been living under the weight of trying to please others, you can turn to God now, repent of the fear of man, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to give you true rest and a firm identity.

What specific situations or relationships make it most difficult for you to set these biblical boundaries?
What does it mean that the workers are few (Matthew 9:37)?

In Matthew 9:37, Jesus says to His disciples, "The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few." This statement comes immediately after a description of Jesus looking out at the crowds and feeling deep compassion for them. He saw the people as "harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd" in Matthew 9:36.

The "harvest" refers to the vast number of people who are spiritually ready or in need of hearing the Good News of God's kingdom. Just as a field of grain must be gathered before it is lost, Jesus sees a world of people who need to be gathered into the safety and care of God's grace. The "laborers" or "workers" are those who are called to go out and share the Gospel, teaching others about the way of salvation and caring for their spiritual needs.

When Jesus says the workers are "few," He is highlighting a significant spiritual problem: the scale of the need far outweighs the number of people currently dedicated to meeting it. This is not just about professional clergy or missionaries, but about all followers of Christ being sent out to witness to His truth.

This passage underscores the urgency of the Gospel. Because God is holy and just, our sin creates a separation from Him that we cannot bridge on our own. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we do.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

Understanding that the workers are few should lead us to two responses. First, as Jesus instructs in Matthew 9:38, we are to "pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers into his harvest." We must recognize that God is the sovereign "Lord of the harvest" who calls and equips people for this work. Second, it serves as a call for every believer to consider how they might participate in this harvest, whether by going, sending, or supporting the spread of the Word in their own communities and beyond.

If you are reflecting on this, what do you think prevents more people from stepping out to be workers in God's harvest today?
What was the purpose of the Levitical Law?

The Levitical Law, given by God to Moses on Mount Sinai, served several vital purposes in the history of redemption and the life of the nation of Israel. To understand its purpose, we must look at how it functioned as a mirror, a fence, and a guide.

First, the Law was designed to reveal the holy character of God. Through the various commandments and civil regulations, God showed Israel what it meant to be His people. He called them to a standard that reflected His own nature, saying, "For I am the Lord your God. Consecrate yourselves therefore, and be holy, for I am holy" (Leviticus 11:44; Leviticus 19:2). By following these laws, Israel was set apart from the surrounding nations as a unique witness to the one true God (Deuteronomy 4:5-8).

Second, the Law served to expose the depth of human sin. While the Law is holy, righteous, and good, it also acts as a mirror that shows us our own stains. By providing a perfect standard that no human could fully keep, the Law made our "transgressions" clear (Galatians 3:19). It was never intended to be a ladder to heaven or a means of earning salvation; rather, it was meant to stop every mouth and make the whole world accountable to God (Romans 3:19-20). The Law teaches us that even our best efforts fall short of God’s glory.

Third, the sacrificial and ceremonial aspects of the Levitical Law were "shadows" of things to come (Hebrews 10:1; Colossians 2:17). The repeated animal sacrifices, the roles of the priests, and the layout of the Tabernacle all pointed forward to the ultimate work of Jesus Christ. The blood of bulls and goats could never truly take away sins, but those sacrifices served as a constant reminder of the need for a perfect, final substitute (Hebrews 10:3-4).

This leads to the Law's ultimate purpose: to act as a guardian or tutor to lead us to Christ. The Apostle Paul explains that "the law was our guardian until Christ came, in order that we might be justified by faith" (Galatians 3:24). By showing us our inability to save ourselves and our desperate need for a Savior, the Law prepares our hearts to receive the Gospel.

The Gospel is the good news that where we failed, Jesus succeeded. God is perfectly holy, and our sin has separated us from Him. Because of His great love, God the Son took on human nature, was born of a virgin, and lived the perfect life of obedience that the Law required but we could not achieve. Jesus then died a substitutionary death on the cross, bearing the punishment for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord of all. Now, salvation is offered as a free gift by grace alone, through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works of the Law (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What do you find most challenging or confusing when you read through the Old Testament laws?
What does the Bible say about confession of sin to a priest?

In exploring what the Bible says about the confession of sin, it is helpful to look at how Scripture describes our access to God, the role of Jesus Christ, and our relationships with one another in the church.

The primary emphasis in the New Testament is that Jesus Christ is our Great High Priest. Because of His perfect life, His sacrificial death on the cross, and His resurrection, believers have direct access to God. Hebrews 4:14-16 encourages us, saying, "Since then we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus, the Son of God, let us hold fast our confession. For we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin. Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need." Because Jesus is our mediator, we do not require an earthly priest to stand between us and God to obtain forgiveness. 1 Timothy 2:5 confirms this, stating, "For there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus."

When we sin, the Bible directs us to confess those sins directly to God, who is the only one with the authority to forgive the eternal debt of sin. 1 John 1:9 promises, "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness." This confession is an act of agreeement with God about our sin and a reliance on His mercy through Christ.

However, the Bible also speaks of confessing sins to other people within the community of faith. James 5:16 says, "Therefore, confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working." In this context, confession to a fellow believer, a pastor, or a trusted elder is not for the purpose of obtaining divine forgiveness (which comes from God alone), but for the purpose of accountability, mutual prayer, and the healing of relationships.

Furthermore, the New Testament describes all believers as part of a "royal priesthood" (1 Peter 2:9). This means that every Christian has the privilege of going to God in prayer and the responsibility of ministering to others. While God appoints leaders and shepherds in the church to provide guidance and oversight, they do not possess a unique power to dispense God's grace that other believers cannot access through faith in Christ.

At its heart, the message of the Bible is that we are all sinners in need of a Savior. Romans 3:23 tells us that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God," and Romans 6:23 explains that "the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." God, in His holiness and justice, must punish sin, but in His love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take that punishment upon Himself. By His death and bodily resurrection, Jesus defeated sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our own works or through religious rituals, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is your understanding of how a person receives forgiveness from God?
Should Christians go to nightclubs? Is clubbing a sin?

The Bible does not contain a specific command regarding nightclubs, so determining whether a Christian should go to one involves applying biblical principles of wisdom, holiness, and stewardship. While being in a certain building or listening to music is not inherently sinful, the environment and activities typically associated with nightclubs require careful consideration of your walk with Christ.

Scripture teaches that while a believer has freedom in Christ, not everything is beneficial for their spiritual health or their witness to others. In 1 Corinthians 10:23, it says, "All things are lawful, but not all things are helpful. All things are lawful, but not all things build up." When deciding whether to enter such an environment, you might ask if being there helps you grow in godliness or if it creates a stumbling block for yourself or others.

One primary concern for a Christian is the temptation found in these settings. Nightclubs are often centered around the consumption of alcohol, and the Bible clearly warns against drunkenness in Ephesians 5:18. Additionally, the atmosphere often promotes sensuality and worldly desires. 1 John 2:16 reminds us that "all that is in the world, the desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life, is not from the Father but is from the world." If an environment consistently promotes things that grieve the Holy Spirit, a believer should exercise great caution.

A Christian is also called to be a light in the world as described in Matthew 5:16. We must consider how our presence in certain places reflects our devotion to Jesus. Because our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit, bought with the price of Christ's blood as noted in 1 Corinthians 6:19-20, we are called to glorify God in how we use our time and where we go. If your conscience is uneasy about an activity, it is important to listen to that conviction, for Romans 14:23 states that "whatever does not proceed from faith is sin."

Ultimately, our choices should flow from a heart that has been transformed by the Gospel. We recognize that God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), and that we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we were separated from God, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are saved not by our own good works or by following a list of rules, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

When we trust in Jesus, He gives us a new heart with new desires. This new life is not about seeing how close we can get to the world without sinning, but about how closely we can follow our Savior. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you weigh this decision, what are the primary reasons you feel drawn to that environment, and do those reasons align with your desire to honor Christ? Seek the Lord in prayer and perhaps talk with a mature believer or pastor in your local church for further guidance.
Does the statue outside the United Nations have any connection to the beast of the end times?

The statue you are referring to, titled "Guardian for International Peace and Security," was donated to the United Nations by the government of Oaxaca, Mexico. It is an alebrije, which is a traditional Mexican folk art sculpture representing a mythical creature. Because the statue combines the features of a jaguar and an eagle, many observers have noted its visual resemblance to certain creatures described in biblical prophecy.

In the book of Daniel, the prophet records a vision of four great beasts representing earthly kingdoms. The first was like a lion and had eagle's wings in Daniel 7:4. Furthermore, the book of Revelation describes a beast coming out of the sea that was like a leopard, with feet like those of a bear and a mouth like the mouth of a lion in Revelation 13:2. Additionally, because the statue is associated with the theme of international peace, some believers point to the warning in 1 Thessalonians 5:3, which states that while people are saying, "Peace and safety," destruction will come on them suddenly.

While the visual similarities are striking to many, Christians hold various perspectives on how modern symbols and political entities relate to end times prophecy. Some see such installations as significant signs of the times, while others view them as coincidental artistic expressions that do not have a direct prophetic link. In these matters, the Bible calls us to exercise discernment and to keep our primary focus on the sovereignty of God rather than on specific earthly symbols.

Prophetic passages are ultimately intended to point us toward the hope we have in Jesus Christ. While human history involves the rise and fall of various powers, God is holy and supreme over all things. Though we are separated from Him by our sin, He demonstrated His love by sending His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature and live a perfect life. Through His sacrificial death on the cross, His burial, and His bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus secured victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What are your thoughts or concerns as you look at these prophetic descriptions in the Bible?
What is the sign of Jonah?

The sign of Jonah is a prophetic comparison that Jesus Christ used to point toward His own death, burial, and resurrection. When the scribes and Pharisees demanded a miraculous sign to prove His authority, Jesus responded that no sign would be given to them except the sign of the prophet Jonah.

In the Gospel of Matthew, Jesus explains the primary meaning of this sign by comparing Jonah's experience to His own coming ordeal. Just as Jonah was in the belly of the great fish for three days and three nights, the Son of Man would be in the heart of the earth for three days and three nights, as recorded in Matthew 12:39-40. This serves as a powerful pointer to the historical reality of Jesus' bodily resurrection, which validates His identity as the Son of God and His victory over sin and death.

Beyond the physical comparison of the three days, the sign of Jonah also involves a message of repentance. In the Gospel of Luke, Jesus notes that Jonah became a sign to the people of Nineveh, and in a similar way, the Son of Man would be a sign to His generation, according to Luke 11:30. The people of Nineveh, who were Gentiles and outsiders to the covenant of Israel, heard Jonah’s preaching and repented. Jesus pointed out that someone greater than Jonah was now present, yet many were refusing to believe, as seen in Matthew 12:41 and Luke 11:32.

This sign is central to the Gospel message because it directs us to the work of Christ for our salvation. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His burial and subsequent resurrection on the third day fulfill the sign of Jonah and demonstrate that God has accepted His sacrifice as sufficient to reconcile us to Himself.

Salvation is not earned by our own efforts or religious works, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him?
What does it mean to walk worthy?

To walk worthy is to live a life that is in balance with the high calling you have received through Jesus Christ. The word "worthy" in the original language of the New Testament carries the idea of a pair of scales. On one side of the scale is the glorious reality of who God is and what He has done for us in the Gospel. To "walk worthy" is to ensure that the other side of the scale, our daily conduct and character, carries the weight and reflects the value of that calling.

The Apostle Paul uses this phrase several times to describe the Christian life. In Ephesians 4:1, he writes, "I therefore, a prisoner for the Lord, urge you to walk in a manner worthy of the calling to which you have been called." He then explains that this looks like living with all humility, gentleness, patience, and bearing with one another in love as seen in Ephesians 4:2.

Walking worthy does not mean walking in a way that makes you deserving of God’s grace. We can never earn God's favor because He is perfectly holy and we are all sinners who have fallen short of His glory as stated in Romans 3:23. Instead, walking worthy is the grateful response of a heart that has been transformed by the Gospel. 

In Colossians 1:10, Paul further describes this walk as "fully pleasing to him: bearing fruit in every good work and increasing in the knowledge of God." This involves a life of progressive sanctification, where the Holy Spirit works within us to make us more like Jesus. It is a commitment to honor God in our relationships, our work, and our private thoughts, seeking to please Him in everything we do.

This lifestyle is only possible because of the work of Jesus Christ. While we were separated from God by our sin, Christ took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is not something we achieve by walking worthy; it is a gift we receive by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you are feeling the weight of your own imperfection or the need for a fresh start with God, know that His mercy is available to you today. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Walking worthy is a journey of daily dependence on God's grace. It means recognizing that you belong to His kingdom and desiring that your life would point others to His goodness. If you are seeking to grow in this walk, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the Word of God, maintain a rhythm of prayer, and find a local, Bible-believing church where you can be encouraged by other believers.

What is one area of your life where you are currently finding it difficult to reflect the character of Christ?
What is a divine appointment?

A divine appointment is an encounter or circumstance that is purposefully arranged by God to accomplish His will in the lives of individuals. While it may look like a coincidence to human eyes, it is actually the result of God's sovereign hand guiding people to cross paths at exactly the right time for a spiritual purpose.

The Bible teaches that God is intimately involved in the details of our lives. Proverbs 16:9 says, "The heart of man plans his way, but the Lord establishes his steps." Similarly, Psalm 37:23 notes that the steps of a man are established by the Lord when he delights in His way. This means that even when we are going about our daily routines, God may be directing us toward a specific person or situation.

A clear example of a divine appointment is found in Acts 8:26-39, where the Holy Spirit leads Philip to a desert road specifically to meet an Ethiopian official who was reading the Isaiah scroll and needed help understanding it. Another instance is found in John 4:1-26, where Jesus meets a Samaritan woman at a well. This meeting was not accidental, as Jesus purposefully went through Samaria to offer her the living water that only He can provide.

These moments are often intended for encouragement, meeting a physical need, or sharing the Gospel. The ultimate purpose of God's interaction with humanity is to reconcile us to Himself. God is perfectly holy, yet all people have sinned and are separated from Him. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature and lived a perfect life. He then died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all, and He offers salvation by grace alone through faith alone in Himself, not based on any works we do.

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Recognizing that God is sovereign over our schedules changes how we view each day. We can pray for the Holy Spirit to give us eyes to see the people He puts in our path and the heart to respond with grace and truth. What holds you back from trusting Him with the appointments in your life today?
What is the Qur’an?

The Qur’an is the central religious text of Islam. Muslims believe it is the literal word of God, revealed to the prophet Muhammad through the angel Gabriel over a period of approximately twenty-three years in the seventh century. It consists of 114 chapters, known as suras, and serves as the primary source of authority for Islamic law, theology, and daily life.

While the Qur’an includes accounts of many people also found in the Bible, such as Abraham, Moses, and Jesus, it presents their roles and messages from a different perspective. For instance, the Qur’an speaks of Jesus with great respect as a prophet, but it explicitly denies His deity, His status as the Son of God, and His death on the cross for the forgiveness of sins.

As a Christian, I believe the Bible is the inspired and final Word of God, as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16. The Bible teaches that Jesus Christ is not merely a prophet, but is truly God and truly man, as described in John 1:1 and John 1:14. This is a vital distinction because the Gospel rests on the fact that Jesus is the holy Son of God who took on human nature to save us from our sins.

The message of the Bible is that all people have sinned and are separated from a holy God, as written in Romans 3:23 and Romans 6:23. Because we cannot save ourselves through our own works or religious observance, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ. He lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment that we deserved. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3–4.

Salvation is a gift of God’s grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, rather than through our own efforts, as taught in Ephesians 2:8–9. Jesus Himself said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me," in John 14:6.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What are your thoughts on how the Bible and the Qur’an describe the identity of Jesus?
What would have happened “if the Lord had not been on our side” (Psalm 124:2)?

Psalm 124:2 poses a question that King David answers with vivid and sobering imagery. If the Lord had not been on our side, the Bible says we would have been "swallowed up alive" by the anger of those who rose against us (Psalm 124:3). 

The psalmist uses the metaphor of a violent, unstoppable flood to describe the alternative to God's presence. Without His intervention, the "flood would have swept us away" and the "raging waters" would have overwhelmed our souls (Psalm 124:4; Psalm 124:5). This represents a total loss of life, hope, and future. It is a picture of being completely consumed by forces far greater than ourselves.

On a deeper spiritual level, this truth points to our desperate need for God's grace in every aspect of existence. Apart from the Lord's intervention, we are not only vulnerable to the trials of this world, but we are also "dead in our trespasses" and without hope (Ephesians 2:1; Ephesians 2:12). Because God is holy and just, and because every human being has sinned and falls short of His glory (Romans 3:23), the natural consequence of being without God's side is to remain under the weight of our own sin and its penalty, which is death (Romans 6:23).

However, the heart of the Christian faith is that God did not leave us to be swept away. The Gospel tells us that God demonstrated His love by sending His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Because of His work, those who trust in Him are reconciled to God. As the Apostle Paul joyfully asks, "If God is for us, who can be against us?" (Romans 8:31).

If the Lord were not on our side, we would be lost to the "broken teeth" of our enemies and the snares of destruction (Psalm 124:6-7). But because He is our help, the snare is broken and we have escaped. Our ultimate help is found "in the name of the Lord, who made heaven and earth" (Psalm 124:8).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to experience His forgiveness and be certain that He is on your side. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What did Jesus mean when He said it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to get into heaven?

Jesus used this striking imagery to illustrate the impossibility of human effort in obtaining salvation. By comparing the largest animal in Israel, a camel, to the smallest opening, the eye of a sewing needle, He was teaching that it is humanly impossible for a person to enter the kingdom of God by their own means.

In the culture of that time, many people believed that wealth was a sign of God’s favor and that the rich were the most likely to be saved. When Jesus spoke these words, His disciples were shocked and asked, "Who then can be saved?" as recorded in Matthew 19:25. Jesus answered them by saying, "With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible" in Matthew 19:26.

The core issue is not the money itself, but where a person places their trust. Wealth often creates a false sense of self-sufficiency, making it difficult for someone to recognize their desperate need for a Savior. To be saved, one must come to God with the humility of a child, admitting they have nothing to offer to earn their way into heaven.

This truth leads us to the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory, as explained in Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we are separated from God and cannot bridge that gap through wealth, good works, or religious effort. However, God is also merciful and loving. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live. Jesus died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, not by our own works. It is the miraculous work of God to change a human heart, making the impossible possible.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the most difficult thing for people to let go of when considering following Jesus?
How do we decide which books belong in the Bible since the Bible does not say which books belong in the Bible?

The question of how we recognize which books belong in the Bible is a vital one because it concerns the very foundation of our faith. While it is true that the Bible does not contain an inspired table of contents, Christians believe that the process of identifying these books was not a matter of human invention, but rather a process of recognizing what God had already breathed out.

The Old Testament canon was already well established by the time of Jesus. The Jewish people recognized the Law, the Prophets, and the Writings as the authoritative Word of God. Jesus Himself confirmed this authority when He spoke of "the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms" in Luke 24:44. Throughout His ministry, Jesus quoted these scriptures as the final word on all matters of truth, and He never questioned the collection of books held by the Jewish community of His day.

For the New Testament, the early church used several guiding principles to recognize which writings were from God. First, they looked for apostolicity, meaning a book was written by an apostle or a close associate of an apostle. For example, Luke was a companion of Paul, and Mark was a companion of Peter. Second, they looked for orthodoxy, ensuring the teaching was consistent with the message of Jesus and the rest of Scripture. Third, they looked for widespread acceptance and use among the faithful churches. As these letters and accounts were read in worship, the Holy Spirit confirmed their divine origin to the body of believers.

The New Testament even shows the beginning of this recognition within its own pages. In 2 Peter 3:15-16, the apostle Peter refers to the letters of Paul as "Scripture," placing them on the same level as the Old Testament. Similarly, in 1 Timothy 5:18, Paul quotes from both Deuteronomy 25:4 and Luke 10:7, calling them both "Scripture."

Ultimately, we trust in the sovereignty of God. If God is powerful enough to inspire His Word, He is faithful enough to preserve it and ensure His people can identify it. We believe the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, serving as our ultimate authority as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16.

The central purpose of all these books, from Genesis to Revelation, is to point us to Jesus Christ. The Scriptures reveal that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and separated ourselves from Him. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

Through Christ alone, we are offered forgiveness and eternal life, not by our own works, but by grace through faith. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you.

As you consider the authority of the Bible, have you ever felt the Holy Spirit speaking to you through its words, or do you have concerns about specific books?
What does it mean to walk by the Spirit (Galatians 5:16)?

To walk by the Spirit means to live your life in a state of active, continuous dependence on the Holy Spirit’s power and guidance. The word walk in the Bible often serves as a metaphor for one's daily conduct and lifestyle. In Galatians 5:16, the Apostle Paul instructs believers to let the Spirit direct their lives so they will not carry out the desires of the "flesh," which refers to our natural human inclination toward sin (Galatians 5:16; Galatians 5:17).

Walking by the Spirit is not a one-time event but a moment-by-moment yielding to God. It involves setting your mind on the things of the Spirit and intentionally choosing to follow His leading as revealed in the Scriptures (Romans 8:5; Romans 8:14). This is a process of progressive sanctification, where the Spirit works within a believer to produce "fruit" such as love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control (Galatians 5:22–23).

This spiritual walk is only possible for those who have been reconciled to God. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us spiritually dead and separated from Him (Isaiah 6:3; Romans 3:23). Because we cannot save ourselves through moral effort or religious works, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth and lived a sinless life (John 1:1; Hebrews 4:15). He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord over all (Philippians 2:9–11). We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ (Romans 3:28; Ephesians 2:8–9).

When you trust in Christ, God gives you His Holy Spirit to dwell within you, changing your heart and giving you the desire and ability to obey Him (Ezekiel 36:26–27). Walking by the Spirit means resting in this finished work of Jesus while actively cooperating with the Spirit to kill sin and grow in holiness (Romans 8:13).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What do you find most difficult about relying on God’s strength rather than your own in your daily life?
What does the Bible mean when it says "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'"?

When the Bible states in Psalm 14:1 and Psalm 53:1 that "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God,'" it is describing more than just an intellectual struggle with the existence of a Creator. In the original Hebrew, the word translated as fool is "nabal," which refers to someone who is morally deficient or spiritually insensitive rather than someone lacking in intelligence.

To say this "in his heart" suggests a deep-seated rejection that governs how a person lives. The heart, in biblical terms, is the center of a person's will, intellect, and emotions (Proverbs 4:23). This phrase describes "practical atheism," where a person chooses to live as if God does not exist, does not see their actions, or will not hold them accountable. The verses immediately following this statement describe the result of this mindset: "They are corrupt, they do abominable deeds; there is none who does good" (Psalm 14:1).

The Bible presents the knowledge of God as something visible to everyone through the things He has made, leaving people "without excuse" (Romans 1:19-20). Therefore, the "foolishness" described is the tragic decision to suppress the truth of God's authority in order to pursue one's own desires.

Scripture teaches that all people have turned away from God and fall short of His holy standards (Romans 3:10-12; Romans 3:23). This separation from God is the result of sin, but God in His great mercy did not leave us in this condition. Because God is holy and just, He must punish sin, yet because He is loving and gracious, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is onanism? Is onanism a sin?

The term onanism is derived from the biblical account of a man named Onan in the book of Genesis. Historically and technically, it refers to the practice of withdrawing during sexual intercourse to prevent conception, which is often called coitus interruptus. In more recent history, the term has also been used more broadly as a synonym for masturbation.

To understand whether this is a sin, it is important to look at the biblical context of Onan's life in Genesis 38:8–10. In that culture, God had established a "Levirate marriage" custom where a man was expected to provide an heir for his deceased brother by marrying the widow. Onan married his brother’s widow, Tamar, but he refused to fulfill his duty to his brother. He intentionally prevented conception so that any child born would not carry on his brother's name and inheritance, likely so that the inheritance would remain with him instead. The Bible says that what he did was wicked in the Lord's sight, and God took his life.

The primary sin of Onan was not necessarily the physical act itself, but his underlying heart of rebellion, selfishness, and greed. He disobeyed God's command and failed to show love and responsibility toward his family.

When considering sexual ethics and practices like masturbation more broadly, the Bible emphasizes the importance of the heart and the purpose of the body. Scripture teaches that our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit and should be used to glorify God, as stated in 1 Corinthians 6:19–20. Furthermore, Jesus taught that sexual sin begins in the heart with lustful intent in Matthew 5:27–28. Believers are called to exercise self-control, which is a fruit of the Holy Spirit according to Galatians 5:22–23, and to reserve sexual intimacy for the covenant of marriage between a man and a woman as described in Hebrews 13:4 and Genesis 2:24.

Whether a person struggles with specific physical acts or the internal battle with lust, the Bible is clear that we all fall short of God’s holy standard. Romans 3:23 reminds us that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. The good news of the Gospel is that God did not leave us in our sin. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfectly pure life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day to defeat death and sin forever.

Through faith in Jesus, we are not only forgiven but also given a new nature and the power of the Holy Spirit to walk in purity and honor God with our bodies as seen in 1 Corinthians 6:11. You can turn from sin today and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive complete forgiveness and new life. What is currently standing in the way of you fully trusting Him with your life and your purity?
Is Israel and the church the same thing? Does God still have a plan for Israel?

The question of the relationship between Israel and the church is one that has been discussed by believers for centuries, as it touches on the very heart of God's faithfulness to His promises. While there are different perspectives among faithful Christians, the Bible provides a beautiful picture of how God is working through history to redeem a people for Himself.

In many ways, there is a profound continuity between Israel and the church. The Apostle Paul describes this using the image of an olive tree in Romans 11:17-24. He explains that some branches (unbelieving Israel) were broken off, and wild shoots (Gentile believers) were grafted in among the others to share in the nourishing root of the tree. This suggests that there is one people of God, rooted in the promises made to Abraham, which find their ultimate "Yes" and "Amen" in Jesus Christ. Paul further writes that "if you are Christ’s, then you are Abraham’s offspring, heirs according to promise" in Galatians 3:29.

However, the Bible also speaks of the church as something distinct in its New Covenant administration. The church is comprised of both Jews and Gentiles who have been reconciled to God and to one another through the cross of Christ. As Ephesians 2:14-16 explains, Jesus has broken down the dividing wall of hostility to create "one new man" in place of the two. While the Old Testament focused on the nation of Israel as the primary vehicle of God's revelation, the church is now the body of Christ, sent out to all nations.

Regarding God’s plan for ethnic Israel, many believers find great hope in the words of Romans 11. Paul asks, "Has God rejected his people? By no means!" in Romans 11:1. He points out that there has always been a "remnant, chosen by grace" in Romans 11:5. Furthermore, Romans 11:25-26 speaks of a mystery where a partial hardening has come upon Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in, and in this way "all Israel will be saved." This leads many to believe that God has a future, sovereign plan to bring a great number of Jewish people to recognize Jesus as their true Messiah and Savior.

Ultimately, the Bible teaches that there is no distinction in how anyone is saved. Both Jew and Gentile are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. As Romans 10:12 states, "For there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; for the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him."

The core of God's plan is the Gospel: that we are all separated from a holy God by our sin, but God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is the only mediator between God and man, as noted in 1 Timothy 2:5.

Whether you are reflecting on your own heritage or God's global purposes, the most important question is your own relationship with the Lord Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, receiving the forgiveness and new life He offers. Is there anything in particular about God’s promises that feels difficult to trust right now?
Are Catholic beliefs and practices biblical?

When considering whether Roman Catholic beliefs and practices are biblical, it is helpful to first recognize the significant areas of agreement between Catholics and other Christian traditions. We share a common foundation in the core "essentials of the faith," such as the doctrine of the Trinity, which teaches that there is one God eternally existing in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). We also agree on the deity and humanity of Jesus Christ, His miraculous virgin birth, His bodily resurrection from the dead, and His future return to judge the living and the dead (John 1:1; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

However, there are important areas where Catholic teaching and the Bible appear to diverge, particularly regarding authority and the way a person is made right with God.

One primary difference involves the authority of Scripture. The Bible teaches that it is the inspired, inerrant, and final authority for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16-17; Isaiah 40:8). While the Catholic Church honors the Bible, it also holds that Sacred Tradition and the teaching authority of the Church (the Magisterium) are equal in authority to the written Word. This often leads to practices and doctrines that are not found in the Bible, such as the veneration of Mary, the doctrine of Purgatory, or the authority of the Pope.

The most critical distinction concerns the Gospel and how we are saved. The Catholic Church teaches that justification is a long process that involves faith, the reception of sacraments, and human cooperation through good works. In contrast, the Bible teaches that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, apart from any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:28; Galatians 2:16).

Scripture presents a clear and beautiful message regarding our salvation:
1. God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3).
2. All human beings have sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23).
3. Out of love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, taking the punishment we deserved (Romans 5:8; 2 Corinthians 5:21).
4. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).
5. He is now exalted as Lord, and salvation is a free gift given to anyone who turns from their sin and trusts in Him (Acts 16:31).

The Bible identifies Jesus Christ as the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5). Because of His finished work on the cross, we can draw near to God with confidence through Him (Hebrews 4:14-16).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in Him today?

I encourage you to read the Gospel of John or the book of Romans in the Bible. These books provide a wonderful look at who Jesus is and how His grace provides everything we need for salvation. Seeking fellowship in a local, Bible-teaching church can also be a great source of clarity and encouragement as you study these truths.
What should we learn from the tribe of Simeon?

The history of the tribe of Simeon offers profound lessons regarding the consequences of uncontrolled anger, the fulfillment of God’s word, and the necessity of depending on His grace rather than our own impulses.

One of the primary lessons from Simeon’s life is the danger of misplaced zeal and violence. In Genesis 34:25-31, Simeon and his brother Levi led a violent massacre against the men of Shechem to avenge their sister, Dinah. While their concern for their sister was understandable, their methods were cruel and deceptive. This event led their father, Jacob, to issue a sobering prophecy on his deathbed. He stated that because of their fierce anger and cruel wrath, they would be divided and scattered throughout Israel (Genesis 49:5-7). We see the fulfillment of this hundreds of years later, as the tribe of Simeon received an inheritance that was actually tucked inside the territory of Judah (Joshua 19:1; Joshua 19:9). Eventually, the tribe largely faded as a distinct political entity, illustrating how sin and a lack of self-control can diminish our influence and lead to lasting consequences.

The tribe of Simeon also serves as a warning about the spiritual danger of turning away from God. During the wilderness wanderings, the tribe experienced a massive population decline, dropping from 59,300 men (Numbers 1:23) to only 22,200 (Numbers 26:14). Many scholars believe this was largely due to the plague that followed the sin at Baal-Peor, where many Israelites engaged in immorality and idolatry. It is notable that the man who openly defied God and Moses by bringing a Midianite woman into the camp was Zimri, a leader from the tribe of Simeon (Numbers 25:6-14). This serves as a reminder that a legacy of faith is not guaranteed by heritage, but must be maintained through personal and communal faithfulness to the Lord.

However, even in the history of Simeon, we see the thread of God’s mercy. Although they were scattered, they were not cast off forever. Members of the tribe are mentioned during the reforms of King Asa (2 Chronicles 15:9) and King Josiah (2 Chronicles 34:6), showing that individuals within the tribe still sought the Lord. Furthermore, in the vision of the redeemed in the book of Revelation, the tribe of Simeon is listed among those sealed by God (Revelation 7:7). This teaches us that while our sins have real consequences, God’s grace is sufficient to preserve and restore those who belong to Him.

Ultimately, the story of Simeon points us to our need for a Savior. We all possess the same capacity for anger, pride, and wandering that Simeon displayed. Scripture teaches that God is holy and just, and our sins separate us from Him (Isaiah 59:2). Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death. Through Christ, the "scattered" can be gathered and the "cursed" can be blessed.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from fully trusting Him today?
What is the hypostatic union?

The hypostatic union is a theological term used to describe the miraculous way that the divine nature and the human nature are united in the one person of Jesus Christ. The word hypostatic comes from the Greek word *hypostasis*, which refers to a person or individual existence. In simple terms, it means that Jesus is one person who is fully God and fully man.

The Bible teaches that Jesus Christ has always existed as the eternal Son of God. In the incarnation, He took on a true human nature without losing His divinity. He is not half God and half man, nor is He a mixture of the two that creates a third kind of being. Instead, He possesses all the attributes of God and all the essential attributes of a human being (except for sin), existing as one undivided person.

Scripture points to this reality in several places:

*   John 1:1 states, "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God."
*   John 1:14 continues, "And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth."
*   Colossians 2:9 declares, "For in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily."
*   Philippians 2:6-7 describes Christ, "who, though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men."

This union of natures is essential for our salvation. Because Jesus is truly man, He could represent us and die as a substitute for human sinners. Because He is truly God, His sacrifice has the infinite power and value necessary to satisfy the righteous judgment of a holy God and to conquer death through His resurrection.

As the God-man, Jesus is the only mediator who can reconcile us to God as mentioned in 1 Timothy 2:5. Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment, but God in His great mercy sent His Son to live a perfect life and die on the cross in our place. Through His bodily resurrection, He proved His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What do you think about the claim that Jesus is both God and man?
Doubting God — how can I overcome doubt in my relationship with God?

It is important to know that you are not alone in your struggle. Many of the most faithful people in the Bible experienced seasons of uncertainty. Doubt is often not a sign of a lack of faith, but rather an indication that you are wrestling deeply with the weight and truth of who God is.

When we feel our faith wavering, we must return to the foundational truths of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, which creates a separation between us and our Creator. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is fully God and fully man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and then rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is not something we earn through the strength of our own feelings or works, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

In the Scriptures, we see that God is patient with those who doubt. In the Gospel of Mark, a father cried out to Jesus, "I believe; help my unbelief!" (Mark 9:24). Jesus did not reject him for his honesty but met him with compassion. Similarly, when Thomas doubted the resurrection, Jesus did not cast him out but showed him His wounds and invited him to believe (John 20:24-29). The Bible even instructs the church to "have mercy on those who doubt" (Jude 1:22).

To navigate this season, consider these biblical paths:

First, be honest with God in prayer. He already knows your heart and welcomes your sincerity. The Psalms are filled with believers crying out to God with their questions and frustrations. You can approach His throne of grace with confidence, knowing that our High Priest, Jesus, understands our weaknesses (Hebrews 4:14-16).

Second, immerse yourself in the Word of God. Faith is nourished by the truth of Scripture. As it says in Romans 10:17, "faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ." Focusing on the historical reality of Christ's life, death, and resurrection provides a firm anchor when your emotions feel unsteady.

Third, stay connected to other believers. Doubt can often lead to isolation, but God designed us to live in community. Share your struggles with a mature brother or sister in Christ or a pastor who can walk alongside you and point you back to the promises of God.

Remember that your security does not rest in the strength of your grip on God, but in the strength of His grip on you. He who began a good work in you will be faithful to bring it to completion (Philippians 1:6). What specific questions or aspects of God's character feel most difficult to trust right now?
Did God create other people in addition to Adam and Eve?

Based on the account given in the Bible, God created Adam and Eve as the first two human beings and the ancestors of the entire human race. The Scriptures do not describe the creation of any other people groups or individuals independent of this original pair.

In the book of Genesis, we read that God formed the first man, Adam, from the dust of the ground and breathed into him the breath of life (Genesis 2:7). Later, God created the first woman, Eve, from Adam’s side to be his companion and helper (Genesis 2:21-22). Genesis 3:20 explicitly identifies Eve as the mother of all living, which points to the reality that every person who has ever lived is a descendant of Adam and Eve. This is further confirmed in the New Testament, where it is written that God made from one man every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth (Acts 17:26).

This truth is central to understanding our identity and our need for a Savior. Because we all descend from Adam, we share in the fallen nature that resulted from his original disobedience to God (Romans 5:12). This sin separates us from a holy and perfect God. However, God in His great mercy sent Jesus Christ, the Son of God, to become a man and live the perfect life that Adam and we could not live. Jesus died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord.

Through Christ, God offers forgiveness and a restored relationship to anyone who believes. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your primary concern or question regarding the origin of humanity?
What does the Bible say about self-discipline?

Self-discipline, often referred to as self-control in the Bible, is not merely a matter of human willpower or moral grit. Instead, the Scriptures present it as a vital virtue produced by the Holy Spirit in the life of a believer. It is the ability to govern one's desires, emotions, and actions in accordance with God's will rather than being a slave to one's impulses.

The Bible teaches that self-discipline is a gift from God. Paul writes to Timothy that God has not given us a spirit of fear, but of power and of love and of a sound mind (2 Timothy 1:7). This "sound mind" is often translated as self-discipline or self-control. It is listed as a fruit of the Spirit, meaning it is the natural byproduct of a life surrendered to God (Galatians 5:22-23). Without this internal restraint, a person is vulnerable to many harms. Proverbs compares a man who lacks self-control to a city whose walls are broken through, leaving it defenseless against any enemy (Proverbs 25:28).

In the Christian life, self-discipline is compared to the rigorous training of an athlete. Paul explains that every athlete exercises self-control in all things to win a prize that will fade, but believers do so for an eternal crown. He speaks of "disciplining his body" and keeping it under control so that he might not be disqualified from the race (1 Corinthians 9:24-27). This discipline is not a means of earning God's favor, but a response to it. The grace of God, which brings salvation, also trains us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age (Titus 2:11-12).

Our consistent failure to remain perfectly self-disciplined points to a deeper reality: we are all prone to sin and unable to save ourselves through our own efforts. God is perfectly holy and just, and our lack of control often leads us into rebellion against Him. However, the Gospel provides hope. Jesus Christ lived a life of perfect self-discipline and obedience, never once succumbing to temptation. He died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. When we trust in Him, we are justified by grace through faith, and the Holy Spirit begins the work of progressive sanctification, empowering us to grow in the very discipline we once lacked. What do you find most difficult about practicing self-discipline in your daily life?
What is original sin?

Original sin refers to the fallen spiritual state of all human beings resulting from the first act of disobedience by Adam in the Garden of Eden. It describes how that initial rebellion affected every person born since, leaving us with both a status of guilt before God and a corrupted nature that is naturally inclined away from Him.

This doctrine is often understood through two primary lenses. First, there is inherited guilt, which means that because Adam acted as the representative head of the human race, his sin is credited to all his descendants. Romans 5:12 explains that sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned.
