Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or financial contributions we could offer (Ephesians 2:8-9).

When we understand the sacrificial love of Christ, our stewardship becomes a joyful response to His grace. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting God with every area of your life, including your finances?
Is intinction the correct way to do communion?

The practice of intinction, where the bread is dipped into the wine or juice before being consumed, is one of several ways that Christians observe the Lord’s Supper. Whether it is considered the "correct" way often depends on how a particular church or denomination interprets the biblical accounts of the institution of the sacrament.

In the New Testament, the accounts of the Last Supper describe Jesus taking the bread and the cup as two distinct actions. For example, in Matthew 26:26–28, Jesus takes the bread, blesses it, breaks it, and tells the disciples to "Eat." Then he takes the cup, gives thanks, and tells them to "Drink." Because of this specific sequence, many churches believe that eating the bread and drinking from the cup separately most closely follows the pattern Jesus established (Mark 14:22–24; Luke 22:14–20; 1 Corinthians 11:23–26).

However, the Bible does not explicitly forbid the practice of intinction. Throughout church history, various traditions have adopted it for practical reasons, such as concerns for hygiene or to simplify the distribution of the elements during large services. Those who practice intinction often emphasize that the essential elements of the bread and the fruit of the vine are both present and that the symbolic act of receiving Christ’s body and blood remains intact. 

Within the framework of Christian doctrine, the specific method of delivery (the mode) is generally considered a secondary or tertiary issue. This means that while it is important to seek a practice that is faithful to Scripture, it is not an essential of the faith that determines one's standing before God or the validity of the sacrament. The focus should remain on the "unity in essential beliefs, liberty in non-essentials, and charity in all things."

The most vital aspect of communion is not the physical method, but the spiritual reality of what it signifies. It is an act of remembrance and a proclamation of the death of Jesus Christ until he returns (1 Corinthians 11:24–26). It reminds us that we are reconciled to God through the sacrifice of his Son.

This sacrifice is the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet every human being has sinned and fallen short of his glory, resulting in our separation from him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Out of his immense love, God sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving his victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now the exalted Lord who offers salvation as a gift of grace. This salvation is received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or religious rituals we perform (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What thoughts do you have about Christ’s sacrifice for you?
What is the church suffering?

Suffering has been a reality for the Church since its beginning, as followers of Jesus Christ are called to share in the sufferings of their Lord. Scripture teaches that suffering is not an accident but is often a means God uses to refine our faith and display His glory. The Church experiences suffering in several distinct ways.

First, the Church suffers through external persecution. Throughout history and in many parts of the world today, believers face imprisonment, physical violence, social exclusion, and even death for their witness to Jesus. This is expected, as Jesus told His disciples that if the world hated Him, it would also hate them (John 15:18-20; Matthew 5:10-12).

Second, the Church suffers from internal trials. This includes the pain caused by sin within the community, the threat of false teaching that distorts the truth, and the burden of division among brothers and sisters (Acts 20:29-30; 2 Corinthians 11:28). The process of being made holy often involves the painful "pruning" of our own sinful desires and worldly attachments.

Third, Christians suffer the common afflictions of a fallen world. Believers are not exempt from the "groaning" of all creation, which includes sickness, mental anguish, poverty, and the death of loved ones (Romans 8:22-23). While we have hope, we still feel the sting of these broken realities.

Ultimately, suffering reminds us that we are not yet home. It points us to the holiness of God and our deep need for His grace. Though God is perfectly holy and just, we have all rebelled against Him through sin, which is why there is suffering and death in the world (Romans 3:23). Out of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, defeating sin and death forever. He is now the exalted Lord of all, and He offers salvation to everyone who believes.

We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9). This is the Gospel that sustains the Church in the midst of every trial.

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the hope of eternal life.

If you are currently walking through a season of suffering, please remember that God is "the Father of mercies and God of all comfort" (2 Corinthians 1:3-4). He promises never to leave or forsake those who belong to Him (Hebrews 13:5). I encourage you to bring your pain to Him in prayer, immerse yourself in the comfort of the Psalms, and seek the support of a local, Bible-believing church where you can be cared for by fellow believers.

What specific trial or question regarding suffering is on your heart today?
What does it mean that a person will not inherit the kingdom of God (1 Corinthians 6:9-11)?

To understand what it means to not inherit the kingdom of God, we must look at both the warning and the hope provided in the context of Paul’s letter. Inheriting the kingdom refers to entering into the fullness of God’s eternal reign, enjoying the presence of God forever, and receiving the glorious future promised to His children (Matthew 25:34; Galatians 5:21).

In 1 Corinthians 6:9–10, the Apostle Paul provides a list of various sins, including sexual immorality, idolatry, adultery, theft, greed, and drunkenness. He explains that those who live a life characterized by these practices will not inherit the kingdom. This does not mean a Christian who stumbles into sin loses their salvation, but rather that a settled, unrepentant lifestyle of sin is evidence that a person has not truly submitted to the Lordship of Christ. Because God is perfectly holy and just, He cannot allow sin to go unpunished or permit unrighteousness in His eternal kingdom (Habakkuk 1:13; Revelation 21:27).

However, the passage does not end with a message of condemnation. 1 Corinthians 6:11 contains one of the most powerful "buts" in the Bible: "And such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God."

This verse highlights several essential truths about our standing before God:

1. The Reality of Sin: We have all sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Left to ourselves, none of us are fit to inherit His kingdom.
2. The Work of Christ: Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).
3. Salvation by Grace: We are not saved by our own efforts or moral improvements. We are justified, or declared righteous, by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Romans 3:24; Ephesians 2:8–9).
4. Transformation: When the Holy Spirit brings us to faith, He washes us and begins a process of making us more like Jesus (2 Corinthians 3:18). Our identity is no longer defined by our past sins, but by our relationship with Christ.

The warning that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom serves as a call to examine ourselves and recognize our need for a Savior. While our sin separates us from a holy God, the Gospel is the good news that Jesus has made a way for us to be reconciled to Him.

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is it wise for Christian teens to be dating?

The question of whether it is wise for Christian teens to date is an important one that requires looking at biblical principles, as the modern concept of dating is not explicitly mentioned in Scripture. While the Bible does not give a specific age for when a person may begin dating, it provides clear guidance on wisdom, purity, and the purpose of relationships.

In the Christian life, the ultimate goal of a romantic relationship is to move toward marriage, which is a lifelong covenant between a man and a woman (Genesis 2:24). If a teenager is not yet in a season of life where marriage is a realistic possibility, one-on-one dating can often lead to unnecessary emotional pain or physical temptation before they are ready to make a lifelong commitment.

Wisdom suggests considering several biblical principles when thinking about teen dating:

1. Guarding the Heart: Scripture tells us to guard our hearts above all else, for everything we do flows from it (Proverbs 4:23). Romantic relationships involve deep emotions that can be difficult to manage during the formative teenage years.

2. Fleeing Temptation: Believers are encouraged to flee youthful passions and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace along with those who call on the Lord from a pure heart (2 Timothy 2:22). Dating creates situations where sexual temptation can be very strong. Maintaining purity is a way to honor God and a future spouse (1 Thessalonians 4:3-5).

3. Honoring Parents: God calls children and teenagers to honor and obey their parents (Ephesians 6:1-2). Seeking parental guidance and following their boundaries regarding dating is a practical way to live out this command.

4. Investing in Spiritual Growth: The teenage years are a vital time to grow in one's relationship with God and to serve the local church. High levels of focus on a single romantic partner can sometimes distract from the "undivided devotion to the Lord" that characterizes a life lived for Him (1 Corinthians 7:35).

Many families find that group activities and friendships within the church community are a wise way for teens to interact without the pressures of a serious dating relationship. This allows for the development of social skills and character while keeping the focus on Christ.

Ultimately, our deepest need for companionship and love is met in Jesus Christ. We are all prone to seek satisfaction in relationships, but true life is found only in Him. Because of our sin, we were separated from God, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He now offers forgiveness and eternal life to all who come to Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider this topic, what do you think is the primary goal of a relationship for a Christian?
Why does God allow us to go through trials and tribulations?

The question of why a good and sovereign God allows His children to experience pain and hardship is one of the deepest struggles of the Christian life. While we may not always understand the specific reasons for a particular trial, the Bible provides several ways that God uses these difficult seasons for our good and His glory.

First, God uses trials to produce spiritual maturity and endurance. In the book of James, we are encouraged to count it all joy when we meet trials of various kinds, for the testing of our faith produces steadfastness, leading us to be perfect and complete, lacking in nothing (James 1:2; James 1:3; James 1:4). Just as a muscle must be strained to grow stronger, our faith is often strengthened through the resistance of life's challenges.

Second, trials serve to refine our faith, much like gold is purified in a furnace. The apostle Peter explains that these various trials exist so that the tested genuineness of our faith, which is more precious than gold that perishes though it is tested by fire, may be found to result in praise and glory and honor at the revelation of Jesus Christ (1 Peter 1:6; 1 Peter 1:7). Through suffering, God often strips away our reliance on worldly comforts, teaching us to find our ultimate satisfaction and security in Him alone.

Third, our trials allow us to experience God's comfort so that we may share it with others. The Bible describes God as the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction, so that we may be able to comfort those who are in any affliction, with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God (2 Corinthians 1:3; 2 Corinthians 1:4). Our pain often becomes the platform for our ministry to others.

Fourth, trials remind us that we are not yet home. We live in a world broken by the fall (Genesis 3:17; Genesis 3:18; Genesis 3:19), and our groaning in this life creates a longing for the day when Christ will return and make all things new (Romans 8:22; Romans 8:23). These light momentary afflictions are preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison (2 Corinthians 4:17; 2 Corinthians 4:18).

Ultimately, we see God's purpose in suffering most clearly at the cross. God is holy and righteous, but our sin has separated us from Him (Isaiah 59:2). In His mercy, God the Son became man in the person of Jesus Christ, living a sinless life to fulfill God's law (Hebrews 4:15). He died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). He is now exalted as Lord over all things. We are saved from the penalty of sin by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, not by our own efforts or works (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). The greatest trial in history, the crucifixion of the Son of God, resulted in the greatest good, our redemption.

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to find forgiveness and a hope that transcends any trial. What is the most difficult aspect of the trials you are currently walking through?
What does Deuteronomy 10:12 tell us God requires of us?

Deuteronomy 10:12 provides a beautiful and comprehensive summary of what God desires from His people. The passage says, "And now, Israel, what does the Lord your God require of you, but to fear the Lord your God, to walk in all his ways, to love him, to serve the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul."

This verse highlights that God is not looking for mere outward rituals, but a heart level devotion that manifests in several specific ways. First, we are to fear the Lord, which means having a holy awe and deep reverence for His majesty, holiness, and authority. Second, we are to walk in all His ways, which involves following His character and the direction He has set for our lives. Third, we are to love Him, placing our highest affection and devotion on God Himself. Finally, we are to serve Him with all our heart and soul, dedicating our entire being and all our actions to His purposes. The following verse, Deuteronomy 10:13, adds that we are to keep His commandments for our own good, showing that God's requirements are ultimately for our benefit and flourishing.

While these requirements are good and holy, the Bible also teaches that because of our sinful nature, none of us can fulfill these commands perfectly on our own power, as expressed in Romans 3:23. We often fail to love God with our whole heart or walk consistently in His ways. This is why the Gospel is such good news for us today. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, came to earth and lived the life of perfect obedience that we could not live. He loved, feared, and served the Father perfectly in our place. He then died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to demonstrate His victory over death as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Through faith in Jesus, we are justified by grace and given the Holy Spirit, who begins a work of sanctification in us. This work enables us to truly begin fearing, loving, and serving God from the heart, not to earn His favor, but in response to the love He has already shown us in Christ as seen in 1 John 4:19.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is it okay to get tattoos if they are of a Christian nature?

The question of whether a Christian can get a tattoo is a matter of Christian liberty, as the New Testament does not give a specific command forbidding or allowing them. When making this decision, it is helpful to look at the biblical principles of conscience, stewardship, and witness.

Many people who express concern about tattoos reference Leviticus 19:28, which says, "You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead or tattoo yourselves: I am the LORD." In its historical context, this command was given to the Israelites to prevent them from participating in the pagan mourning rituals of the nations around them. As believers under the New Covenant, we are no longer bound by the ceremonial laws of the Old Testament, yet the principle of being distinct and set apart for God remains relevant.

When considering a tattoo, even one with a Christian theme, you might reflect on these areas:

1. **Motive and Conscience:** Why do you want this tattoo? If your heart is set on honoring God, that is a positive starting point. However, Romans 14:23 teaches that "whatever does not proceed from faith is sin." If you feel a lack of peace or if your conscience is troubled by the idea, it is wise to wait.

2. **Stewardship of the Body:** 1 Corinthians 6:19 and 1 Corinthians 6:20 remind us that "your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit" and that we are to "glorify God in your body." Consider whether getting a tattoo is the best way to care for and use the body God has entrusted to you.

3. **Influence and Witness:** In 1 Corinthians 10:23 and 1 Corinthians 10:24, the apostle Paul writes that while "all things are lawful," not all things are helpful or build up others. Think about how a tattoo might affect your relationships and your ability to share the Gospel in your specific community or culture.

Ultimately, the most important "mark" a person can have is not on their skin, but the internal transformation of the heart. The Bible tells us that God is holy and just, but because of our sin, we are separated from Him, as stated in Romans 3:23. Out of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His power over sin and death. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our outward appearance or works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your primary goal in wanting to get a Christian tattoo?
What day is the Sabbath, Saturday or Sunday?

In the biblical record, the Sabbath is the seventh day of the week, which is Saturday. This was established at creation when God rested from His work (Genesis 2:2-3) and was later formalised in the Ten Commandments given to Israel (Exodus 20:8-11).

However, most Christians throughout history have gathered for worship on the first day of the week, which is Sunday. This shift occurred because Jesus Christ rose from the dead on the first day of the week (Matthew 28:1; Mark 16:2). Following the resurrection, the early church began meeting on the first day to celebrate Christ’s victory over sin and death (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2). This day is often referred to in the New Testament as the Lord’s Day (Revelation 1:10).

Within the Christian faith, there are different perspectives on how these days relate to one another:

1. Many believers hold to a view often called the "Christian Sabbath." They believe that the moral principle of setting aside one day in seven for rest and worship remains in effect, but that the specific day shifted from the seventh to the first to honour the new creation begun by Jesus' resurrection.

2. Other believers emphasize Christian liberty, noting that the specific day is less important than the heart behind the worship. They often point to the teachings of the Apostle Paul, who wrote that we should not let anyone judge us regarding a Sabbath day, as these were a shadow of things to come, but the substance belongs to Christ (Colossians 2:16-17). He also noted that some esteem one day as better than another while others esteem all days alike, urging each person to be fully convinced in their own mind (Romans 14:5-6).

Ultimately, the Sabbath points us to the eternal rest found only in Jesus Christ. While the Old Testament Sabbath provided a physical rest from labour, Jesus provides a spiritual rest for our souls. He invites all people, saying, "Come to me, all who labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28).

True rest is not found merely in a day of the week, but in a relationship with God through His Son. Because of our sin, we are restless and separated from God, but through the perfect life, sacrificial death, and bodily resurrection of Jesus, we can be forgiven and reconciled to Him. You can experience this peace and rest today by turning from your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone as your Saviour and Lord.

How do you personally view the importance of a day of rest in your own life?
Why did God punish Pharaoh for Abram’s lie (Genesis 12:17)?

The account of Abram, Sarai, and Pharaoh in Genesis 12:10-20 can be difficult to process because, at first glance, it seems that the person who told the lie escaped consequences while the person who was deceived suffered. To understand why God struck Pharaoh and his house with plagues, it is helpful to look at the larger context of God's plan for redemption and His character as a protector.

The primary reason for God's intervention was the protection of the covenant promise. Just a few verses earlier, in Genesis 12:1-3, God promised Abram that He would make him into a great nation and that through his offspring, all the families of the earth would be blessed. This promise eventually finds its fulfillment in the birth of Jesus Christ. If Sarai had remained in Pharaoh’s harem and a child had been born to them, the lineage of the promised Savior would have been compromised. God intervened with "great plagues" (Genesis 12:17) to prevent Pharaoh from touching Sarai, thereby preserving the purity of the family line through which the Messiah would come.

While Abram’s lie was an act of fear and a failure to trust God, Pharaoh’s actions, even if based on a misunderstanding, involved taking a woman into his palace for his own use. In the ancient world, kings often had the power to take whoever they wanted, but God’s intervention served as a boundary. By sending these afflictions, God alerted Pharaoh that something was wrong. In this sense, the plagues were not just a punishment but a divine warning and a means of restraint. They prevented Pharaoh from committing a greater sin of adultery and allowed Sarai to be returned to her husband.

This story also highlights the sovereignty and faithfulness of God. Abram was clearly in the wrong for his deception and for putting his wife in danger. However, the Bible shows that God’s purposes are not thwarted by human failure. God remained faithful to His promise even when His servant was unfaithful (2 Timothy 2:13). The incident served to show both the Egyptians and Abram that the God of the Covenant is supreme over all earthly powers, including the mighty Pharaoh.

This account ultimately points us to our need for a perfect Savior. Like Abram, we often fail, let fear dictate our choices, and act in ways that are deceptive or selfish. We see in this narrative that while humans are inconsistent and sinful, God is holy, just, and merciful. He protects His plan of salvation so that He can bring us back to Himself.

The same God who protected the line of Abram is the one who sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived the perfect life that Abram and all of us have failed to live, and He died on the cross to take the punishment for our sins. He rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death and His power to save (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

God’s holiness requires that sin be dealt with, but His love provides the way for us to be forgiven. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness and new life that only He can offer.

As you reflect on this passage, does it change the way you view God’s faithfulness in the midst of your own failures?
Christmas Story — Day 4 — Do Christians worship Mary?

In the historic Christian faith, we do not worship Mary. While she is rightly honored as a woman of incredible faith and the mother of our Lord Jesus Christ, worship is a sacred act reserved for God alone. The Bible is very clear that we are to have no other gods before the Lord, as seen in Exodus 20:3-5, and Jesus Himself reaffirmed this when He said, "Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only," in Matthew 4:10.

Mary is a wonderful example of what it looks like to trust God. When the angel Gabriel told her she would conceive the Son of God by the Holy Spirit, her response was one of humble submission. She said, "I am the Lord’s servant. May your word to me be fulfilled," in Luke 1:38. Christians look to her as a sister in Christ who was "highly favored" (Luke 1:28) and "blessed among women" (Luke 1:42), but we recognize that she was a human being who, like all of us, needed a Savior.

In her own beautiful song of praise, often called the Magnificat, Mary said, "My soul glorifies the Lord and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior," in Luke 1:46-47. By calling God her Savior, Mary acknowledged her own need for the redemption that only her Son, Jesus, could provide.

The focus of the Christmas story and the heart of the Christian faith is Jesus Christ. He is the eternal Son of God who took on human flesh to do what no one else could do. Because God is perfectly holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are separated from Him. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

The Bible teaches that there is only one way to be reconciled to God. 1 Timothy 2:5 says, "For there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus." We do not pray to Mary or worship her because our direct access to the Father is through Jesus Christ alone (John 14:6; Acts 4:12).

This Christmas, as we remember the miracle of the incarnation, we thank God for Mary’s obedience, but we fix our eyes on Jesus. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What is it about the person of Jesus that stands out most to you as you consider the Christmas story?
What is Kemetic spirituality / Kemetism?

Kemetic spirituality, often referred to as Kemetism or Kemetic Paganism, is a modern religious movement that seeks to revive or be inspired by the beliefs and practices of ancient Egypt. The term comes from "Kemet," which was the ancient name for Egypt, meaning "the black land" in reference to the fertile soil surrounding the Nile River.

Adherents of Kemetism typically focus on the worship of the Egyptian pantheon, known as the Netjeru. This includes well known figures such as Ra, Isis, Osiris, Horus, and Anubis. A foundational concept in this belief system is "Ma'at," which represents the principles of truth, balance, order, and justice. Followers often strive to live in accordance with Ma'at to maintain harmony in the world and to prepare for the afterlife.

From a Christian perspective, we recognize the human longing for order, justice, and a connection to the divine as a reflection of being created in the image of God (Genesis 1:26–27). However, the Bible teaches that there is only one true and living God who created all things and who exists eternally in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). While Kemetism looks to many deities or various manifestations of a divine essence, Scripture reveals that God is the sovereign and holy Creator who does not share His glory with idols or other gods (Isaiah 42:8; Isaiah 44:6).

The pursuit of Ma'at suggests that humans can achieve a state of righteousness through their own efforts and rituals. In contrast, the Gospel acknowledges that God is perfectly holy, but it also honestly addresses the fact that all people have sinned and fallen short of God's standard (Romans 3:23). Because of this separation, we cannot earn our way to God or balance our own scales of justice. 

The good news is that God provided the way for us to be reconciled to Him through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfectly righteous life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). This victory over sin and death demonstrates His authority as Lord. Salvation is not something we earn through rituals or moral balance, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9).

I invite you to turn from any other spiritual path and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive complete forgiveness and new life. What do you find most compelling about the ancient Egyptian view of the world?
Should Christians be concerned about the idea of Sharia Law?

The question of how Christians should view Sharia Law, or any legal system based on a different religious foundation, involves reflecting on our dual identity as citizens of an earthly nation and citizens of the kingdom of heaven. While we are called to be peaceful and productive members of society, our primary concern remains the glory of God and the advancement of the Gospel of Jesus Christ.

From a biblical perspective, there are several reasons why a Christian might view the implementation of Sharia Law with caution. First and foremost is the principle of religious liberty. The Bible teaches that God has given individuals the responsibility to respond to Him, and true faith cannot be coerced by the state. When any legal system mandates specific religious observances or restricts the freedom to proclaim the Gospel, it comes into conflict with the Great Commission, where Jesus commanded His followers to make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:18-20). We see in the book of Acts that when human laws forbade the preaching of Christ, the apostles responded by saying they must obey God rather than men (Acts 5:29).

Furthermore, Christians hold that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and final authority for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16-17). Any legal system that derives its ultimate authority from a source other than the Word of God will necessarily diverge from the biblical understanding of justice, human dignity, and the nature of God. While Christians believe that God has established civil government to maintain order and punish wrongdoing (Romans 13:1-4), we also believe that every human being is made in the image of God (Genesis 1:26-27). This inherent dignity is best protected in systems that recognize universal human rights and the freedom of conscience.

It is also important to consider the distinction between law and grace. A legalistic system, whether religious or secular, often focuses on outward conformity to a set of rules. The Bible teaches that the law is good because it shows us God's holy standard, but it also reveals our inability to keep that standard perfectly (Romans 3:20). No legal code can change a person's heart or reconcile them to God.

This leads us to the heart of the Christian message. We believe that God is perfectly holy and just, and that all of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment. However, God, in His great love and mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to the earth. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, fulfilling the requirements of God's law on our behalf. He then died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice, taking the punishment that our sins deserved. Three days later, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming that He is the Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is not achieved by following a legal code or doing good works, but is a gift received through grace alone, by faith alone, in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). When we recognize our need for a Savior and put our trust in what Jesus has done, we are justified and given a new life through the Holy Spirit.

While it is natural to be concerned about legal changes that might impact our freedom to worship or share our faith, our ultimate hope is not in earthly governments or legal systems. We trust in the sovereignty of God, who holds the hearts of kings in His hand (Proverbs 21:1). As we engage with these topics, we should do so with prayer and a commitment to love our neighbors, including those with whom we deeply disagree.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything in particular about these different legal systems that worries you the most as you think about your faith?
What is the meaning of the foxes in Song of Solomon 2:15?

In Song of Solomon 2:15, the "foxes" symbolize the subtle threats, sins, or distractions that can damage the intimacy and health of a relationship. The verse says, "Catch the foxes for us, the little foxes that spoil the vineyards, for our vineyards are in blossom." In the context of this poetic book, the vineyard represents the blossoming love and fruitfulness between the couple. Because the foxes mentioned are "little," the passage suggests that it is often the small, seemingly insignificant issues that do the most harm.

While a large threat is easy to see and defend against, these little foxes sneak in quietly. In a marriage or a close friendship, these might represent small lies, unresolved irritations, or a lack of intentional time together. If these are not "caught" or dealt with early, they can chew away at the roots of trust and affection, preventing the relationship from reaching its full maturity.

From a spiritual perspective, these foxes can also represent "small" compromises or secret sins that hinder your fellowship with God. Scripture warns that even "a little leaven leavens the whole lump," as noted in Galatians 5:9. Believers are encouraged to be vigilant and to "give no opportunity to the devil," as stated in Ephesians 4:27.

Ultimately, your ability to guard your heart and your relationships comes from the grace of God. While sin once separated us from Him, Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose again on the third day to reconcile us to God. Through His victory over sin and death, we are given the Holy Spirit to help us identify and remove these "little foxes" in our lives. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What do you feel are the biggest "little foxes" or distractions currently affecting your spiritual walk?
What is the false cause fallacy?

The false cause fallacy occurs when someone incorrectly assumes that because two events happen together, or one follows the other, the first event must be the cause of the second. In logic, this is often expressed by the Latin phrases "post hoc ergo propter hoc" (after this, therefore because of this) or "cum hoc ergo propter hoc" (with this, therefore because of this). Just because a correlation exists between two things does not mean there is a causal relationship between them.

In a biblical context, we see an example of this fallacy in the book of Job. When Job experienced immense suffering and loss, his friends assumed that his pain was a direct result of some hidden sin he had committed. Eliphaz, one of his friends, argued that those who sow trouble reap it in Job 4:7-8. While it is true that sin can lead to suffering, his friends committed a false cause fallacy by assuming Job's specific trials were definitely caused by a specific moral failure. Jesus also corrected this type of thinking when His disciples asked if a man was born blind because of his own sin or his parents' sin in John 9:1-3. Jesus explained that the man's condition was not caused by a specific sin but was an opportunity for the works of God to be displayed.

Developing discernment is essential for every believer so that we do not make hasty or incorrect judgments about why things happen in our lives or the lives of others. Proverbs 14:15 reminds us that the simple believes everything, but the prudent gives thought to his steps. While we may struggle to understand the immediate causes of trials or blessings in this world, Scripture provides clarity on the most significant cause and effect in human history.

The ultimate cause of the brokenness, suffering, and death we see in the world is human sin, which separates us from a holy and perfect God as described in Romans 3:23. However, God in His great mercy provided the only solution through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord as seen in Romans 5:8 and 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Salvation is not something we cause or earn through our own good works, but it is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, who is the only way to the Father according to John 14:6 and Ephesians 2:8-9.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is it a sin to share copyrighted material on the internet?

Sharing copyrighted material without the permission of the owner is generally considered a sin because it involves both a violation of the law of the land and the biblical principle against theft.

The Bible instructs believers to submit to the governing authorities that God has established. Romans 13:1 states, "Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God." Additionally, 1 Peter 2:13–14 encourages Christians to "Be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution." Since copyright laws are part of the legal framework of our society, breaking them is a failure to honor the authorities God has placed over us, unless those laws require us to directly disobey God.

Sharing material that you do not have the right to distribute is also a form of taking something that does not belong to you. Exodus 20:15 provides the clear command, "You shall not steal." Creators and authors spend time, effort, and resources to produce their work, and they have a right to the fruits of their labor. Taking or giving away their intellectual property without authorization deprives them of what is rightfully theirs. Ephesians 4:28 says, "Let the thief no longer steal, but rather let him labor, doing honest work with his own hands, so that he may have something to share with anyone in need."

Furthermore, Jesus taught that the second greatest commandment is to "love your neighbor as yourself" in Matthew 22:39. Loving our neighbor includes respecting their work, their property, and their ability to provide for themselves and their families through their creations.

While it can be easy to view digital sharing as a victimless or minor issue, God cares about the integrity of our hearts in every area of life, including how we handle the property of others. This reminder of our own imperfection and our tendency to seek our own convenience over God's standards points to our deep need for a Savior. 

The Gospel message is that while God is perfectly holy and we have all failed to keep His laws, He has provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived a sinless life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was raised from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. We are not saved by our own good works or our ability to follow every law perfectly, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What is it about this particular issue that is weighing on your heart today?
What does the Bible say about Christian fathers?

The Bible presents fatherhood as a high calling and a sacred stewardship, where an earthly father is called to reflect the character and love of God the Father. Scripture provides clear guidance on the responsibilities, heart posture, and spiritual leadership required of a Christian father.

**Instruction and Spiritual Leadership**
A primary role of a father is to lead his children in the knowledge of God. This is not meant to be a cold or academic exercise, but a consistent, daily sharing of faith. Fathers are encouraged to talk about God’s Word in the natural rhythm of life, whether sitting at home or traveling (Deuteronomy 6:6-7). The goal is to bring children up in the "discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4). This involves teaching them the wisdom of Scripture and helping them understand how to live a life that honors God (Proverbs 22:6).

**Gentle Encouragement and Discipline**
The Bible balances the need for discipline with the need for tenderness. Fathers are specifically commanded not to provoke their children to anger or discourage them (Colossians 3:21). This means exercising authority with patience and grace rather than through harshness or unfairness. Discipline is viewed as an expression of love, modeled after how God disciplines His own children for their good (Hebrews 12:7-11; Proverbs 13:24). A godly father seeks to be "merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love," mirroring the heart of the Lord (Psalm 103:8; Psalm 103:13).

**Provision and Protection**
Fathers are also called to be providers and protectors of their households. Scripture speaks strongly about the necessity of caring for one's family, noting that providing for the physical and spiritual needs of the household is a fundamental aspect of living out the faith (1 Timothy 5:8). This provision is a reflection of how God, our Heavenly Father, knows our needs and provides for us (Matthew 7:11).

**The Need for Grace**
While the biblical standard for fathers is high, the Bible also recognizes that no human father is perfect. Every father falls short of the glory of God and needs the same grace he is called to extend to his children. This is where the Gospel is central to fatherhood. 

God is perfectly holy, yet because of our sin, we are all separated from Him (Isaiah 59:2). In His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering sin and death. Jesus is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not earned by being a "good father" or through any other works; it is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What was the process of deciding on the Old Testament canon?

The formation of the Old Testament canon was a process of recognition rather than an act of creation. The community of God's people did not grant authority to these books, but rather recognized the authority already inherent in them because they were inspired by the Holy Spirit. This recognition happened progressively over many centuries as God spoke through His prophets.

The Hebrew Bible, often referred to as the Tanakh, is traditionally divided into three sections: the Law (Torah), the Prophets (Nevi'im), and the Writings (Ketuvim). The Law, consisting of the first five books written by Moses, was recognized as authoritative almost immediately by the Israelites (Exodus 24:3, Deuteronomy 31:24-26). As God raised up prophets to speak to His people, their writings were also collected and preserved because they were recognized as the very words of God (Jeremiah 36:1-4, Daniel 9:2). By the time of the third century before Christ, the vast majority of the Old Testament was already established and used in Jewish worship.

A primary test for whether a book belonged in the canon was prophetic authorship. The people of God looked for books written by a prophet or someone with a recognized prophetic gift, ensuring the message was consistent with previous revelation. These books also demonstrated a unique power to transform lives and were used consistently in the corporate worship of Israel.

For Christians, the highest authority for the Old Testament canon is Jesus Christ Himself. Throughout His ministry, Jesus treated the Hebrew Scriptures as a completed and authoritative body of work. He frequently quoted from all three sections of the Hebrew Bible, famously telling His disciples after His resurrection that everything written about Him in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms must be fulfilled (Luke 24:44). By referring to the Scriptures in this way, Jesus affirmed the collection of books that we recognize today as the Old Testament.

The Old Testament serves as the foundation for the Gospel, documenting God's holiness and the history of humanity's fall into sin (Genesis 3:1-19). Because God is perfectly just and holy, He cannot overlook sin, yet in His great mercy, He promised a Redeemer who would crush the head of the serpent (Genesis 3:15). The entire Old Testament points toward this promised Savior, Jesus Christ. 

Jesus is the eternal Son of God who took on human nature (John 1:1, John 1:14), lived a perfect life of obedience that we could never live, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What is your current understanding of how the Bible influences your daily life?
Is cyber-sex / phone sex a sin?

The Bible teaches that God designed sexual intimacy to be a beautiful and holy expression of love between a husband and a wife within the covenant of marriage (Genesis 2:24; Hebrews 13:4). When we look at the question of cyber-sex or phone sex, we must look at both the physical actions and the heart intentions behind them.

Jesus clarified that God's standard for purity involves not only our outward actions but also the desires of our hearts. He taught, "But I say to you that everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart" (Matthew 5:28). Because these activities involve cultivating and acting upon sexual desire outside of the marital bond, they fall under the biblical definition of sexual immorality.

Scripture calls believers to "flee from sexual immorality" and reminds us that our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit, bought with the price of Christ's blood (1 Corinthians 6:18; 1 Corinthians 6:19–20). We are also encouraged that among followers of Christ, there should not even be a "hint" of sexual immorality or any kind of impurity (Ephesians 5:3). Engaging in these activities can grieve the Holy Spirit and hinder our progressive sanctification, which is the process of becoming more like Jesus.

The struggle with these behaviors often reveals a deeper heart-longing for intimacy, comfort, or escape that only God can truly satisfy. While it is painful to recognize where we have fallen short of God’s holy standard, the Gospel provides a path for healing and restoration. God is perfectly holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, in His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived the perfect, sinless life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Salvation and forgiveness are not earned by our own efforts or by "cleaning ourselves up" first. Instead, we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

If you are struggling with this, please know there is no shadow of sin that the light of Christ cannot overcome. I encourage you to seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can find pastoral guidance and a community of believers to walk alongside you. Walking in the light with others is a vital part of finding freedom (1 John 1:7).

What holds you back from trusting Him with this area of your life today?
Do unbelievers immediately go to hell when they die?

The Bible teaches that there is an immediate conscious reality for every person after death. For those who have not placed their trust in Jesus Christ, Scripture describes an immediate state of separation from God. In the account of the rich man and Lazarus, Jesus describes the rich man as being in a place of torment immediately after he died (Luke 16:22–23). This intermediate state is a waiting period until the final judgment. The writer of Hebrews explains that people are appointed to die once, and after that comes judgment (Hebrews 9:27).

Ultimately, at the end of the age, Jesus Christ will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead. Those who have rejected God's grace will face the Great White Throne Judgment and be cast into the lake of fire, which is the final and eternal state of hell (Revelation 20:11–15). Because God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just, He cannot leave sin unpunished.

This reality is why the message of Jesus is so vital and is truly "good news." We have all sinned and fallen short of God's glory, earning the penalty of spiritual death (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, because of His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take our place. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the full debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts or good works, but it is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How should Christians react to persecution against the LGBTQ community?

The Christian response to persecution against any group, including the LGBTQ community, must be rooted in the fundamental truth that every human being is created in the image of God (Genesis 1:27). Because all people possess inherent dignity and worth, Christians are called to treat every individual with kindness, respect, and compassion. Scripture commands believers to love their neighbors as themselves (Matthew 22:39) and to seek justice for those who are being oppressed or mistreated (Isaiah 1:17; Psalm 82:3).

Persecution, which often involves violence, dehumanization, or the stripping of basic human rights, is contrary to the heart of God. While Christians hold to a biblical understanding of marriage and sexuality as defined in the Word of God, this conviction should never be used as a justification for malice, hatred, or harm. Jesus Christ modeled a perfect balance of grace and truth (John 1:14). He engaged with those marginalized by society with profound love, yet He also called everyone to repentance and holiness.

Followers of Christ are called to be peacemakers (Matthew 5:9) and to manifest the fruit of the Spirit, which includes love, peace, and gentleness (Galatians 5:22-23). This means standing against cruelty and protecting the vulnerable, even when there are deep theological or moral disagreements. Our goal is to reflect the character of God, who is patient and does not wish for any to perish (2 Peter 3:9).

Ultimately, our interactions should point toward the Gospel. We recognize that every person, regardless of their background, is separated from God by sin and is in need of the reconciliation that only Jesus Christ provides. God is holy and just, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise again to defeat death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What questions do you have about how the love of Christ relates to these difficult cultural issues?
How did Satan and other angels sin if heaven is sinless?

The origin of sin in a perfect environment is one of the deepest mysteries of the Christian faith. To understand how Satan and the fallen angels could sin, we must look at how God created them and the nature of moral choice.

God is holy and cannot be the author of sin (James 1:13). When He created the angelic realm, He declared His creation "very good" (Genesis 1:31). This means that angels were created with a perfect nature, but they were also created as personal, moral beings with a will. For a creature to truly love, worship, and obey God, there must be the possibility of choosing not to do so.

Scripture indicates that sin originated not from an external temptation, but from within the heart of the creature. In the case of Satan, often identified in prophetic lamentations that look beyond earthly kings to the spiritual power behind them, the root of sin was pride. Isaiah 14:13-14 describes this internal rebellion: "You said in your heart, 'I will ascend to heaven; above the stars of God I will set my throne on high... I will make myself like the Most High.'" Similarly, Ezekiel 28:15-17 speaks of a high-ranking cherub who was "blameless" until "unrighteousness was found" in him because his heart became proud due to his beauty.

While heaven is the dwelling place of God’s holiness, it was not "sinless" in the sense that it was a place where sin was impossible, but rather a place where sin had not yet occurred. The moment Satan and the angels who followed him exercised their wills to rebel against God’s authority, they became sinful. Because God is perfectly just and holy, He did not allow this rebellion to remain in His immediate, blissful presence. These angels "did not stay within their own position of authority, but left their proper dwelling" (Jude 1:6) and were cast out to await final judgment (2 Peter 2:4).

This rebellion in the heavenly places eventually reached humanity in the Garden of Eden. Just as the angels fell through pride and disobedience, the first humans chose to trust themselves over God’s Word. This fall brought sin, suffering, and death into our world, separating us from our holy Creator.

However, the story does not end with our separation. Because God is merciful and loving, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. While there is no redemption offered for the fallen angels, God offers grace to every person. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. Is there anything in particular that makes it difficult for you to trust Him today?
What is the greatest sin?

When considering what the greatest sin is, the Bible provides a few different perspectives that help us understand the nature of our rebellion against God.

From one perspective, the greatest sin is the violation of the greatest commandment. When Jesus was asked which commandment was the most important, He replied, "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind. This is the great and first commandment" in Matthew 22:37-38. Therefore, the most fundamental sin is failing to love God with our entire being, which results in putting ourselves or other things in His rightful place.

Many also point to pride as the root of all other sins. It was pride that led to the fall of humanity in the Garden of Eden when Adam and Eve desired to be "like God" as described in Genesis 3:5. Pride is a heart attitude that rejects God’s authority and asserts its own will above His.

In a final sense, the most consequential sin is the persistent rejection of the Holy Spirit’s testimony about Jesus Christ. Jesus spoke of an "eternal sin" or blasphemy against the Holy Spirit in Matthew 12:31-32. This is generally understood as a hardened, final refusal to repent and believe the Gospel. Since Jesus is the only way to the Father according to John 14:6, rejecting Him leaves a person without a remedy for their sins.

It is important to remember that God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just. Because of this, even what we might consider a "small" sin is a serious offense against an infinite God. Scripture tells us that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" in Romans 3:23 and that "the wages of sin is death" in Romans 6:23.

The beauty of the Gospel is that while our sin is great, God’s grace is greater. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of obedience that we could never live. He then took our sin upon Himself, dying a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. Because of His work, salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or merit.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about sedition?

The Bible generally views sedition, which involves inciting rebellion or stirring up strife against established authority, as a violation of the order God has ordained for human society. Scripture teaches that human authorities are ultimately established by God to maintain peace and restrain evil.

In the New Testament, the apostle Paul provides clear instruction regarding the relationship between a believer and the state. He writes that every person should be subject to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God. Therefore, whoever resists the authorities resists what God has appointed, and those who resist will incur judgment. Romans 13:1; Romans 13:2. This teaching emphasizes that resisting legitimate authority is, in a sense, resisting the providence of God.

The apostle Peter also encourages a spirit of submission for the sake of the Gospel. He instructs believers to be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution, whether it be to the emperor as supreme or to governors as sent by him to punish those who do evil and to praise those who do good. 1 Peter 2:13; 1 Peter 2:14. Peter explains that this conduct honors God and serves as a witness to the world. 1 Peter 2:15.

Furthermore, sedition is often associated with the "works of the flesh" in the New Testament. In some translations, the word for "dissensions" or "factions" is rendered as "seditions," appearing in a list of behaviors that are contrary to the Spirit of God. Galatians 5:19; Galatians 5:20; Galatians 5:21. The Old Testament also warns against a spirit of lawlessness, stating that rebellion is as the sin of divination, and presumption is as iniquity and idolatry. 1 Samuel 15:23.

While the Bible commands submission, it also establishes that God is the highest authority. If a human government commands a believer to sin or to violate a direct command from God, the believer's primary loyalty must be to the Lord. When the apostles were ordered to stop preaching about Jesus, they replied that they must obey God rather than men. Acts 5:29. However, this is presented as a commitment to faithful obedience to God rather than a call to violent insurrection or the pursuit of political power through sedition.

Jesus Christ Himself was falsely accused of sedition and stirring up the people during His trial before Pontius Pilate. Luke 23:2; Luke 23:5. Though He was innocent of any crime against the state, He submitted to the cross to fulfill the Father's plan for our salvation. 1 Peter 2:21; 1 Peter 2:22; 1 Peter 2:23.

The Bible teaches that we are all naturally rebels against God's perfect authority. This rebellion is called sin, and it separates us from our holy Creator. But God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die an atoning death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our own efforts or our citizenship, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As we live in light of this grace, we are called to pray for all who are in high positions, that we may lead a peaceful and quiet life, godly and dignified in every way. 1 Timothy 2:1; 1 Timothy 2:2.
What is the International Christian Church (ICC), and what do they believe?

The International Christian Church (ICC), also known as the Sold-Out Discipling Movement, is a religious organization founded in 2007 by Kip McKean. It began as a split from the International Churches of Christ (ICOC) after McKean was asked to step down from leadership there. The ICC identifies itself as a restorationist movement, aiming to return to the practices and spirit of the first-century church as described in the book of Acts.

To understand what the ICC believes, it is helpful to look at several of their core convictions, many of which are shared with other Restoration Movement churches, though the ICC applies them with a particular intensity.

**1. The Doctrine of Discipleship and Baptism**
The ICC teaches that a person must first become a "disciple" before they can be baptized. In their view, baptism is the exact moment of salvation and the point at which one receives the Holy Spirit (Acts 2:38). However, they emphasize that if a person was baptized without a full understanding of what it means to be a "sold-out" disciple, that baptism is invalid. Consequently, they often require new members who come from other Christian backgrounds to be rebaptized within their movement.

**2. Exclusivity and the "One True Church"**
A significant part of ICC teaching is the belief that they are the only true movement of God on earth today. They often teach that other denominations are not part of the true church because they do not follow the same strict requirements for discipleship and baptism. This exclusive view is a point of concern for many historical Christian traditions, as the Bible teaches that the church is composed of all who truly call upon the name of the Lord Jesus Christ (1 Corinthians 1:2).

**3. Hierarchical Authority and Discipling Relationships**
The ICC utilizes a very structured, top-down leadership model. Every member is assigned a "discipling partner" or "mentor" to whom they are expected to be accountable. While the Bible encourages believers to sharpen one another (Proverbs 27:17), critics and former members often describe these ICC relationships as high-control, noting that mentors may give direction on personal matters like finances, dating, and career choices.

**4. The Great Commission and Evangelism**
The movement is known for its intense focus on world evangelism. They believe every member must be actively making disciples to remain in a right relationship with God. This often leads to a high-pressure environment where spiritual "fruitfulness" is measured by the number of people a member brings to services or studies.

**Biblical Discernment**
While the ICC emphasizes total commitment to Jesus, it is important to weigh their teachings against the full counsel of Scripture. The New Testament teaches that salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any human works or level of commitment (Ephesians 2:8-9). While true faith will naturally produce good works and a desire to obey Christ (James 2:17), these works are the result of salvation, not the cause of it.

Furthermore, the Gospel is centered on the finished work of Jesus Christ. The Bible declares that Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He did this to reconcile us to a holy God, satisfying God's justice and displaying His immense love.

The call of the Gospel is not a call to join a specific human organization or to achieve a certain level of performance. It is a call to recognize our sin and separation from God and to put our total trust in the Lord Jesus Christ. As the Scripture says, "If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved" (Romans 10:9).

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

If you are exploring these things, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John or the book of Romans to see how the apostles described the path to peace with God. What are your thoughts on how a person becomes right with God?
Do some Christmas traditions have pagan origins?

Many historians and scholars agree that several traditions associated with Christmas have roots in the ancient Roman and Germanic cultures that predated the spread of Christianity. For example, the date of December 25th coincided with the Roman festival of Saturnalia and the birthday of the Unconquered Sun, while the use of greenery and evergreen trees has links to ancient winter solstice celebrations like Yule in Northern Europe.

As the Gospel spread across the world, the early church often chose to repurpose existing cultural dates and symbols to celebrate the birth of Jesus Christ. Rather than participating in the original religious meanings of those customs, Christians redirected the focus toward the "Light of the World" (John 8:12). This is a practice often viewed through the lens of Christian liberty. The Apostle Paul writes that one person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems all days alike, and each should be fully convinced in his own mind (Romans 14:5). He also encourages believers not to let anyone pass judgment on them in questions of food and drink, or with regard to a festival or a new moon or a Sabbath, as these are a shadow of the things to come, but the substance belongs to Christ (Colossians 2:16–17).

While the historical origins of specific customs can be complex, the heart of the Christmas season for a believer is the miracle of the Incarnation. This is the truth that God the Son took on human nature, being born of a virgin (Matthew 1:23), to dwell among us. He lived a sinless life and ultimately gave Himself as a sacrifice on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over death and His identity as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Regardless of where a tradition began, it can be used to point to the glory of God's grace. The most important thing is not the history of a tree or a date, but our response to the Savior who came to seek and save the lost. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What has your own experience been with these traditions as you seek to honor God?
Where does the Old Testament predict the coming of Christ?

The Old Testament is filled with promises and "shadows" that point directly to the coming of Jesus Christ. From the very beginning of human history, God promised a Savior who would undo the effects of sin and reconcile humanity to Himself.

In the garden of Eden, immediately after the fall of man, God promised that the "seed" of the woman would eventually crush the head of the serpent (Genesis 3:15). This is often called the first Gospel message, as it foretells Christ’s ultimate victory over Satan through His death and resurrection.

Regarding His birth and identity, the prophets were remarkably specific. Isaiah 7:14 predicted that a virgin would conceive and bear a son named Immanuel, which means "God with us." Centuries later, Micah 5:2 named the small town of Bethlehem as the birthplace of the one whose "goings forth are from of old, from everlasting." Isaiah 9:6 expanded on His divine nature, calling Him "Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace."

The nature of His mission and His suffering was also clearly laid out. Psalm 22:16-18 describes the piercing of His hands and feet and the casting of lots for His clothing, details that were literally fulfilled during the crucifixion. Perhaps most profoundly, Isaiah 53 describes the "Suffering Servant" who would be "pierced for our transgressions" and "crushed for our iniquities" (Isaiah 53:5). This passage explains the core of the Gospel: that Christ took the punishment we deserved so that by His wounds we might be healed (Isaiah 53:4-6).

Beyond His death, the Old Testament also points to His resurrection. Psalm 16:10 declares that God would not let His "Holy One see corruption," a passage the apostles later used to show that Jesus rose bodily from the grave.

These prophecies demonstrate that Jesus is not just a moral teacher, but the eternal Son of God who became man to save sinners. Because God is holy and we have all sinned against Him, we are separated from His presence. However, out of His great love, God sent Jesus to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death, and rise again to secure our forgiveness. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As you consider these ancient promises and their fulfillment in Jesus, what holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is spiritual adultery?

Spiritual adultery is a biblical metaphor used to describe the unfaithfulness of God's people toward Him. Just as physical adultery involves a person breaking their marriage vows by being intimate with someone other than their spouse, spiritual adultery occurs when those in a covenant relationship with God turn their hearts, worship, and primary affections away from Him and toward idols or the things of this world.

In the Old Testament, God frequently used the marriage bond to illustrate His relationship with Israel. He was the faithful Husband, and Israel was His bride. When the people of Israel began worshipping the gods of the surrounding nations, the prophets often used the language of adultery and prostitution to describe their sin. For example, the Lord told the prophet Hosea to marry an unfaithful woman to provide a living picture of how the nation had "committed great whoredom by forsaking the Lord" (Hosea 1:2). The prophet Jeremiah also recorded God's grief over His people, noting that they had "polluted the land with your vile prostitution" by serving other gods (Jeremiah 3:1-2; Jeremiah 3:8-9).

The New Testament continues this theme by identifying the Church as the Bride of Christ (Ephesians 5:25-27; Revelation 19:7). Spiritual adultery in this context is often linked to worldliness and divided loyalties. The apostle James used strong language when he wrote, "You adulterous people! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God?" (James 4:4). This teaches that spiritual adultery is not limited to the worship of physical statues; it happens whenever we allow any person, pursuit, or desire to take the place that rightfully belongs to God alone.

At its core, spiritual adultery is a violation of the greatest commandment to love the Lord our God with all our heart, soul, mind, and strength (Matthew 22:37). It is an attempt to find the security, identity, and satisfaction that only God can provide in created things rather than the Creator (Romans 1:25). Because God is perfectly holy and loves His people deeply, He is "jealous" for our undivided devotion (Exodus 34:14).

Despite our tendency to wander and be unfaithful, the Gospel provides a beautiful resolution. Jesus Christ is the perfectly faithful Bridegroom who laid down His life to wash His Bride, the Church, and make her pure. Even when we are unfaithful, God remains faithful to His promises (2 Timothy 2:13). Through the death and resurrection of Jesus, the penalty for our spiritual unfaithfulness is paid, and we are offered the grace to return to a right relationship with Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and the idols you have chased, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him with your whole heart today?
How can we rely on the power of God?

Relying on the power of God begins with a humble recognition of our own limitations. The Bible teaches that God's strength is most clearly seen when we acknowledge our need for Him. In 2 Corinthians 12:9, the Lord tells the Apostle Paul, "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness." This means that instead of trying to be strong in ourselves, we look to the one who is sovereign and all powerful.

To rely on His power, we must first abide in Jesus Christ. Jesus says in John 15:5, "I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing." We abide in Him through regular prayer and by immersing ourselves in the Word of God, which is living and active (Hebrews 4:12). When we pray, we are not just reciting words, but we are communicating with our Creator and asking the Holy Spirit to guide and empower us.

Furthermore, we rely on God's power by walking in obedience and faith. Ephesians 6:10 encourages us to "be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might." This strength is not something we possess inherently, but it is something we receive as we trust in His promises and put on the spiritual armor He provides. It is important to remember that God is faithful and good, and He empowers His children to face trials and serve others for His glory.

Ultimately, the greatest demonstration of God's power is found in the Gospel. Though we were separated from God by our sin and deserved His just judgment, God showed His love by sending His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died on the cross as a substitute for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). This salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you seek to grow in this reliance, I encourage you to talk to God directly in prayer and find a local church where you can fellowship with other believers. What specific area of your life do you feel you need God's strength in today?
What does it mean that Jesus fulfilled the law, but did not abolish it?

In Matthew 5:17, Jesus says, "Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them." This statement is central to understanding how the Old Testament relates to the life and ministry of Jesus Christ.

To abolish the Law would mean to set it aside as if it were flawed, irrelevant, or wrong. However, Jesus affirms that the Law is the holy and perfect expression of God’s character. Instead of discarding it, He came to "fulfill" it, which means to bring it to its intended goal, to complete its purpose, and to satisfy its every requirement.

Jesus fulfilled the Law in several profound ways. First, He fulfilled its moral requirements through His perfect obedience. While every other human being has failed to keep God's commands (Romans 3:23), Jesus lived a sinless life, perfectly loving God and neighbor (Hebrews 4:15). Second, He fulfilled the ceremonial requirements. The animal sacrifices and temple rituals of the Old Testament were "shadows" pointing toward the substance, which is Christ (Colossians 2:16-17). As the Lamb of God, His one-time sacrifice on the cross satisfied the Law’s demand for justice against sin (Hebrews 10:1-14). Finally, He fulfilled the prophetic requirements, as the entire Old Testament pointed forward to His birth, ministry, death, and resurrection (Luke 24:44).
