This is the heart of the Gospel message. God is holy and His Law is a righteous standard that we, in our sinfulness, have broken. Because God is just, sin must be punished. Yet, because God is merciful and loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved to die. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming His identity as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Because Jesus fulfilled the Law, we are no longer justified by our own ability to keep commands, which is impossible for us. Instead, we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Galatians 2:16). His perfect record of obedience is credited to those who believe in Him. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and the gift of eternal life.

What makes it difficult for you to think about resting in Christ's finished work rather than your own efforts?
What are the Major Prophets and Minor Prophets?

In the Old Testament, the books of the prophets are generally divided into two categories: the Major Prophets and the Minor Prophets. This distinction is based primarily on the length of the books rather than the importance of the prophet or the significance of their message. All of these men were called by God to deliver His word to the people, often calling them to repentance and pointing toward the hope of a coming Savior.

The Major Prophets consist of five books: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel. They are called "major" because their writings are quite long. For example, the book of Isaiah contains sixty-six chapters and provides profound details about the character of God and the future work of the Messiah, as seen in Isaiah 53:5-6. Jeremiah is often referred to as the weeping prophet because of his deep sorrow over the sins of Israel, yet he also spoke of the hope of a New Covenant in Jeremiah 31:31-34. Although Lamentations is a shorter book of poems, it is traditionally grouped with the Major Prophets because it was written by Jeremiah.

The Minor Prophets, also known as "The Twelve," consist of twelve shorter books: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi. In the original Hebrew scriptures, these were often written together on a single scroll. While these books are shorter, their messages are vital. They address themes of social justice, God's sovereignty over the nations, and His steadfast love. Micah 6:8, for instance, provides a concise summary of what God requires of His people: to do justice, to love kindness, and to walk humbly with God.

Ultimately, all the prophets point us toward Jesus Christ. Their writings reveal that God is perfectly holy and just, as described in Isaiah 6:3, and that human sin has caused a deep separation between us and our Creator. Because of our sin, we cannot save ourselves through our own works or moral efforts. However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature and live a perfect life. Jesus died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why believe in Jesus?

Believing in Jesus is the most significant decision a person can make because it addresses our greatest need: reconciliation with God. To understand why we believe in Him, we must look at who He is, what He did, and what His life means for us.

God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just. However, every human being has sinned and fallen short of His perfect standard, which results in spiritual separation from Him (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). Because God is just, He cannot simply ignore sin, yet because He is merciful and loving, He provided a way for us to be forgiven.

Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14). He came to earth through the virgin birth, lived a sinless life, and perfectly obeyed God’s law. On the cross, Jesus took the penalty for our sins upon Himself as a substitute. His sacrificial death is necessary and sufficient to reconcile sinners to God, satisfying God's justice while demonstrating His profound love (Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 3:18). 

The historical foundation for this belief is the resurrection. After His death and burial, Jesus rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming His identity as the Son of God (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4). He is now exalted as Lord over all creation.

We believe in Jesus because He is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6). Salvation is not earned by our own efforts, good works, or religious rituals. Instead, we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). This faith is a gift that allows us to rest in the finished work of Jesus for our forgiveness and eternal life.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is a Bible Baptist Church, and what do Bible Baptists believe?

A Bible Baptist Church is typically an independent congregation that identifies with the historic Baptist tradition, placing a primary emphasis on the Bible as the sole and final authority for all matters of faith and practice. While many of these churches are part of the Bible Baptist Fellowship International, others are entirely unaffiliated, yet they generally share a commitment to what they call the "Fundamentals" of the faith.

The beliefs of Bible Baptists are often summarized through several key distinctives:

**Biblical Authority**
They believe the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God. It is the complete revelation of God's will for humanity and serves as the ultimate guide for how a Christian should live (2 Timothy 3:16-17; 2 Peter 1:20-21).

**The Autonomy of the Local Church**
Each Bible Baptist church is self-governing and self-supporting. While they may associate with other like-minded churches for missions or fellowship, there is no outside hierarchy or denominational body that has authority over the local congregation. They believe Christ is the head of the church (Colossians 1:18; Ephesians 1:22).

**The Priesthood of All Believers**
Every born-again believer has direct access to God through Jesus Christ, our Great High Priest. This means a Christian does not need a human mediator to pray to God or to study and understand the Scriptures (1 Peter 2:9; 1 Timothy 2:5).

**Two Ordinances**
They practice two symbolic acts commanded by Jesus: baptism and the Lord's Supper. Baptism is practiced by immersion for believers only, as an outward sign of an inward change and identification with Christ's death, burial, and resurrection (Matthew 28:19-20; Romans 6:3-5). The Lord's Supper is a memorial meal observed in remembrance of Christ's sacrifice (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

**Individual Soul Liberty**
They believe that every individual has the liberty to choose what they believe about God and is personally responsible to God for those choices. No person should be forced to follow a particular religion by the state or any other person (Romans 14:5; Romans 14:12).

**Saved, Baptized Church Membership**
Membership in a Bible Baptist church is reserved for those who have personally trusted in Jesus Christ for salvation and have subsequently been baptized by immersion as a testimony of their faith (Acts 2:41-42).

**Two Offices**
They recognize two biblical offices within the local church: the pastor (also referred to as an elder or overseer) and the deacon. The pastor provides spiritual leadership and teaching, while deacons serve the physical and practical needs of the congregation (1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9).

**The Core Gospel Message**
At the heart of Bible Baptist teaching is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. They believe that God is perfectly holy and just, yet humanity is separated from Him because of sin (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:1-4). He is now exalted as Lord at the right hand of God.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any human works or religious rituals (Ephesians 2:8-9; Titus 3:5). Those who truly trust in Him are kept by God's power and are secure in their salvation forever (John 10:28-29).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is sexual ethics?

Sexual ethics is the study of how human beings understand and express their sexuality, encompassing the moral principles and values that guide sexual behavior, identity, and relationships. From a Christian perspective, sexual ethics is not merely a collection of prohibitions, but a positive vision rooted in God’s character and His creative design for humanity as revealed in the Bible.

The foundation of biblical sexual ethics begins in the book of Genesis, where God created humanity in His own image as male and female (Genesis 1:27). This suggests that our bodies and our sexuality are inherent to our personhood and are "very good" in God's sight. Scripture teaches that the intended context for the expression of sexual intimacy is the lifelong, exclusive covenant of marriage between one man and one woman (Genesis 2:24; Matthew 19:4–6). Within this boundary, sexual union is viewed as a sacred gift that fosters deep companionship, enables procreation, and provides a physical picture of the spiritual union between Christ and His Church (Ephesians 5:31–32).

Christian sexual ethics also addresses the condition of the human heart. Jesus taught that God’s standard for purity involves not only our physical actions but also our thoughts and desires (Matthew 5:27–28). Believers are encouraged to seek self-control and holiness, recognizing that their bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit and have been bought with the precious price of Christ's sacrifice (1 Corinthians 6:18–20; 1 Thessalonians 4:3–5).

Because of the fall, every person experiences brokenness in their desires and relationships (Romans 3:23). However, the central message of the Christian faith is one of restoration. While sexual sin is a reality that separates us from a holy God, it is not beyond the reach of His grace. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave to defeat death. Through Him, we are offered complete forgiveness and the gift of the Holy Spirit to help us live in a way that honors God’s design (1 Corinthians 6:11; Titus 2:11–14).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. Is there anything specific about this biblical perspective that you find difficult or would like to explore further?
What is spiritual sickness? What does it mean to be spiritually sick?

Spiritual sickness describes a state where a person's soul is out of harmony with God, often characterized by the presence of sin, a hardened heart, or a lack of spiritual vitality. In the most fundamental sense, every human being is born with a spiritual condition that the Bible describes as being dead in trespasses and sins, as noted in Ephesians 2:1. This condition means we are separated from the life of God because of our rebellion against His holy standards.

When Jesus began His ministry, He used the imagery of sickness to describe why He came to earth. In Mark 2:17, He said, "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners." Here, Jesus identifies spiritual sickness with sin. Just as physical sickness weakens the body and can lead to death, spiritual sickness weakens our relationship with God, clouds our judgment, and, if left untreated, leads to eternal separation from Him.

For someone who does not yet know Christ, spiritual sickness manifests as a lack of peace, a sense of purposelessness, or a persistent struggle with guilt and shame. Romans 3:23 reminds us that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." This is the universal "ailment" of humanity. It is a condition of the heart that no amount of human effort, good works, or religious ritual can cure.

For a believer, spiritual sickness can refer to a period of backsliding or spiritual lethargy. This happens when we harbor unconfessed sin or neglect our relationship with the Lord. King David described the physical and emotional weight of spiritual sickness in Psalm 32:3-4, saying, "When I kept silent, my bones wasted away through my groaning all day long. For day and night your hand was heavy on me; my strength was sapped as in the heat of summer." He felt the "sickness" of his soul until he confessed his sin to God.

The only cure for this spiritual condition is found in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Because God is perfectly holy and just, He cannot simply overlook sin, yet because He is merciful and loving, He provided the way for our healing. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of obedience that we could never live. He then took our spiritual sickness and the punishment for our sins upon Himself when He died on the cross. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over sin and death, offering us new and eternal life.

Salvation is a gift secured by Christ's work alone, and it is received by grace through faith, not by our own works. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and a restored relationship with Him. What is currently holding you back from trusting Him with your life?

If you find yourself feeling spiritually weary or distant from God, I encourage you to spend time in prayer and in the reading of His Word. Scripture is the ultimate authority for our faith and a primary means by which God restores our souls. You might start by reading the Gospel of John to see the heart of the Great Physician who loves you and gave Himself for you. Seeking fellowship within a local church can also provide the accountability and encouragement needed for spiritual health. James 5:16 tells us to "confess your sins to each other and pray for each other so that you may be healed," highlighting the importance of the Christian community in our ongoing growth and healing.
What is fellowship with God?

Fellowship with God is the intimate, personal relationship where a believer shares in the life and presence of God Himself. It is far more than just knowing facts about God or following a set of religious rules. Instead, it is a living partnership and a deep communion rooted in the grace of the Gospel. 

In the Bible, the word often translated as fellowship is "koinonia," which implies a close association, a sharing in common, and a mutual participation. The Apostle John explains that this fellowship is shared with the Father and His Son, Jesus Christ, in 1 John 1:3. It is the restoration of the relationship for which humans were originally created, allowing us to walk with God in trust and love.

Because God is perfectly holy, our sin creates a barrier that separates us from Him, as noted in Isaiah 59:2. We cannot achieve fellowship with God through our own good works or efforts. However, God in His mercy reached out to us. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over sin and death, as described in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Through Christ, the way to the Father is opened. When a person is justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, they are reconciled to God. This fellowship is then sustained and deepened by the Holy Spirit, who dwells within every believer, as mentioned in 2 Corinthians 13:14. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and enter into this eternal fellowship.

In our daily lives, fellowship with God involves several key aspects:

1. **Abiding in His Word:** We listen to God as He speaks to us through the Bible, which is the ultimate authority for our faith and practice, as shown in 2 Timothy 3:16-17.
2. **Prayer:** We respond to God by bringing our praises, thanksgivings, and requests to Him, resting in the truth that He hears us because of Jesus, our Great High Priest, in Hebrews 4:14-16.
3. **Walking in the Light:** Fellowship requires a life of progressive sanctification, where we strive to turn from sin and walk in obedience to God's commands, as taught in 1 John 1:6-7.
4. **The Body of Christ:** We experience fellowship with God alongside other believers in the local church, sharing in the Lord’s Supper and encouraging one another in the faith, according to Hebrews 10:24-25.

What has been your experience with seeking to know God personally? If you find yourself feeling distant from Him, remember that He is faithful and just to forgive us and cleanse us when we confess our sins to Him, as promised in 1 John 1:9.
What role does Iran play in the end times?

When searching the Scriptures for the role of modern nations like Iran in the end times, we look to the names used in the ancient world. Historically, the region of Iran is identified in the Bible as Persia and Elam.

The most prominent mention of Persia in a prophetic context is found in the book of Ezekiel. In Ezekiel 38:5, Persia is listed as one of the nations that joins a confederation led by a figure named Gog to move against Israel in the "latter years" (Ezekiel 38:8). This passage describes a large assembly of nations, but it emphasizes that God will intervene to display His greatness and holiness (Ezekiel 38:23).

Additionally, the prophet Jeremiah speaks regarding Elam, which was located in the western part of modern Iran. In Jeremiah 49:34-39, a prophecy describes God's judgment coming upon Elam, yet it concludes with a promise that God will "restore the fortunes of Elam in the latter days" (Jeremiah 49:39).

Because these are considered tertiary matters of eschatology, faithful Christians hold different views on how these prophecies will be fulfilled. Some interpret these passages as literal future military conflicts, while others view them as symbolic of the ongoing spiritual struggle between the kingdom of God and the kingdoms of this world. Regardless of the specific interpretation, the Bible is clear that God remains sovereign over the rise and fall of nations.

The ultimate focus of the "end times" is not the movement of earthly armies, but the glorious return of Jesus Christ. Scripture teaches that Christ will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead and to establish His eternal kingdom (Revelation 19:11-16; Revelation 21:1-4).

In light of these future events, the most important question is our standing before God. Every person has sinned and fallen short of God's holy standard (Romans 3:23). However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything specific about these prophecies or about following Jesus that you find difficult to understand?
What does Jesus mean when He says, “Verily, verily” or “Truly, truly,” in the gospels?

When Jesus begins a statement with the words "Verily, verily" or "Truly, truly," He is using a translation of the Hebrew and Aramaic word "Amen." While it was common in the ancient world for people to say "Amen" at the end of a prayer or a legal statement to confirm what had been said, Jesus did something unique by placing it at the very beginning of His teachings.

By using this phrase, Jesus is asserting His divine authority. He does not need to wait for someone else to speak so that He can agree with them. Instead, He speaks on His own authority as the Son of God. When He says "Truly," He is essentially saying, "What I am about to tell you is the absolute truth, backed by the authority of God Himself."

In the Gospel of John, Jesus frequently repeats the word to say, "Truly, truly." This repetition serves as a solemn "double stamp" of certainty. It highlights that the teaching following the phrase is of the utmost importance and carries eternal weight. For example, in John 3:3, Jesus uses this phrase to introduce the essential truth that a person must be born again to see the kingdom of God. He also uses it in John 6:47 to emphasize that whoever believes in Him has everlasting life.

These statements are not mere suggestions or opinions. They are the declarations of the One who is the Truth (John 14:6). Because Jesus is God in the flesh, His words are perfectly reliable and serve as the foundation for our faith and practice (Matthew 7:24; John 1:1, John 1:14). When He speaks with this level of certainty, He is inviting us to listen with total attention and to build our lives upon His Word.

Jesus often used these "truly, truly" statements to reveal the way to eternal life and the reality of our need for a Savior. Because of our sin, we are separated from a holy God, but Christ came to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross, and rise from the grave to reconcile us to God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes it difficult for you to trust in His words today?
What should we learn from the tribe of Asher?

The tribe of Asher provides us with a profound study of God's abundant provision, the subtle dangers of prosperity, and the beauty of a humble heart that seeks the Savior. As the eighth son of Jacob, Asher's very name means happy or blessed, and his history reflects a life marked by significant material wealth and a unique place in the unfolding story of redemption.

One of the primary lessons we learn from Asher is the reality of God's goodness and provision. When Jacob blessed his sons, he prophesied that Asher’s food would be rich and that he would provide royal delicacies in Genesis 49:20. Later, Moses blessed the tribe by saying they would be favored by their brothers and dip their feet in oil, with strength that would last as long as their days in Deuteronomy 33:24–25. The territory assigned to Asher was among the most fertile in the Promised Land, famous for its olive groves. This reminds us that every good gift comes from the hand of God, and He delights in providing for the needs of His people.

However, the history of Asher also serves as a warning about the spiritual risks of comfort and security. In the book of Judges, we see that the tribe of Asher failed to fully drive out the Canaanites from their allotted land, choosing instead to live among them in Judges 1:31–32. Furthermore, when the judge Deborah called the tribes to battle against the oppressors of Israel, the tribe of Asher remained on the coast, staying by their landings instead of joining the fight in Judges 5:17. Their material abundance may have led to a lack of urgency or a reluctance to risk their comfort for the sake of God's kingdom. We are reminded that earthly blessings should never become a substitute for spiritual zeal or obedience to God's commands.

Despite these periods of complacency, the tribe of Asher also demonstrates the power of humility and repentance. During the reign of King Hezekiah, when he sent a call throughout all Israel to return to Jerusalem for the Passover, many mocked the messengers. However, the Bible records that some men from Asher humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem in 2 Chronicles 30:11. This teaches us that no matter our past failures or the spiritual climate of our surroundings, God always welcomes those who humble themselves and seek His face.

Perhaps the most beautiful lesson from Asher comes in the New Testament through the person of Anna. She was a prophetess from the tribe of Asher who spent her life in the temple, fasting and praying. When the infant Jesus was brought to the temple, she was among the first to recognize Him as the long awaited Redeemer, giving thanks to God and speaking of Him to all who were looking for the redemption of Jerusalem in Luke 2:36–38. Anna’s faithfulness shows us that God preserves a remnant from every tribe and that true "happiness" or "blessedness" is found in recognizing and following Jesus Christ.

The story of the tribe of Asher ultimately points us toward the greatest blessing of all. While Asher enjoyed rich food and olive oil, Jesus Christ is the Bread of Life and the One anointed by the Holy Spirit to bring us eternal life. Because of our sin, we are separated from the holiness of God, but God in His mercy sent His Son to live a perfect life, die an atoning death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not by our own works or heritage. 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. 

As you reflect on the history of this tribe, what do you find most challenging or encouraging about the way they handled God's blessings?
Why does the Bible speak against braided hair?

The mention of braided hair in the Bible is found in two primary passages, 1 Timothy 2:9 and 1 Peter 3:3. To understand why these instructions were given, it is helpful to look at the historical context and the heart of the message regarding modesty and worship.

In the first-century Greco-Roman world, extremely elaborate hairstyles involving intricate braids, gold ornaments, and expensive pearls were often used as a public display of immense wealth, high social status, or even vanity. When the apostles Paul and Peter addressed this, their concern was not with a specific hairstyle itself, but with the heart's attitude and the potential for distraction within the church.

In 1 Timothy 2:9 through 1 Timothy 2:10, Paul writes that women should adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control, not with braided hair and gold or pearls or costly attire, but with what is proper for women who profess godliness, with good works. The concern here was that ostentatious displays of riches could create social divisions or draw attention away from the glory of God during worship.

Similarly, in 1 Peter 3:3 through 1 Peter 3:4, Peter encourages believers by saying, Do not let your adorning be external, the braiding of hair and the putting on of gold jewelry, or the clothing you wear, but let your adorning be the hidden person of the heart with the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which in God's sight is very precious. The biblical principle is that a Christian's true beauty and identity should be rooted in their character and their relationship with God, rather than in outward fashion or status symbols.

These passages remind us that while we live in a world that often prizes physical appearance and material success, God looks at the heart as seen in 1 Samuel 16:7. The goal for every believer is to live in a way that points others toward the grace of God rather than toward ourselves.

Ultimately, our greatest "adornment" is not anything we can wear, but the righteousness of Jesus Christ. We are all prone to seeking value in worldly things, but the Bible teaches that we are separated from God by our sin and cannot save ourselves through our own efforts or appearance. God, in His holiness and love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice, He defeated sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is your understanding of how God views our inner life compared to our outward appearance?
What is the difference between a covenant and a contract?

While the terms are often used interchangeably in modern conversation, there are profound differences between a contract and a biblical covenant. Understanding these differences helps us see the depth of God’s love and the security we have in Him.

A contract is generally a legal agreement between two or more parties to exchange goods or services. It is often based on a "this for that" arrangement (quid pro quo). If one person fails to meet their obligations, the contract can be terminated or penalized. Contracts are typically built on mutual suspicion and protected by law to ensure both parties get what they are owed.

In contrast, a covenant is a solemn, binding commitment that establishes a deep personal relationship. While a contract is about an exchange of property or services, a covenant is about the giving of persons. It is a bond of loyalty and love that is intended to be permanent. In a covenant, the focus is not on "what can I get?" but rather "how can I serve and remain faithful?"

Marriage is a primary example of a human covenant (Malachi 2:14), where two people commit to one another regardless of changing circumstances. However, the greatest example is the way God relates to His people. Throughout the Bible, God initiates covenants to show His faithfulness, such as His promise to Abraham (Genesis 15:1-21) and the New Covenant promised to His people (Jeremiah 31:31-34).

The beauty of God’s covenant with us is that it does not depend on our ability to be perfect. Because God is perfectly holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), we could never satisfy the requirements of a legal contract with Him. We deserve the penalty for our sin, which is death (Romans 6:23).

This is why the Gospel is such good news. God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to fulfill the requirements of the covenant on our behalf. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is not a contract we earn through works, but a gift of grace we receive through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). Through Jesus, we enter into a new covenant where God promises to be our God and remember our sins no more (Hebrews 8:10-12).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?
If Jesus was God, why did He say "No one is good but God alone"?

This statement, found in Mark 10:18 and Luke 18:19, is a profound moment where Jesus uses a question to reveal the condition of a man's heart. When the rich young ruler addressed Jesus as "Good Teacher," he was likely using "good" as a polite title for a human rabbi. Jesus responded by asking, "Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone" (Mark 10:18).

Jesus was not denying His own deity or His own sinlessness. Instead, He was challenging the man's superficial understanding of goodness and his incomplete understanding of who Jesus really was. By pointing out that absolute goodness belongs to God alone, Jesus was forcing the man to face a logical conclusion: if he truly believed Jesus was "good" in the ultimate sense, he must also believe that Jesus is God.

This interaction also served to highlight the man's own lack of goodness. The young man believed he had kept all the commandments from his youth, as recorded in Mark 10:20. By establishing God as the only standard of perfect goodness, Jesus was preparing to show the man that he fell short of that standard because he loved his wealth more than God. 

Throughout the Gospels, Jesus made many claims to be one with God. For example, He stated, "I and the Father are one" (John 10:30) and "Truly, truly, I say to you, before Abraham was, I am" (John 8:58). His question to the young ruler was a pedagogical tool, intended to move the man from seeing Jesus as merely a moral teacher to seeing Him as the holy Lord who has the authority to demand everything.

This truth is central to the message of the Bible. God is perfectly holy and good, while all human beings have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His just judgment. However, because God is also merciful and loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfectly good and sinless life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Jesus Christ alone, not by our own works or perceived goodness (Ephesians 2:8-9).

I invite you to consider the goodness of God and your own need for a Savior. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

What is your current understanding of what it means to be "good" in God's eyes?
What does it mean that God will reveal the hidden things (Daniel 2:22 and 28)?

In the book of Daniel, the truth that God reveals "hidden things" points to His absolute omniscience and His sovereign control over human history. When Daniel speaks these words, he is responding to a crisis where King Nebuchadnezzar demanded to know both the content and the meaning of a dream he had forgotten. Daniel 2:22 declares that God "reveals deep and hidden things; he knows what is in the darkness, and the light dwells with him." This teaches us that while certain truths are obscured from human sight, nothing is secret to God because He is the source of all light and truth (Job 12:22; Psalm 139:11–12).

In the specific context of Daniel 2:28, Daniel explains that "there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries." Here, the "hidden things" refer to God’s providential plan for the rise and fall of earthly kingdoms. By revealing the dream to Daniel, God demonstrated that He alone holds the keys to the future and that no human wisdom or occult power can compare to His divine revelation (Isaiah 46:9–10).

On a deeper level, the Bible uses the concept of "mysteries" or "hidden things" to describe God’s plan of redemption. While the Old Testament hinted at how God would save the world, the full reality remained partially hidden until the coming of Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul explains that the Gospel is the revelation of a mystery that was kept secret for long ages but has now been disclosed through the prophetic writings (Romans 16:25–26). This mystery is that through Christ, people from every nation can be reconciled to God and receive the riches of His glory (Colossians 1:26–27; Ephesians 3:4–6).

Because God reveals what is hidden, we must also recognize that He sees the secrets of our own hearts. Scripture warns that God will one day judge the secrets of men through Christ Jesus (Romans 2:16). While this is a sobering reality because of our sin, it is also the foundation of the Gospel’s hope. God, knowing every hidden fault we possess, still chose to send His Son to die in our place. Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all.

God does not wish for His grace to remain a mystery to you. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is your current understanding of how a person can be right with God?
What items should be included in a church constitution?

A church constitution serves as a vital tool to ensure that a local body of believers functions in a way that honors God and maintains peace, order, and doctrinal purity. Scripture reminds us that all things should be done decently and in order in 1 Corinthians 14:40. While the specific needs of a congregation may vary, several essential elements are typically included to provide a clear framework for ministry and fellowship.

**Name and Purpose**
The constitution should begin by stating the legal and ecclesiastical name of the church and its primary reason for existence. This purpose is ultimately to glorify God through worship, the edification of believers, and the fulfillment of the Great Commission as seen in Matthew 28:18-20.

**Statement of Faith**
This is the doctrinal anchor of the church. It outlines the core beliefs that all members agree to uphold. It should clearly define the church's stance on the Trinity, the authority and inerrancy of Scripture, the deity and humanity of Jesus Christ, and the nature of salvation.

At the very heart of this statement is the Gospel. We believe that God is holy and supreme, yet humanity is separated from Him by sin. In His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who was born of a virgin, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any human works as stated in Ephesians 2:8-9 and Titus 3:5.

If you have not yet placed your hope in this Good News, I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life.

**Church Covenant**
A covenant is a solemn promise made between the members to live in a way that reflects Christ. It often includes commitments to regular fellowship, mutual encouragement, financial support of the ministry, and pursuit of holiness in daily life. This reflects the biblical call to love one another as Christ has loved us in John 13:34-35.

**Membership**
This section defines who may become a member, the process for joining (usually involving a profession of faith and believer's baptism), and how membership may be terminated. It emphasizes that while the universal church includes all believers, the local church is a specific, committed expression of the body of Christ according to Romans 12:4-5.

**Leadership and Officers**
The constitution should outline the biblical roles and qualifications for church leadership. This typically includes Elders (or Pastors) who provide spiritual oversight and teaching, and Deacons who serve the physical and practical needs of the body. The qualifications for these offices are found in 1 Timothy 3:1-13 and Titus 1:5-9.

**Ordinances**
It is important to define how the church practices the ordinances of Baptism and the Lord's Supper. Baptism by immersion is an outward sign of an inward grace, identifying the believer with Christ's death and resurrection as shown in Romans 6:3-4. The Lord's Supper is a regular remembrance of Christ's sacrifice and a means of spiritual nourishment for the gathered church in 1 Corinthians 11:23-26.

**Church Discipline**
Following the instructions of Jesus in Matthew 18:15-20, a constitution should include a process for addressing unrepentant sin among members. The goal of this process is always the restoration of the wandering believer, the purity of the church, and the protection of the name of Christ.

**Meetings and Governance**
This section describes how the church conducts business, including the frequency of congregational meetings, the requirements for a quorum, and the process for voting on major decisions such as calling a pastor or approving a budget. This ensures transparency and accountability between the leadership and the congregation.

**Amendments and Dissolution**
Finally, a constitution should include procedures for making future changes to the document and instructions for how the church's assets would be distributed if the congregation were to ever dissolve.

In drafting these documents, it is helpful to remember that they are meant to support the organic life of the church, not replace the leading of the Holy Spirit. If you are in the process of organizing a church, I encourage you to seek the wisdom of seasoned pastors or a biblical counselor to ensure your documents reflect your specific convictions and provide a healthy environment for spiritual growth. For further guidance, you may find it helpful to look at the historical 1689 London Baptist Confession of Faith or similar confessions that have served the church well for centuries.
What is topical preaching? Should a pastor preach topically?

Topical preaching is a method of teaching where the sermon is organized around a specific subject or theme rather than following a single passage of Scripture verse by verse. In a topical sermon, the pastor identifies a theme, such as "prayer," "marriage," or "the holiness of God," and then brings together various portions of the Bible to explain what the whole of Scripture teaches on that particular matter.

Whether a pastor should preach topically is a subject where faithful Christians often exercise liberty and wisdom. There are distinct benefits to this approach, as it allows a pastor to address specific needs within the congregation or to provide a systematic overview of a complex doctrine. For example, a topical series on the Trinity or the nature of the church can help believers ground their faith in essential truths. The Bible itself contains examples of topical instruction, such as when Jesus explained to His disciples all the things concerning Himself in the Law and the Prophets in Luke 24:27.

However, many pastors and theologians recommend that the primary "diet" of a church should be expository preaching. Expository preaching involves moving through a book of the Bible systematically, allowing the structure and intent of the biblical author to set the agenda. This protects the pastor from only preaching on his favorite subjects and ensures the congregation hears the "whole counsel of God" as mentioned in Acts 20:27.

There are a few cautions to consider regarding topical preaching. First, there is a risk of taking verses out of their original context to support a preconceived idea. Second, if a pastor only preaches topically, the congregation may not learn how to study the Bible for themselves in its literary and historical flow. Scripture is the inspired and inerrant Word of God, and it is profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16-17. Therefore, any preaching, whether topical or expository, must remain submitted to the actual meaning of the text.

The ultimate goal of all preaching is to herald the truth of God and point listeners to the person and work of Jesus Christ. Paul exhorted Timothy to "preach the word" in 2 Timothy 4:2, which means the message must always be grounded in the authority of the Bible. 

At the heart of the biblical message is the Gospel, the good news that although we have all sinned and fallen short of God’s holiness, God has provided a way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord, and salvation is found in Him alone through grace, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and eternal life.

If you are looking for a church or evaluating a preaching style, it is helpful to ask if the sermons consistently lead you to a deeper love for God and a clearer understanding of His Word. Does the preaching regularly center on the cross and the resurrection? You might consider asking your pastor how he selects his sermon topics to better understand his heart for the spiritual growth of the congregation.
What are tribulation saints?

The term tribulation saints generally refers to those who come to faith in Jesus Christ during a period of unprecedented global distress known as the Great Tribulation. While Christians hold different views on the specific timing and sequence of end-times events, this group is most clearly described in the book of Revelation.

In Revelation 7:9-14, the apostle John sees a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, tribe, people, and language, standing before the throne of God. When asked who they are, an elder explains, "These are the ones coming out of the great tribulation. They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb" (Revelation 7:14).

These believers are characterized by several things:

First, they are saved by the same Gospel that saves all people. Their robes are made white not by their own works or endurance, but by the blood of Jesus Christ, which cleanses from all sin (1 John 1:7). They acknowledge that salvation belongs to God and to the Lamb (Revelation 7:10).

Second, they often face intense persecution and martyrdom for their witness. Scripture indicates that during this time, many will be put to death because of their testimony and because they refuse to worship the beast or receive its mark (Revelation 13:7; Revelation 20:4). Their faithfulness even unto death demonstrates the power of the Holy Spirit sustaining them in the midst of trial.

Third, they are a testament to God's mercy. Even during a season of divine judgment upon a rebellious world, God continues to draw people to Himself. The "eternal gospel" is still proclaimed to those who dwell on the earth (Revelation 14:6), showing that God is patient and desires that all should come to repentance (2 Peter 3:9).

Among believers, there is some disagreement regarding whether these saints are part of the Church or a separate group of believers who find Christ after the Church has been caught up to be with Him. Those who hold to a pre-tribulation view of the rapture see them as a distinct group of converts, while those who hold to other views see them as the faithful Church remaining on earth to endure the final trials. Regardless of these interpretive differences, we can agree that God is sovereign over history and faithful to those who call upon His name.

The reality of these future events reminds us that while God is a God of love and mercy, He is also holy and will eventually judge all sin. The good news is that Jesus Christ took the judgment we deserve upon Himself on the cross. He died for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death. Anyone who turns from their sin and trusts in Him alone receives forgiveness and the gift of eternal life.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to be your Savior and Lord. What thoughts or questions do you have about trusting Him today?
Who are the Black Hebrews / Black Israelites?

The Black Hebrew Israelites are a diverse group of people, primarily African Americans, who believe they are the physical and spiritual descendants of the ancient biblical Israelites. While the movement is not a single, unified denomination, its various branches share the core conviction that the true identity of the biblical Hebrews was lost through the transatlantic slave trade and that they are now reclaiming that heritage.

The movement began in the late 19th century in the United States through leaders like Frank Cherry and William Crowdy, who claimed to have received visions revealing that African Americans were the true House of Israel. Many in the movement point to Deuteronomy 28, specifically the curses listed for disobedience, as evidence. They argue that the historical experiences of slavery and systemic oppression mirror the biblical descriptions of the curses placed upon the Israelites. Deuteronomy 28:68 is often cited as a key text in this interpretation.

It is important to recognize that the movement exists on a wide spectrum. Some groups are relatively quiet and focus on observing Old Testament laws, such as the Sabbath and dietary restrictions, while also believing in Jesus (often referred to by the Hebrew name Yeshua). Other groups are more radical or "camp" based, sometimes expressing exclusionary or confrontational views toward those they do not consider part of the chosen lineage.

From a biblical perspective, while we recognize the deep historical pain and the search for identity that often leads individuals to this movement, the New Testament provides clear guidance on the nature of God's people. The Apostle Paul addressed the tendency to rely on physical lineage for standing before God. In Romans 2:28-29, the Bible says that a person is not a Jew who is one only outwardly, nor is circumcision merely outward and physical. No, a person is a Jew who is one inwardly; and circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code.

Furthermore, the Gospel clarifies that our primary identity and our salvation are found in Jesus Christ rather than in our ethnicity or genealogy. Galatians 3:28-29 states, "There is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus. If you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham’s seed, and heirs according to the promise."

The core of the Christian faith is built upon these truths: God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own or by our physical ancestry (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in Him as your Savior and Lord today?
What does the Bible say about church-hopping?

The Bible does not use the modern phrase "church-hopping," but it provides extensive guidance on the nature of the local church and the believer's responsibility to a specific community of faith. While there are valid reasons to leave a congregation, such as a move to a new city or a church's departure from biblical truth, a pattern of frequent moving from one church to another can hinder the spiritual growth God intends for His children.

Scripture describes the church as the body of Christ, where every member is vital and interconnected. In 1 Corinthians 12:12-27, the Apostle Paul explains that just as a physical body has many parts that rely on one another, the church is composed of individuals who need each other to function properly. When a person "hops" from church to church without committing, it is like a hand trying to function without being attached to an arm. This prevents the individual from receiving the support they need and prevents the congregation from benefiting from the unique gifts God has given that individual (1 Peter 4:10).

Furthermore, the Bible calls believers to live under the care and oversight of spiritual leaders. Hebrews 13:17 instructs believers to "Obey your leaders and submit to them, for they are keeping watch over your souls, as those who will have to give an account." This relationship of accountability and pastoral care requires a steady, committed presence. It is difficult for elders to shepherd a soul they do not know, and it is difficult for a believer to be truly known and cared for if they are always moving.

The local church is also the primary context where we fulfill the many "one another" commands of Scripture, such as "love one another" (John 13:34), "bear one another's burdens" (Galatians 6:2), and "confess your sins to one another" (James 5:16). These commands require deep, honest, and sometimes difficult relationships that can only be forged over time through consistent fellowship. Hebrews 10:24-25 explicitly warns against the habit of neglecting to meet together, encouraging believers instead to stir one another up to love and good works.

Sometimes, the desire to move between churches stems from a consumer mindset, where we look for what a church can do for us rather than how we can serve God and others. However, our ultimate satisfaction and identity are not found in the perfect church program or worship style, but in Jesus Christ Himself. 

The Gospel tells us that though we were separated from God by our sin, God in His holiness and love sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or church attendance. 

If you find yourself searching for a home or feeling restless, I encourage you to look to the one who offers true rest. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Once you have found rest in Him, He will lead you to a biblical community where you can grow, serve, and be known. Are there specific concerns or experiences that have made it difficult for you to find a long term church home?
Dance in worship - what does the Bible say?

Throughout the Bible, dance is often portrayed as a natural and joyful response to the goodness and deliverance of God. It is seen as a way to engage the whole person, body and soul, in the act of praise.

In the Old Testament, we see several prominent examples of this. After the Lord miraculously led the Israelites through the Red Sea, Miriam the prophetess took a tambourine and led the women in dancing as they sang of God's victory in Exodus 15:20-21. Later, King David famously danced before the Lord with all his might when the Ark of the Covenant was brought into Jerusalem, demonstrating a heart completely abandoned to God's glory rather than his own reputation in 2 Samuel 6:14-16.

The Book of Psalms specifically encourages the use of dance in worship. Psalm 149:3 says, "Let them praise his name with dancing, making melody to him with tambourine and lyre." Similarly, Psalm 150:4 calls the faithful to "praise him with tambourine and dance." These passages suggest that physical movement can be a legitimate and God-honoring way to express spiritual exultation. There is also a recognition that there is a proper time for every emotion, including "a time to mourn, and a time to dance" in Ecclesiastes 3:4.

While the New Testament does not provide specific instructions for dance within the formal gatherings of the early church, it emphasizes that our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit and should be used to glorify God in 1 Corinthians 6:19-20. The primary concern for corporate worship in the New Testament is that all things be done decently and in order for the edification of the believers in 1 Corinthians 14:40.

When considering dance today, the focus should remain on the posture of the heart. Worship is not a performance to be seen by others, but an offering to God. Because God looks at the heart rather than outward appearances (1 Samuel 16:7), any expression of worship should be motivated by a genuine love for Him and a desire to celebrate His work.

Ultimately, our greatest reason for rejoicing is the Gospel: the reality that God is holy, but we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory. In His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What are your thoughts on how physical expression helps you connect with God in worship?
What is secular humanism?

Secular humanism is a worldview that centers on human reason, ethics, and social justice while specifically rejecting religious dogma, supernaturalism, and the existence of God. It suggests that human beings are capable of being ethical and moral without religion or a divine Creator. In this framework, the physical universe is all that exists, and science and rational inquiry are the primary tools for understanding reality and solving human problems.

From a biblical perspective, secular humanism represents a shift in focus from the Creator to the creature. It rests on several key pillars that contrast with Christian faith. First, it prioritizes human autonomy, believing that individuals have the right and responsibility to give meaning and shape to their own lives. This differs from the biblical teaching that we are created by God for His glory and that our true purpose is found in Him (1 Corinthians 10:31; Colossians 1:16).

Second, secular humanism relies on naturalism, the belief that there is no spiritual realm. However, Scripture teaches that God is spirit and that He is the sustainer of all things (John 4:24; Hebrews 1:3). Third, secular humanism views morality as a human construct evolved for social cooperation. The Bible teaches that morality is grounded in the unchanging, holy character of God, and that His law is written on the human heart (Romans 2:15).

While secular humanism often emphasizes many good things, such as kindness, compassion, and the pursuit of knowledge, it lacks a foundation for why these things are objectively "good" or why human beings have inherent dignity. The Bible explains that every person has immense value because we are made in the image of God (Genesis 1:27).

The central challenge of secular humanism is its reliance on human effort to improve the world and the self. While humans can achieve much through science and reason, these tools cannot solve the core problem of the human condition, which is sin. God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), yet every person has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from God, and no amount of human reasoning or social progress can bridge that gap.

Because of His great love, God provided the only solution through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is not something we earn through our own goodness or intellect; it is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Ultimately, while secular humanism looks to humanity for answers, the Gospel invites us to look to the One who created humanity. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does the Bible predict the destruction of Damascus?

Yes, the Bible contains specific prophecies regarding the destruction of Damascus, which is one of the oldest continually inhabited cities in the world. These prophecies are primarily found in the books of the prophets Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Amos.

In the book of Isaiah, the prophet delivers a heavy burden against the city, stating, "Behold, Damascus will cease to be a city and will become a heap of ruins" (Isaiah 17:1). This prophecy describes a time of great distress where the fortified cities of Israel (Ephraim) and the sovereignty of Damascus would both fade (Isaiah 17:3). Similarly, the prophet Jeremiah spoke of Damascus losing its strength and being seized by panic and anguish (Jeremiah 49:23-27). The prophet Amos also recorded God’s judgment against Damascus because of its cruelty toward the people of Gilead, promising that God would "send a fire into the house of Hazael" and "break the gate-bar of Damascus" (Amos 1:3-5).

Historically, many of these predictions saw a significant fulfillment in 732 B.C. when the Assyrian King Tiglath-Pileser III conquered Damascus, killed its king, and deported its inhabitants. However, because Damascus exists today, many Bible students and scholars hold different views on whether these prophecies have been completely fulfilled. Some believe the historical destruction by the Assyrians satisfied the prophecy, while others look to the language of Isaiah 17:1 as pointing to a final, total destruction that is yet to occur in the future. As with many matters of eschatology (the study of end times), faithful Christians may differ on the exact timing and nature of these events.

These prophecies serve as a sobering reminder of the holiness of God and His sovereign authority over the nations. They demonstrate that God is just and will eventually address all sin and rebellion. While the judgment of cities and nations is a heavy reality in Scripture, it points us toward our own need for a Savior. Just as God is just to punish sin, He is also incredibly merciful to those who seek Him.

We are taught in Scripture that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23), and the consequence of that sin is separation from Him. Yet, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over death, He offers forgiveness and eternal life to everyone who turns to Him. Salvation is not something we can earn through our own good works, but it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. Is there anything currently standing in your way of placing your trust in Him?
What does it mean to watch and pray?

To watch and pray is a call to spiritual stay-at-all-times alertness combined with a deep dependence on God. This instruction was most famously given by Jesus to His disciples in the Garden of Gethsemane when He told them, "Watch and pray that you may not enter into temptation. The spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak" (Matthew 26:41).

To "watch" means to be spiritually vigilant and alert. Just as a sentry stays awake to guard a city from an approaching enemy, a believer is called to be aware of the reality of spiritual warfare and the subtle ways temptation can enter the heart. It involves recognizing our own tendencies toward sin and being mindful of the world's influences. Scripture encourages this posture of readiness in many places, such as when the Apostle Peter writes, "Be sober-minded; be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour" (1 Peter 5:8). Watching is not about living in fear, but about living with a clear-eyed understanding of the spiritual significance of our choices and the promise of Christ's return (Mark 13:33-37).

To "pray" is the necessary companion to watching. While watching identifies the danger or the need, prayer is the means by which we lay hold of God's strength to meet it. Jesus noted that while our spirits may desire to follow Him, our human nature (the flesh) is fragile and prone to failure. We cannot remain faithful through sheer willpower or intellect. Prayer is an admission of our weakness and an expression of trust in God's grace. It is the way we "draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need" (Hebrews 4:16). Paul also links these two actions when he tells the church to "continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving" (Colossians 4:2).

Ultimately, the call to watch and pray reminds us that we are entirely dependent on the work of Jesus Christ. In the garden, the disciples failed to watch and pray, falling asleep in the hour of Jesus' greatest distress. Their failure highlights our own inability to save ourselves or even to remain perfectly faithful on our own. Jesus, however, watched and prayed perfectly. He remained vigilant and submitted to the Father's will, even unto death on a cross.

Because of His perfect life, His substitutionary death for our sins, and His bodily resurrection, we are offered forgiveness and new life. He took the judgment we deserved so that we could be reconciled to a holy God by grace through faith. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is one area of your life right now where you feel your "flesh is weak" and you need to ask God for His strength?
How is eternity in Hell a fair punishment for sin?

This is one of the most difficult and weightiest questions in the Christian faith, as it touches on our understanding of God’s character, the gravity of sin, and the nature of justice. To understand how the Bible presents eternal punishment as fair, we must look at who God is and what sin actually represents.

First, the fairness of a punishment is often measured by the dignity of the person against whom the offense is committed. In a human context, an insult directed at a peer is treated differently than an assault on a high official. Because God is an infinitely holy, eternal, and sovereign Creator, a rebellion against Him carries an infinite weight. When we sin, we are not just breaking a rule; we are rejecting the very Source of life, goodness, and holiness (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). Since the offense is against an infinite Being, the consequences have an eternal dimension.

Second, God’s justice is inseparable from His holiness. Because God is perfectly just, He cannot simply overlook sin or pretend it did not happen. To do so would be to deny His own righteous nature (Psalm 89:14; Romans 3:25-26). Hell is the place where God’s perfect justice is satisfied for those who have chosen to remain in rebellion against Him.

Third, many perspectives within Christian thought suggest that Hell is also the ultimate expression of God’s respect for human will. If a person spends their entire life saying "No" to God and desiring to live independently of Him, God eventually honors that choice. As the Scripture indicates, those who reject the light choose to remain in darkness (John 3:19). In this sense, Hell is the tragic result of a person receiving exactly what they sought, which is an existence entirely separated from the presence and grace of God.

However, the most important part of this discussion is that God does not desire for anyone to experience this judgment. He is patient and full of mercy, wanting all to come to repentance (2 Peter 3:9). Because He is both perfectly just and perfectly loving, He provided the solution Himself. 

Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of obedience that we could not live. He then died on the cross, taking upon Himself the full weight of the punishment that our sins deserved. Through His death and His bodily resurrection on the third day, He satisfied the demands of God’s justice and defeated the power of sin and death (Romans 5:8; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This means that salvation is a free gift offered to everyone, not based on our own works or being "good enough," but based entirely on what Christ has done for us.

The reality of Hell shows us just how serious sin is, but the reality of the Cross shows us just how much God loves us. He has made a way for you to be reconciled to Him and to enjoy eternal life in His presence. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life (John 14:6; Acts 17:30; Romans 10:9).

What are your thoughts on how God balances His perfect justice with His great love for us?
How is Satan god of this world (2 Corinthians 4:4)?

In 2 Corinthians 4:4, the Apostle Paul refers to Satan as "the god of this world" not because Satan possesses ultimate sovereignty or creative power, but because he exercises a temporary, pervasive influence over the current fallen age. To understand this title, it is helpful to distinguish between God’s absolute ownership of the universe and the temporary dominion exercised by the enemy within the realm of human rebellion.

The Bible affirms that "The earth is the Lord’s and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell therein" (Psalm 24:1). However, since the fall of man, humanity has largely chosen to follow the patterns of sin rather than the commands of God. In this sense, Satan is the "ruler of this world" (John 12:31; John 14:30) because the world system, including its philosophies and values that exclude God, aligns with his rebellious nature. He operates within the "sons of disobedience" (Ephesians 2:2) and holds sway over those who remain in spiritual darkness (1 John 5:19).

His primary work as the god of this world is spiritual blindness. Paul explains that he "has blinded the minds of the unbelievers, to keep them from seeing the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God" (2 Corinthians 4:4). He uses deception, pride, and the distractions of this life to prevent people from recognizing their need for a Savior. While his power is real, it is also limited and ultimately defeated. He can only act within the boundaries God permits (Job 1:12), and his reign is coming to an end because of the work of Jesus Christ.

This reality highlights the great need for the Gospel. Every person is born into this world system, separated from a holy God by sin and blinded by the enemy's influence. But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. By His bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus proved His victory over sin, death, and the devil. Salvation is a work of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

When a person trusts in Christ, God "has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son" (Colossians 1:13). We are no longer subjects of the "god of this world" but are children of the Most High God, living under the authority of the King of kings.

What holds you back today from trusting in the victory of Jesus Christ?
Is faith intellectual assent?

In the Christian tradition, faith includes intellectual assent, but it is much more than that. While having faith requires you to understand and agree with certain truths, biblical faith involves a deep, personal trust and reliance on God.

Theologians often describe saving faith as having three essential parts. First is "notitia," which is the knowledge of the facts of the Gospel. Second is "assensus," which is the intellectual agreement that these facts are true. Third, and most importantly, is "fiducia," which is a personal trust and resting in Christ alone for salvation. 

The Bible teaches that intellectual agreement alone is insufficient for salvation. In James 2:19, the author notes that even the demons believe that God is one, and they shudder, yet they do not have saving faith because they do not love or trust Him. True faith involves the heart and the will, leading a person to commit their life to Jesus as Lord. It is the difference between believing that a boat can float and actually stepping into the boat to cross a river.

This faith is necessary because of our standing before God. Scripture reveals that God is holy and righteous (Isaiah 6:3), while every human being has sinned and is separated from Him (Romans 3:23). We cannot bridge this gap through our own efforts or good works. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die on the cross as a sacrifice for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, demonstrating His power over death and His identity as the Son of God (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He now reigns at the right hand of the Father as Lord over all.

Salvation is a gift provided by God’s grace and is received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). This faith is a confident assurance in things we hope for and a conviction regarding things we cannot see (Hebrews 11:1). It is the means by which we are justified, or declared righteous, before God (Romans 5:1). When someone has saving faith, they do not just agree that Jesus died for sinners in a general sense; they trust that He died for them specifically and acknowledge Him as their King (Romans 10:9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, as your only hope for forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the biggest challenge for you in moving from intellectual agreement to personal trust in Him?
What does it mean to be saved by grace?

To be saved by grace means receiving a gift from God that we could never earn, deserve, or achieve through our own efforts. It is the core of the Christian message and the only way a person can be reconciled to a holy God.

The Bible teaches that every person has sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly just and holy, our sin creates a separation that we cannot bridge on our own. No amount of good works, religious rituals, or moral living can compensate for our rebellion against Him. The "wages" or natural result of our sin is spiritual death (Romans 6:23).

Grace is often defined as "unmerited favor." It is God's kindness toward those who deserve His judgment. This grace was expressed most perfectly through Jesus Christ. God the Father sent His Son, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we failed to live and to die the death we deserved. On the cross, Jesus took the punishment for our sins upon Himself as a substitute. Three days later, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift that is received through faith alone, not by works. As Ephesians 2:8-9 states, "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast." This means that God does the work of saving us, and we simply trust in what He has done through Christ. Even the faith to believe is a gift from Him.

Because salvation is by grace, it is secure. It does not depend on our ability to keep ourselves saved through our own strength, but on God's faithfulness to keep those who belong to Him (Philippians 1:6; John 10:28-29). This truth brings great peace and humility, as it removes the burden of trying to "earn" God's love and instead invites us to rest in the love He has already proven through the cross.

God’s holiness is satisfied, His justice is met, and His love is displayed all at once through this act of grace. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the kalam cosmological argument for the existence of God?

The Kalam cosmological argument is a logical way to think about how the existence of the universe points back to a Creator. It is a philosophical argument that has been used for centuries by both Christian and other thinkers to show that it is reasonable to believe in God. The argument follows a simple three step structure:

1. Whatever begins to exist has a cause.
2. The universe began to exist.
3. Therefore, the universe has a cause.

The first step, or premise, is based on the common sense observation that things do not just pop into existence out of nothing without a reason. This aligns with the biblical truth that God is the source of all created things, as noted in Hebrews 3:4, which says that every house is built by someone, but the builder of all things is God.

The second step suggests that the universe had a definitive starting point. Philosophically, many argue that it is impossible to have an infinite number of past events because we would never be able to reach the present moment. Scientifically, many modern discoveries about the expansion of the universe and the laws of heat and energy also point toward a beginning. This supports the very first sentence of the Bible in Genesis 1:1, which states, "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth."

The conclusion of the argument is that the universe must have a cause. If the universe consists of all space, time, and matter, then the cause of the universe must be something that exists outside of space, time, and matter. This cause must be unimaginably powerful to bring everything into being and personal enough to choose to create. In the Christian faith, we recognize this cause as the God described in Colossians 1:16-17, where it is written that all things were created through Him and for Him, and He is before all things.

While this argument helps us understand that a Creator exists, the Bible goes further by revealing who this Creator is. God is holy and supreme, yet He is also deeply loving and merciful. Although our sin has separated us from our holy Creator, God made a way for us to be reconciled to Him. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life. Jesus died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death, as we see in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

This salvation is a gift from God, and it is received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, not by our own good works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What thoughts do you have about the universe having a beginning?
Why is the virgin birth so important?
