The virgin birth is a foundational truth of the Christian faith because it reveals how Jesus Christ is both fully God and fully man. This doctrine is not merely a biological miracle, it is the essential means by which God entered into His own creation to rescue humanity.

First, the virgin birth fulfills specific biblical prophecy, demonstrating God's faithfulness to His Word. Centuries before Jesus was born, the prophet Isaiah declared that the Lord Himself would give a sign: "Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign. Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel" (Isaiah 7:14). This was confirmed in the New Testament when the angel told Joseph that the child conceived in Mary was from the Holy Spirit, fulfilling what the Lord had spoken through the prophet (Matthew 1:22-23).

Second, the virgin birth is necessary for the "hypostatic union," which is the union of Christ's divine and human natures in one person. Because He was conceived by the Holy Spirit, He is truly God (Luke 1:35). Because He was born of a woman, He is truly man (Galatians 4:4). To be our mediator, Jesus had to be human so He could represent us and die in our place, but He also had to be God so that His sacrifice would have infinite value and power to overcome death.

Third, this miraculous birth allowed Jesus to be born without the inheritance of sin. Scripture teaches that through the first man, Adam, sin entered the world and passed to all of humanity (Romans 5:12). By not having a human father, Jesus was not born under the federal headship of Adam and did not inherit a fallen, sinful nature. This made Him the "Second Adam," a perfect and sinless High Priest who could offer Himself as an unblemished sacrifice for our sins (Hebrews 4:15; 1 Peter 1:18-19).

The importance of the virgin birth ultimately points us to the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all rebelled against Him through sin, deserving His righteous judgment. Because of His great love, God the Son took on human flesh, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can I overcome disappointment with life?

It is deeply painful when life does not turn out the way you hoped or expected. Disappointment can feel like a heavy weight, leaving you weary and questioning the purpose of your struggles. Please know that your feelings are seen by God, who is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit (Psalm 34:18).

Overcoming disappointment begins by bringing your honest heart to God. The Bible is filled with people who expressed their sorrow and confusion to Him. In the midst of your pain, you can find comfort in knowing that God is sovereign, good, and faithful, even when circumstances seem to suggest otherwise. He promises that for those who love Him, He works all things together for good (Romans 8:28). This does not mean that everything that happens is good in itself, but that God is capable of redeeming even our deepest disappointments for His glory and our ultimate sanctification.

True and lasting hope is found not in our circumstances changing, but in the person and work of Jesus Christ. We live in a world broken by sin, which is why we experience loss, frustration, and unmet desires. God is perfectly holy, and our sin has separated us from Him. Yet, because of His immense mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day. He has defeated sin and death, and He is now the exalted Lord who offers us a hope that can never be taken away.

When we place our identity and happiness in worldly outcomes, disappointment is inevitable. However, when we anchor our souls in Christ, we receive a peace that surpasses understanding (Philippians 4:7). This perspective allows us to see our current trials as light and momentary compared to the eternal weight of glory that is being prepared for us (2 Corinthians 4:17).

I invite you to turn to God now. You can repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and a new life that is defined by His grace rather than your disappointments.

As you walk through this season, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the Word of God. The Psalms are a wonderful place to start, as they give voice to the full range of human emotion while pointing back to God's character. Additionally, do not walk this path alone. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can find fellowship and support from other believers.

What is the biggest thing weighing on your heart today? Reflecting on this can help you bring specific burdens to the Lord in prayer.
What is the Gemara?

The Gemara is a fundamental text in Rabbinic Judaism that serves as a vast collection of scholarly discussions, legal analysis, and ethical teachings. It is the component of the Talmud that provides commentary and expansion on the Mishnah, which is an earlier written compilation of Jewish oral traditions. 

The term Gemara comes from a root word meaning to learn or to complete. After the Mishnah was compiled around 200 AD, generations of Jewish scholars known as Amoraim spent several centuries debating its applications and exploring its meanings. These extensive records of their discussions were eventually compiled into the Gemara. When the Mishnah and the Gemara are studied together, they form what is known as the Talmud. There are two versions of this work: the Jerusalem Talmud, completed in the fourth century, and the more extensive and widely studied Babylonian Talmud, completed around the end of the fifth century.

From a Christian perspective, we view the Gemara as an important historical and religious document that reflects the development of Jewish thought after the time of Jesus. While it contains much wisdom and preserved history, we believe that the Bible alone is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, serving as our ultimate authority for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16–17). In His earthly ministry, Jesus often addressed the weight of human traditions, encouraging His followers to prioritize the heart of God's Word over the "tradition of the elders" (Mark 7:1–13).

The discussions within the Gemara often focus on how to live out the requirements of the Mosaic Law in every detail of life. As believers, we recognize that the Law is holy and good, yet its ultimate purpose is to point us toward our need for a Savior (Romans 3:19–20; Galatians 3:24). Because no person can perfectly keep the Law, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Salvation is not found in the meticulous keeping of laws or traditions, but is a gift of grace received through faith in the finished work of Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What are your thoughts on how Jesus relates to the traditions of the Law?
How can I stop being a people-pleaser?

It is a heavy burden to carry the expectations of others as your primary guide for life. Many people find themselves trapped in a cycle of seeking approval because they deeply value peace and connection, but the Bible describes this struggle as the "fear of man." When our desire to please people outweighs our desire to please God, it becomes a snare that limits our freedom and our ability to serve Him effectively.

To begin walking in freedom, it is helpful to understand the root of why we seek this approval. Proverbs 29:25 says, "The fear of man lays a snare, but whoever trusts in the Lord is safe." People-pleasing is often an attempt to find a sense of worth or safety in the opinions of others. However, human approval is fickle and can never provide the soul with the lasting security it craves.

The ultimate solution to people-pleasing is found in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We often seek the "well done" of others because we are looking for justification, a way to feel that we are "enough." The Gospel tells us that while God is perfectly holy and His standards are righteous (Isaiah 6:3; Matthew 5:48), we have all fallen short of His glory through our sin (Romans 3:23). No amount of human approval can fix our separation from God. 

However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). When you put your faith in Jesus, you are justified by grace alone, not by your works or by how well you please others (Ephesians 2:8-9). In Christ, the only opinion that eternally matters, God the Father’s opinion, is already settled. He looks at you and sees the righteousness of His Son.

You can turn from the exhaustion of people-pleasing today. Repent of seeking your worth in others and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and a new identity.

As you grow in this grace, consider these practical steps grounded in Scripture:

First, reorient your primary audience. The Apostle Paul wrote in Galatians 1:10, "For am I now seeking the approval of man, or of God? Or am I trying to please man? If I were still trying to please man, I would not be a servant of Christ." When you face a decision, ask yourself if you are choosing a path out of fear of rejection or out of love for God.

Second, practice speaking the truth in love. People-pleasing often leads to dishonesty because we are afraid to say "no" or to disagree. Ephesians 4:15 encourages believers to speak the truth in love, which helps us grow into maturity. Saying "no" when necessary is not unkind; it is an act of integrity that honors God and respects the other person.

Third, find your rest in the finished work of Christ. When you feel the pressure to perform for others, remind yourself that your value is not a variable to be earned but a gift already given through the cross. Spending time in daily prayer and reading the Word will help settle your heart in the truth of God's love for you (Psalm 119:105; Hebrews 4:16).

What do you feel is the biggest thing holding you back from resting in God's approval instead of seeking it from people?
What is the golden chain of salvation?

The "Golden Chain of Salvation" is a theological term used to describe the sequence of God’s sovereign actions in redeeming His people. This concept is primarily drawn from the book of Romans and highlights that salvation is entirely a work of God’s grace from beginning to end. It provides comfort to believers by showing that those whom God sets His love upon will certainly be brought to eternal glory.

The scriptural foundation for this chain is found in Romans 8:29-30, which says, "For those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, in order that he might be the firstborn among many brothers. And those whom he predestined he also called, and those whom he called he also justified, and those whom he justified he also glorified."

Each link in this chain represents a specific aspect of God’s work in the life of a believer:

1. Foreknowledge: This refers to God setting His special, sovereign love upon individuals before the foundation of the world (Romans 8:29; 1 Peter 1:2). It is more than just knowing facts in advance, it is a relational commitment.
2. Predestination: God sovereignly decrees and appoints those He loves to be conformed to the likeness of His Son, Jesus Christ (Ephesians 1:5; Ephesians 1:11).
3. Calling: This is the effectual work of the Holy Spirit where God summons a person to Himself through the message of the Gospel, opening their heart to respond in faith (Romans 1:6; 1 Corinthians 1:24).
4. Justification: When a person trusts in Christ, God legally declares them righteous. This is not based on their own moral effort but is a result of the perfect life and sacrificial death of Jesus being credited to them by grace through faith (Romans 3:24; Romans 5:1).
5. Glorification: This is the final step where the believer is made perfect in both soul and body at the return of Christ, dwelling with God forever in a renewed creation (Romans 8:18; 1 John 3:2).

Because God is the one who initiates, sustains, and completes this process, the believer can have absolute confidence that God will finish the good work He started (Philippians 1:6). This sequence emphasizes that salvation is not a result of human merit but of God's unmerited favor.

This entire process is made possible through the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, yet all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ (who is truly God and truly man), to live the perfect life we could not live and to die on the cross as a substitute for sinners. Jesus was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; 1 Peter 3:18).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Should Mark 16:9-20 be in the Bible?

The question of whether Mark 16:9-20 belongs in the Bible is a significant topic in biblical studies that touches on how God has preserved His Word. Most modern translations include these verses but place them in brackets or include a footnote explaining that the earliest and most reliable Greek manuscripts do not contain them.

The two oldest and most respected complete Greek manuscripts of the New Testament, Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, both end the Gospel of Mark at Mark 16:8. Additionally, the style and vocabulary of these final verses differ somewhat from the rest of the book. For these reasons, many scholars believe that the original ending of Mark may have been lost or that Mark intentionally ended his account abruptly at Mark 16:8 to leave the reader with the awe and fear of the empty tomb.

However, the church has recognized these verses as part of the biblical canon for many centuries. They appear in the vast majority of later manuscripts and were known to early church fathers. Even if they were added shortly after Mark wrote his Gospel to provide a more traditional conclusion, the content of these verses is consistent with the rest of the New Testament. The resurrection appearances and the command to go into all the world to proclaim the gospel in Mark 16:15 align with the Great Commission found in Matthew 28:18-20 and the accounts in Luke 24:36-49.

Because of this, we can be confident that while there is a textual question about their original placement, the truths they contain are supported by the rest of inspired Scripture. The Gospel remains intact regardless of this specific variant. The Bible teaches that God is holy and that all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory, which creates a separation between us and our Creator. In His mercy, God sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death as seen in Mark 16:6 and 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Salvation is not something we can earn through our own efforts or works; it is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone, in Christ alone. As the Bible says, if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved, as stated in Romans 10:9.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What questions do you have about how we can trust the reliability of the Bible today?
What is the difference between a sect and a cult?

In a general sense, the terms sect and cult are often used to describe groups that have separated from a larger body, but in a Christian and theological context, there are distinct differences between the two.

A sect is typically a smaller group that has broken away from a larger, established religious body or denomination. While a sect may have unique interpretations of secondary or tertiary doctrines, such as specific views on baptism, worship styles, or end-times prophecy, they often still hold to the essential core of the Christian faith. For example, a sect might disagree with a parent organization on how a church should be governed, yet they still affirm the Trinity, the deity of Jesus Christ, and salvation by grace through faith. Scripture encourages us to maintain unity in the essentials while showing charity in these secondary matters, as seen in Romans 14:1 through 14:4.

A cult, however, is defined more by its deviation from the fundamental, essential truths of the Gospel. Theologically, a cult is a group that claims to be Christian or "the only true way" but denies one or more of the core doctrines that define historic Christianity. These deviations often include denying the full deity of Jesus Christ, rejecting the Trinity, or adding human works and requirements to the finished work of Christ on the cross. The Apostle Paul warned against this in Galatians 1:6 through 1:9, where he spoke of those who preach a gospel that is contrary to the one revealed by God.

Beyond doctrine, cults often exhibit sociological characteristics such as an authoritarian leader who claims unique or divine authority, the isolation of members from their families and society, and the use of fear or manipulation to maintain control. While a sect might simply be an insular or narrow-minded group, a cult often demands total allegiance to a human leader or a specific set of extra-biblical writings, effectively replacing the authority of the Bible with their own.

The standard for discerning the difference is always the Word of God. We are called to contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints, as stated in Jude 1:3. We must test every teaching against the person and work of Jesus Christ.

The heart of the Christian faith is the good news that God, who is perfectly holy, has provided a way for sinful people to be reconciled to Him through His Son. We have all sinned and fallen short of God's glory, as noted in Romans 3:23, but God in His mercy sent Jesus Christ, truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any human effort or works, which is clear in Ephesians 2:8 through 2:9.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is "name it claim it" teaching biblical?

The "name it and claim it" teaching, often associated with the Word of Faith movement or the Prosperity Gospel, is not a biblical approach to faith and prayer. While it draws on certain verses about the power of words and the goodness of God, it often distorts the character of God and the nature of the Christian life.

Scripture teaches that God is the supreme and sovereign Creator who does all that He pleases (Psalm 115:3; Isaiah 46:9–10). The "name it and claim it" theology tends to reverse this relationship, suggesting that human beings can use faith as a tool or a force to compel God to act according to their desires. This shifts the focus from God’s will to human will.

Biblical prayer is not about demanding our own way, but about aligning our hearts with the purposes of God. Jesus modeled this in the Garden of Gethsemane when He prayed, "not my will, but yours, be done" (Luke 22:42). We are encouraged to bring our requests to God, but we must do so with the understanding that He answers according to His perfect wisdom and will (1 John 5:14–15).

Furthermore, the Bible does not guarantee that every believer will experience material wealth or physical health in this life. In fact, Scripture frequently speaks of the necessity of suffering and the refinement that comes through trials (Romans 5:3–5; James 1:2–4; 1 Peter 1:6–7). Many faithful servants of God, including the apostles, faced poverty, sickness, and persecution (2 Corinthians 11:23–29; 2 Timothy 4:20). Our primary hope is not in earthly comfort, but in the eternal inheritance kept for us in heaven (1 Peter 1:4).

The danger of this teaching is that it can obscure the true Gospel. The greatest need of humanity is not financial prosperity or physical healing, but reconciliation with a holy and just God. Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is a gift secured by Christ’s work alone. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or the "strength" of our spoken words (Ephesians 2:8–9). True faith is not a means to get things from God, but a humble reliance on who God is and what He has done for us in Jesus.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about cryonics or cryogenics?

The Bible does not explicitly mention cryonics or cryogenics, as these are modern scientific developments. However, Scripture provides several principles regarding life, death, and the nature of our hope that help us think through this topic with a biblical perspective.

First, the Bible teaches that God is the sovereign author of life and the one who determines the length of our days. Job 14:5 says that a person's days are determined and that God has set limits no one can exceed. Similarly, Psalm 139:16 tells us that all the days ordained for us were written in God's book before one of them came to be. From this perspective, while medical technology can be a blessing used to preserve life, the attempt to bypass death indefinitely through cryonics may reflect a struggle to trust God’s timing and sovereignty over the end of our earthly journey.

Second, the Bible defines death not merely as a biological state, but as the separation of the soul or spirit from the physical body. Ecclesiastes 12:7 describes death by saying the dust returns to the ground it came from, and the spirit returns to God who gave it. James 2:26 further notes that the body without the spirit is dead. From a Christian standpoint, once the spirit has departed to be with the Lord or to face judgment, as described in Hebrews 9:27, no amount of physical preservation can "pause" or "trap" the soul within the body.

Third, there is a significant difference between the biological revival sought by cryonics and the glorious resurrection promised in Scripture. The hope of cryonics is to eventually repair a mortal body using future technology. In contrast, the Christian hope is the bodily resurrection performed by God. In 1 Corinthians 15:42-44, the Bible explains that the body is sown in corruption but raised in incorruption, sown in weakness but raised in power. Our ultimate hope is not in the preservation of our current, fallen bodies, but in the transformation into a resurrection body like that of Jesus Christ.

Finally, the motivation behind cryonics often stems from a natural fear of death. While death is an enemy, Jesus Christ has defeated it. 2 Timothy 1:10 tells us that Christ Jesus has destroyed death and has brought life and immortality to light through the gospel. 

The most important question is not how we might preserve our physical bodies, but where we stand with the One who holds power over both life and death. The Bible teaches that we are all separated from God by our sin, but God, in His holiness and love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over the grave. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not by any human works or technology.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. Have you ever considered what it means to place your ultimate hope for the future in Jesus rather than in human technology?
How can I obtain favor from the Lord (Proverbs 8:35)?

In Proverbs 8:35, the speaker is Wisdom personified, saying, "For whoever finds me finds life and obtains favor from the Lord." To understand how to obtain this favor, we must look at what it means to find the wisdom of God.

Scripture teaches that the "fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom" (Proverbs 9:10). This does not mean a terrifying dread, but a profound reverence, awe, and submission to God as the Creator and Ruler of all things. Obtaining favor begins with a heart that seeks God and values His truth above all earthly treasures (Proverbs 8:11; Proverbs 8:19).

However, the Bible also makes it clear that we cannot earn God's favor through our own wisdom or good works. While God is holy and just, we have all sinned and turned away from His perfect standard (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from the life and favor mentioned in Proverbs. The ultimate expression of God’s wisdom is found in the person of Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul describes Jesus as the "wisdom of God" (1 Corinthians 1:24) and notes that in Him "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3).

True favor is found through the Gospel. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life of wisdom that we could not live. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, He defeated sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Because of His work, favor is not something we achieve, but something we receive as a gift of grace. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts (Ephesians 2:8-9). 

To obtain the favor of the Lord in its fullest sense is to be reconciled to Him through His Son. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive His forgiveness and new life. 

As you walk with Him, you continue to obtain favor by growing in wisdom through the study of His Word, persistent prayer, and the guidance of the Holy Spirit. God delights in those who seek Him with a humble heart (James 4:6; Psalm 51:17). 

What is it that draws you to seek God's favor at this time in your life?
Why is solo Christo important?

*Solus Christus*, or Christ alone, is the foundational conviction that salvation is found in the person and work of Jesus Christ alone, without any addition of human merit, religious rituals, or other mediators. This truth is essential because it protects the purity of the Gospel and ensures that the hope of the believer rests on the only foundation that cannot be shaken.

The importance of this doctrine is seen in several key areas:

**The Unique Identity of Christ**
Jesus Christ is the only person who is truly God and truly man. Because of His divine nature, His sacrifice has infinite value, and because of His human nature, He can represent humanity before God. The Bible teaches in Acts 4:12 that "there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved." No other person, prophet, or religious figure can bridge the gap between a holy God and sinful people.

**The One Mediator**
Scripture is clear that there is only one path to reconciliation with the Father. 1 Timothy 2:5 says, "For there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus." To suggest that we need other mediators, such as saints or priests, to gain access to God's grace is to diminish the finished work of Christ. Jesus Himself declared in John 14:6, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."

**The Sufficiency of His Work**
The doctrine of Christ alone emphasizes that His life, death, and resurrection are completely sufficient for our redemption. When Jesus was on the cross, He cried out, "It is finished," as recorded in John 19:30. This signifies that the debt of sin was paid in full. If we were required to add our own works or goodness to what He has done, it would imply that His sacrifice was insufficient. Hebrews 10:12 reinforces this by stating, "But when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God."

**The Gospel Message**
At the heart of the Christian faith is the reality of our condition and God's gracious response. God is perfectly holy and just, while we are fallen and separated from Him by our sin. We are unable to save ourselves through moral effort or religious devotion. In His great mercy, God sent His Son to live a sinless life, die a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His power over death. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God right now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for your forgiveness and new life. What makes it difficult for you to rely on Christ's work alone rather than your own efforts?
Does Genesis chapter 1 literally mean 24 hour days?

The question of whether the days in Genesis chapter 1 refer to literal 24 hour periods is a subject that faithful Christians have studied and discussed for centuries. Because the Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God (2 Timothy 3:16), believers approach this text with a deep desire to understand what God intended to communicate. Within the church, there are several respected views on this topic, as it is considered a tertiary issue where there is room for disagreement while maintaining Christian unity.

Many Christians hold to the Literal or Young Earth view, which maintains that the days are 24 hour periods. This interpretation is primarily based on the use of the Hebrew word "yom" (day) accompanied by the phrase "evening and morning" and a numerical qualifier (first day, second day, etc.). In the rest of the Old Testament, this specific combination almost always refers to a literal day. This view is also supported by the Sabbath commandment in Exodus 20:11, where God links the human six day work week to His six days of creation.

Other faithful believers hold to the Day-Age view or Old Earth Creationism. They point out that the word "yom" can also refer to an indefinite period of time or an era, such as in Genesis 2:4 or Psalm 90:4. Since the sun and moon were not created to mark time until the fourth day in Genesis 1:14–19, some suggest the first three "days" might not have been measured by solar cycles as we know them today. Furthermore, 2 Peter 3:8 reminds us that with the Lord a day is like a thousand years, suggesting that God’s perspective on time differs from our own.

Another perspective is the Framework Hypothesis, which views the seven day structure of Genesis chapter 1 as a literary or poetic framework rather than a strict chronological timeline. In this view, the first three days describe God forming the world (creating light, sky, and land), and the next three days describe God filling those realms (with stars, birds, fish, animals, and humans). The goal of the text, from this perspective, is to show God’s perfect order and His sovereignty over all creation.

While Christians may differ on the duration of these days, we find common ground in the essential truth that God is the almighty Creator who brought all things into existence out of nothing by the power of His Word (Genesis 1:1; Hebrews 11:3). This account reminds us that we are not the products of blind chance, but are intentionally created by a holy and sovereign God.

This same holy God who created the stars also created you for His glory. However, Scripture teaches that every human has turned away from God through sin, which separates us from His perfect holiness (Romans 3:23). Because God is just, He must punish sin, but because He is merciful, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to reconcile us to Himself. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As you reflect on the wonder of creation, what holds you back from trusting the Creator with your life?
What is the power of the Holy Spirit?

The power of the Holy Spirit is the very power of God active in the world and in the hearts of people. The Holy Spirit is not a vague force or energy, but the third Person of the Trinity, who is fully God and equal in essence, glory, and majesty with the Father and the Son (Matthew 28:19; Acts 5:3–4).

His power is displayed in several vital ways throughout the life of a believer and the history of the church:

**The Power of New Life**
The most foundational display of the Spirit's power is the work of regeneration, or the "new birth." Because of sin, every human being is spiritually dead and separated from a holy God (Romans 3:23; Ephesians 2:1). The Holy Spirit sovereignly works to open blind eyes and soften hard hearts, enabling a person to see their need for a Savior and to believe the Gospel (John 3:5–8). 

The Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a perfect and sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, triumphed over death, and is now exalted as Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). This salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8–9). 

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

**The Power for Transformation**
Once a person trusts in Christ, the Holy Spirit indwells them, providing the power to change (1 Corinthians 6:19). This is the power of sanctification, where the Spirit helps the believer put to death sinful habits and instead produce "fruit" such as love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control (Galatians 5:22–23; Romans 8:13). He transforms our desires so that we begin to love what God loves.

**The Power for Witness and Service**
Jesus promised His followers that they would receive power when the Holy Spirit came upon them to be His witnesses to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8). This power is not for personal status, but for the bold proclamation of the truth. The Spirit also grants spiritual gifts to every believer for the purpose of building up the church and serving others (1 Corinthians 12:4–7).

**The Power of Comfort and Assurance**
The Holy Spirit is often called the "Helper" or "Comforter." He provides a deep, internal sense of peace and reminds the believer of the promises of God (John 14:26). Furthermore, He provides assurance of salvation by "bearing witness with our spirit that we are children of God" (Romans 8:16).

If you find yourself hungering for this kind of spiritual life and strength, what do you feel is currently holding you back from fully trusting in Jesus Christ?
How is the word to be read and heard, that it may become effectual to salvation?

For the Word of God to become effectual to salvation, it must be attended to with diligence, preparation, and prayer. Reading and hearing the Bible is not merely an intellectual exercise but a spiritual one that requires the engagement of the heart and the illumination of the Holy Spirit. 

First, we should approach the Scriptures with a spirit of reverence and a sincere desire to learn what God has revealed. This involves setting aside distractions and preparing our hearts to receive the truth, much like the Bereans who "received the word with all readiness" (Acts 17:11). We must also pray for understanding, asking God to "open my eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of your law" (Psalm 119:18).

Second, the Word must be received with faith and love. The Bible warns that the message heard by some in the past did not benefit them because it was not "joined by faith in those who heard" (Hebrews 4:2). We are called to believe that what God has spoken is true and to treasure it as the source of life. This means laying aside our own prejudices or worldly wisdom to "receive with meekness the implanted word, which is able to save your souls" (James 1:21).

Finally, for the Word to be truly effectual, we must hide it in our hearts and practice it in our lives. We are encouraged not to be "hearers who forget, but doers who act" (James 1:25). This transformative process is the work of the Holy Spirit, who uses the Word to convict us of sin, lead us to Christ, and conform us to His image.

The central message of the Word, which leads to salvation, is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. The Scriptures reveal that God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3), but all human beings have sinned and are separated from Him (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we cannot save ourselves through our own works or efforts. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord over all, and He offers salvation as a free gift of grace to all who believe (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is millennialism?

Millennialism is the study of the thousand-year reign of Jesus Christ mentioned in the Book of Revelation. It specifically focuses on the interpretation of Revelation 20:1–6, where the Bible describes a period when Christ reigns and Satan is bound. While Christians agree on the central truth that Jesus will return in glory to judge the world and usher in the eternal state, they have historically held different views on the timing and nature of this millennium.

One perspective is Premillennialism, which holds that Jesus Christ will return to earth bodily before a literal thousand-year kingdom is established. In this view, Christ will reign over the nations, fulfilling specific promises made in the Old and New Testaments. This perspective often emphasizes a period of tribulation preceding Christ's arrival, as seen in Matthew 24:21–30.

Another view is Amillennialism. The prefix "a-" suggests "no literal millennium." Proponents of this view believe the thousand years represent a symbolic, perfect period of time, namely the current church age. They believe Christ is reigning now from His throne in heaven and through the hearts of His believers. The binding of Satan is understood as a restriction of his power to deceive the nations, allowing the Gospel to go forth as described in Matthew 28:18–20.

A third view is Postmillennialism, which teaches that Christ will return after the millennium. In this framework, the millennium is a long period of peace and righteousness brought about by the successful spread of the Gospel and the influence of the Holy Spirit in the world. Christ returns to a world that has been largely prepared for His arrival through the fulfillment of the Great Commission.

These different views are considered secondary or tertiary doctrines. This means that while they are important for our understanding of Scripture, they are not essential for salvation. Faithful Christians who love the Word of God often disagree on these points while remaining united in the core Gospel. As Romans 14:1–5 suggests, we should hold our convictions with humility and charity toward those who see things differently.

The most important reality is that Jesus Christ is the King of kings and Lord of lords. Because of our sin, we were all separated from God, but out of His great love, God sent His Son to take our punishment. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death as stated in 1 Corinthians 15:3–4. He ascended to heaven and will one day return to bring final justice and restoration, as promised in Acts 1:11.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider these different views on the end times, what holds you back from trusting Him with your future today?
What does the Bible say about war?

The Bible presents war as a somber reality of a world broken by sin, while simultaneously pointing us toward a future where God will bring all conflict to an end. From the earliest pages of Scripture, we see that war is a consequence of human rebellion and the disordered desires of the heart. James 4:1-2 asks, "What causes quarrels and what causes fights among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you? You desire and do not have, so you murder. You covet and cannot obtain, so you fight and quarrel."

In the Old Testament, God is sometimes described as a warrior who fought for His people, Israel, to fulfill His holy purposes and execute judgment on wicked nations, Exodus 15:3; Joshua 10:42. However, these specific historical commands were unique to that era of redemptive history and do not serve as a universal mandate for nations today. Even then, God’s ultimate desire was for His people to live in peace and righteousness.

When Jesus Christ came, He revealed a different path for His followers. He is called the Prince of Peace, Isaiah 9:6. In His Sermon on the Mount, Jesus taught, "Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God," Matthew 5:9. He commanded His disciples to love their enemies and pray for those who persecute them, Matthew 5:44. This shift emphasizes that the primary battle for the Christian is not against flesh and blood, but a spiritual struggle against evil, Ephesians 6:12.

While Christians have historically held different views on a believer's participation in war, such as Just War theory or pacifism, the Bible is clear that the ultimate solution to war is the return of Christ. God promises a day when "He shall judge between the nations, and shall decide disputes for many peoples; and they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore," Isaiah 2:4.

The root of all external conflict is the internal conflict between humanity and God caused by our sin. Because God is holy and just, our sin separates us from Him and leads to the brokenness we see in the world. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Through Christ, we can be reconciled to God and experience a peace that surpasses understanding.

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. Have you ever considered how the peace Jesus offers might change the way you view the conflicts in the world today?
Are there errors in the Qur'an (Koran)?

From the standpoint of the Bible, which Christians believe to be the inspired, inerrant, and final authority for truth (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20-21), there are fundamental contradictions in the Qur'an regarding the nature of God, the identity of Jesus Christ, and the way of salvation. Because the Bible is the historically prior and divinely revealed Word of God, these contradictions are understood by Christians as significant errors in the Qur'an's claims.

The most central contradiction concerns the person and work of Jesus. The Qur'an denies the deity of Christ, the doctrine of the Trinity, and the historical reality of the crucifixion. However, the Bible explicitly teaches that Jesus is God manifest in the flesh (John 1:1; John 1:14; Colossians 2:9). Furthermore, the Bible emphasizes that the death and bodily resurrection of Jesus are the essential facts of the Gospel. Without the cross, there is no payment for sin, and without the resurrection, the Christian faith would be futile (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; 1 Corinthians 15:14-17).

The Bible reveals that God is perfectly holy and just, while all human beings have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from God and deserves His righteous judgment. Because we cannot save ourselves through our own works or religious devotion, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice on our behalf, and rose again on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 2:24). Salvation is a gift of grace that is received through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; John 14:6).

While Muslims regard the Qur'an as the final revelation, Christians maintain that the New Covenant established by Jesus is the completion of God's redemptive plan (Hebrews 1:1-2). Any teaching that denies the finished work of Christ on the cross or His identity as the eternal Son of God is viewed as a departure from the truth revealed by God.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What is the spiritual significance to a déjà vu experience?

The experience of déjà vu, that sudden and often startling feeling that a current situation has happened before, is something many people encounter. While the Bible does not explicitly mention or define déjà vu, we can look to Scriptural principles to understand how a believer might process such an experience.

From a biblical perspective, it is important to first clarify what déjà vu is not. Some people suggest that these feelings are evidence of past lives or reincarnation. However, Scripture is clear that each person lives one life on this earth. Hebrews 9:27 teaches that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment. Therefore, we can be confident that these sensations are not memories of a previous existence.

Instead, we might view these moments through the lens of God’s sovereignty and His relationship to time. We live within the linear flow of time, but God exists outside of it. He is the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end, as stated in Revelation 22:13. God knows every detail of our lives before they happen. Psalm 139:16 says that all the days ordained for us were written in His book before one of them came to be. While déjà vu may be a result of how our brains process memory and perception, it can serve as a small reminder of how vast and complex God’s creation is and how limited our own understanding remains.

Rather than seeking a deep spiritual or prophetic significance in a fleeting physical sensation, the Bible encourages us to ground our lives in the solid foundation of God’s revealed truth. 2 Timothy 3:16–17 reminds us that all Scripture is breathed out by God and is sufficient to equip us for every good work. If an experience like déjà vu leaves you feeling uncertain or searching for deeper meaning, it is always best to return to the Gospel, which provides the ultimate clarity for our lives.

The Bible explains that we were created by a holy God to be in relationship with Him, but our sin has separated us from Him. Because of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all, offering reconciliation to all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

Does the experience of déjà vu cause you any spiritual concern, or are you primarily curious about why it happens?
What are the four beasts in Daniel chapter 7?

In Daniel 7, the prophet Daniel receives a vision of four great beasts emerging from the sea. These beasts represent four successive earthly kingdoms or empires that would rise to power, as explained later in the same chapter (Daniel 7:17). Most biblical scholars and historians identify these empires as Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, and Rome.

The first beast was like a lion and had eagle's wings (Daniel 7:4). This symbolizes the Babylonian Empire, known for its majesty and power. The description of its wings being plucked and the beast being made to stand on two feet like a man and given a human mind likely refers to the humbling and subsequent restoration of King Nebuchadnezzar (Daniel 4:34-37).

The second beast was like a bear, raised up on one of its sides, with three ribs in its mouth (Daniel 7:5). This represents the Medo-Persian Empire. The fact that it was raised on one side reflects the dominance of the Persians over the Medes within the alliance. The three ribs are often understood to represent the three major territories conquered by this empire: Lydia, Babylon, and Egypt.

The third beast was like a leopard, which had four wings of a bird on its back and four heads (Daniel 7:6). This symbolizes the Grecian Empire. The leopard and its wings represent the incredible speed with which Alexander the Great conquered the known world. The four heads represent the four divisions of the empire among Alexander's generals following his untimely death.

The fourth beast was terrifying, dreadful, and exceedingly strong, having great iron teeth and ten horns (Daniel 7:7). This represents the Roman Empire, which was characterized by its immense military power and its ability to crush and devour its opponents. Daniel 7:8 describes a "little horn" that rises among the ten horns, speaking boastful words and making war against the saints of God.

While these earthly kingdoms are characterized by power and often by cruelty, the vision shifts from these temporary empires to a celestial courtroom. Daniel sees the "Ancient of Days" taking His seat in judgment (Daniel 7:9). He then sees one like a "Son of Man" coming with the clouds of heaven, who is given an everlasting dominion and a kingdom that shall not be destroyed (Daniel 7:13-14).

This Son of Man is Jesus Christ. While earthly nations rise and fall, Christ's kingdom is eternal and founded on His perfect righteousness and His victory over sin and death. He lived the life we could not live and died the death we deserved on the cross to reconcile us to God. Through His bodily resurrection, He proved His authority over all things. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What do you find most striking about the contrast between these earthly kingdoms and the kingdom of Christ?
What did Paul mean when he said, “I die daily” in 1 Corinthians 15:31?

When the Apostle Paul says, "I die daily" in 1 Corinthians 15:31, he is primarily speaking about the constant physical danger and the threat of martyrdom he faced for the sake of the gospel. In the context of 1 Corinthians 15, Paul is defending the reality of the physical resurrection of the dead. He argues that his willingness to face death every day would be meaningless if there were no future resurrection. He asks in the preceding verse, "Why are we in danger every hour?" (1 Corinthians 15:30), and follows in the next verse by mentioning his "humanly speaking" battle with "beasts at Ephesus" (1 Corinthians 15:32).

Paul's life was a daily sacrifice because he was fully convinced that Jesus Christ had been raised from the dead. This conviction is the heart of the Christian faith. We are all separated from our holy God by our sin, but God, in His mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature and live a perfect life. He died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, was buried, and was raised bodily on the third day, demonstrating His authority and victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4; 1 Corinthians 15:20). He is now the exalted Lord of all, and because He lives, those who belong to Him through faith will also be raised to eternal life.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in the hope of the resurrection that Paul describes?
What does the Bible say about motivation?

The Bible teaches that our motivations are rooted in the condition and direction of our hearts. While the world often focuses on external success and visible results, God is deeply concerned with the reasons behind our actions. Scripture suggests that the most profound and lasting motivation does not come from self-interest or the pursuit of human praise, but from a heart transformed by God's grace.

The primary motivation for a believer is the glory of God. In 1 Corinthians 10:31, the Apostle Paul instructs, "Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God." This means that even the most mundane tasks of life can be infused with purpose when they are done as an act of worship toward our Creator.

When we view our work or our service through this lens, it changes who we are trying to please. Colossians 3:23–24 says, "Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men, knowing that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward. You are serving the Lord Christ." This perspective frees us from the exhausting cycle of seeking constant approval from others, as we find our primary satisfaction in serving the Lord.

However, the Bible also acknowledges that our hearts can be deceptive. Proverbs 16:2 notes, "All the ways of a man are pure in his own eyes, but the Lord weighs the spirit." Because God sees what is hidden, He desires truth and sincerity in our inner being. This is why 1 Samuel 16:7 reminds us that "the Lord sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the Lord looks on the heart."

The most powerful "engine" for Christian motivation is the love of Christ. Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 5:14, "For the love of Christ controls us, because we have concluded this: that one has died for all, therefore all have died." When we truly understand the magnitude of what Jesus has done for us, our primary drive becomes a grateful response to His mercy rather than a fearful attempt to earn His favor.

True motivation is a matter of the heart being reconciled to God. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, but because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him. In His great love, God the Son became man in the person of Jesus Christ. He lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. We are not justified by our own works or our own "good" motivations, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. 

When you trust in Him, the Holy Spirit begins a work of progressive sanctification, aligning your desires and motivations with God’s will. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What do you feel is currently the primary driver or "why" behind the things you do each day?
What does it mean to put off the old man (Ephesians 4:22)?

To put off the old man means to renounce and discard the former way of life that was dominated by sin and spiritual darkness. When the Apostle Paul writes this instruction in Ephesians 4:22, he uses the imagery of taking off a set of old, filthy garments that no longer fit who you are in Christ.

The old man refers to the fallen human nature we inherited from Adam. This nature is characterized by a heart that is hardened toward God, an understanding that is darkened, and desires that are corrupt and deceitful, as described in Ephesians 4:17–19. Before someone trusts in Jesus, they are spiritually dead and governed by these sinful impulses.

Putting off the old man involves several key biblical truths:

First, it is a response to the Gospel. This is not a matter of self-improvement or moral reform through human effort. It is only possible because of what Jesus Christ accomplished. Through His death on the cross, He paid the penalty for our sins, and through His resurrection, He broke the power of death and sin. When you are united to Christ by faith, your old self is considered crucified with Him so that the body of sin might be brought to nothing, as Paul explains in Romans 6:6.

Second, it involves a change of mind. Ephesians 4:23 says that we are to be renewed in the spirit of our minds. This means shifting your perspective from the values of the world to the truths of God’s Word. It is a daily process of letting the Holy Spirit reshape how you think, feel, and choose.

Third, it leads to putting on the new man. You do not simply stop doing bad things; you replace them with the righteous fruit of the Spirit. Ephesians 4:24 describes this new man as being created according to the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness. For example, Paul follows this instruction by saying that instead of lying, we speak the truth; instead of stealing, we work to give to others; and instead of bitterness, we practice forgiveness, as seen in Ephesians 4:25–32.

This transformation is the outward evidence of the inward change God works in a believer. As 2 Corinthians 5:17 states, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; old things have passed away and all things have become new.

While believers still struggle with the presence of sin, we are no longer slaves to it. We rest in the promise that God, who began this good work in us, will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ, according to Philippians 1:6. 

If you find yourself struggling with the weight of your past or the power of sin, know that Jesus offers full forgiveness and the power to change. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive a new life and a new identity. What is it that makes you hesitate to fully surrender your old life to Him?
What is a freethinker?

A freethinker is generally defined as a person who forms their opinions and beliefs based on reason, logic, and empirical evidence rather than on tradition, authority, or religious dogma. The term gained prominence during the Enlightenment, a period when many sought to elevate human intellect as the primary means of understanding the world, often intentionally distancing themselves from the teachings of the church.

In many contexts, freethought is associated with atheism, agnosticism, or secular humanism. The underlying assumption is that for a person to be truly "free" in their thinking, they must be unencumbered by the perceived restrictions of divine revelation or established religious institutions.

From a Christian perspective, we recognize that God is the author of reason and that He has invited us to use our minds to seek and understand truth. We are made in the image of God, which includes our capacity for rational thought (Genesis 1:27). However, Scripture also teaches that human reason is not neutral or infallible. Because of the fall of humanity into sin, our thinking can be clouded by our own desires and limited perspectives. The Bible suggests that while God has revealed His power and nature through creation, humans often suppress this truth (Romans 1:18–20).

True freedom, according to Jesus, is not found in the absence of all authority but in the presence of the Truth. Jesus said, "If you abide in my word, you are truly my disciples, and you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free" (John 8:31–32). For the believer, "thinking freely" means being liberated from the darkness of sin and error so that we can see reality as it truly is, grounded in the character and Word of God.

The most profound truth any of us can consider is the Gospel. Though God is perfectly holy and just, we have all turned away from Him to follow our own paths, which leads to spiritual death. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, defeating sin and death. He now reigns as Lord and offers forgiveness and eternal life to all who believe.

Salvation is not earned by our own intellectual efforts or moral works, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is an overseer in the church?

In the New Testament, the term overseer comes from the Greek word *episkopos*, which refers to someone who has the responsibility of watching over, managing, or caring for a group of people. In the context of the local church, the Bible uses the terms overseer, elder, and pastor interchangeably to describe the same office of leadership. For example, in Acts 20:17 and Acts 20:28, the apostle Paul calls for the "elders" of the church and then refers to them as "overseers" whom the Holy Spirit has appointed to "shepherd" (or pastor) the church of God.

The role of an overseer is defined by specific character qualifications and spiritual responsibilities. According to 1 Timothy 3:1–7 and Titus 1:5–9, an overseer must be above reproach, faithful in marriage, temperate, self controlled, respectable, hospitable, and able to teach. Their primary duties include leading the congregation, teaching sound doctrine, protecting the church from false teachings, and caring for the spiritual well being of the believers under their charge as noted in 1 Peter 5:1–2 and Hebrews 13:17.

The ultimate purpose of an overseer is to point the congregation toward Jesus Christ, who is called the "Shepherd and Overseer of your souls" in 1 Peter 2:25. The most important truth an overseer guards is the Gospel. This message begins with the holiness of God, who created us for His glory. However, all of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory, which creates a separation that we cannot bridge by our own efforts or good works as stated in Romans 3:23 and Ephesians 2:8–9.

Because of His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord of all according to 1 Corinthians 15:3–4 and Romans 10:9. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What is your current understanding of who Jesus is and what He has done for you?
Someone gave me a prophetic word/prophesied over me. What is the interpretation?

It is understandable that receiving a word someone describes as prophetic can lead to many questions. When seeking to understand such an experience, the most important step is to bring everything to the light of the Bible. God has given us His Word as the final, sufficient, and perfect authority for our lives (2 Timothy 3:16-17).

While Christians hold different views on how God speaks today, ranging from those who believe miraculous signs were intended primarily for the apostolic age to those who believe they continue in a limited way, all agree that no personal word can ever contradict or add to the authority of Scripture. If you are trying to interpret or evaluate what was said to you, here are several biblical principles to guide you.

First, test the word against the Bible. God will never tell you to do something that contradicts His commands or the character He has revealed in Scripture (Galatians 1:8). If the word encourages sin, pride, or a departure from biblical truth, it is not from God.

Second, consider the focus of the message. The Holy Spirit’s work is always to glorify Jesus Christ (John 16:14). If a word draws more attention to the person speaking or to a mystical experience than it does to the Lord Jesus and His finished work, you should exercise great caution.

Third, seek the counsel of your local church. God has placed you in a community of believers with elders and pastors who are called to shepherd your soul (Hebrews 13:17). Shared wisdom and mature spiritual oversight are vital when navigating spiritual impressions or claims of prophecy.

Fourth, remember that God’s primary "word" to us is His Son, Jesus Christ. Hebrews 1:1-2 tells us that while God spoke in various ways in the past, He has now spoken to us by His Son. Our greatest need is not a new revelation about our circumstances, but a deeper trust in the Gospel. 

The Gospel is the message that though we are separated from a holy God by our sin, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

If you are feeling uncertain or seeking direction for your life, the most certain place to find God's will is in the pages of Scripture. You can turn to Him today by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

As you reflect on what was shared with you, I encourage you to ask: Does this lead me to love Jesus more and obey His Word more faithfully? I would also suggest speaking with a trusted pastor in your local church to help you discern these things with wisdom and care. What part of the message you received is causing the most uncertainty for you?
What is/isn’t a Christian married couple allowed to do, sexually?

The Bible teaches that sex is a beautiful gift created by God to be enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of marriage between one man and one woman. Because God is the author of sex, He provides us with principles that promote the flourishing of the marriage relationship while protecting the dignity of both spouses.

The primary biblical principle for marital intimacy is found in Hebrews 13:4, which says, "Let marriage be held in honor among all, and let the marriage bed be undefiled, for God will judge the sexually immoral and adulterous." This verse suggests that within the privacy of marriage, there is a vast amount of freedom for a husband and wife to enjoy one another. The Song of Solomon also provides a vivid picture of the delight, passion, and physical enjoyment that God intends for a married couple.

However, this freedom is guided by several important biblical boundaries:

First, the marriage bed must remain exclusive. Anything that introduces a third party into the intimacy of the marriage is prohibited. This includes adultery, which is forbidden in Exodus 20:14, and the use of pornography. Jesus taught in Matthew 5:27-28 that to look at another person with lustful intent is to commit adultery in the heart. Pornography is not a "victimless" addition to a marriage; it involves others in your private intimacy and fuels desires for someone other than your spouse.

Second, sexual intimacy must be characterized by mutual consent, love, and selflessness. In 1 Corinthians 7:3-5, the apostle Paul explains that the husband and wife have a responsibility to fulfill each other's needs and that they should not deprive one another except by mutual agreement for a short time of prayer. This means that sex should never be used as a tool for manipulation or control. It should be a gift of self-giving love. If an activity makes one spouse feel degraded, unloved, or uncomfortable, it violates the command to love your neighbor (and your spouse) as yourself.

Third, the principle of conscience is vital. In Romans 14:23, Scripture notes that "whatever does not proceed from faith is sin." If a couple considers an activity that one or both feel is wrong or violates their conscience before God, they should refrain from it. A husband and wife should be able to enjoy their intimacy with a clear conscience, knowing they are honoring God and each other.

Ultimately, marriage and the physical union of sex are meant to be a living picture of the relationship between Jesus Christ and His Church, as described in Ephesians 5:31-32. Just as Christ gave Himself up for the Church out of sacrificial love, our marital intimacy should reflect that same holiness, joy, and devotion.

If you find yourself struggling with guilt over past mistakes or wondering how to align your life with God's design, please know that God is holy and just, yet He is also full of mercy. We have all fallen short of His perfect standards and are separated from Him by our sin. However, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross to pay for our sins, and rise again on the third day to defeat death. Through His sacrifice, we can be forgiven and reconciled to God.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why is God going to give us a white stone with a new name (Revelation 2:17)?

The promise of the white stone and the new name in Revelation 2:17 is a beautiful image of the intimacy, acceptance, and transformation that believers receive through Jesus Christ. While the Bible does not give one single definition for the stone, scholars often look at the historical context of the city of Pergamum and the broader themes of Scripture to understand its significance.

In the ancient world, a white stone was often used in legal trials. When a jury deliberated, a black stone signified a vote for "guilty," while a white stone signified "not guilty" or acquittal. For the believer, receiving a white stone from God serves as a powerful symbol of our justification. Because of the sacrifice of Jesus, the charges against us are wiped away, and we are declared righteous in God's sight, as described in Romans 8:1.

Additionally, white stones were sometimes used as "tesserae," which were essentially admission tickets to special banquets or public festivals. In this sense, the white stone represents our invitation to the eternal feast in the kingdom of God, often called the Marriage Supper of the Lamb in Revelation 19:9. It is a token of our belonging and our right to enter into God's presence forever.

The "new name" written on the stone points to a deep, personal intimacy between God and the believer. In the Bible, God often changed a person's name to reflect a new identity or a new direction in their life, such as when Abram became Abraham or Simon became Peter. This new name, which "no one knows except him who receives it," suggests a unique relationship with Christ that is personal and transformative. It signifies that God knows you completely, loves you individually, and has given you a permanent identity in His family, as seen in Isaiah 62:2 and Revelation 3:12.

Ultimately, these gifts are promised to "the one who conquers." We do not conquer through our own strength or moral effort, but through faith in the one who has already conquered sin and death. The Gospel tells us that God is holy and just, and our sin separated us from Him. However, out of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day.

Through Christ’s victory, we are offered forgiveness and the promise of this "white stone" of acquittal and invitation. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive this new life and new identity.

What does it mean to you to know that God desires to give you a name and an identity that is uniquely known by Him?
What is the meaning of the strange fire in Leviticus 10:1?

In Leviticus 10:1, the "strange fire" offered by Nadab and Abihu refers to fire that was unauthorized or profane. The Hebrew word used for "strange" is *zarah*, which carries the meaning of being foreign, outside the law, or not according to the prescribed pattern.

This event took place immediately after the Tabernacle was completed and the priesthood was inaugurated. God had already manifested His presence by sending fire from heaven to consume the burnt offering on the altar in Leviticus 9:24. The priests were commanded to keep this specific fire burning and to use it for their offerings (Leviticus 6:12-13). By offering "strange fire," Nadab and Abihu likely used fire from a common source rather than the altar fire God had ignited, or they offered incense at a time or in a manner that God had not commanded.

The meaning of this account centers on the absolute holiness of God and the necessity of approaching Him on His terms, not our own. As Moses explained in Leviticus 10:3, the Lord said, "Among those who are near me I will be sanctified, and before all the people I will be glorified." Their sin was not merely a technical error, but an act of presumption and disobedience that treated the worship of a holy God as something they could define for themselves.

This passage serves as a solemn reminder of God's character. God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just, and His wrath against sin is real (Isaiah 6:3; Hebrews 12:28-29). Because of our own sin and rebellion, we are all like Nadab and Abihu, separated from God and unable to approach Him through our own "unauthorized" efforts or good works (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 64:6).

However, the Gospel provides the hope that the "strange fire" account highlights by contrast. While Nadab and Abihu were consumed for their disobedience, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be our perfect High Priest. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature through the virgin birth and lived a life of perfect obedience to the Father. Unlike the sons of Aaron, Jesus never failed in His duty. He offered Himself as a final, sufficient sacrifice through His substitutionary atoning death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

We cannot reach God through our own rituals or moral improvements. Salvation is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). Because of Jesus' exaltation and Lordship, those who trust in Him can now draw near to God with confidence, knowing that Christ has satisfied the requirements of God's holiness on our behalf (Hebrews 4:14-16).

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is it possible to sell your soul to the devil?

The idea of selling one's soul to the devil is a common theme in literature and folklore, but it is not a biblical reality in the way it is often portrayed. From a scriptural perspective, you do not have the authority to sell your soul because you do not own it. God is the Creator and the ultimate owner of every human life. In the book of Ezekiel, God declares, "Behold, all souls are mine; the soul of the father as well as the soul of the son is mine" (Ezekiel 18:4).

While you cannot enter into a legal contract to trade your soul, the Bible does describe a serious spiritual condition common to all people. Apart from Christ, every person is naturally "sold under sin" (Romans 7:14) and lives in a state of spiritual bondage. This does not mean a person has made a deal with the devil, but rather that human nature is fallen and separated from God because of sin (Romans 3:23). Satan is described as a deceiver and "the god of this world" who blinds the minds of unbelievers (2 Corinthians 4:4), but he has no power to "buy" what God has created.

The wonderful news of the Bible is that although we were enslaved by sin, Jesus Christ came to pay a "ransom" to set us free (Mark 10:45). This is the core of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, and because of our sin, we deserve His judgment. However, because of His immense love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to earth. Jesus is truly God and truly man. He lived a perfect, sinless life and then voluntarily died on a cross, taking the punishment that we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming His authority as Lord of all.

Because of Christ’s finished work, salvation is a free gift. You cannot earn it through works or lose your soul through a "contract." Salvation is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. When a person trusts in Jesus, they are "bought with a price" (1 Corinthians 6:20) and belong forever to God. Jesus promised that no one can snatch His followers out of His hand (John 10:28).
