The historic biblical Gospel is built upon several essential truths found in Scripture:

First, God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). Because of His holiness, He cannot ignore sin. Second, all human beings have sinned and fallen short of God's glory, leading to spiritual separation from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). 

Third, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment that sinners deserved (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24). He was buried and physically rose from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Fourth, salvation is not earned by human effort or automatically granted without faith. It is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus Himself taught the exclusivity of this path when He said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). Other apostles affirmed this, stating that there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12).

The gospel of inclusion tends to remove the urgency of the biblical call to "repent and believe" (Mark 1:15). In contrast, the Bible presents a clear and beautiful invitation to respond to what God has done in Christ.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What did Paul mean by the “Holy Scriptures” in 2 Timothy 3:15?

When the Apostle Paul refers to the "Holy Scriptures" in 2 Timothy 3:15, he is primarily referring to the Hebrew Bible, which we now know as the Old Testament. He reminds Timothy that from his childhood, he had been acquainted with these "sacred writings." Since Timothy was raised by a Jewish mother and grandmother, he would have been deeply immersed in the Law, the Prophets, and the Writings from a very young age (2 Timothy 1:5; Acts 16:1).

Paul’s point in this passage is that these Old Testament Scriptures are not merely historical records or moral codes, but are "able to make you wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus" (2 Timothy 3:15). This highlights a vital truth in Christian doctrine: the Old Testament points directly to the person and work of Jesus Christ. It provides the necessary foundation for understanding God's holiness, the reality of human sin, and the promise of a coming Savior.

While Timothy’s childhood instruction was rooted in the Old Testament, Paul moves in the very next verse to say that "All Scripture is breathed out by God" (2 Timothy 3:16). This broader term, "Scripture," encompasses both the Old Testament and the New Testament writings that were being recognized as authoritative by the early church. For example, in 1 Timothy 5:18, Paul quotes from both Deuteronomy 25:4 and the Gospel of Luke 10:7, calling both of them "Scripture." Similarly, the Apostle Peter recognized Paul’s own letters as part of the "Scriptures" (2 Peter 3:16).

Ultimately, the goal of all Scripture is to reveal the Gospel. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works we can perform (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What do you find most compelling or challenging about the idea that the entire Bible centers on the person of Jesus?
Why is there a curse associated with hanging on a tree?

The association of a curse with hanging on a tree finds its roots in the Law given by God to Moses. In the book of Deuteronomy, God instructed the Israelites that if a person committed a crime worthy of death and was executed and then hung on a tree, their body should not remain there overnight. The scripture explains that anyone who is hung on a tree is under God’s curse (Deuteronomy 21:22-23). In the ancient world, this was a public display of shame and a sign that the individual was rejected by God and cut off from the community of His people because of their sin.

This physical curse pointed toward a profound spiritual reality. God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just. Because all people have sinned and failed to keep His perfect standard, every person is naturally under the curse of the Law, which results in spiritual death and separation from a holy God (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23).

The significance of this curse is most clearly seen in the death of Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul explains that Christ redeemed us from the curse of the Law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written that everyone who is hanged on a tree is cursed (Galatians 3:10-13).

Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man. He was born of a virgin and lived a perfect, sinless life, fulfilling every requirement of God's Law. Out of His great love and mercy, He willingly went to the cross, which was the "tree" of His day. On that cross, He took the curse that we deserved upon Himself. He suffered the penalty for our sins through His substitutionary atoning death, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Because of Christ's work, salvation is not earned by our own efforts or works. Instead, we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. Through His sacrifice, those who trust in Him are reconciled to God and freed from the curse of sin.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is your understanding of what Jesus accomplished on the cross?
How can we learn to trust God like the prophet Habakkuk (Habakkuk 3:17-19)?

Learning to trust God as the prophet Habakkuk did begins with understanding that his radical joy was not based on his circumstances, but on the unchanging character of God. In Habakkuk 3:17-19, the prophet describes a total collapse of the economy and food supply. Yet, even in the face of such devastation, he makes a conscious decision to rejoice in the Lord.

To develop this kind of trust, we can look at the journey Habakkuk took throughout his short book. He began with honest lament, bringing his confusion and pain directly to God (Habakkuk 1:2-4). Trusting God does not mean we must pretend everything is fine. Instead, it means being honest with Him about our fears while refusing to let those fears have the final word.

Habakkuk also practiced the discipline of waiting and listening. He resolved to stand at his watchpost and wait for God's answer (Habakkuk 2:1). In our own lives, trust is often built in the quiet moments of prayer and the study of Scripture, where we learn to hear God's voice over the noise of our anxieties. We are reminded that the righteous shall live by his faith (Habakkuk 2:4).

Furthermore, Habakkuk looked backward at God's past faithfulness to fuel his future hope. Much of the third chapter is a song recounting how God had rescued His people in the past (Habakkuk 3:3-15). For us as believers today, our greatest "looking back" is toward the cross of Jesus Christ. We see that God is holy and just, yet so merciful that He took the punishment for our sin upon Himself. Because Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death, and rose bodily from the grave, we know that God is for us (Romans 8:31-32). If He has already solved our greatest problem (sin and eternal separation) through the Gospel, we can trust Him to sustain us through temporal trials.

Trusting God like Habakkuk means declaring that God Himself is our strength and our portion, even if everything else is taken away (Habakkuk 3:19). It is a gift of grace to see that while the fig tree may not blossom, the Lord remains the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8).

This journey of trust begins with a personal relationship with the Creator through His Son. You can turn to God now (repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you) for forgiveness and a hope that cannot be shaken by earthly circumstances. What is it in your life right now that makes it difficult to say, "Yet I will rejoice in the Lord"?
Why are we supposed to give thanks in everything (1 Thessalonians 5:18)?

The command to "give thanks in all circumstances; for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you" from 1 Thessalonians 5:18 is one of the most challenging yet transformative instructions in Scripture. It is important to notice that the Bible says to give thanks *in* everything, not necessarily *for* everything. We do not thank God for evil, sin, or the pain they cause, but we give thanks to Him in the midst of those trials because of who He is and what He has done.

We give thanks because it is a direct expression of our trust in God's sovereignty. To give thanks in difficult times is to declare that God is still on His throne and that He is working all things together for the good of those who love Him and are called according to His purpose (Romans 8:28). It acknowledges that our Heavenly Father is holy, wise, and good, even when we cannot see the end of the path we are walking (Psalm 100:4–5; Psalm 145:17).

Practicing gratitude also protects our hearts and minds. When we focus on God's blessings and His character rather than just our problems, it guards us against bitterness and despair. Scripture encourages us not to be anxious about anything, but in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving to let our requests be made known to God, and His peace will guard our hearts (Philippians 4:6–7). Gratitude is a spiritual discipline that reminds us of our total dependence on God’s grace.

Ultimately, our greatest reason for thanksgiving is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Even if every earthly comfort is taken away, the believer possesses a treasure that can never be lost. Because of our sin, we were separated from God and deserved His judgment, but God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son (Ephesians 2:4–5). Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). 

By grace alone, through faith alone in Christ alone, we are forgiven and reconciled to God (Ephesians 2:8–9; Romans 5:1). This reality provides a foundation for joy that is not dependent on our external situation. We give thanks because we have been rescued from darkness and given eternal life in Christ (Colossians 1:12–14).

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness and hope that makes true gratitude possible. 

What is a specific circumstance in your life right now that makes it difficult for you to give thanks?
Why did Jesus refer to the temple as “my Father’s house” (John 2:16)?

Jesus referred to the temple as "my Father's house" primarily to reveal His unique identity as the Son of God and to restore the sanctity of worship. By using this specific phrase, He was asserting His divine authority and His intimate, unique relationship with God the Father. This was not merely a general statement about God being the Father of all people, but a claim to a personal and essential Sonship.

Even as a child, Jesus expressed this deep understanding of His identity when He asked His parents, "Did you not know that I must be in my Father's house?" in Luke 2:49. In the context of John 2:16, Jesus was moved by a righteous zeal because the house intended for prayer and fellowship with God had been turned into a place of commerce and exploitation. His actions fulfilled the words of Psalm 69:9, which says, "For zeal for your house has consumed me."

Furthermore, this statement points toward the greater reality of who Jesus is. While the physical temple was the place where God’s presence dwelt under the Old Covenant, Jesus came as the true Temple. He is the one in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily, as described in Colossians 2:9. He is the ultimate meeting place between God and man.

This reveals the heart of the Gospel. Because God is holy and supreme, and we have all turned away from Him through sin, we are naturally separated from His presence. However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature, live a perfect life, and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death and His authority as Lord. 

Through Christ's work, salvation is offered by grace alone through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most compelling or perhaps challenging about Jesus' claim to be the Son of God?
What does the Bible say about child abuse?

If you or someone you know is in immediate danger, experiencing abuse, or if a child is currently at risk, please contact emergency services (such as 911) or your local law enforcement immediately. You may also contact the Childhelp National Child Abuse Hotline at 1-800-422-4453. As a Christian guide, I must clarify that I do not provide medical, legal, or psychiatric advice, and situations involving the safety of a child require the immediate intervention of qualified professionals and authorities.

The Bible speaks clearly about the value of children and the grave sin of mistreating the vulnerable. Scripture teaches that children are a heritage from the Lord and a reward from Him (Psalm 127:3). Because they are created in the image of God, they deserve protection, care, and dignity.

God expresses deep concern for those who cannot protect themselves. He describes Himself as a father to the fatherless and a protector of widows (Psalm 68:5). The Bible mandates that we seek justice, correct oppression, and bring justice to the fatherless (Isaiah 1:17). Consequently, any form of abuse is a violation of God’s design for the family and a rebellion against His character.

Jesus Christ placed a high priority on the well-being of children. He welcomed them when others tried to push them away, saying, "Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven" (Matthew 19:14). He also issued one of the most sobering warnings in all of Scripture regarding the harm of children: "Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him to have a great millstone fastened around his neck and to be drowned in the depth of the sea" (Matthew 18:6).

While the Bible instructs parents to provide discipline and guidance, it explicitly forbids harshness or provocation that causes harm. Fathers are commanded not to provoke their children to anger, but to bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord (Ephesians 6:4). Parallel to this, Colossians 3:21 warns, "Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged." Biblical discipline is rooted in love and the goal of restoration, which is the opposite of the fear, trauma, and degradation caused by abuse.

Furthermore, God has established civil authorities to maintain order and punish wrongdoing. Romans 13:1-4 explains that the governing authorities are "God's servant for your good" and that they carry the sword to execute God's wrath on the wrongdoer. Child abuse is not only a sin but also a crime that must be reported to the proper legal authorities so that justice may be served and the victim protected.

The reality of abuse is a painful reminder of the brokenness of our world due to sin. Sin separates all of us from a holy God, leading to every kind of evil and suffering. However, God in His holiness and love did not leave us without hope. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. Jesus is now exalted as Lord over all, offering salvation by grace alone through faith alone to all who believe. He provides the only way for true healing, forgiveness, and the restoration of a right relationship with God.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

If you are a victim of abuse, please know that God sees your pain and cares for you (1 Peter 5:7). I encourage you to seek support from a local church, a pastor, or a certified biblical counselor who can walk with you toward healing in a safe environment. What steps can I help you take to find a safe person to talk to today?
What is Christianophobia?

Christianophobia is a term used to describe the irrational fear, hatred, or prejudice directed toward Christians and the Christian faith. It can manifest in many forms, ranging from social marginalization and mockery to systemic discrimination and, in many parts of the world, violent persecution.

From a biblical perspective, the hostility that believers face is not entirely unexpected. Jesus told His disciples in John 15:18-19 that if the world hates them, they should remember that it hated Him first. He explained that because believers are called out of the world, the world may respond with animosity. Similarly, 2 Timothy 3:12 states that everyone who wants to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted.

This opposition often arises because the Gospel makes exclusive claims that can be uncomfortable or confrontational to a world that prefers to define its own morality. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and that all human beings have sinned, falling short of His glory, which creates a separation between us and our Creator as seen in Romans 3:23. 

However, the heart of the Christian message is not one of condemnation, but of reconciliation through Jesus Christ. God, in His great mercy, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, He proved His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or efforts.

When Christians encounter Christianophobia or hostility, the Bible calls for a response characterized by the same love Christ showed. In Matthew 5:44, Jesus instructs His followers to love their enemies and pray for those who persecute them. We are encouraged in 1 Peter 4:12-14 not to be surprised by fiery trials, but to rejoice that we share in the sufferings of Christ, knowing that the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon us.

If you are feeling the weight of this hostility or are curious about why the message of Jesus causes such a strong reaction, I invite you to consider the hope found in Him. You can turn to God today by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. 

What thoughts or questions do you have about how Christians should navigate these challenges?
How can I know that my future sins are forgiven?

The assurance that your future sins are forgiven rests entirely on the finished work of Jesus Christ rather than your own ability to remain perfect. When Christ died on the cross two thousand years ago, all of your sins were in the future. Scripture teaches that His sacrifice was so powerful and perfect that it covered the entirety of a believer's life.

In Hebrews 10:12-14, the Bible says, "But when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God, waiting from that time until his enemies should be made a footstool for his feet. For by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified." This single offering is sufficient for every sin you have committed or will ever commit.

Your standing before God is based on justification, which is a legal declaration by God that you are righteous because of your faith in Christ. Romans 5:1 states, "Therefore, since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." Because this peace is established by God through Christ, it is not something that is turned on and off based on your daily performance. Romans 8:1 further assures us, "There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus."

When you do sin in the future, it does not mean you have lost your salvation or that those sins are not covered. Instead, it means your fellowship with God is hindered, much like a child might upset a parent without ceasing to be their child. 1 John 2:1-2 encourages us by saying, "My little children, I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin. But if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world." Jesus stands as your Advocate, constantly representing His finished work on your behalf before the Father.

This great hope is the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and cannot look upon sin (Habakkuk 1:13). Because all humans have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), we are naturally separated from Him and deserve His just judgment. However, out of His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life of obedience that we could never live. Jesus then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment we deserved upon Himself. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and His identity as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9). Because Christ is the one who holds you, you can trust that He will complete the work He started in you (Philippians 1:6). If you are feeling the weight of your sin or fear for the future, you can turn to God now. Repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for total forgiveness and new life.

What specific fears or doubts make it difficult for you to rest in the promise that Christ's work is finished?
Book of Malachi - Bible Survey

The book of Malachi serves as the final prophetic word of the Old Testament, written approximately 100 years after the Jewish people returned from exile in Babylon. During this time, the initial excitement of rebuilding the temple had faded into spiritual apathy, cynical worship, and moral compromise. Malachi, whose name means "my messenger," was sent by God to confront this lethargy and call the people back to a sincere relationship with their Creator.

The book is uniquely structured around six "disputes" or dialogues between God and His people. In each instance, God makes a charge, the people respond with a skeptical question, and God provides a detailed answer that exposes their hearts.

**1. God’s Love for His People (Malachi 1:2-5)**
God begins by declaring, "I have loved you." The people respond by asking how He has loved them. God points to His sovereign choice of Jacob over Esau as evidence of His covenant faithfulness. This reminds us that God's love is not based on our merit, but on His character and promises.

**2. The Corruption of the Priesthood (Malachi 1:6-2:9)**
The Lord rebukes the priests for offering blind and lame animals as sacrifices, showing contempt for His Name. God demands honor as a Father and a Master. He warns that if they do not take His commandments to heart, He will turn their blessings into curses.

**3. Treachery in Relationships (Malachi 2:10-16)**
The prophet addresses social and marital unfaithfulness. The men of Judah had been divorcing their wives to marry women who worshiped foreign gods. God declares that He "hates divorce" (Malachi 2:16) because it violates the covenant of marriage and the unity He desires for His people.

**4. The Coming Messenger of Judgment (Malachi 2:17-3:5)**
The people grow weary of God's justice, asking where the "God of justice" is. God responds by promising to send His messenger to prepare the way, followed by the Lord Himself coming to His temple. This refers to John the Baptist and the first coming of Jesus Christ, who purifies His people like a refiner's fire.

**5. Robbing God (Malachi 3:6-12)**
God identifies His unchanging nature as the reason Israel has not been consumed (Malachi 3:6). He accuses them of "robbing" Him by withholding tithes and offerings. He invites them to test His faithfulness, promising that if they return to Him in obedience, He will pour out a blessing that there is not room enough to receive.

**6. The Faithful Remnant and the Day of the Lord (Malachi 3:13-4:6)**
The final dispute concerns the perceived futility of serving God. While the wicked seem to prosper, God reassures those who fear Him that a "book of remembrance" is written before Him. The book concludes with a vision of the "Day of the Lord," where the "Sun of Righteousness" will rise with healing in its wings (Malachi 4:2). Malachi ends with a promise that Elijah the prophet will come before that great day to turn the hearts of fathers to their children.

**The Gospel Connection**
Malachi’s message highlights the holiness of God and the consistent failure of humanity to keep His law. This gap between God’s perfection and our sin points directly to our need for a Savior. The New Testament identifies John the Baptist as the promised "Elijah" (Matthew 11:13-14) and Jesus Christ as the Messenger of the Covenant.

Jesus fulfilled the requirements of the Law that we could not keep. He lived a perfect life, took the judgment we deserved through His substitutionary death on the cross, and rose from the grave to defeat sin and death forever. Salvation is not something we earn through religious rituals or moral efforts, but a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you recognize that you have been distant from God or have tried to find meaning apart from Him, I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What is your current understanding of who Jesus is and what He has done for you?
What are phylacteries?

Phylacteries, also known by the Hebrew term *tefillin*, are small black leather boxes containing parchment scrolls with specific verses from the Torah. According to Jewish tradition and law, these boxes are strapped to the forehead and the arm during morning prayers. 

The practice of wearing phylacteries is based on several passages in the Old Testament where God commands His people to bind His words as a sign on their hands and as symbols on their foreheads, as seen in Exodus 13:9; Exodus 13:16; Deuteronomy 6:8; and Deuteronomy 11:18. The scrolls inside the boxes contain these very passages, which remind the wearer of God’s deliverance of Israel from Egypt and the duty to love and obey Him with all one's heart, soul, and strength.

In the New Testament, Jesus mentions phylacteries in Matthew 23:5. In this passage, He critiques certain scribes and Pharisees, noting that "they make their phylacteries wide and their tassels long." Jesus was not condemning the use of phylacteries themselves, but rather the heart behind how some were using them. They were enlarging these religious symbols to appear more pious to others, prioritizing outward show over inward transformation.

As believers, we see in this a reminder that while outward symbols can be helpful, God is most concerned with the state of the heart. The Old Testament pointed toward a day when God would write His law directly on the hearts of His people, a promise fulfilled through the New Covenant in Jesus Christ.

While we may strive to remember God's Word, the reality is that we all fail to keep His commands perfectly. This is why the Gospel is such good news. God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him. However, out of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life of obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice, we are not justified by our outward religious works, but by grace alone through faith in Him.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from placing your trust in Him today?
Who are true worshipers (John 4:23)?

In John 4:23, Jesus describes true worshipers as those who worship the Father in spirit and truth. This statement was made during a conversation with a Samaritan woman to explain that the focus of worship was shifting from a specific physical location, like a mountain or a temple, to the internal condition of the heart and the reality of God’s revelation.

To worship in spirit means that worship is not merely an external ritual or a formal ceremony. Instead, it is an engagement of the human spirit, empowered and directed by the Holy Spirit. Because God is spirit, He seeks a spiritual connection that is sincere and heart-felt, rather than one that is only outward (John 4:24; Philippians 3:3).

To worship in truth means to worship God according to who He truly is, as He has revealed Himself in the Bible. It requires that our worship be grounded in the truth of the Gospel and the person of Jesus Christ, who said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). We cannot worship God according to our own imagination or false ideas; we must respond to the truth of His Word.

The Bible teaches that we are all naturally separated from God's holiness because of our sin (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because God is perfectly just, our sin deserves His judgment. However, because God is also merciful and loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be the Savior of the world. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Through His work, we are offered salvation by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or religious efforts (Ephesians 2:8–9). It is only through this faith that our relationship with God is restored, making us the "true worshipers" that the Father is seeking.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him or worshiping Him in this way today?
Book of Obadiah - Bible Survey

The Book of Obadiah is the shortest book in the Old Testament, yet it carries a powerful message regarding God's justice, His sovereignty over the nations, and His commitment to His covenant people. Obadiah, whose name means servant of the LORD, delivers a focused prophecy against the nation of Edom.

The historical context of the book centers on the long standing rivalry between the descendants of Jacob (Israel) and the descendants of Esau (Edom). Although they were brothers, Edom maintained a history of hostility toward Israel. This prophecy likely arose after the fall of Jerusalem to the Babylonians in 586 B.C. When Judah was being destroyed, the Edomites did not help their kin. Instead, they rejoiced in Judah's misfortune, looted their property, and even captured survivors to hand them over to their enemies (Obadiah 1:10-14).

A major theme in Obadiah is the judgment of pride. The Edomites lived in a mountainous, rocky region and believed their natural defenses made them invincible. God, however, challenged their arrogance, stating that even if they soared like the eagle and set their nest among the stars, He would bring them down (Obadiah 1:3-4). This serves as a pastoral reminder that God is holy and supreme, and He opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble (James 4:6).

Obadiah also speaks of the Day of the LORD. This is a biblical concept referring to a time of God’s decisive intervention in human history to judge wickedness and restore righteousness. Obadiah warns that as Edom has done to others, it will be done to them (Obadiah 1:15). Yet, the book ends with a note of hope for the people of God. It promises that a remnant will be delivered and that the kingdom shall be the LORD’S (Obadiah 1:17; Obadiah 1:21).

As a believer, we see the ultimate fulfillment of these themes in the person and work of Jesus Christ. The judgment that Edom deserved for their sin is a judgment that all of humanity faces because of our own rebellion against a holy God. However, the Gospel tells us that God, in His great mercy, sent His Son. Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is the King who has established the kingdom Obadiah foresaw.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

As you study this book, it is helpful to reflect on God's faithfulness. Even when His people are in exile or suffering, He is working to bring about justice and restoration. If you are going through a season of difficulty or feeling forgotten, remember that God is the sovereign judge who sees every injustice and remains committed to those who trust in Him. I encourage you to read the twenty one verses of Obadiah today and consider how God's justice and mercy intersect in your own life. What holds you back from fully trusting in His sovereignty today?
What does it mean to gain the whole world but lose your soul?

Jesus spoke these profound words in the context of what it truly means to follow Him. In Matthew 16:26, He asks, "For what will it profit a man if he gains the whole world and forfeits his soul? Or what shall a man give in return for his soul?" This warning highlights the tragic trade many people make, choosing temporary earthly satisfaction over eternal life with God.

Gaining the whole world refers to the pursuit of everything this life can offer, such as wealth, fame, power, and physical pleasure. While these things are not always sinful in themselves, they become a snare when they are treated as the ultimate goal of life. The world and everything in it is temporary and passing away (1 John 2:17). To gain the world is to accumulate things that cannot be kept beyond the grave.

Losing or forfeiting the soul refers to the eternal consequence of living apart from God. The soul is the eternal part of a person, and its value is infinite because it was created by God to be in relationship with Him. To lose one's soul is to face eternal separation from God’s presence, which the Bible describes as the ultimate loss. Jesus is teaching that no amount of earthly success can compensate for the spiritual bankruptcy of being unreconciled to our Creator.

This teaching points us to our desperate need for a Savior. God is perfectly holy and supreme, but we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are naturally inclined to seek satisfaction in the world rather than in God. However, God, in His great mercy and love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3,4). He did this to reconcile us to God and to give us a life that truly satisfies, both now and for eternity.

Salvation is not something we can earn by our own works or by trying to balance our good deeds against our bad. It is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8,9). When we trust in Him, we no longer have to fear losing our souls, because He holds us securely.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is it about the things of this world that makes them feel more valuable or more certain to you than the promise of eternal life in Christ?
Why did God send the flood when He knew sin would continue after the flood?

The decision of God to send the Flood was rooted in His perfect holiness and His righteous justice. Scripture describes the world at that time as being so thoroughly corrupted by violence and rebellion that "every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually" (Genesis 6:5). Because God is holy and just, He cannot ignore sin or allow wickedness to destroy His creation without restraint (Habakkuk 1:13). The Flood served as a necessary judgment on the extreme corruption of that specific generation, while also preserving a remnant through Noah to ensure the continuation of the human race and the eventual arrival of the promised Savior.

You correctly observe that sin continued after the waters receded. In fact, God Himself acknowledged this reality immediately after Noah exited the ark, noting that "the intention of man's heart is evil from his youth" (Genesis 8:21). This reveals an important truth: while a global judgment can address the outward fruit of sin, physical water cannot wash away the inward nature of sin. The persistence of evil after the Flood demonstrates that the human problem is not merely environmental or social, but a deep-seated spiritual condition that requires a more profound remedy than physical destruction.

The Flood serves as a historical warning and a shadow of the salvation found only in Jesus Christ (1 Peter 3:20-21). Just as the ark was the only place of safety from the rising waters, Jesus is the only "Ark" of safety from the final judgment of sin. We have all sinned and fallen short of God’s glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all.

Through the Flood, God showed that judgment is certain, but through the Gospel, He shows that mercy is available. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Romans 3:24-25). This is the only way for the human heart to be truly changed and reconciled to a holy God.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Should a church tithe 10% of the offerings it receives?

The Bible does not provide an explicit command requiring a local church, as a corporate entity, to tithe 10 percent of the offerings it receives to other organizations. While the Old Testament established the tithe (meaning a tenth) for the people of Israel to support the Levites and the temple (Leviticus 27:30; Numbers 18:21), the New Testament focus for the church shifts toward the principles of sacrificial and cheerful generosity.

In the New Testament, we see local churches collecting funds not only for their own needs but also to support the spread of the Gospel and to care for believers in other regions. For example, the church in Philippi sent financial support to the Apostle Paul multiple times to assist in his missionary work (Philippians 4:15–16). Similarly, Paul organized a collection among several churches to help the suffering believers in Jerusalem (First Corinthians 16:1–3).

Many churches today choose to set aside a percentage of their budget (often 10 percent or more) for missions, church planting, or local benevolence. They do this not because of a legalistic requirement, but as a way to model the same stewardship and generosity they encourage in their members. The guiding principle for any Christian giving is found in Second Corinthians 9:7, which states that each person (or by extension, each community) should give what they have decided in their heart to give, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.

Ultimately, the resources of a church belong to the Lord, and the elders or leaders are called to be faithful stewards of those funds to fulfill the Great Commission. This generosity is a response to the greatest gift ever given. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy, yet all of humanity has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on a cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering sin and death (First Corinthians 15:3–4).

Salvation is a gift of God’s grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we can perform (Ephesians 2:8–9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

How does the idea of God’s grace in the Gospel change the way you think about giving and stewardship?
Easter Bible reading plan

Easter is the pinnacle of the Christian faith, as it commemorates the historical events of the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. This eight-day reading plan, often called Holy Week, follows the journey of Jesus from His entrance into Jerusalem to His victory over the grave.

**Day 1: Palm Sunday (The Triumphal Entry)**
Read Matthew 21:1-11. Jesus enters Jerusalem as the promised King, fulfilling the prophecy that He would come in humility to save His people.

**Day 2: Monday (Cleansing the Temple)**
Read Mark 11:12-19. Jesus demonstrates His authority and zeal for God's holiness by clearing the temple, calling it a house of prayer for all nations.

**Day 3: Tuesday (Teaching in Jerusalem)**
Read Matthew 22:15-40. Jesus engages with the religious leaders, teaching about the resurrection and the greatest commandment, which is to love God and your neighbor.

**Day 4: Wednesday (The Plot Against Jesus)**
Read Luke 22:1-6. While Jesus continues His ministry, the religious leaders conspire with Judas Iscariot to betray Him, setting the stage for the sacrifice to come.

**Day 5: Maundy Thursday (The Last Supper)**
Read John 13:1-17 and Matthew 26:36-46. Jesus demonstrates the heart of a servant by washing His disciples' feet and then experiences deep agony in the Garden of Gethsemane as He prepares to take on the sins of the world.

**Day 6: Good Friday (The Crucifixion)**
Read John 19:16-30. Jesus is mocked, beaten, and crucified. On the cross, He declares, "It is finished," signifying that the debt for sin has been paid in full through His substitutionary death.

**Day 7: Holy Saturday (The Tomb)**
Read Matthew 27:57-66. Jesus is buried in a tomb, and a large stone is rolled in front of it. This day reminds us of the weight of death and the waiting period before God's ultimate victory.

**Day 8: Easter Sunday (The Resurrection)**
Read Luke 24:1-12. The tomb is empty! Jesus has risen bodily from the dead, proving His deity and His power over sin and the grave.

The heart of this story is the Gospel message. God is perfectly holy, yet we are all sinners separated from Him by our rebellion (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). Because we cannot save ourselves through good works, Jesus Christ, who is both God and man, lived the perfect life we could not live. He died an atoning death on the cross to satisfy God's justice, was buried, and rose again on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now the exalted Lord who offers total forgiveness to all who believe. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As you read these passages, I encourage you to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to reveal the depth of God's love for you. Reflecting on these truths is a way to grow in your relationship with the Lord. You might also consider joining a local church for their Easter services to worship alongside other believers. For further study, reading the entire Gospel of John can provide a beautiful and detailed look at the life and ministry of Jesus.
What is ignosticism? What is an ignostic?

Ignosticism is the philosophical position that the question of God's existence is meaningless because the term "God" has no coherent or unambiguous definition. An ignostic is someone who holds this view, arguing that before one can even discuss whether God exists (as a theist, atheist, or agnostic might), a clear and verifiable definition of what is meant by "God" must first be established.

While an atheist denies the existence of God and an agnostic claims that God's existence is unknown or unknowable, an ignostic maintains that the word "God" itself is cognitively meaningless. They often suggest that different people use the word in so many contradictory ways that it fails to refer to anything specific. For the ignostic, the conversation about God cannot begin because the subject of the conversation has not been properly defined.

From a biblical perspective, the Christian faith acknowledges that while God is infinite and beyond our total comprehension, He is not undefined or unknown. Scripture teaches that God has clearly revealed Himself to humanity through both creation and His Word. In Romans 1:19-20, the Bible states that what can be known about God is plain to everyone because God has shown it through the things He has made, leaving no one with an excuse for ignoring Him.

Furthermore, Christians believe that God provided the ultimate definition of Himself by entering human history in the person of Jesus Christ. John 1:18 explains that no one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is Himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made Him known. In Jesus, the "undefined" becomes personal, relatable, and visible. Hebrews 1:1-3 describes Jesus as the radiance of God's glory and the exact representation of His being.

The Bible presents God not as a vague concept, but as a holy and sovereign Creator who is deeply concerned with His creation (Isaiah 6:3; Colossians 1:16-17). Because God is holy and we are sinful, we are naturally separated from Him (Romans 3:23). However, because of His great love, Jesus Christ (the eternal Son of God) took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His power over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord over all. This salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God right now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. What do you find to be the most difficult part of understanding who God is?
What does it mean that Jesus is the Lord of the Sabbath?

When Jesus declares Himself "Lord of the Sabbath," as recorded in Matthew 12:8, Mark 2:28, and Luke 6:5, He is making a profound statement about His identity, His authority, and the nature of true rest.

First, this is a direct claim to deity. In the beginning, God Himself established the Sabbath as a day of holy rest after the work of creation in Genesis 2:2–3. By claiming to be "Lord" over this day, Jesus is identifying Himself as the one who instituted it and the one to whom the day belongs. He is not merely a teacher interpreting the Law, He is the Lawgiver Himself.

Second, Jesus was correcting the legalism of the religious leaders of His time. The Pharisees had added hundreds of burdensome regulations to the Sabbath, turning a day intended for blessing into a day of heavy labor and anxiety. In Mark 2:27, Jesus explains that "The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath." As Lord of the Sabbath, He has the authority to restore its true purpose, which is to provide mercy, healing, and spiritual restoration for people.

Third, Jesus is the ultimate fulfillment of the Sabbath. The Old Testament Sabbath was a shadow of the things to come, while the substance belongs to Christ as seen in Colossians 2:16–17. The weekly rest was a sign pointing toward the eternal rest that God provides. Today, believers find their ultimate rest not in a specific day of the week, but in the person and finished work of Jesus Christ. As Hebrews 4:9–10 explains, those who have entered God's rest through faith have ceased from their own works to earn God's favor, just as God rested from His.

The reason we need this rest is that we are all separated from our holy and just God by our sin. No amount of religious effort or "sabbath-keeping" can bridge that gap. However, God in His mercy sent Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life of obedience and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His power over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is not something we work for, but a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find the forgiveness and rest your soul needs.

Is there anything in your life right now that makes it difficult for you to trust in Christ's finished work for your salvation?
What does it mean that Jesus Christ conquered death?

The belief that Jesus Christ conquered death is the foundation of the Christian faith. It means that through His physical resurrection from the grave, Jesus broke the power of sin and the finality of death, securing eternal life for all who believe in Him.

To understand this victory, we must first look at the problem of death. Scripture teaches that death is not a natural part of God's original design but is the result of human sin. Romans 6:23 states, "For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." Because every human has sinned, everyone was subject to the power of death and separation from God.

Jesus Christ, who is eternally God, became a man to solve this problem. He lived a perfect, sinless life, fulfilling the requirements of God's holiness. On the cross, He took the punishment that we deserved. His death was a substitutionary sacrifice, meaning He died in our place to satisfy God's justice.

The victory was made manifest three days later. Jesus did not remain in the grave; He rose bodily from the dead, demonstrating His power over the afterlife and proving that His sacrifice was sufficient. In 1 Corinthians 15:54-57, the Bible proclaims, "Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting? The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ."

When we say Jesus conquered death, it has several profound meanings for us today:

First, it means the penalty of sin is paid. For those who are in Christ, death is no longer a judicial punishment from God, but a transition into His presence. Romans 8:1 assures us, "There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus."

Second, it means we have the hope of a future resurrection. Just as Jesus was raised with a glorified, physical body, believers are promised that they too will be raised to life at His return. As Jesus says in John 11:25-26, "I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live, and everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die."

Third, it removes the fear of death. We no longer have to live as slaves to the dread of the unknown. Hebrews 2:14-15 explains that Jesus took on human flesh so that through death He might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.

This victory is a gift offered to you. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. However, because of His great love, Jesus Christ took our place, died for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day. He is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5). Salvation is not something we can earn through good works; it is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?
What are the books of the Bible? What does it mean that the Bible is composed of different books?

The Bible is a collection of 66 distinct books, written by about 40 different authors over a span of approximately 1,500 years. Even with this diversity of human authors, it is one unified story centered on God's redemption of humanity through Jesus Christ.

The Old Testament contains 39 books:
*   The Pentateuch: Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy.
*   Historical Books: Joshua, Judges, Ruth, 1 Samuel, 2 Samuel, 1 Kings, 2 Kings, 1 Chronicles, 2 Chronicles, Ezra, Nehemiah, and Esther.
*   Poetic and Wisdom Books: Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Song of Solomon.
*   Major Prophets: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel.
*   Minor Prophets: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi.

The New Testament contains 27 books:
*   The Gospels: Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John.
*   History: Acts.
*   Pauline Epistles: Romans, 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 Thessalonians, 2 Thessalonians, 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon.
*   General Epistles: Hebrews, James, 1 Peter, 2 Peter, 1 John, 2 John, 3 John, and Jude.
*   Prophecy: Revelation.

The fact that the Bible is composed of different books means that God chose to reveal Himself through various human experiences, cultures, and literary styles. While human authors used their own language and personalities, they were carried along by the Holy Spirit so that what they wrote was exactly what God intended (2 Peter 1:20-21). This is why we call the Bible "inspired," meaning it is "breathed out" by God and serves as our final authority for faith and life (2 Timothy 3:16-17).

The diversity of these books, ranging from history and law to poetry and personal letters, demonstrates how God meets people in every area of life. Despite these different forms, every book contributes to a single, unfolding narrative. This narrative begins with God’s perfect creation, describes the tragedy of human sin and separation from God, and points toward the ultimate solution in Jesus Christ.

At the heart of all these books is a message of great hope. God is holy and perfect, yet humans have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is not earned by our own good works, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the creation mandate / cultural mandate?

The creation mandate, often called the cultural mandate, refers to the divine commission God gave to humanity at the beginning of creation. This command is found in Genesis 1:28, which says, "And God blessed them. And God said to them, 'Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth and subdue it, and have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over every living thing that moves on the earth.'"

This mandate reveals several key truths about our purpose as human beings:

First, it establishes that we are created in the image of God to act as His representatives on earth (Genesis 1:26; Genesis 1:27). As image-bearers, we are called to reflect His character by stewarding the world He made.

Second, it shows that human activity, including work, family, and the development of culture, is a gift from God. In Genesis 2:15, the Bible notes that "The Lord God took the man and put him in the garden of Eden to work it and keep it." This means that things like art, science, agriculture, and technology are not secular distractions, but ways we can honor God by uncovering the potential of the world He created.

Third, it provides a foundation for human dignity. Because every person is called to participate in this mandate, every person possesses inherent value and a significant role in God's world.

While the Fall brought sin into the world, corrupting our work and making our efforts difficult (Genesis 3:17; Genesis 3:18; Genesis 3:19), the mandate remains. However, we cannot fulfill our purpose perfectly on our own. Our sin has separated us from our holy Creator, leading to brokenness in our relationships and our work.

The good news is that Jesus Christ, the perfect image of God (Colossians 1:15), came to reconcile us to the Father. He lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory, He restores our relationship with God and empowers us by His Spirit to live out our calling with new hearts. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works or cultural achievements (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As you look at the world around you, what helps you see your daily work or activities as a way to honor God?
What does it mean that friendship with the world is enmity with God (James 4:4)?

In James 4:4, the apostle James uses strong language to warn believers about where they place their primary loyalty. He writes, "You adulterous people! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? Therefore whoever wishes to be a friend of the world makes himself an enemy of God."

When the Bible speaks of the world in this context, it is not referring to the physical earth or the people in it, whom God loves (John 3:16). Instead, it refers to a fallen system of values, attitudes, and desires that are organized in rebellion against God. It is a mindset that prioritizes self-satisfaction, pride, and earthly power over the will of the Creator (1 John 2:15–16).

To be a friend of this system means to embrace its priorities, seek its approval, and adopt its standards. James calls this spiritual adultery because God desires our wholehearted devotion. For the believer, to pursue the world's ways is to break a covenant with Him. We cannot divide our heart's ultimate loyalty between God's kingdom and a system that rejects Him, for no one can serve two masters (Matthew 6:24).

This verse exposes a deep problem in the human heart. By nature, because of sin, we all start as friends of the world and objects of God's righteous wrath (Ephesians 2:3). Our sin separates us from God and places us in a position of hostility toward His holiness.

However, the beauty of the Gospel is that while we were still enemies, Christ died for us (Romans 5:10). Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life that we could not live. He took the punishment for our rebellion by dying on the cross as our substitute, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is not something we earn by trying to be less worldly through our own effort. Instead, we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now. Repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why did God harden Pharaoh’s heart?

The account of God hardening Pharaoh's heart is a significant demonstration of God's absolute sovereignty, His justice, and His plan to reveal His glory to the world. To understand why this happened, we must look at the different purposes revealed in the biblical text.

First, God hardened Pharaoh's heart to display His supreme power and to ensure that His name would be known throughout the earth. In Exodus 9:16, God tells Pharaoh through Moses, "But for this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth." By allowing Pharaoh to resist, God performed greater signs and wonders that proved He was the only true God, far above the many idols of Egypt. This served as a testimony not only to the Egyptians but also to the Israelites and the surrounding nations for generations to come (Exodus 10:1-2; Exodus 15:14-15).

Second, the hardening of Pharaoh’s heart was a form of divine judgment. It is important to note that the Book of Exodus describes Pharaoh hardening his own heart several times before the text says that God hardened it (Exodus 8:15; Exodus 8:32; Exodus 9:34). Pharaoh was already a proud and oppressive ruler who had enslaved the Israelites and ordered the murder of their children. God did not take a righteous man and make him evil; rather, He gave an unrepentant man over to the stubbornness of his own sinful desires. As the Apostle Paul explains in Romans 9:17-18, God uses such instances to show that He has mercy on whomever He wills, and He hardens whomever He wills, demonstrating that His purposes in history will always be accomplished.

Finally, this narrative highlights the fundamental problem of the human condition. Like Pharaoh, all of humanity has a heart that is naturally inclined toward rebellion against God’s authority. Scripture teaches that we are all separated from a holy God by our sin, deserving of His just wrath. However, God in His great love did not leave us without hope. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. Through His burial and bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus defeated sin and death, offering reconciliation to all who believe.

While Pharaoh responded with pride, God calls you to respond with faith. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How should we treat our enemies according to Proverbs 25:21–22?

In Proverbs 25:21–22, the Bible instructs us to respond to our enemies with active, practical kindness rather than seeking revenge. The passage states that if your enemy is hungry, you should give him bread to eat, and if he is thirsty, you should give him water to drink. This teaching emphasizes that a believer should meet the physical needs of those who oppose them, showing them the same mercy we have received from God.

The passage goes on to say that by doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head, and the Lord will reward you (Proverbs 25:21–22). Many scholars believe the metaphor of burning coals refers to the way unexpected kindness can bring a sense of conviction or a healthy shame to an enemy, potentially softening a hard heart and leading to a change in their behavior. This principle is so central to the heart of God that the apostle Paul quotes it in the New Testament to illustrate how Christians should overcome evil with good (Romans 12:20). Jesus also expanded on this in the Sermon on the Mount, commanding His followers to love their enemies and pray for those who persecute them (Matthew 5:44).

Treating enemies with such grace is only possible when we understand how God has treated us. Though God is perfectly holy and supreme, every human being has sinned and lived in rebellion against Him, making us His spiritual enemies (Romans 3:23; Romans 5:10). Despite our sin, God showed His love for us by sending His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment that our sins deserved. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, confirming His victory over sin and death and His identity as Lord (Romans 5:8; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Salvation and the power to love our enemies are gifts from God. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or moral efforts (Ephesians 2:8–9). When we trust in Christ, the Holy Spirit begins a work of sanctification in us, enabling us to reflect God's character by showing grace even to those who may wish us harm.

You can turn to God now, repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive His forgiveness and a new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about indecision / being indecisive?

The Bible addresses indecision by pointing us toward the character of God, the necessity of faith, and the gift of wisdom. While it is natural to weigh options carefully, Scripture often characterizes chronic indecision as a state of being "double-minded" or lacking trust in God's providential care.

One of the most direct passages on this topic is found in the book of James. The author explains that if we lack wisdom, we should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault. However, he adds a caveat about the nature of our asking: "But let him ask in faith, with no doubting, for the one who doubts is like a wave of the sea that is driven and tossed by the wind. For that person must not suppose that he will receive anything from the Lord; he is a double-minded man, unstable in all his ways" (James 1:6–8). This suggests that indecision often stems from a divided heart that is not fully resting in God's promises.

Indecision can also be rooted in a fear of the future or a desire for perfect certainty before acting. Scripture encourages us to move forward by trusting in God's sovereignty rather than our own limited understanding. Proverbs 3:5–6 instructs us to "Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding. In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths." When we realize that God is in control of the outcomes, the pressure to make a "perfect" choice is relieved, allowing us to act in faith.

Furthermore, the Bible emphasizes the importance of being people of clear conviction and integrity. In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus taught, "Let what you say be simply ‘Yes’ or ‘No’; anything more than this comes from evil" (Matthew 5:37). While this specifically addresses oaths, it reflects a broader biblical principle of decisiveness and honesty in our commitments.

If you find yourself paralyzed by a choice, the Bible offers practical steps to move toward a decision:

1. Seek Wisdom through Prayer: Acknowledge your need for God’s perspective (James 1:5).
2. Consult Godly Counsel: Indecision is often cured by the perspective of others. Proverbs 11:14 notes that "where there is no guidance, a people falls, but in the abundance of counselors there is safety," and Proverbs 15:22 says, "Without counsel plans fail, but with many advisers they succeed."
3. Examine Your Motives: Ask if your indecision is rooted in a desire to please God or a fear of man (Galatians 1:10).

Ultimately, the greatest decision any person faces is what they will do with Jesus Christ. Many people remain indecisive about Him, trying to wait for more information or a more convenient time. However, the Gospel calls for a clear response. We are all separated from a holy God by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that God will wipe away every tear (Revelation 21:4)?

The promise that God will wipe away every tear is one of the most comforting descriptions of the future hope for those who belong to Jesus Christ. Found in Revelation 21:4, this image follows the vision of the new heaven and the new earth, where God makes His dwelling place with His people.

To understand what it means for God to wipe away every tear, we must look at why tears exist in the first place. When sin entered the world through the fall of humanity, it brought death, mourning, and pain (Genesis 3:16-19; Romans 5:12). This verse signifies the total and permanent reversal of the effects of sin. It means that the causes of our sorrow, such as death, sickness, betrayal, and injustice, will be no more.

The act of God "wiping away" tears suggests a deeply personal and tender care. It is not merely that He removes the circumstances that cause crying, but that He Himself comforts His children. This fulfills the longing of the human heart for perfect peace and reconciliation with our Creator.

This hope is grounded in the character and work of God. God is holy, sovereign, and good (Psalm 145:9; Isaiah 6:3). Because of our sin, we are all separated from His holy presence and deserve His judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation and the promise of this tearless eternity are not earned by our own good works or efforts. They are received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). It is only through Christ's finished work that we can look forward to a day when "death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away" (Revelation 21:4).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him with your sorrows and your future?
What is the meaning of “there but for the grace of God go I”?

The phrase "there but for the grace of God go I" is a humble acknowledgment that any person's standing, morality, or circumstances are the result of God's unmerited favor rather than their own inherent goodness or strength. It is traditionally attributed to the 16th century English reformer and martyr John Bradford. While watching criminals being led to their execution, he reportedly remarked, "But for the grace of God, there goes John Bradford."

This expression reflects a deep understanding of two primary biblical truths: the universal presence of sin in the human heart and the sovereign kindness of God in restraining that sin or providing a way of escape. It serves as a reminder that apart from the intervening hand of God, any one of us is capable of the same failures, sins, or tragedies we observe in others. The Apostle Paul expressed a similar sentiment when he wrote, "But by the grace of God I am what I am, and his grace toward me was not in vain" (1 Corinthians 15:10).

From a pastoral perspective, this phrase guards the heart against pride and judgmentalism. When we see someone struggling with addiction, facing the consequences of poor choices, or enduring immense suffering, this mindset reminds us that we are not inherently better or more deserving of peace than they are. Scripture teaches that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). Any stability or righteousness we possess is a gift from the Lord, as "the steps of a man are established by the Lord, when he delights in his way" (Psalm 37:23).

This reality points us directly to the heart of the Gospel. Because God is holy and we are all sinners by nature and by choice, we are separated from Him and unable to save ourselves through our own efforts. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, ascending to heaven as Lord over all. 

Salvation is not something we earn by being "better" than the person next to us; it is a gift received through faith alone. "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast" (Ephesians 2:8–9). 

Recognizing that we are kept only by His grace allows us to look at others with compassion rather than contempt. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can we walk in God’s ways (Psalm 128:1)?

Walking in God’s ways is a beautiful description of a life lived in harmony with our Creator, resulting in the "blessedness" promised in Psalm 128:1. To walk in His ways means to order our daily conduct, decisions, and heart attitudes according to His revealed will.

The foundation of this walk is the "fear of the Lord" (Psalm 128:1; Proverbs 9:10). This is not a cowering terror, but a profound holy awe and a deep reverence for who God is. When we rightly understand His holiness, power, and love, we naturally desire to honor Him with our lives. This reverence creates a heart that is soft toward His leading and hesitant to grieve Him through sin.

To walk in His ways, we must first know what those ways are. This requires a diligent and prayerful study of the Bible, which serves as a lamp to our feet and a light to our path (Psalm 119:105). As we meditate on Scripture, the Holy Spirit renews our minds, helping us to discern what is good, pleasing, and perfect in God's sight (Romans 12:2). We look to the person of Jesus Christ as our ultimate example, for He is "the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). To walk in God’s ways is, ultimately, to follow in the footsteps of Jesus (1 Peter 2:21).

Because of our fallen nature, we cannot walk in God's ways perfectly through our own willpower. We must rely on the Holy Spirit, who dwells within every believer. When we "walk by the Spirit," He empowers us to overcome the desires of the flesh and produces in us fruit such as love, joy, peace, and self-control (Galatians 5:16; Galatians 5:22-23). It is a journey of progressive sanctification, where we daily surrender our will to His.

While Psalm 128 points us toward the path of blessing, we must honestly acknowledge that we have all stepped off that path. The Bible tells us that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). Our failure to walk in His ways separates us from Him and leads to spiritual death. However, God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to bridge that gap. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (Romans 5:8). He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and offering eternal life to all who believe.

Walking in God's ways begins with a single step of faith. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Once we are reconciled to God through faith alone, we are given a new heart and the gift of the Holy Spirit, enabling us to walk in a manner worthy of the Lord (Colossians 1:10).

Is there a specific area of your life where you find it difficult to follow God’s lead?
What does the Bible say about the ice age?

While the Bible does not use the specific term ice age, many Christians and biblical scholars believe the scriptures provide a framework for understanding how such a period fits into human history. The most common perspective among those who hold to a literal historical reading of Genesis is that a singular, major Ice Age occurred as a direct consequence of the global Flood described in Genesis 6 through Genesis 9.

According to this view, the breaking up of the fountains of the great deep mentioned in Genesis 7:11 would have released significant amounts of heat and minerals into the oceans. This would result in warm oceans and high rates of evaporation. Simultaneously, volcanic activity associated with the Flood would have placed vast amounts of ash and aerosols into the atmosphere, reflecting sunlight and cooling the land. These conditions, warm moist air meeting cold land masses, would create the perfect environment for massive snowfall and the rapid buildup of glaciers over several centuries.

The book of Job, which many scholars believe is one of the oldest books in the Bible and set in the generations following the Flood, contains several references to ice and snow that seem to describe a climate more severe than what is seen in that region today. For example, Job 37:10 states, "By the breath of God ice is given, and the broad waters are frozen fast." Additionally, Job 38:29-30 asks, "From whose womb did the ice come forth, and who has given birth to the hoarfrost of heaven? The waters become hard like stone, and the face of the deep is frozen." These passages suggest that Job was familiar with significant freezing events.

The Bible also records God's promise to Noah after the Flood in Genesis 8:22, "While the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night shall not cease." This indicates a restoration of seasons and a stabilizing of the climate, eventually leading to the recession of the ice as the volcanic dust cleared and the oceans cooled.

The study of the earth's past, including the Ice Age, reminds us of the profound impact of the Flood, which was God's holy judgment on human sin. However, even in judgment, God provided a way of salvation through the ark for Noah and his family. This points us to a much greater reality. Just as the ark saved Noah from the waters of judgment, Jesus Christ saves us from the ultimate consequences of our sin.

God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, because of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works.
