Why is the humanity of Jesus important?

The humanity of Jesus is a cornerstone of the Christian faith, for without it, the work of salvation could not have been accomplished. Scripture teaches that Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man, a mystery often called the Incarnation, where the Word became flesh and dwelt among us as seen in John 1:14.

His humanity is vital for several reasons. First, it was necessary for Jesus to be human so that He could represent us as our substitute. Just as sin and death entered the world through one man, Adam, so also righteousness and life had to come through a man, Jesus Christ, according to Romans 5:18-19. Because He took on our nature, He could stand in our place and pay the penalty for our sins. Hebrews 2:17 explains that He had to be made like His brothers in every respect so that He might become a merciful and faithful high priest in the service of God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people.

Second, the humanity of Jesus allows Him to be the perfect mediator between a holy God and sinful humanity. 1 Timothy 2:5 states that there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. Because He is both God and man, He perfectly bridges the gap that sin created, representing God to us and representing us before God.

Third, because Jesus experienced the fullness of human life, including hunger, weariness, and temptation, He is a sympathetic Savior. Hebrews 4:15 tells us that we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin. This gives us great comfort, knowing that when we suffer or face trials, our Lord understands our struggles from personal experience.

Ultimately, the humanity of Jesus is central to the Gospel. Because of our sin, we are separated from a holy God and deserve His just judgment as noted in Romans 3:23 and Romans 6:23. However, God in His great love sent His Son to become a man, live a perfect life of obedience, and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Salvation is not something we earn by our own works, but a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone according to Ephesians 2:8-9.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What do you find most encouraging about the fact that Jesus understands your human experience?
What is the new birth?

The new birth, often called regeneration, is the miraculous and sovereign work of God by which He grants spiritual life to a person who was previously spiritually dead because of sin. Jesus Christ famously spoke of this necessity when He told a religious leader named Nicodemus, "Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God" in John 3:3.

This transformation is not something a person can achieve through their own effort, moral improvement, or religious rituals. It is a work of the Holy Spirit that changes a person's heart and nature. As the Bible describes in Titus 3:5, "he saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy, by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit." Through the new birth, God fulfills His promise to "give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh" in Ezekiel 36:26.

The reason we need this new birth is that every human being is born with a sinful nature, separated from a holy and perfect God. We are unable to save ourselves or even desire God rightly on our own. However, because God is rich in mercy and love, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of obedience, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

The new birth happens when the Holy Spirit enables a person to respond to this Good News with faith. It results in a life that begins to love God, hate sin, and desire to follow Jesus Christ. This is the starting point of a relationship with God that lasts for eternity.

I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life.

What does the Bible say about education?

The Bible views education not merely as the acquisition of information, but as the formation of the whole person in relation to God and His world. From a biblical perspective, all truth is God's truth, and learning is a means by which we come to know our Creator more deeply and serve Him more effectively.

**The Foundation of Knowledge**
The Scriptures teach that the starting point for any true education is a right relationship with God. Proverbs 1:7 states, "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of knowledge; fools despise wisdom and instruction." Without acknowledging God as the source of life and truth, human learning remains incomplete. Furthermore, Colossians 2:3 reminds us that in Christ "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge." Therefore, a biblical education seeks to see every subject, whether science, history, or art, through the lens of God's character and His sovereign rule over creation.

**The Role of Parents and the Home**
God has given parents the primary responsibility for the education and spiritual formation of their children. In Deuteronomy 6:6-7, God commands His people, "And these words that I command you today shall be on your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, and when you walk by the way, and when you lie down, and when you rise." This suggests that education is a constant, relational process that happens throughout the day. Similarly, Ephesians 6:4 instructs fathers to "bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord." While parents may utilize teachers or schools to assist them, the ultimate biblical mandate for a child's upbringing rests with the family.

**Wisdom and Transformation**
The goal of biblical education is the development of wisdom, which is the ability to apply God's truth to the complexities of life. This goes beyond intellectual mastery and moves toward heart transformation. Romans 12:2 says, "Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewal of your mind, that by testing you may discern what is the will of God, what is good and acceptable and perfect." Education should lead a person to love God with all their mind, as Jesus commanded in Matthew 22:37. It equips the believer to do good works and to give a reason for the hope they have in Christ, as noted in 1 Peter 3:15.

**The Greatest Lesson**
While the Bible encourages the pursuit of knowledge and the development of skills, it also warns that knowledge can "puff up" or lead to pride if it is not seasoned with love, as mentioned in 1 Corinthians 8:1. The most important lesson any person can learn is the reality of their need for God. Scripture reveals that we are all separated from God by our sin, but God, in His great holiness and love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is not something we can earn through education, good works, or human effort. It is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is the Edenic covenant?

The Edenic covenant, often referred to by theologians as the Covenant of Works, describes the relationship God established with Adam in the Garden of Eden before the fall of humanity into sin. While the word covenant is not explicitly used in the early chapters of Genesis, many believers recognize the elements of a formal covenantal agreement, including parties, requirements, promises, and penalties. Some find support for this in Hosea 6:7, which speaks of how "like Adam they transgressed the covenant."

In this arrangement, God acted as the sovereign Creator, and Adam acted as the representative head of all humanity. God blessed Adam and Eve, commanding them to be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth, and exercise dominion over creation in Genesis 1:28. The specific requirement of this covenant was perfect and personal obedience, symbolized by the command in Genesis 2:16-17: "And the Lord God commanded the man, saying, 'You may surely eat of every tree of the garden, but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.'"

The promise attached to this obedience was continued life and fellowship with God. However, the penalty for disobedience was death, both physical and spiritual. When Adam and Eve chose to eat the forbidden fruit, they broke this covenant. This act brought sin and death into the world, affecting all of their descendants. As Romans 5:12 explains, "Therefore, just as sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men because all sinned."

Because Adam failed to keep this covenant, all of humanity is born in a state of sin and separation from God's holiness. We cannot save ourselves through our own works or efforts because we have already fallen short of God's perfect standard. This is why the Gospel is such good news. Where the first Adam failed, Jesus Christ, who is called the "last Adam," succeeded in 1 Corinthians 15:45. Jesus lived a life of perfect obedience to God, fulfilling every requirement of the law. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Through Christ, God offers a new way to be reconciled to Him, not based on our own works, but based on the finished work of Jesus. Romans 5:18-19 tells us, "Therefore, as one trespass led to condemnation for all men, so one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all men. For as by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous." Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

What is Wahhabism? What is Wahhabi Islam?

Wahhabism is a conservative reform movement within Sunni Islam that emerged in the 18th century in the Arabian Peninsula. It was founded by a scholar named Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who believed that Islam had been corrupted by religious innovations and practices that he viewed as being contrary to the original teachings of the faith. His primary goal was to return Muslims to what he considered the "pure" Islam practiced by the first three generations of believers.

The central pillar of this movement is an uncompromising emphasis on the absolute oneness of God, known as "tawhid." Because of this focus, followers often call themselves "Muwahhidun," meaning "Unitarians" or "those who uphold the oneness of God." They strongly oppose any practice they perceive as idolatry, such as the veneration of saints, the building of elaborate tombs, or seeking the intercession of any person other than God. Historically, this movement gained significant influence through a political and religious alliance with the House of Saud, which eventually led to the founding of the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

While Wahhabism emphasizes a strict adherence to religious law and a "pure" form of monotheism, the Bible presents a unique perspective on how humanity relates to the one true God. Scripture affirms that God is holy and supreme (Isaiah 6:3), and that He is the only God (Isaiah 45:5). However, the Bible also reveals that because of human sin, we are all separated from this holy God and cannot reach Him through our own religious efforts or strict adherence to laws (Romans 3:23; Galatians 2:16).

The Gospel, or the "good news," is that God reached down to us. He did this through Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14). Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3, 4). Salvation is not earned by our works or our purity, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8, 9). Jesus is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6; Acts 4:12).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life (Romans 10:9). What is the Watchtower Bible and Tract Society?

The Watchtower Bible and Tract Society is the primary legal and administrative organization used by Jehovah’s Witnesses to support their global activities, including the publication of their literature and the management of their religious affairs. While they identify as Christians and use the Bible, their core teachings differ significantly from historic, biblical Christianity on several essential doctrines.

One of the most significant differences lies in the nature of God and the person of Jesus Christ. The Watchtower Society teaches that God is a single person, Jehovah, and denies the doctrine of the Trinity. They believe that Jesus Christ is not God, but rather a created being, specifically identifying him as Michael the Archangel in his prehuman existence. In contrast, the Bible teaches that Jesus is truly God and truly man, as seen in John 1:1; John 8:58; and Colossians 2:9. Biblical Christianity affirms that there is one God who eternally exists in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, according to Matthew 28:19.

The Watchtower Society also emphasizes a system of salvation that relies heavily on a person’s works, door-to-door ministry, and loyalty to the organization. This contrasts with the biblical Gospel, which declares that salvation is a gift from God. The Bible teaches that we are all sinners separated from a holy God by our transgressions (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because God is just and holy, He must punish sin, but because He is loving and merciful, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take that punishment upon Himself.

Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, not by any works or organizational membership we can offer (Ephesians 2:8-9; Titus 3:5).

Another distinction is their use of the New World Translation, a version of the Bible produced by the society that contains specific translation choices designed to support their unique doctrines. For example, they translate John 1:1 as "the Word was a god" rather than "the Word was God," which obscures the deity of Christ that is clear in the original Greek manuscripts.

If you are exploring these topics or feeling a sense of spiritual uncertainty, I encourage you to look directly to the person of Jesus Christ as revealed in the Scriptures. You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and the gift of eternal life.

What has led you to look into the teachings of the Watchtower Society?
What sort of interfaith ministries are appropriate?

When considering interfaith engagement, it is helpful to distinguish between civic cooperation, respectful dialogue, and spiritual union. For the Christian, the goal of any interaction with those of other faiths is to love our neighbors as ourselves (Mark 12:31) while remaining faithful to the exclusive claims of Jesus Christ.

Appropriate interfaith involvement typically falls into two categories. The first is civic or social cooperation. This involves working alongside people of various religious backgrounds to serve the common good of the community, such as operating food pantries, advocating for justice, or providing disaster relief. Scripture encourages us to do good to everyone (Galatians 6:10). These efforts do not require theological agreement, as they are based on common humanity and the "common grace" God gives to all people.

The second category is evangelistic dialogue. This involves building relationships to understand the beliefs of others and to share the hope of the Gospel clearly and respectfully. The Apostle Paul modeled this in Athens when he engaged with the philosophers of his day, acknowledging their religiosity while pointing them to the one true God (Acts 17:16-34). In these settings, Christians must remain "prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you; yet do it with gentleness and respect" (1 Peter 3:15).

However, it is not appropriate for a Christian to participate in interfaith "spiritual union" or joint worship. The Bible warns believers not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers in ways that compromise the truth of the Gospel (2 Corinthians 6:14-18). Because the Christian faith rests on the unique deity and finished work of Jesus Christ, participating in prayers or rituals that treat all gods as equal or all paths as valid contradicts the words of Jesus, who said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). There is salvation in no one else (Acts 4:12).

The core of our message to everyone, regardless of their religious background, is the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, but because of our sin, we are all naturally separated from Him and deserve His judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation cannot be earned through our own religious efforts or good works; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is presuppositional apologetics?

Presuppositional apologetics is a method of defending the Christian faith that focuses on the foundational assumptions, or presuppositions, through which every person interprets reality. Unlike other methods that might start by presenting historical or scientific evidence to a supposedly neutral observer, the presuppositional approach begins with the conviction that no one is truly neutral. Every person approaches the world with a set of core beliefs that govern how they think, reason, and live.

This approach is rooted in the belief that the God of the Bible is the necessary foundation for all knowledge, logic, and morality. Advocates of this view often point to Colossians 2:3, which states that in Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge. They argue that without the Triune God as the starting point, human experience becomes unintelligible. For example, the very laws of logic or the consistency of nature that scientists rely upon can only be consistently explained if a sovereign, rational God created and sustains the universe, as described in Hebrews 1:3.

A key concept in this framework is that all people actually know God exists because He has revealed Himself through creation and the human conscience. However, because of sin, people suppress this truth in unrighteousness, as described in Romans 1:18-21. Therefore, the goal of this type of apologetics is not just to provide more information, but to show that the unbeliever's own worldview is inconsistent and that they are actually borrowing from a Christian foundation (such as the belief in objective morality or the reliability of reason) while denying the God who provides it.

Ultimately, this method seeks to bring every thought captive to the obedience of Christ, acknowledging His supreme authority over all of life, as seen in 2 Corinthians 10:5.

While we discuss how we know truth, it is vital to remember the core truth of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leading to our separation from Him. In His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you think about the idea that God is the necessary foundation for everything we know?
What does the Bible say about chanting? Should a Christian chant?

The Bible does not use the specific word "chanting" in the way many modern or Eastern religions do, but it provides clear principles regarding repetitive prayer, the use of the mind in worship, and the singing of Scripture. Whether a Christian should chant depends largely on how the term is defined and the intent of the heart.

In many contexts, chanting refers to the rhythmic repetition of a word or phrase to enter a different state of consciousness. Jesus specifically cautioned against this kind of "vain repetition" in prayer. He taught that we do not reach God through the sheer volume or repetition of our words, but through a sincere relationship with a Father who already knows what we need. In Matthew 6:7, Jesus says, "And when you pray, do not heap up empty phrases as the Gentiles do, for they think that they will be heard for their many words."

True Christian prayer and worship are meant to involve the engagement of both the spirit and the mind. The Apostle Paul emphasized that our worship should be intelligible and grounded in truth. In 1 Corinthians 14:15, he writes, "I will pray with my spirit, but I will also pray with my mind; I will sing praise with my spirit, but I will also sing with my mind." If chanting is used to bypass the mind or enter a trance-like state, it departs from the biblical model of "reasonable service" described in Romans 12:1.

However, there is a long history in the church of "chanting" Scripture, such as the Psalms. This practice, often called plainsong or Gregorian chant, is essentially a way of singing the Word of God to simple melodies. This can be a beautiful way to fulfill the command found in Colossians 3:16: "Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom, singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, with thankfulness in your hearts to God." When the focus is on the meaning of the biblical text and the glory of God, such rhythmic singing is a form of worship.

Furthermore, biblical meditation often involves a quiet, repetitive speaking of God’s Word to oneself. The Hebrew word for meditate often carries the idea of "muttering" or speaking softly. Joshua 1:8 encourages us to "meditate on it day and night," which means constantly reflecting on and speaking God's truth to align our hearts with His.

Ultimately, we do not gain God’s favor through rituals, vocal techniques, or repetitive formulas. We are reconciled to God through the finished work of Jesus Christ. Because of His perfect life, His death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection, we can approach God with confidence. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not through the performance of religious rituals.

If you find yourself seeking a closer connection to God through rituals or chanting, I encourage you to look to Jesus, who is the only mediator between God and man as stated in 1 Timothy 2:5. You can turn to God right now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and new life.

Does the idea of repetitive prayer feel like a way to reach God, or are you looking for a way to better memorize His Word?
Who is Jesus — Day 4 — Jesus the Prophet

In the Old Testament, God promised His people that He would one day raise up a prophet like Moses from among their own brothers (Deuteronomy 18:15). When Jesus began His ministry, the people began to recognize that He was not just another teacher, but "the Prophet" who was to come into the world (John 6:14).

A prophet is someone who speaks the words of God to the people. Jesus fulfilled this role perfectly because He is the Word of God made flesh (John 1:14). While the prophets of old said, "Thus says the Lord," Jesus often spoke with His own divine authority, saying, "Truly, truly, I say to you" (John 3:3). In the past, God spoke through many prophets, but in these last days, He has spoken to us by His Son (Hebrews 1:1-2).

As a prophet, Jesus’ message was direct and life-changing. He preached, "The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel" (Mark 1:15). He spoke the truth about God's holiness and the reality of human sin, which separates us from our Creator. Yet, Jesus did not merely point to the truth; He is the Way, the Truth, and the Life (John 14:6).

The message Jesus proclaimed as a prophet is the very Gospel that saves us. Because God is holy and we have all sinned, we deserve His just judgment. However, out of His great love, God sent Jesus to live a perfect life on our behalf. Jesus then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His exaltation as Lord of all. This salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we do.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Will there be a second chance for salvation after death?

The Bible teaches that our opportunities to respond to God's grace and receive salvation occur during our earthly lives. Scripture indicates that death marks the end of the time for decision, followed by God's righteous judgment.

In the book of Hebrews, the author writes that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment (Hebrews 9:27). This suggests a finality to the human experience on earth, where our standing before God is determined by our response to Him while we are living.

Jesus also provided a vivid illustration of this reality in the account of the rich man and Lazarus. In that passage, Jesus describes a great chasm that has been fixed between those in the presence of God and those separated from Him, such that none can cross from one side to the other (Luke 16:26). Because our time is limited and the moment of our death is unknown, the Bible emphasizes the urgency of the present moment. The apostle Paul writes that now is the favorable time, and now is the day of salvation (2 Corinthians 6:2).

The Gospel is the good news that although God is perfectly holy and we have all separated ourselves from Him through sin, He has provided a way for us to be reconciled. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works or merits of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is biblical homemaking?

Biblical homemaking is the intentional stewardship of the domestic sphere for the glory of God, the nurturing of the family, and the service of others. Rather than being defined merely by a list of chores or a specific personality type, it is a spiritual calling that recognizes the home as a primary place for ministry and discipleship.

At its heart, biblical homemaking involves creating an environment where the Gospel can be lived out and shared. This is seen in several key areas of Scripture:

1. Stewardship and Diligence: The description of the excellent wife in Proverbs 31:10-31 shows a woman who manages her household with wisdom, industry, and foresight. She is not merely occupied with tasks but is invested in the well-being of those under her care, ensuring the household functions in a way that honors God.

2. Loving and Nurturing Relationships: In Titus 2:3-5, the Apostle Paul encourages women to be "workers at home" and to love their husbands and children. This highlights that the ultimate goal of homemaking is the cultivation of godly relationships and the preservation of a peaceful, Christ-centered family life.

3. Spiritual Instruction: The home is meant to be a center for learning about God. Deuteronomy 6:6-9 commands believers to teach God's Word to their children diligently throughout the day. Homemaking provides the context for this constant, life-on-life discipleship.

4. Hospitality and Service: Romans 12:13 and Hebrews 13:2 emphasize the importance of practicing hospitality. A biblical home is not a fortress, but a place of welcome where the love of Christ is extended to neighbors, strangers, and the body of Christ.

While these principles provide a framework for the home, it is important to remember that our ability to build a godly home is rooted in our relationship with the Lord. Scripture teaches that God is holy and supreme, yet we are separated from Him because of our sin. No amount of earthly diligence or "good works" in the home can bridge that gap. However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

The home we build on earth is ultimately a reflection of the eternal home Christ is preparing for those who love Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today or seeking His grace in your daily work at home?
How should Christians view statues of Jesus?

The question of how to view statues or visual representations of Jesus is one that Christians have discussed and occasionally disagreed upon for centuries. The perspective often depends on how a believer understands the balance between the biblical warnings against idolatry and the reality of Jesus becoming a physical man.

The primary concern regarding statues comes from the Second Commandment, where God instructs His people not to make any carved image or likeness of anything in heaven or on earth for the purpose of bowing down to or serving them, as seen in Exodus 20:4-5. Many Christians, particularly within the Reformed tradition, believe that because Jesus is fully God, any attempt to represent Him in stone or wood is an attempt to limit His divine glory to a human creation. They argue that God is Spirit and must be worshipped in spirit and truth, as stated in John 4:24. From this view, statues can inadvertently become objects of misplaced devotion or lead to a distorted understanding of Christ’s majesty.

On the other hand, many other Christians throughout history have pointed to the Incarnation as a turning point. They argue that when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, as described in John 1:14, God made Himself visible to human eyes. Since Jesus is the image of the invisible God, according to Colossians 1:15, some traditions use statues and icons as teaching tools or aids to help the mind focus on the historical reality of Christ’s life and sacrifice. In this context, the statue is not the object of worship itself but a pointer toward the living Savior.

Regardless of where one falls on the use of religious art, the Bible is clear that we must guard our hearts against idolatry. An idol is anything that takes the place of God in our affections or trust. While a statue may be intended as a reminder, it can never fully capture the holiness, power, and love of the Lord Jesus Christ. Our primary "image" of Christ should be drawn from the Holy Scriptures, which are the inspired and sufficient Word of God, as noted in 2 Timothy 3:16-17.

The most important thing for any person is not the image they see with their physical eyes, but their relationship with the living Christ. The Bible teaches that we are all separated from God by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to bridge that gap. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or religious rituals we perform, according to Ephesians 2:8-9. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ today, for He is the only one who can provide true forgiveness and eternal life.

How does understanding Jesus as the living, risen Lord change the way you think about representing Him today?
I am in the process of getting a divorce. Can I start dating, or do I have to wait until the divorce is final?

Going through a divorce is a deeply painful and complex season of life. In these moments, it is natural to long for companionship and a fresh start, but the Scriptures provide wisdom on how to navigate this time with integrity and spiritual health.

From a biblical perspective, marriage is a sacred covenant that remains in effect until it is legally and scripturally dissolved. As long as you are still legally married, entering into a new romantic relationship is considered a violation of the marriage bond. Jesus taught that marriage is a union that God Himself has joined together (Matthew 19:4-6), and the apostle Paul noted that a person is bound by law to their spouse as long as that spouse lives (Romans 7:2-3). Therefore, dating while still in the process of divorce is essentially engaging in a relationship outside of your current marriage, which the Bible describes as adultery (Matthew 5:32; Luke 16:18).

Beyond the legal and scriptural boundaries, there are several pastoral reasons to wait. This season of your life is intended for healing, reflection, and seeking the Lord's direction. Entering a new relationship too quickly can mask the pain of the divorce rather than allowing God to heal the underlying wounds. It also complicates the legal process and can be confusing or hurtful to children and family members involved.

Your priority right now should be your relationship with Jesus Christ and your involvement in your local church. This is a time to lean on the grace of God, who is "the Father of mercies and God of all comfort" (2 Corinthians 1:3). Even in the brokenness of a failing marriage, God is holy and just, yet He is also merciful to those who come to Him.

We all fall short of God's perfect standard, and the pain of divorce reminds us of the brokenness of our world and our own hearts. The beauty of the Gospel is that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day to defeat death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not by our own efforts or moral standing. 

You can turn to God now, repent of any sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and a new life.

I would strongly encourage you to seek the guidance of your local church elders or a trusted pastor. They can provide personal oversight, help you discern the biblical grounds of your situation, and offer the support you need as you walk through this transition. If you are feeling overwhelmed, remember that the Lord is near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18). What do you feel is the greatest challenge you are facing in waiting until the divorce is final?
What is the importance of the empty tomb?

The empty tomb is the physical evidence of the greatest turning point in human history, the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. It serves as the indispensable foundation of the Christian faith because, as the Apostle Paul wrote, if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is useless and so is our faith (1 Corinthians 15:14).

First, the empty tomb provides divine vindication of Jesus Christ's identity. During His earthly ministry, Jesus repeatedly claimed to be the Son of God and predicted His own death and resurrection. The fact that the tomb could not hold Him proves that He is exactly who He claimed to be (Romans 1:4). If the tomb were occupied, Jesus would be seen as a failed teacher or a martyr, but because it is empty, He is revealed as the sovereign Lord over life and death.

Second, the empty tomb signifies victory over sin and death. The Bible teaches that the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23), and death entered the world through human rebellion. By rising from the grave, Jesus demonstrated that the power of sin has been broken and the "last enemy," which is death, has been defeated (1 Corinthians 15:26; 1 Corinthians 15:54–57). This gives every believer the assurance that death is not the end, but a doorway into the presence of God.

Third, the empty tomb is the guarantee of our justification. In Romans 4:25, Scripture says that Jesus was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification. His resurrection was God the Father's "amen" to the work of Christ on the cross. It proves that the sacrifice for our sins was fully accepted and that the debt we owed to God has been paid in full. Because He lives, those who trust in Him are declared righteous in God’s sight.

Finally, the empty tomb offers a living hope for our own future resurrection. Jesus is described as the "firstfruits" of those who have fallen asleep (1 Corinthians 15:20–23). Just as God raised Jesus from the dead, He will also raise those who belong to Christ when He returns to make all things new. This hope sustains Christians through suffering and grief, knowing that our physical bodies will one day be redeemed and glorified (Philippians 3:20–21).

The message of the empty tomb is central to the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment. However, out of His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise again on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is traducianism?

Traducianism is the theological view that the human soul, as well as the body, is transmitted from parents to their children through the natural process of procreation. The term is derived from the Latin word *tradux*, which means a vine shoot or sprout, suggesting that the soul of a child is a "branch" or "offshoot" from the souls of the parents.

In this perspective, when God created Adam and breathed into him the breath of life, as seen in Genesis 2:7, He endowed humanity with the capacity to reproduce the entire human person, both material and immaterial. Those who hold to traducianism often cite Genesis 1:28, where God commanded humanity to be fruitful and multiply, and Genesis 5:3, which notes that Adam fathered a son in his own likeness and after his image. They argue that because God rested from His work of creation on the seventh day, as stated in Genesis 2:2; Genesis 2:3, He does not perform a new act of creation for every individual soul at conception.

A significant reason many theologians favor traducianism is that it offers a clear explanation for the transmission of original sin. If the soul is derived from the parents, it is easier to understand how a fallen, sinful nature is passed down from Adam to all his descendants. This aligns with the biblical teaching that we are conceived in sin, as described in Psalm 51:5, and that through one man, sin entered the world, as noted in Romans 5:12. Another passage used to support this continuity is Hebrews 7:9-10, which describes Levi as being "in the loins" of his ancestor Abraham.

While the specific origin of the soul is a topic where sincere believers may differ, the Bible is clear about our shared human condition. We are all born with a sinful nature that separates us from a holy and just God. Because of this, we cannot save ourselves through our own good works or intentions.

The good news is that God provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus, who is fully God and fully man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died on the cross as a sacrifice for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death. Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, who is our only mediator. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
I am a Buddhist, why should I consider becoming a Christian?

It is a privilege to share the hope of the Christian faith with you. While Buddhism and Christianity both recognize that the world is filled with suffering and that human life involves a search for peace, the Christian faith offers a unique perspective on who we are, why we are here, and how we find lasting liberation.

In Christianity, you are invited to know a personal Creator who is not an impersonal force, but a loving Father. This God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just, yet He is also gracious and merciful. Scripture teaches that every person is created in the image of God (Genesis 1:26, 27) and possesses immense value. However, we are separated from God by sin, which is our tendency to go our own way rather than following His perfect will (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23).

The primary reason to consider Christianity is the person of Jesus Christ. In many traditions, liberation depends on one's own efforts, discipline, or the accumulation of merit. Christianity teaches that we cannot bridge the gap between ourselves and a holy God through our own works. Because God is loving, He did for us what we could never do for ourselves. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a perfect life, and died a sacrificial death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (John 1:1, 14; 1 Peter 2:24).

His bodily resurrection from the dead three days later demonstrated His victory over sin and death, offering us the hope of eternal life (1 Corinthians 15:3, 4). This means that salvation is not a reward for the righteous, but a gift for the repentant. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8, 9). Instead of the endless cycle of trying to earn peace, Christianity offers the "peace of God, which surpasses all understanding" (Philippians 4:7), rooted in the finished work of Jesus.

In Christ, suffering is not something to merely be escaped or extinguished; it is something God Himself entered into so that He might redeem us and one day wipe away every tear (Revelation 21:4). Christianity promises that you can be fully known and fully loved by your Creator, finding rest from the burden of self-effort.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

What are your thoughts on the idea of receiving salvation as a free gift rather than earning it through personal effort?
How should a Christian view the intellect?

A Christian should view the intellect as a beautiful, God-given gift intended for His glory, while also recognizing its limitations and the need for its redemption. Because we are created in the image of God (Genesis 1:26-27), our ability to reason, analyze, and learn reflects the nature of our Creator, who is the source of all wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3).

However, it is essential to understand that the human intellect is not neutral or perfect. Because of the Fall, every part of the human person, including the mind, has been affected by sin. This is often called the "noetic effects of sin," where our thinking can become darkened or futile when we are separated from God (Romans 1:21; Ephesians 4:17-18). We cannot think our way to God on our own, nor can human logic serve as the final judge of God's truth. Instead, the intellect must be submitted to the Lordship of Christ and the authority of His Word.

The Scriptures teach us that loving God involves the whole person, including the mind. When Jesus was asked about the greatest commandment, He said, "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind" (Matthew 22:37). This means that intellectual pursuit, when done in a spirit of worship, is a way to honor God. Whether we are studying science, history, or theology, we are exploring the world God made and the truths He has revealed.

The Christian life involves a continual renewal of the mind. Romans 12:2 instructs believers, "Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewal of your mind, that by testing you may discern what is the will of God, what is good and acceptable and perfect." This renewal happens as the Holy Spirit works through the Word of God to align our thoughts with His truth. We are called to "take every thought captive to obey Christ" (2 Corinthians 10:5), ensuring that our reasoning serves our faith rather than undermining it.

While the intellect is vital, it is not the means of our salvation. No amount of human brilliance can bridge the gap between a holy God and sinful humanity. We are all separated from God by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own intellectual achievements or works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider the role of the mind in your walk with God, do you find it easy or difficult to submit your reason to the truths of Scripture?
What does the Bible say about being zealous / having zeal?

The Bible describes zeal as a fervent, burning desire or passion, most often directed toward God and His purposes. The term often carries the imagery of "heat" or "boiling," suggesting a spirit that is active and intense rather than lukewarm or indifferent.

Scripture speaks of zeal in several ways, beginning with the character of God Himself. God is described as having a righteous zeal for His own glory, for His people, and for justice. In Isaiah 9:7, the prophet speaks of the coming Messiah and His eternal kingdom, declaring that "The zeal of the Lord of hosts will perform this." This reminds us that our hope is not built on human effort, but on God’s passionate commitment to fulfill His promises.

For the believer, zeal is a vital part of the Christian life. In Romans 12:11, the Apostle Paul instructs Christians to "not be slothful in zeal, be fervent in spirit, serve the Lord." This fervency is a response to the mercy God has shown us. Furthermore, Titus 2:14 explains that Jesus Christ "gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness and to purify for himself a people for his own possession who are zealous for good works." True biblical zeal is not merely an emotion, it is a devotion that leads to active service and obedience to God.

However, the Bible also warns that zeal must be grounded in the truth of God's Word. Paul speaks of his fellow Israelites in Romans 10:2, saying, "For I bear them witness that they have a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge." Before his conversion, Paul himself was extremely zealous for the traditions of his fathers, yet that zeal led him to persecute the church (Philippians 3:6; Galatians 1:14). Zeal without the knowledge of the Gospel can lead to self-righteousness or legalism.

If we find our zeal fading, Jesus offers a correction. To the church in Laodicea, which had become lukewarm, He said, "Those whom I love, I reprove and discipline, so be zealous and repent" (Revelation 3:19). The remedy for a cold heart is to turn back to the Lord in repentance and rediscover the fire of His love.

This enduring passion for God is only possible because of what Jesus Christ has done. While we were separated from God by our sin, falling short of His perfect holiness, God sent His Son. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or even the intensity of our own zeal.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the grace of God?

The grace of God is His unmerited favor toward those who deserve His judgment. It is often described as God giving us what we do not deserve, whereas mercy is God not giving us the punishment we do deserve. This grace is rooted in the character of God, who is sovereign, holy, and abundant in lovingkindness (Exodus 34:6).

In the Bible, grace is most clearly revealed through the person and work of Jesus Christ. Because God is perfectly holy and all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), we are naturally separated from Him and unable to save ourselves. However, God, being rich in mercy, acted on our behalf. Salvation is a gift of grace that cannot be earned through our own moral efforts, good works, or religious rituals (Ephesians 2:8-9; Titus 3:4-5).

The Gospel is the supreme expression of this grace. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a life of perfect obedience. He then suffered and died on a cross, taking the penalty for our sins as a substitutionary sacrifice. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His lordship over all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His death and resurrection, Christ secured reconciliation for all who believe.

Grace does not only provide the way for us to be justified before God, it also sustains the believer in daily life. It is the grace of God that teaches us to say no to ungodliness and empowers us to grow in holiness (Titus 2:11-12). It is also the strength that sustains us in our weakness and trials (2 Corinthians 12:9). This grace is a constant reminder that our standing with God is based on the finished work of Christ alone, not on our performance.

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How young can you be to ask Jesus to be your Savior? Can a 3 or 4 year old truly believe?

The Bible does not provide a specific age at which a person becomes capable of faith. Instead, Scripture emphasizes that God is the one who opens hearts to believe, regardless of age. Jesus Himself displayed great tenderness toward children, saying, "Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven" (Matthew 19:14). He even taught that adults must become like children, possessing a simple and humble trust, to enter the kingdom of God (Mark 10:15).

A child of 3 or 4 can certainly have a genuine, albeit simple, understanding of the Gospel. While they may not grasp complex theological nuances, they can understand that they have done wrong things (sin), that God is holy and good, and that Jesus died on the cross to take the punishment for their sins and rose again. If a child expresses a desire to trust in Jesus, it is a cause for joy. We should encourage their faith while continuing to disciple them as their intellectual and emotional maturity grows.

The message of the Gospel is clear for people of all ages:
1. God is holy and perfect (Habakkuk 1:13).
2. All people have sinned and are separated from God (Romans 3:23).
3. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect, sinless life (John 1:14; Hebrews 4:15).
4. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins (1 Peter 2:24).
5. He was buried in a tomb.
6. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).
7. He is now exalted as Lord over all (Acts 2:33).
8. Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we do (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What has led you to think about the spiritual lives of very young children today?
Is it wrong for a Christian husband and wife to attend separate churches?

The question of whether a husband and wife should attend separate churches is one that touches on the heart of biblical marriage and the nature of the local church. While the Bible does not contain a specific command stating that a couple must always sit in the same building on Sunday mornings, it provides clear principles regarding the unity of marriage and the role of the church that suggest attending separate congregations is generally not the healthiest path for a Christian home.

Marriage is described in Scripture as a "one flesh" union. This is first established in Genesis 2:24 and later reaffirmed by Jesus in Matthew 19:6, where He says, "So they are no longer two but one flesh. What therefore God has joined together, let not man separate." This unity is not merely physical or legal, it is profoundly spiritual. Since the local church is the primary place where believers receive spiritual nourishment, exercise their spiritual gifts, and find community, attending separate churches can create a spiritual "two-ness" in a relationship meant to reflect "one-ness."

There are several biblical principles to consider when navigating this situation:

The Goal of Spiritual Unity
In Ephesians 5:22–33, the apostle Paul describes marriage as a living picture of the relationship between Christ and His Church. Just as Christ is not divided from His body, a husband and wife are called to walk in a shared spiritual direction. When a couple attends different churches, they are sitting under different teaching, serving with different groups of people, and often following different visions for ministry. This can make it difficult to "be of the same mind, having the same love, being in full accord and of one mind," as encouraged in Philippians 2:2.

The Role of Leadership and Submission
Scripture calls the husband to be the spiritual head of the home, loving his wife as Christ loved the church (Ephesians 5:23–25). Part of this leadership involves ensuring the family is integrated into a healthy, Bible-believing local body. If a couple cannot agree on a church, it often reveals deeper issues of communication or a lack of shared theological conviction. In such cases, the couple should prayerfully seek a congregation where both can grow, even if it requires one or both to sacrifice personal preferences for the sake of unity.

The Impact on the Family
If there are children in the home, attending separate churches can be particularly confusing. The church is meant to be a secondary family that supports the parents in raising children in the "discipline and instruction of the Lord," as noted in Ephesians 6:4. When parents are divided in their church attendance, it can undermine the child's understanding of the importance of the local church and the unity of the faith.

Practical Next Steps
If you find yourselves in this position, I encourage you to consider the following steps:

1. Pray together. Ask God to give you a shared desire for a single church home and the humility to put the other's needs above your own (Philippians 2:3–4).
2. Identify the core issues. Is the disagreement over essential doctrines, or is it about secondary matters like music style or programming? If it is about essential doctrines, you must search the Scriptures together to find the truth (Acts 17:11).
3. Seek godly counsel. Speak with a trusted pastor or elder who can help you navigate these differences with wisdom and charity.
4. Prioritize unity over preference. Sometimes, the most Christ-like action is to join a church that may not be your first choice for the sake of worshiping alongside your spouse.

Ultimately, the goal of our lives and our marriages is to bring glory to God by reflecting the Gospel. The Gospel is the good news that although we were separated from God by our sin, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise again on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Through faith in Him, we are reconciled to God and joined to His body, the Church.

If you have not yet placed your trust in Christ, I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. Knowing His love and forgiveness provides the strongest foundation for a unified and flourishing marriage. What is it that makes it difficult for you to seek this kind of spiritual unity together?
What is the Druze religion?

The Druze religion, known by its followers as al-Muwahhidun (the Monotheists), is a unique ethno-religious faith that emerged in the 11th century as a branch of Isma’ili Shia Islam. While it has roots in Islamic thought, it is a distinct system that incorporates elements of Neoplatonism, Gnosticism, and various other philosophical traditions. Because of its history of persecution, it became a closed and secretive community, neither accepting converts nor allowing its members to leave the faith for another.

The Druze believe in one God who is transcendent and ultimately unknowable, yet they believe He has manifested Himself in human form, specifically in the Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah. This stands in sharp contrast to the biblical teaching that Jesus Christ is the unique and eternal Word of God who became flesh (John 1:1; John 1:14).

A central pillar of Druze belief is the transmigration of souls, or reincarnation. They believe that at the moment of death, a soul is immediately reborn into a new body. This process continues until the soul reaches a state of perfection and union with the Creator. However, the Bible teaches that every person is created as a unique image bearer of God and that human life follows a different path. Scripture says, "And just as it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment" (Hebrews 9:27).

The community is divided into two groups: the *uqqal* (the wise or initiated) and the *juhhal* (the ignorant or uninitiated). Only the initiated are permitted to read their sacred scriptures, the Epistles of Wisdom, and participate in their private religious services. In contrast, the Gospel of Jesus Christ is a public and universal message intended for all people, regardless of their status or intellect. Jesus told His followers to "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations" (Matthew 28:19).

As we look at these different beliefs, we see a fundamental difference in how we find peace with God. In many systems, including the Druze faith, spiritual progress often depends on a person’s own journey or merit over many lifetimes. The Christian faith offers a different hope grounded in the character of God. God is perfectly holy and just, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him. No amount of time or effort on our part can bridge that gap.

Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is not something we earn through cycles of rebirth, but a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most challenging or interesting about the idea that salvation is a gift from God rather than something earned through effort?
What is an invocation prayer?

An invocation prayer is a prayer offered at the beginning of a religious service, ceremony, or formal gathering. The term comes from the Latin word *invocare*, which means "to call upon." In a Christian context, the purpose of an invocation is to intentionally call upon the name of God, acknowledging His presence and asking for His guidance, blessing, and leadership over the event.

While God is omnipresent, meaning He is everywhere at all times (Psalm 139:7; Psalm 139:8; Psalm 139:9; Psalm 139:10), an invocation is a conscious act of the heart to welcome Him into a specific moment. It serves as a humble admission that we are dependent on Him for everything we do. Jesus taught that when two or three are gathered in His name, He is there among them (Matthew 18:20). An invocation lays hold of this promise, asking the Holy Spirit to work in the hearts of those present.

Typically, an invocation includes several elements:
1. Adoration: Acknowledging God’s character, holiness, and sovereignty.
2. Invitation: Asking the Lord to be manifest in the gathering and to lead the thoughts and words of the participants.
3. Petition: Requesting that the proceedings would honor God and be fruitful for the benefit of the people.

This practice reminds believers that they do not gather for their own sake, but for the glory of God. It sets a spiritual tone of reverence and focus. The ability to call upon a holy God in this way is only possible because of the work of Jesus Christ. Because of human sin, we were separated from God, but Jesus, the Son of God, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Through faith in Christ alone, we are justified and given access to the Father. You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. If you have placed your trust in Him, you can approach God’s throne with confidence, knowing He hears you (Hebrews 4:16).

If you are interested in learning how to pray more effectively, a wonderful place to start is by reading the book of Psalms or the Lord's Prayer in the Gospel of Matthew (Matthew 6:9; Matthew 6:10; Matthew 6:11; Matthew 6:12; Matthew 6:13). These provide a biblical pattern for how we can rightly call upon the Lord.
What is the spiritual gift of prophecy?

In the context of the New Testament, the spiritual gift of prophecy is generally understood as the Spirit-empowered ability to communicate a message from God to a specific people at a specific time for the purposes of "upbuilding and encouragement and consolation" as described in 1 Corinthians 14:3. While the term is used in various ways throughout the Bible, its primary function in the church is to point people toward God's truth and strengthen their faith.

In the Old Testament, prophets often received direct, infallible revelation from God to call the nation of Israel to repentance or to foretell future events (Hebrews 1:1). In the New Testament, the gift is often associated with the foundation of the early church (Ephesians 2:20). There are two primary perspectives within the Christian tradition regarding how this gift functions today:

The first view, often called cessationism, holds that the miraculous sign gifts, including prophecy, served to authenticate the message of the apostles and were no longer necessary once the foundation of the church was laid and the New Testament was completed. For those who hold this view, the "prophetic" function today is fulfilled through the bold, accurate preaching and application of the written Word of God, which is our final and sufficient authority for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16-17).

The second view, often called continuationism, suggests that the gift of prophecy continues to operate in the church today. Proponents of this view typically distinguish modern prophecy from the infallible authority of the Bible. They see it as a spontaneous impression or word prompted by the Holy Spirit that must be carefully tested against the Scriptures (1 Thessalonians 5:19-21; 1 Corinthians 14:29).

Regardless of these interpretations, the ultimate purpose of prophecy is to testify to the person and work of Jesus Christ. As Revelation 19:10 states, "the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy." All spiritual gifts are given by the Holy Spirit to glorify Christ and serve the body of believers.

The heart of all God's communication to us is the Gospel, the good news that although we are separated from a holy God by our sin, He has provided a way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He has ascended to heaven and is Lord over all. We are justified before God by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about purification?

The Bible teaches that purification is necessary because God is perfectly holy (Habakkuk 1:13), while humanity is stained by sin and separated from Him (Isaiah 59:2). Throughout Scripture, purification is presented in two primary ways: outward ritual and inward spiritual transformation.

In the Old Testament, God established specific laws for ceremonial purification (Leviticus 11:1 through Leviticus 15:33). These involved washings with water, the offering of sacrifices, and the use of blood to cleanse people from ritual defilement or "uncleanness." These laws were not merely about physical hygiene, but were intended to teach the Israelites that God is set apart and that approaching Him requires purity. However, the prophets often reminded the people that external rituals were insufficient if the heart remained hard (Isaiah 1:16; Ezekiel 36:25). King David recognized this when he prayed, "Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean; wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow" (Psalm 51:7).

The New Testament reveals that these Old Testament rituals were shadows pointing toward Jesus Christ (Hebrews 10:1). Jesus did not just observe purification laws; He fulfilled them. By touching lepers and healing the sick, He showed that His holiness was powerful enough to make the unclean clean. The author of Hebrews explains that if the blood of animals could provide a temporary, outward cleansing, "how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God?" (Hebrews 9:13-14).

The core message of the Bible is that we cannot purify our own souls through moral effort or religious rituals. Because God is holy and we are sinful (Romans 3:23), we are in need of a Savior. In His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. You can be purified from all sin and justified before God not by your own works, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

For the believer, purification is also an ongoing process called sanctification. While we are legally declared clean the moment we trust in Christ (1 Corinthians 6:11), we are also called to daily put to death sinful habits and be renewed in our minds (2 Corinthians 7:1; Romans 12:2). This happens as we remain in God's Word, which has a purifying effect on our lives (John 15:3; Ephesians 5:26). The apostle John encourages us that as we look forward to Christ's return, this hope motivates us to pursue purity (1 John 3:3). If you are struggling with a sense of guilt, remember that "if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9).
Does the Bible say that an increase in technology is a sign of the end times?

The Bible does not use the word technology, but it does contain passages that many believers interpret as referring to a significant increase in human knowledge and global connectivity during the period leading up to the return of Jesus Christ.

The most prominent reference is found in the book of Daniel, where an angel tells the prophet, "But you, Daniel, roll up and seal the words of the scroll until the time of the end. Many will go here and there to increase knowledge" (Daniel 12:4). While some scholars interpret this "increase in knowledge" as a deeper understanding of Daniel’s prophecies, many others see it as a broader description of the rapid advancement in travel and information that characterizes the modern era.

Furthermore, certain events described in the book of Revelation seem to require a level of global communication that was impossible for most of human history. For example, Revelation 11:9 states that for three and a half days, people from every tribe, people, language, and nation will gaze upon the bodies of two witnesses in Jerusalem. Similarly, the description of a global economic system in Revelation 13:16-17, where no one can buy or sell without a specific mark, suggests a highly integrated and technologically advanced society.

While technology itself is a tool and not inherently evil, the Bible warns that human pride and the pursuit of autonomy from God often increase alongside our capabilities, much like the account of the Tower of Babel in Genesis 11:1-9. These signs are not meant to cause fear, but to serve as a reminder that the world is moving toward a definite conclusion under God's sovereign hand.

The ultimate purpose of biblical prophecy is to lead us to a state of spiritual readiness. Scripture teaches that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, He has defeated death and is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone, and not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about caring for our old parents?

The Bible speaks clearly and beautifully about the responsibility children have to care for their parents as they age. This duty is not seen as a burden in the eyes of God, but rather as a vital expression of our faith and a way to honor the family structure He created.

The foundation of this care is the Fifth Commandment, which tells us to honor our father and mother (Exodus 20:12). This is the first commandment with a promise, noting that it may go well with us in the land the Lord is giving us (Ephesians 6:2-3). While honor begins with obedience in childhood, it matures into practical care, provision, and respect as parents grow older. 

The Apostle Paul provides very specific instructions regarding the practical side of this care. He writes that if a widow has children or grandchildren, they should first learn to show godliness to their own household and to make some return to their parents, for this is pleasing in the sight of God (1 Timothy 5:4). He goes even further to emphasize the seriousness of this responsibility, stating that if anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever (1 Timothy 5:8).

Jesus Himself upheld the importance of caring for parents. He rebuked the religious leaders of His day who tried to use religious excuses (traditions they called Corban) to avoid financially supporting their aging parents (Mark 7:9-13). Even while Jesus was suffering on the cross for the sins of the world, He took a moment to ensure His mother would be cared for by entrusting her to His disciple, John (John 19:26-27).

Caring for elderly parents often requires great patience, sacrifice, and humility. It is an opportunity to reflect the heart of God, who is described as a Father to the fatherless and a protector of the vulnerable (Psalm 68:5). Leviticus 19:32 also reminds us to stand up before the gray head and honor the face of an old man, showing that aging carries a dignity that deserves our reverence.

In our own strength, providing this level of care can feel overwhelming. We may feel the weight of our own limitations or the strain of past hurts. These moments remind us of our deep need for the grace of God. The Gospel tells us that while we were still weak and separated from God by our sin, Christ died for us to reconcile us to the Father (Romans 5:8). He lived the perfect life of honor that we could not live and took the punishment for our failures upon Himself. Through His death and bodily resurrection, He offers us forgiveness and the gift of the Holy Spirit to empower us to love others, including our parents, with His selfless love.

If you find yourself struggling with the weight of these responsibilities or with a sense of guilt over past relationships, you can turn to God right now. Repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find forgiveness and the strength to walk in obedience. 

As you navigate this season of life, remember that God sees your service. Seeking wisdom from your local church elders or a Christian counselor can also provide the practical and emotional support needed to honor your parents well. What is the most challenging part of this journey for you right now?
Does salvation affect more than just the afterlife?

Salvation is a comprehensive work of God that transforms a person’s past, present, and future. While the promise of eternal life in the presence of God is a glorious reality, the Bible teaches that salvation begins the moment a person places their trust in Jesus Christ. It is not merely a "ticket to heaven" but a total restoration of our relationship with the Creator that impacts every facet of our life today.

In the present, salvation changes our identity and our standing before God. Through faith, we are justified, which means we are declared righteous by God because the righteousness of Christ is credited to us (Romans 5:1). This results in an immediate peace with God that replaces the enmity and guilt caused by our sin. We are also adopted into God's family, allowing us to approach Him as a loving Father (Galatians 4:4-7). This new identity as a child of God provides a profound sense of security and purpose that the world cannot offer.

Salvation also involves an ongoing process called sanctification. The Holy Spirit dwells within the believer, providing the power to turn away from sin and to grow in likeness to Christ (Philippians 2:12-13). This internal transformation affects our character, our relationships, and our priorities. Instead of being driven by selfish desires, we are empowered to produce the fruit of the Spirit, which is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control (Galatians 5:22-23). Salvation gives us a new reason for living, as we are created in Christ Jesus for good works that God prepared beforehand (Ephesians 2:10).

Ultimately, our need for salvation stems from the fact that God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3) and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Our sin separates us from God and deserves His just judgment. However, because of His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was bodily resurrected on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by our own works or efforts. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

This transformation starts now and continues until the day we see Him face to face, at which point our salvation will be completed in glorification (Philippians 1:6). What is your understanding of how someone enters into this relationship with God?
What is the difference between Judaism and Islam?

Judaism and Islam are both monotheistic faiths that trace their lineage back to the patriarch Abraham, but they differ significantly in their scriptures, their view of God’s nature, and their understanding of salvation.

Judaism is rooted in the covenant between God and the people of Israel. Its primary authority is the Tanakh, which Christians refer to as the Old Testament, specifically the Torah, the first five books of the Bible. Jewish life and faith are centered on the Law given to Moses at Mount Sinai and the hope of a coming Messiah who will redeem Israel and bring peace to the world.
