Islam was established in the 7th century AD by Muhammad, whom Muslims believe to be the final prophet of God. Islam considers the Quran to be the literal, perfect word of God, delivered to Muhammad by the angel Gabriel. While Muslims recognize many biblical figures as prophets, including Abraham, Moses, and Jesus, they believe that the Jewish and Christian scriptures have been corrupted over time. Islamic practice is built upon the Five Pillars, which are the declaration of faith, daily prayer, fasting during Ramadan, giving to the poor, and a pilgrimage to Mecca.

A primary difference lies in their view of Jesus Christ. Judaism generally views Jesus as a historical figure or a teacher but does not accept Him as the Messiah or the Son of God. Islam honors Jesus as a great prophet born of a virgin, but it explicitly denies His divinity and His death on the cross for the sins of the world.

From a biblical perspective, both Judaism and Islam emphasize human effort and adherence to religious law to maintain a relationship with God. However, the Bible teaches that no one can be justified by the works of the law because all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, as seen in Romans 3:20 and Romans 3:23. 

The Gospel provides a different message, revealing that Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death. Salvation is not a reward for our religious works or submission, but a gift of grace from God. Ephesians 2:8–9 explains that it is by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast. Jesus Himself declared in John 14:6, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."

You can turn to God right now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for total forgiveness and new life. What do you find most striking about the way these different faiths view the person and work of Jesus?
What does it mean to trust in the person and work of Jesus Christ?

Trusting in the person and work of Jesus Christ is the heart of the Christian faith. It means moving beyond simply knowing facts about Him to placing your entire hope and confidence in Him for your standing before God.

To trust in the **person** of Jesus is to recognize His unique identity as revealed in Scripture. He is the eternal Son of God, the second person of the Trinity, who became a human being (John 1:1; John 1:14). Christians believe He is truly God and truly man, possessing a divine nature and a human nature in one person (Colossians 2:9). Because He is God, His life has infinite value, and because He is man, He can truly represent humanity. Trusting His person means accepting His authority as Lord and believing that He is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5).

To trust in the **work** of Jesus is to rely on what He accomplished during His time on earth, specifically His life, death, and resurrection. Jesus lived a life of perfect obedience to God's law, something no other human has ever done (Hebrews 4:15). In His death on the cross, He acted as a substitute for sinners, taking the punishment that our sins deserved to satisfy the justice of a holy God (Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 2:24). His bodily resurrection from the dead on the third day is the ultimate proof that God accepted His sacrifice and that He has victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 4:25).

This trust is necessary because of the spiritual reality we all face. God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). However, every human being has sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Our sin separates us from God and deserves His righteous judgment. We cannot bridge this gap through our own good works, religious rituals, or moral improvements, because salvation is a gift of God's grace that cannot be earned (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Trusting in Jesus means you stop trying to save yourself and instead rest entirely in what He has already done. It involves repentance, which is a sincere turning away from sin and self-reliance, and faith, which is a turning toward Christ alone for forgiveness and reconciliation with God (Acts 17:30; Romans 10:9-10). When you trust Him, His righteousness is credited to you, and your sins are forgiven.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is it that makes it difficult for you to rely on His work rather than your own efforts?
Who was Plato? How did Platonism / Platonic thought influence society in Bible times?

Plato was a Greek philosopher who lived in Athens approximately four hundred years before the birth of Jesus Christ. As a student of Socrates and the teacher of Aristotle, he became one of the most influential figures in Western thought. His philosophy, often called Platonism, centered on the idea that the physical world we see and touch is merely an imperfect shadow of a higher, eternal, and unchanging reality called the world of Forms or Ideas.

By the time of the New Testament, Platonic thought had deeply permeated the Mediterranean world through a process called Hellenization. This influence shaped society and the intellectual climate in several significant ways.

One major impact was the promotion of dualism, the belief that the spiritual realm is inherently good while the physical or material world is lesser, or even evil. In the centuries surrounding the life of Christ, this led some to devalue the human body and physical life. This worldview eventually contributed to the rise of Gnosticism, a heresy that the early church had to confront. The Apostles strongly countered this by emphasizing that God created the physical world and called it good in Genesis 1:31, and most importantly, by teaching that the Word of God became flesh in the person of Jesus as seen in John 1:14.

Platonism also influenced how people thought about the soul. Plato taught that the soul was immortal and trapped within the body, seeking release to return to the realm of pure spirit. While Christianity also teaches that human beings have an eternal soul, the biblical hope is distinct from Platonic thought. The Bible does not focus on a permanent escape from the body but on the glorious promise of the bodily resurrection and the renewal of all creation, as promised in 1 Corinthians 15:52 and Revelation 21:1.

The language of Greek philosophy provided a framework that the early Christians sometimes used to communicate the Gospel to a Gentile audience. For example, when the Apostle Paul stood before the philosophers at Mars Hill in Athens, he engaged with their search for truth to point them toward the one true God in Acts 17:22-31. While Plato sought ultimate reality through human reason and logic, the Bible reveals that ultimate reality is found in a Person.

The Gospel tells us that we are separated from our holy Creator by our sin, and no amount of human philosophy or intellectual striving can bridge that gap. God, in His great love and mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice and victory over death, we are offered reconciliation with God by grace alone through faith alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the gift of eternal life. What do you find most interesting or perhaps challenging about the difference between human philosophy and the message of the Bible?
Is a virtual/online communion service biblical?

The question of whether a virtual communion service is biblical requires us to look at the purpose and nature of the Lord's Supper as described in the New Testament. The Scriptures place a significant emphasis on the physical gathering of the local church. In 1 Corinthians 11:17, 1 Corinthians 11:18, 1 Corinthians 11:20, and 1 Corinthians 11:33, the Apostle Paul repeatedly uses the phrase "when you come together" as a prerequisite for observing the Lord's Supper. This suggests that the ordinance is intended to be a corporate act of the gathered body of believers.

The Lord's Supper is described as a participation or fellowship, often called koinonia, in the body and blood of Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:16. This fellowship is not only between the believer and the Lord but also among the members of the church. Paul explains in 1 Corinthians 10:17 that "Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, for we all partake of the one bread." Many pastors and theologians argue that the physical sharing of the elements in one location is essential to representing this spiritual unity and the reality of the one body.

Furthermore, the Bible gives the elders or overseers of the church the responsibility to shepherd the flock and guard the purity of the church, as seen in Acts 20:28 and Hebrews 13:17. In a physical gathering, elders can more effectively oversee the table and ensure that those participating are doing so in a "worthy manner," providing pastoral care to those who may be struggling with sin or in need of guidance, as instructed in 1 Corinthians 11:27-29.

While a virtual service can be a helpful tool for teaching or for those who are physically unable to attend due to health or distance, it faces challenges in fulfilling the biblical pattern of a gathered assembly. Because the Bible does not explicitly address digital technology, Christians may hold varying views on this as a matter of secondary church practice. However, the historical and scriptural pattern points toward a physical, local assembly as the proper context for communion.

Ultimately, communion is a proclamation of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. It points us to the holiness of God and our own need for a Savior due to our sin and separation from Him. It celebrates the central truths of our faith: that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature to live the perfect life we could not live. He died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, as confirmed in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is found only by God's grace through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works, according to Ephesians 2:8-9.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most meaningful about the practice of the Lord's Supper?
Why do some churches thrive while others die?

The question of why some churches flourish while others decline is one that involves both the sovereign will of God and the faithfulness of His people. Ultimately, Jesus Christ promised that He would build His church and that the gates of hell would not prevail against it (Matthew 16:18). While individual local congregations may rise or fall, the universal Church of God is secure in His hands.

One primary reason a church thrives is its commitment to the authority of Scripture and the centrality of the Gospel. When a church remains rooted in the Word of God, it remains connected to the source of life. In the Book of Revelation, Jesus warns the church in Ephesus that if they abandon their first love, He will remove their lampstand from its place (Revelation 2:4-5). Churches that thrive often prioritize the faithful preaching of the Bible, the administration of the sacraments, and a dependence on prayer. They recognize that apart from Christ, they can do nothing (John 15:5).

Conversely, many churches begin to die when they drift from these essentials. This drift often manifests as a slow compromise with cultural values that contradict Scripture, or a shift toward legalism or moralism that obscures the grace of God. When a congregation loses its focus on the Great Commission, which is the command to make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:18-20), it often becomes inward-focused and stagnant. Healthy churches tend to be those that look outward with compassion, seeking to share the love of Christ with their neighbors and the world.

Unity and love within the body are also vital. Jesus said that the world would know His disciples by their love for one another (John 13:35). A church marked by bitterness, division, or pride is a church that grieves the Holy Spirit and loses its witness to the community. Progressive sanctification, which is the process of being made more like Christ through the power of the Holy Spirit, should be evident in the lives of the members and leaders alike.

At the heart of all church health is the Gospel itself. The Gospel is the good news that although God is perfectly holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, He has provided a way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8-9).

A church thrives when its members are not just attending a service, but are truly transformed by this message. If you find yourself wondering about the health of a church or your own spiritual state, the most important question is where you stand with Jesus Christ. He is the Lord who died and rose again to offer forgiveness and new life to all who call upon Him. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation. What is it that might be holding you back from fully trusting Him today?
How much authority should a pastor have over a church?

The question of pastoral authority is a vital one for the health and peace of any local church. In the New Testament, the authority of a pastor (also called an elder or overseer) is presented as a real but limited responsibility, always exercised under the supreme authority of Jesus Christ, who is the Head of the church as noted in Colossians 1:18.

The authority of a pastor is primarily a derivative authority. This means a pastor has no inherent power in himself, but rather carries authority only as he accurately teaches and applies the Word of God. When a pastor speaks according to Scripture, the church is called to listen and follow, because the Word itself carries God’s authority.

Biblical leadership is characterized by several key principles:

First, pastoral authority is for the purpose of spiritual oversight and protection. Hebrews 13:17 instructs believers to obey their leaders and submit to them, for they are keeping watch over your souls as those who will have to give an account. This oversight includes teaching sound doctrine, refuting those who contradict it as seen in Titus 1:9, and caring for the spiritual needs of the members.

Second, this authority must be exercised with humility and a servant’s heart. 1 Peter 5:2-3 tells elders to shepherd the flock of God that is among you, exercising oversight, not under compulsion, but willingly, as God would have you, not for shameful gain, but eagerly, not lording it over those in your charge, but being examples to the flock. A pastor is not a CEO or a dictator, but an under-shepherd who serves the Chief Shepherd, Jesus.

Third, the New Testament suggests a model of elder-led congregationalism. This means that while the elders lead and oversee the daily ministry and direction of the church, the congregation as a whole has a final responsibility in certain matters. For example, the congregation is involved in the final steps of church discipline in Matthew 18:15-17 and is responsible for guarding the purity of the Gospel preached to them, as Paul indicates in Galatians 1:8-9.

A pastor’s authority does not extend to every personal detail of a believer's life, such as their choice of career, schooling, or specific diet, unless those choices involve clear sin. His role is to equip the saints for the work of ministry and to help them grow in the knowledge of Christ according to Ephesians 4:11-13.

Ultimately, all authority in heaven and on earth belongs to Jesus Christ (Matthew 28:18). He is the one who lived perfectly, died for our sins, and rose again to reconcile us to God. Salvation is found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, you can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

What is your current understanding of how a church should be led?
What is Luciferianism?

Luciferianism is a belief system or philosophy that centers on the figure of Lucifer. While the term is often associated with Satanism, many Luciferians distinguish themselves by focusing on qualities they attribute to Lucifer, such as enlightenment, independence, and the pursuit of knowledge.

There are generally two main branches of this belief. Theistic Luciferians view Lucifer as a literal deity or a benevolent spirit who brought wisdom to humanity. In contrast, atheistic or symbolic Luciferians view Lucifer as an archetype or a symbol of human potential and rebellion against what they perceive as the restrictive authority of traditional religion.

From a biblical perspective, the name Lucifer comes from a Latin translation of the Hebrew word for "Day Star" in Isaiah 14:12. Scripture describes this figure as a created being who fell from heaven because of pride and a desire to be like the Most High. While Luciferianism presents this figure as a bringer of light, the Bible warns that Satan disguises himself as an angel of light in 2 Corinthians 11:14. Jesus also describes the adversary as a murderer from the beginning and the father of lies in John 8:44.

The core of Luciferian philosophy often revolves around self-exaltation and the idea that humans can become like gods through their own intellect or hidden knowledge. This is the very temptation presented in the Garden of Eden, where the serpent suggested that eating the forbidden fruit would make humans like God, knowing good and evil, in Genesis 3:5.

In contrast to the pursuit of self-deification, the Bible teaches that we are created by a holy and sovereign God but are separated from Him by our sin and pride. True enlightenment and life are not found within ourselves or through secret knowledge, but through the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus is the true Light of the World in John 8:12; John 1:4-9. He is the eternal God who took on human nature through the incarnation, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and His exaltation as Lord.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone and not by our own works or hidden wisdom. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What is the satisfaction or commercial theory of the atonement?

The satisfaction theory of the atonement, often called the commercial theory, was most famously developed by Anselm of Canterbury in the eleventh century. This view seeks to explain why it was necessary for God to become man to redeem humanity.

At the heart of this theory is the concept of God's infinite honor. Anselm argued that sin is not merely a mistake, but a direct insult to the honor of the Almighty. Because God is infinite and holy, any sin against Him incurs a debt of infinite proportions (Habakkuk 1:13; Romans 3:23). According to this framework, justice demands that this debt be repaid or satisfied to restore the order of the universe and God's honor.

The dilemma presented by this theory is that human beings, being finite and already sinful, have nothing of their own to offer that could possibly satisfy an infinite debt. However, justice also requires that a human must pay the debt, since it was humanity that sinned. This led Anselm to conclude that only a God-man could accomplish our salvation. As a man, Jesus could represent humanity and pay the debt on our behalf. As God, His life and sacrifice possessed infinite value, more than enough to satisfy the requirements of God's honor (1 Timothy 2:5-6; Hebrews 9:14).

While this theory is sometimes termed commercial because it uses the language of debt and payment, it is rooted in the biblical reality that sin has objective consequences that must be addressed by God's holiness and justice. In later centuries, this foundation was further developed by Reformers into the doctrine of penal substitutionary atonement. That view emphasizes that Christ did not just satisfy God's honor, but also bore the specific legal penalty and the righteous wrath of God that we deserved because of our transgressions (Isaiah 53:5; Romans 3:25-26; Galatians 3:13).

This truth reminds us that we cannot save ourselves through our own efforts or good works. We are saved by grace alone through faith in what Jesus Christ has already finished on the cross. Because of His perfect life, sacrificial death, and bodily resurrection, the debt of sin is fully paid for all who trust in Him (Colossians 2:13-14).

God is perfectly holy, yet He is also rich in mercy. He sent His Son to do for us what we could never do for ourselves. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in the sufficiency of Christ's work for you?
Who are the Shakers?

The Shakers, formally known as the United Society of Believers in Christ’s Second Appearing, are a religious group that emerged in the eighteenth century. They were originally a branch of the Quakers in England before moving to the United States under the leadership of Ann Lee, often called Mother Ann Lee, in 1774.

The Shakers are perhaps best known for their simple lifestyle, communal living, and their belief in celibacy. They derived their name from their early style of worship, which involved ecstatic dancing and physical shaking, which they believed was a way to shake off sin.

From a biblical perspective, it is important to note several areas where Shaker theology differs significantly from historic, orthodox Christianity:

1. The Identity of Christ: Shakers believed that God has a dual nature, both male and female. They taught that while Jesus was the male manifestation of Christ, Ann Lee was the female manifestation of Christ’s second coming. However, the Bible teaches that Jesus Christ is the unique, eternal Son of God who will return personally and bodily in glory (Acts 1:11; Hebrews 9:28; Revelation 1:7).

2. Salvation and Celibacy: The Shakers believed that the root of all sin was the sexual relationship, and therefore they required strict celibacy for all members. While Scripture honors those who choose singleness for the sake of the kingdom, it also affirms that marriage is a good gift from God and that we are saved by grace through faith in Christ alone, not by our own ascetic practices (Genesis 2:24; Ephesians 2:8–9; 1 Timothy 4:1–3).

3. Authority of Scripture: While they used the Bible, Shakers often prioritized new revelations given through Ann Lee and other leaders above the written Word of God. Christians believe that the Bible is the final and sufficient authority for all matters of faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16–17).

Because of their practice of celibacy, the Shakers could only grow through converts or by adopting children. Today, there is only one remaining active Shaker community in Sabbathday Lake, Maine, with very few members left.

The history of the Shakers reminds us of the human desire for holiness and community, yet it also highlights the vital importance of staying grounded in the true Gospel. The Good News is not that we must attain purity through our own efforts or through a new human leader, but that Jesus Christ, the perfect God-man, died for our sins and rose again to reconcile us to God. 

You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the favor of God, and how can I get it?

The favor of God is His gracious kindness, approval, and blessing bestowed upon people. In the Bible, the word for favor is often interchangeable with the word grace. It is not a reward for our good works or personal merit, but is rooted entirely in God’s character. God is holy, sovereign, and good (Psalm 145:17; Psalm 100:5), and He chooses to extend His favor to those who rely on Him.

While God shows a general favor or common grace to all of creation by providing life, breath, and the cycles of nature (Matthew 5:45; Acts 17:25), the fullness of His favor is found in a restored relationship with Him. Because God is perfectly holy and just, our sin creates a barrier between us and His favor (Isaiah 59:2). We have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), and on our own, we cannot earn His approval through religious rituals or moral efforts (Galatians 2:16; Ephesians 2:8-9).

The way to receive the favor of God is through the person and work of Jesus Christ. Jesus is truly God and truly man, and He lived the perfect life of obedience that we could not live. Out of love, He took the judgment we deserved for our sins by dying on the cross. He was buried and then rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Because of what Jesus has done, God offers His favor as a free gift. To receive it, you must stop trying to earn your way to God and instead place your total trust in Jesus Christ. This involves repentance, which means turning away from your sin and self-reliance and turning toward God. When you trust in Christ, His righteousness is credited to you, and you are justified (declared right) before God by grace alone through faith alone (Romans 5:1; Titus 3:4-7).

Through this faith, you enter into a permanent state of favor where you can approach God with confidence, knowing He is your loving Father (Hebrews 4:16). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that Jesus is the Savior?

To understand what it means that Jesus is the Savior, we must first understand what He saves us from and why we need a rescuer. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, righteous, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Habakkuk 1:13). He created us to be in a relationship with Him, but every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glorious standard (Romans 3:23). Because God is just, He cannot simply overlook sin, and the consequence of our rebellion is spiritual death and eternal separation from Him (Romans 6:23).

Jesus is the Savior because He is the only one who can bridge this gap and rescue us from the judgment we deserve. This rescue involved several vital components:

First, Jesus is truly God and truly man. As the eternal Son of God who took on human flesh, He was able to live the perfect, sinless life that we could never live (John 1:1; John 1:14; Hebrews 4:15).

Second, Jesus acted as our substitute. On the cross, He willingly took the punishment for our sins upon Himself. He bore the wrath of God that was meant for us, satisfying the demands of God's justice (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24). This is often called penal substitutionary atonement, meaning He paid the penalty in our place.

Third, Jesus proved His victory over sin and death by rising bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). His resurrection confirms that His sacrifice was accepted by the Father and that He has the power to give eternal life to all who believe in Him.

To call Jesus "Savior" means acknowledging that we cannot save ourselves through good works, religious rituals, or moral effort. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). He rescues us from the penalty of sin (hell), the power of sin in our daily lives, and one day, He will rescue His people from the very presence of sin in His eternal kingdom (Philippians 3:20-21).

Jesus Himself said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). He is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is baby dedication? Is child / baby dedication biblical?

Baby dedication is a symbolic act and a public ceremony in which Christian parents commit themselves to raising their child in a way that honors God. During this time, parents stand before their church community to acknowledge that their child is a gift from God and to vow that they will provide a home environment where the child can learn about the love of Jesus Christ. The congregation also often commits to supporting the family in this spiritual journey.

While the Bible does not explicitly command a ceremony called "baby dedication," the practice is rooted in several biblical principles and examples. In the Old Testament, we see Hannah presenting her son Samuel to the Lord at the temple, recognizing that he belonged to God (1 Samuel 1:24-28). In the New Testament, Mary and Joseph brought the infant Jesus to the temple in Jerusalem to present Him to the Lord according to the custom of the law (Luke 2:22). We also see Jesus expressing deep care for children, taking them in His arms and blessing them while telling His disciples that the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these (Matthew 19:13-15).

It is important to distinguish baby dedication from baptism. In many traditions that practice believer's baptism, dedication is not seen as a means of salvation or a replacement for baptism. Instead, it is a formal recognition of the parents' responsibility to teach their children the Scriptures and to model a life of faith (Deuteronomy 6:6-7; Ephesians 6:4). The hope of every Christian parent is that the child will eventually come to a personal point of repentance and faith in Christ.

Ultimately, every person, including children as they grow, must face the reality of their own spiritual condition. The Bible teaches that God is holy and perfect, but all human beings have fallen short of His glory through sin (Romans 3:23). Because of this separation, we cannot save ourselves through our own good works or through the faith of our parents.

God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to bridge this gap. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift of grace that is received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your current understanding of how someone begins a relationship with God?
Will God continue to forgive you if you commit the same sin over and over again?

The short answer is yes, God will continue to forgive you if you are truly His child. His grace is not a limited resource that runs out when we struggle. When Jesus was asked how many times we should forgive someone who sins against us, He told His disciples to forgive "seventy times seven" (Matthew 18:22). This was not a literal limit of 490 times, but a way of saying our forgiveness should be limitless, reflecting the infinite forgiveness God offers us.

If you are in Christ, your standing before God is not based on your perfect performance, but on the perfect righteousness of Jesus. The Bible promises that "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9). This promise is for the believer who continues to struggle. There is no sin so repetitive that it exceeds the power of Christ's blood to wash it away. As the apostle Paul wrote, "where sin increased, grace abounded all the more" (Romans 5:20).

However, it is important to distinguish between "struggling" with a sin and "settling" into a sin. A person who has been changed by God will feel the weight of their failure and desire to be free from it. The Holy Spirit dwells within believers to convict them and help them grow in holiness over time. If you find yourself grieved by your repeated failure, that very grief is often a sign that the Holy Spirit is at work in you, drawing you back to the Father. If there is no desire to change or no conviction of sin, it may be a time to examine whether you have truly surrendered your life to Christ.

The struggle you are feeling is one many believers face. Even Paul described a war within himself, doing the very things he hated (Romans 7:15). Yet he concluded that "There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1). God's goal for you is not just to forgive you, but to transform you into the image of His Son through a process called sanctification.

This hope is rooted in the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and cannot tolerate sin (Isaiah 6:3), and we have all rebelled against Him (Romans 3:23). But because of His great mercy, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth and lived a life of perfect obedience. He died on the cross as a substitute, taking the punishment we deserved for our sins, was buried, and rose again bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now the exalted Lord of all. We are justified, or made right with God, by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or our ability to stop sinning (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God right now, even in the midst of your struggle. Repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for total forgiveness and new life.

What is it about this particular struggle that makes you doubt God's willingness to forgive you?
Can monotheism be proven?

The question of whether monotheism can be proven involves both logical reasoning and the historical self-revelation of God. While "proof" in a strict scientific sense is usually reserved for the natural world, there are powerful philosophical and biblical arguments that point to the reality of one single, supreme Creator.

Logically, many thinkers have argued that the existence of more than one infinite being is a contradiction. If God is defined as the most supreme, all-powerful, and infinite Being, then having two such beings would mean they limit one another. If one "god" has power that the other does not, or if they occupy different "spaces" of authority, then neither is truly infinite or supreme. The order, unity, and consistent laws of the physical universe also suggest a single, coherent Mind behind creation rather than a collection of competing deities. This is reflected in Romans 1:20, which states that God’s invisible attributes, namely His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made.

Scripture provides the clearest "proof" through God's own words and actions in history. The Bible consistently affirms that there is only one God. Deuteronomy 6:4 declares, "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one." God Himself says in Isaiah 45:5, "I am the Lord, and there is no other, besides me there is no God." As Christians, we believe this one God exists eternally in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, which is a mystery that does not contradict His oneness but reveals the depth of His nature.

The most profound evidence for monotheism is the person and work of Jesus Christ. Jesus claimed to be one with the Father in John 10:30 and demonstrated His divine authority through His perfect life, His miracles, and His bodily resurrection from the dead. His resurrection serves as the historical vindication of His claims and the reality of the one true God He represented. 

This one true God is perfectly holy and just, which creates a serious problem for us because we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, as noted in Romans 3:23. Because God is just, He must punish sin, but because He is loving, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die on the cross as a substitute, taking the punishment for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Salvation is not something we can earn through our own efforts or good works; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). This one God invites everyone to enter into a relationship with Him. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What is it that makes the idea of one supreme God or the person of Jesus difficult for you to embrace?
What is impartation?

In a biblical context, impartation refers to the act of sharing, giving, or communicating something spiritual from one person to another, or from God to humanity. The term is derived from the Greek word "metadidomi," which means to give a share of or to pass on. It is used in the New Testament to describe the sharing of spiritual gifts, the Gospel, and even one’s very life for the sake of others.

There are three primary ways impartation is understood within the Christian faith:

First, there is the impartation of the Gospel and fellowship. The Apostle Paul wrote to the believers in Rome, saying, "For I long to see you, that I may impart to you some spiritual gift, so that you may be established" (Romans 1:11). He also expressed this heart for the church in Thessalonica, stating, "So, affectionately longing for you, we were well pleased to impart to you not only the gospel of God, but also our own lives, because you had become dear to us" (1 Thessalonians 2:8). This suggests that impartation involves a deep, personal investment where believers strengthen one another through the Word and mutual encouragement.

Second, many Christians distinguish between "imputed" righteousness and "imparted" righteousness. Imputation is a legal term meaning that God credits the perfect righteousness of Jesus Christ to a believer's account the moment they trust in Him (Romans 4:3; 2 Corinthians 5:21). Impartation, however, is often associated with progressive sanctification. This is the process where the Holy Spirit actually works righteousness into a believer’s character, enabling them to live out the holiness that God has already declared they have in Christ (Philippians 2:12-13).

Third, in some traditions, impartation refers to the "laying on of hands" to commission someone for ministry or to recognize a spiritual gift. Paul reminded Timothy, "Do not neglect the gift that is in you, which was given to you by prophecy with the laying on of the hands of the eldership" (1 Timothy 4:14). While God is the ultimate source of every spiritual gift, He often uses the community of believers to confirm and activate those gifts in the lives of individuals.

At its core, the most vital thing God imparts to us is new life through His Son. Because of God’s holiness and our own sin, we are naturally separated from Him. However, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. Through His victory over death, He offers forgiveness and eternal life to all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything specific about how God works in a believer's life that you would like to explore further?
What is Atenism? Did either Judaism or Christianity borrow from Atenism?

Atenism was a religious system in ancient Egypt established by Pharaoh Akhenaten (originally Amenhotep IV) during the 14th century BC. It is often described as one of the earliest recorded forms of monotheism, or more accurately, monolatry. Akhenaten abolished the traditional worship of the many Egyptian gods and mandated the exclusive worship of the Aten, represented as the disk of the sun. Unlike the earlier Egyptian gods who had human or animal forms, the Aten was seen as the life-giving force of the sun itself.

The question of whether Judaism or Christianity borrowed from Atenism has been a subject of historical and academic debate for over a century. The most famous proponent of this theory was Sigmund Freud, who suggested that Moses was an Egyptian priest or follower of Akhenaten who brought these monotheistic ideas to the Hebrews. However, most biblical scholars and historians note significant differences that make direct borrowing unlikely.

While there are literary similarities between the "Great Hymn to the Aten" and certain biblical passages, such as Psalm 104:1-35, these are often attributed to a shared cultural and linguistic environment in the Ancient Near East rather than direct theological dependence. The differences between the two systems are profound. Atenism was a form of solar monotheism where the sun disk itself was the deity, whereas the God of the Bible is the transcendent Creator who is distinct from and Lord over the sun, moon, and stars (Genesis 1:14-19; Romans 1:20-25). Furthermore, Atenism was centered entirely on the Pharaoh as the sole mediator between the Aten and the people, while the God of Israel revealed Himself through a covenant to an entire nation and established a moral law, the Ten Commandments, which has no parallel in the remains of Atenist thought.

Christianity, being rooted in the fulfillment of the Old Testament promises, shares the same foundational monotheism as Judaism. It teaches that there is only one God, eternally existing in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). While Atenism focused on the physical light of the sun, Christianity centers on Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, as the "Light of the World" (John 1:1-9; John 8:12).

The core of the Christian faith is not found in ancient Egyptian sun worship, but in the historical person and work of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that God is holy and supreme, yet we have all turned away from Him through sin, which separates us from His life (Romans 3:23). In His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who was born of a virgin, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What do you find most interesting or perhaps most challenging about the idea that God is the personal Creator of the sun rather than the sun itself?
What happens to babies and young children when they die? Where do I find the age of accountability in the Bible?

The question of what happens to infants and young children who pass away is one that touches the deepest parts of the human heart. While the Bible does not explicitly use the phrase "age of accountability," it provides several principles that offer great comfort and hope regarding God's mercy toward those who are not yet able to discern right from wrong.

The concept of an age of accountability is drawn from passages that describe a time in a child's life before they have the capacity for moral reasoning. For instance, in Deuteronomy 1:39, God speaks of the "little ones" and "children, who today have no knowledge of good or evil." Similarly, Isaiah 7:16 speaks of a time before a child "knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good." These verses suggest that God recognizes a distinction between those who willfully rebel against Him and those who do not yet have the cognitive or spiritual capacity to understand His law or their need for a Savior.

We also see a beautiful example of hope in the life of King David. When his infant son died, David said in 2 Samuel 12:23, "I shall go to him, but he will not return to me." David expressed a confident expectation that he would be reunited with his child in the presence of God. Furthermore, Jesus showed a special tenderness toward children, saying in Matthew 19:14, "Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven."

While we believe that every human being is born with a sinful nature inherited from Adam, as noted in Psalm 51:5 and Romans 5:12, we also trust in the perfect character of God. He is holy, just, and full of mercy. Many believers find peace in the conviction that God, in His sovereign grace, applies the atoning work of Jesus Christ to those who die in infancy or who have mental disabilities that prevent them from understanding the Gospel. As Genesis 18:25 asks, "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is right?"

At the heart of this discussion is the Gospel itself. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and that our sin separates us from Him. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not something we earn by our own goodness or by reaching a certain age; it is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone.

For those of us who can understand this message, the call is to respond to God's kindness. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

If you are grieving the loss of a little one, please know that God is the "Father of mercies and God of all comfort" as described in 2 Corinthians 1:3. You might find it helpful to speak with a pastor or a mature believer in your local church who can walk alongside you and pray with you during this time. What are your thoughts on how God's mercy interacts with His justice?
What is the Church of Spiritualism?

The Church of Spiritualism, often associated with the broader Spiritualist movement that began in the mid-19th century, is a religious organization centered on the belief that the spirits of the dead can and do communicate with the living. This communication typically occurs through individuals known as mediums. While Spiritualists may use Christian terminology or include hymns in their services, their core beliefs and practices differ significantly from historic, biblical Christianity.

Spiritualism is founded on the idea that there is no "death" in the traditional sense, but rather a transition to a different plane of existence where spirits continue to evolve and learn. They often emphasize personal experience and messages from the spirit world over the objective authority of the Bible. In many Spiritualist circles, Jesus is viewed not as the unique Son of God and Savior of the world, but as an exceptionally gifted medium or an advanced spiritual teacher.

From the perspective of the Bible, which is the final authority for Christian faith and practice, there are several serious concerns regarding these beliefs. The Scriptures explicitly forbid seeking out mediums or attempting to communicate with the dead. In Deuteronomy 18:10-12, God warns His people that anyone who practices divination, tells fortunes, or "inquires of the dead" is doing something that is an abomination to Him. Similarly, Leviticus 19:31 instructs believers not to turn to mediums or necromancers, for seeking them out results in becoming defiled.

The Bible teaches that after death, a person does not remain in a state of communication with the living to offer guidance. Instead, Hebrews 9:27 states that "it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment." Our eternity is not a gradual progression of the soul through different levels of existence based on our own efforts, but is determined by our relationship with Jesus Christ during our life on earth.

While the desire to know what lies beyond the grave is a natural human longing, the Gospel of Jesus Christ provides the only sure hope and true answer to the reality of death. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, but humanity is separated from Him by sin (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is not found in secret knowledge or messages from spirits, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. As Jesus said in John 14:6, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me." There is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is Chrislam?

Chrislam is a term used to describe a religious movement that attempts to blend the teachings and practices of Christianity and Islam. It primarily emerged in Nigeria during the 1970s and 1980s. Proponents of this movement generally believe that because both religions are monotheistic and share certain historical figures, they can be treated as compatible paths to the same God.

In practice, Chrislam often involves the use of both the Bible and the Quran during worship services. Followers may view Jesus and Muhammad as equally significant prophets. However, from the perspective of historic, biblical Christianity, this syncretism creates significant theological contradictions that cannot be reconciled without compromising the essential truths of the Gospel.

The Bible teaches that God is one God eternally existing in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). This doctrine of the Trinity is central to the Christian faith, yet it is explicitly rejected in Islamic teaching. Furthermore, the Bible identifies Jesus Christ not merely as a prophet, but as the eternal Word of God who became flesh (John 1:1; John 1:14). He is truly God and truly man, the only one capable of reconciling humanity to God.

Most importantly, the core of the Christian faith rests on the sacrificial death and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. Scripture teaches that all people have sinned and fallen short of God's holy standard (Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly just, sin must be punished, yet because He is perfectly loving, He provided a Savior. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by human works or the mixing of different religious systems (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus Himself made the exclusive claim, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). Because of this, Christianity maintains that there is only one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5; Acts 4:12).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What questions do you have about the unique role of Jesus in salvation?
Why should I forgive?

Forgiving others is one of the most challenging calls in the Christian life, but it is deeply rooted in the heart of who God is and what He has done for us. When we struggle to forgive, it is helpful to look at the reasons the Bible gives for this essential practice.

The primary reason we forgive is that God has forgiven us. Scripture says, "Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you" (Ephesians 4:32). When we realize the magnitude of our own debt against a holy God, it puts the offenses committed against us into a different perspective.

The Bible teaches that every human being has sinned and fallen short of God’s perfect holiness (Romans 3:23). This sin creates a debt we can never pay and separates us from our Creator. However, because God is rich in mercy and love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature, live a perfect life, and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Through Christ’s work alone, and not by any works of our own, we are reconciled to God when we believe in Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Beyond our gratitude for the Gospel, here are several other biblical reasons why we should forgive:

First, forgiveness is an act of obedience to God. Jesus taught His disciples to pray, "and forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors" (Matthew 6:12). He further explained that if we do not forgive others, we hinder our own fellowship with the Father (Matthew 6:14–15). This does not mean we lose our salvation, but it does mean that holding onto bitterness disrupts our spiritual walk and peace.

Second, forgiveness releases us from the burden of bitterness. Unforgiveness is often compared to a prison or a poison that hurts the person holding onto it more than the offender. In the Parable of the Unforgiving Servant, Jesus describes the torment that comes to those who refuse to show the same mercy they have received (Matthew 18:21–35). By forgiving, we entrust justice to God, who is the only perfectly just Judge (Romans 12:19). This allows us to move forward in the freedom and peace that Christ provides.

Third, forgiving others reflects the character of Christ to a watching world. When we extend grace to those who do not deserve it, we model the very grace that Jesus showed while He was being crucified, as He prayed, "Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do" (Luke 23:34). It is a powerful testimony to the transforming power of the Holy Spirit in a believer's life.

Forgiveness is not the same as forgetting, nor does it necessarily mean an immediate restoration of trust or the removal of healthy boundaries. It is a decision to let go of the right to seek revenge and to desire God's best for the other person. As you navigate this difficult process, remember that God is near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18) and provides the strength to do what seems humanly impossible.

What makes it most difficult for you to consider forgiving right now?
Who is Jesus — Day 2 — Jesus the Miracle Worker

In the Gospels, we see that the miracles of Jesus were never performed as mere spectacles or to satisfy curiosity. Instead, they served as "signs," a term often used in the Gospel of John to describe how His works pointed toward His true identity as the Son of God and the long-awaited Messiah. These miracles provide a glimpse into the heart of God and the restoration of the world that He is bringing about through His Kingdom.

Jesus demonstrated absolute authority over every realm of existence. He showed power over nature by calming a violent storm with a single command (Mark 4:35–41) and walking on water (Matthew 14:22–33). He exhibited power over physical illness, restoring sight to the blind, hearing to the deaf, and strength to the lame (Matthew 11:4–5). He also showed His supreme authority over the spiritual realm by casting out demons (Luke 11:14–20) and over death itself by raising people like Lazarus to life (John 11:38–44). 

These acts were the fulfillment of ancient prophecies. Hundreds of years before Jesus was born, the prophet Isaiah spoke of a time when God would come to save His people, saying that "the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf unstopped" (Isaiah 35:5–6). By performing these miracles, Jesus was testifying that the King had arrived.

However, the miracles were always secondary to a much deeper work. In one instance, a paralyzed man was lowered through a roof to reach Jesus. Before healing the man’s legs, Jesus said, "Son, your sins are forgiven" (Mark 2:1–12). He then performed the physical miracle specifically to prove that He had the divine authority to forgive sins. This reveals our greatest need. While we may seek God for physical help or a change in circumstances, our most profound problem is our spiritual separation from a holy God because of our sin. 

The greatest miracle of all is the Gospel itself. Though God is holy and just, and our sin deserves judgment, He is also merciful and loving. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death once and for all. This salvation is a gift that cannot be earned by our own works; it is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

As you look at these accounts of His power, what do you find most striking about the way Jesus used His authority to help those in need?
Who are the Mormons and what do they believe?

Mormons are members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, a religious movement that began in the United States during the early nineteenth century under the leadership of Joseph Smith. While they use the name of Jesus Christ and value the Bible, their core doctrines differ significantly from the historic, biblical Christian faith in several foundational ways.

One of the primary differences lies in the authority of Scripture. While Christians believe the Bible is the complete, inspired, and final Word of God (2 Timothy 3:16; Revelation 22:18-19), the Latter-day Saint movement adds other books, including the Book of Mormon, the Doctrine and Covenants, and the Pearl of Great Price. They also believe in ongoing, modern-day revelation through their church presidency and apostles that can supplement or clarify their teachings.

Another significant distinction involves the nature of God. Historic Christianity teaches the doctrine of the Trinity, that there is only one God who eternally exists in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; Deuteronomy 6:4). Mormonism teaches that the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost are three distinct gods who are "one in purpose" but not one in essence. They also teach that God the Father has a physical body of flesh and bone and was once a man who progressed to godhood, a concept that contradicts the biblical truth that God is Spirit (John 4:24) and has been God from everlasting to everlasting (Psalm 90:2).

Regarding Jesus Christ, the Bible teaches that He is the uncreated Creator of all things, truly God and truly man (John 1:1-3; Colossians 1:15-17). In Mormon doctrine, Jesus is described as the firstborn spirit child of Heavenly Father and a Heavenly Mother, making Him the elder brother of all humans and even of Lucifer.

The most vital difference concerns the Gospel and how a person is saved. The Bible teaches that salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, apart from any human works or merit (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:28). In contrast, the Book of Mormon states that "it is by grace that we are saved, after all we can do" (2 Nephi 25:23). This places a heavy emphasis on human effort, temple rituals, and keeping commandments as necessary requirements to achieve the highest level of heaven, known as exaltation.

The biblical Gospel is the good news that although we have all sinned and fallen short of the holiness of God (Romans 3:23), God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (1 Peter 2:24; 1 Corinthians 15:3). On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:4). Because of His finished work, forgiveness and eternal life are freely offered to everyone who repents and trusts in Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Does this help clarify the distinctions between these beliefs and the teachings of the Bible?
Is it allowable for a Christian to eat halal food?

Yes, it is biblically allowable for a Christian to eat halal food. The New Testament provides clear guidance on dietary restrictions, teaching that food itself does not affect our standing before God.

Jesus taught that it is not what goes into a person from the outside that defiles them, but what comes out of the heart. In Mark 7:18-19, the Gospel writer notes that by saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean. This was later confirmed to the Apostle Peter in a vision, where God told him not to call anything impure that God has made clean, as recorded in Acts 10:15.

The most direct parallel to halal food in the Bible is the discussion of meat sacrificed to idols. Halal meat is prepared according to Islamic law, which includes a prayer offered at the time of slaughter. In 1 Corinthians 8:4, the Apostle Paul explains that an idol is nothing at all in the world and that there is no God but one. Because of this, Paul states in 1 Corinthians 10:25-26 that believers may eat anything sold in the meat market without raising questions of conscience, for the earth is the Lord's, and everything in it, as also stated in Psalm 24:1.

While you have the freedom to eat halal food, Scripture provides two important considerations regarding how you use that liberty:

1. Consideration for other believers: If eating such food would trouble the conscience of a fellow Christian who views it as sinful, Paul suggests abstaining out of love for them, as mentioned in 1 Corinthians 8:9-13 and Romans 14:13-21. Our goal is to build others up rather than cause them to stumble.

2. Consideration for your witness: In 1 Corinthians 10:27-29, Paul notes that if an unbeliever specifically points out that the food was used in a religious ritual, you might choose to abstain for the sake of their conscience and your testimony. The goal is to ensure that your actions point people toward the Gospel rather than creating a misunderstanding about who you worship.

Ultimately, our relationship with God is not based on dietary rules but on His grace. We are all separated from a holy God by our sin, but God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our works or what we eat.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What questions or thoughts do you have about trusting Him?
What does the Bible say about the role of youth pastor / minister?

The Bible does not explicitly use the title "youth pastor" or "youth minister," as these are modern designations within church leadership. However, the role is deeply rooted in biblical principles regarding the discipleship of the next generation and the general qualifications for ministry.

**The Responsibility to the Next Generation**
The primary foundation for youth ministry is found in the command to pass the knowledge of God to children and young people. In the Old Testament, God commanded His people to teach His words diligently to their children, speaking of them in the home and throughout daily life (Deuteronomy 6:6-7). This theme continues in the Psalms, which express the necessity of telling "the generation to come the praises of the Lord, and His strength and His wonderful works that He has done" (Psalm 78:4). A youth minister serves as a formal extension of this biblical mandate, assisting parents and the church in grounding young people in the truth of God.

**Qualifications and Character**
Because a youth pastor is a leader within the church, they are held to high standards of character and conduct. If the role is considered a pastoral office in a specific local church, the qualifications for elders or overseers found in 1 Timothy 3:1-7 and Titus 1:5-9 apply. These include being above reproach, temperate, able to teach, and not greedy. Even if the role is not classified as an elder, those who serve in ministry are expected to be examples to the believers in "word, in conduct, in love, in spirit, in faith, in purity" (1 Timothy 4:12).

**The Work of Discipleship**
The role of a youth minister involves specific exhortation tailored to the needs of young people. Titus 2:6-8 instructs that young men should be encouraged to be sober-minded, with the leader showing themselves to be a pattern of good works and sound speech. The goal is spiritual maturity, as described in Ephesians 4:12-13, where ministry is intended for the "equipping of the saints for the work of ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ," until all come to the unity of the faith and the knowledge of the Son of God.

**The Central Message of Ministry**
The heart of any Christian ministry is the Gospel. A youth minister’s most vital task is to proclaim that God is holy and that every person has sinned, falling short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day, conquering death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God’s grace, received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why do Jehovah's Witnesses refuse blood transfusions?

Jehovah's Witnesses refuse blood transfusions because of their specific interpretation of several biblical passages regarding the sanctity of blood. They believe that blood represents life and belongs solely to God. This belief is rooted in passages such as Genesis 9:4, where God forbids eating flesh with its lifeblood, and Leviticus 17:10, where God speaks against anyone who consumes blood. They also apply the New Testament instruction in Acts 15:28-29 to "abstain from blood" as a prohibition against taking blood into the body through medical transfusions. For them, receiving a transfusion is viewed as a serious violation of God's law that could jeopardize their relationship with Him.

In contrast, the historic Christian faith generally interprets these passages as dietary or ceremonial laws that do not apply to life-saving medical procedures. Most believers see blood transfusions as a compassionate use of medical technology to preserve the gift of life. While Jehovah's Witnesses focus on the physical avoidance of blood to maintain their standing, the Bible directs our attention to a far more significant reality: the shed blood of Jesus Christ.

God is holy and supreme (Isaiah 6:3), yet all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory, leading to spiritual separation from Him (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross, shedding His blood to pay the penalty for our sins (Romans 3:24; Romans 3:25; 1 Peter 1:18-19). He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is not earned by our own works or by following specific prohibitions, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does God pursue us?

The Bible reveals that God is not a distant observer, but a loving Creator who actively seeks a relationship with His people. From the very beginning, when humanity first turned away from Him in the Garden of Eden, it was God who took the first step to find them (Genesis 3:9).

In the New Testament, Jesus describes this heart of God through parables, such as the shepherd who leaves ninety-nine sheep to find the one that is lost (Luke 15:4–7). This pursuit is most clearly seen in the person of Jesus Christ. While we were still sinners and separated from God by our own rebellion, God demonstrated His love by sending His Son to die for us (Romans 5:8).

The Gospel is the ultimate story of God’s pursuit. Because God is perfectly holy and just, He cannot ignore sin, which separates us from Him. Yet, because He is rich in mercy, He took on human flesh in Jesus Christ. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved on the cross, taking the punishment for our sins upon Himself. He was buried and then rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Our salvation is not a result of us finding God through our own effort, but of God finding us and opening our hearts to believe by His grace alone (Ephesians 2:4–5; Ephesians 2:8–9). He loves us first, and it is His kindness that leads us to repentance (1 John 4:19; Romans 2:4). He continues to pursue His children throughout their lives, promising that nothing can separate them from His love (Romans 8:38–39).

You can turn to God now. Repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is God imaginary?

The Bible teaches that God is not a product of human imagination, but is the eternal, self-existent Creator of all that exists. While we cannot see Him with our physical eyes, He has made Himself known through what He has created and, most clearly, through His involvement in human history.

Scripture explains that the universe itself serves as evidence of God's existence and power. Psalm 19:1 states, "The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork." Similarly, Romans 1:20 teaches that God's invisible attributes, namely, his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made. The order, complexity, and beauty of the world point to a purposeful Designer rather than random chance or a figment of the mind.

The most profound evidence that God is real is that He entered our world in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus was not a myth or a concept, but a historical person who was truly God and truly man. John 1:1 and John 1:14 explain that "the Word was God" and "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us." By living a perfect life, performing miracles, and teaching with authority, Jesus revealed the character and reality of God to humanity. Colossians 1:15 describes Jesus as "the image of the invisible God."

The reality of God is also seen in the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all turned away from Him through sin, which is our rebellion against His good and right authority. This sin separates us from the source of life. However, because God is also merciful and loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to save us. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is not something we can earn through our own efforts or good works. It is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

Knowing God is not about following an imaginary idea, but entering into a real relationship with the living God through the work of His Holy Spirit. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What is a mandala in Hinduism?

In Hinduism, the word mandala comes from the Sanskrit term for circle. It is a geometric design that represents the cosmos or the universe, often used as a tool for meditation, prayer, and ritual. These symbols are intended to help a person focus their mind and represent the journey from the external world to an inner core or divine center. Mandalas are also significant in Hindu temple architecture, where the ground plan of a temple is often designed as a mandala to align the physical structure with spiritual and cosmic principles.

While these symbols reflect a deep human desire for order and connection with the divine, the Bible provides a different understanding of our relationship with the world and the Creator. Scripture teaches that God is the personal and holy Creator who exists independently of His creation (Genesis 1:1; Isaiah 40:25-26). He did not create us to find Him through geometric patterns or internal meditation, but through a personal relationship made possible by His Son.

The primary issue we face is not a lack of cosmic alignment, but sin, which creates a separation between us and God (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly just and holy, our efforts to reach Him through rituals or works are insufficient. However, because God is also merciful and loving, He provided the only way for us to be reconciled to Him.

Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died on the cross as a substitute for sinners. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His power over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith in Christ alone, not earned by any human effort or spiritual practice (Ephesians 2:8-9). Through Jesus, we do not just find a symbolic center, but we find the Prince of Peace who restores us to the Father (John 14:6; Isaiah 9:6).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What are your thoughts on how a person can find true peace with God?
Who is the angel Moroni?

The figure known as the angel Moroni is central to the history and theology of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, often referred to as Mormonism. According to the accounts of Joseph Smith, the founder of that movement, Moroni was a resurrected being who visited him several times beginning in 1823. Smith claimed that Moroni directed him to a set of golden plates buried in a hill in New York, which Smith then translated to produce the Book of Mormon.

In the context of historic, biblical Christianity, the angel Moroni is not a figure found in the Holy Scriptures. From a pastoral perspective, it is important to understand how the Bible guides us in evaluating such claims of new or additional revelations.

First, the Bible teaches that the faith was once for all delivered to the saints, as stated in Jude 1:3. This means that the revelation of God's plan for salvation is complete in the person and work of Jesus Christ and was fully recorded by His apostles and prophets in the Bible. Because the Bible is the inspired and sufficient Word of God, Christians are cautious about any teaching that claims to add to or change the message of the Gospel (2 Timothy 3:16-17).

Second, the Apostle Paul provided a stern warning to the early church regarding messages that differ from the Gospel originally preached. He wrote in Galatians 1:8, "But even if we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel to you than what we have preached to you, let him be accursed." This verse emphasizes that the message of salvation through Jesus Christ is the standard by which all spiritual claims must be tested. Furthermore, 2 Corinthians 11:14 reminds believers that "Satan himself transforms himself into an angel of light," which calls for great discernment when someone claims to have received a message from a supernatural being.

The core of the biblical Gospel is that God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or additional rituals we perform (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Because Jesus is the final and supreme revelation of God (Hebrews 1:1-2), we do not need further angelic visitations or new books of scripture to know how to be reconciled to God. I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life.

If you are exploring these topics, what has led you to look into the identity of Moroni or the claims of other scriptures?
Is God a moral monster?

The question of whether God is a "moral monster" often arises from a sincere struggle with difficult passages in the Bible, particularly in the Old Testament. When we look at accounts of divine judgment or ancient laws, it can be challenging to reconcile them with the God of love. However, from a biblical perspective, God is not only the author of morality but the very standard of what is good and just.

God's character is defined by His holiness, justice, and goodness (Psalm 145:17). Because He is perfectly holy, He cannot ignore sin or injustice. What we might perceive as harshness is often God’s righteous judgment against practices that are deeply destructive, such as the child sacrifice and systemic evil found in ancient cultures (Deuteronomy 12:31). It is also important to note that God is incredibly patient, often waiting hundreds of years for people to turn from their ways before He acts in judgment (Genesis 15:16; 2 Peter 3:9).

To call God a "monster" requires us to use an objective standard of "good" and "evil."
