If there is no God, morality is merely a matter of personal or cultural preference, and there would be no objective ground to judge anything as truly "monstrous." By evaluating God’s actions, we are actually acknowledging a moral law that points back to Him as the Lawgiver.

The ultimate answer to our questions about God’s character is found in Jesus Christ. Although we are all separated from God by our own sin and are rightfully subject to His judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23), God did not leave us without hope. Because of His great love, God the Son took on human nature, lived a perfect and sinless life, and willingly died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, demonstrating His power over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). On the cross, God’s perfect justice and His perfect love met. He did not ignore the debt of sin; He paid it Himself so that we could be reconciled to Him by grace through faith (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What specific parts of God's character or actions in the Bible do you find most difficult to understand?
What are the main arguments against limited atonement?

The doctrine of limited atonement, also known as particular redemption, is the belief that Christ died specifically and only for the elect. While this is a significant part of the Reformed tradition, many faithful Christians throughout history have raised scriptural arguments against it, preferring a view of universal or general atonement.

The most common argument involves the use of universal language in the New Testament. Critics of limited atonement point to passages that state Christ died for "the world" or for "all." For example, John 3:16 says that God so loved the world that He gave His only Son. Similarly, 1 John 2:2 states that Jesus is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world. Those who hold to general atonement argue that "the world" should be understood in its natural sense as the entire human race rather than just the world of the elect.

Another major argument is based on the revealed will of God for the salvation of all people. Scripture frequently describes God as desiring every person to come to repentance. In 1 Timothy 2:3-4, the Bible says that God our Savior desires all people to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth. Additionally, 2 Peter 3:9 explains that the Lord is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance. Critics argue that if Christ only died for a specific group, then God’s desire for the salvation of all would seem inconsistent with the provision He made on the cross.

There is also the argument regarding the sincere offer of the Gospel. If Christ did not die for every individual, some ask how the Gospel can be truly offered to every person. Proponents of general atonement believe that for the call to "repent and believe" to be authentic for any hearer, there must be a sacrifice already made on their behalf. They often cite Titus 2:11, which says the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation for all people.

Finally, some point to passages that suggest Christ died even for those who might ultimately reject Him or perish. In 2 Peter 2:1, the apostle speaks of false prophets who bring in destructive heresies, even denying the Master who bought them. This is often interpreted to mean that Christ’s purchasing work on the cross extended even to those who would turn away from Him. Hebrews 2:9 also states that Jesus tasted death for everyone.

While these doctrinal discussions are important for understanding the depth of God's work, the central truth of the Bible is that we are all in need of a Savior. God is perfectly holy, yet every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory, resulting in spiritual separation from Him. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him today?
Christmas Story — Day 5 — Did Jesus, the Messiah, come to save the Jews only?

While Jesus was born as a Jew to fulfill the specific promises God made to the nation of Israel, His mission was never intended for only one group of people. From the very beginning of the biblical story, God promised that through the offspring of Abraham, all the families of the earth would be blessed (Genesis 12:3).

The prophets also looked forward to a time when the Messiah would be more than just a local redeemer. God spoke through the prophet Isaiah, saying it was too small a thing for the Messiah only to restore the tribes of Jacob. Instead, He would be a light to the nations so that God's salvation might reach to the end of the earth (Isaiah 49:6).

When Jesus was a baby, a man named Simeon recognized this global purpose. Holding the child in his arms, he praised God that Jesus was prepared in the presence of all peoples, a light for revelation to the Gentiles and for glory to your people Israel (Luke 2:30-32). After His resurrection, Jesus made this explicit by commanding His followers to go and make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:19).

This is wonderful news because every person, regardless of their background or heritage, is separated from God by sin (Romans 3:23). God is perfectly holy and just, yet He is also rich in mercy. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die on the cross as a substitute for our sins. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, defeating death and proving He is Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or ethnic identity (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything in particular that makes it difficult for you to believe that God's love extends to you personally?
How does prayer work?

Prayer is the humble and joyful communication of a believer with God. It is not a magical formula to gain desired outcomes or a way to bend God's will to our own. Instead, it is a relational act where we express our dependence on Him, align our hearts with His purposes, and enjoy fellowship with our Creator.

The Bible teaches that prayer works through the unique roles of the Trinity. We pray to God the Father, in the name of God the Son, by the power of God the Holy Spirit. Because God is holy and we are sinful, our sin once separated us from Him (Isaiah 59:2). However, through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, the way to God has been opened. Jesus serves as our Great High Priest, allowing us to approach the throne of grace with confidence to find help in our time of need (Hebrews 4:14-16).

When we do not know how to pray as we should, the Holy Spirit helps us in our weakness, interceding for us according to the will of God (Romans 8:26-27). This means that prayer is a partnership where God Himself helps us communicate with Him.

While God is sovereign and already knows what we need before we ask (Matthew 6:8), He invites us to pray because He has ordained prayer as a primary means by which He works in the world and in our hearts. Prayer changes us by increasing our faith and conforming our desires to His. When we pray according to His will, we have the assurance that He hears us and will answer in His perfect timing and wisdom (1 John 5:14-15).

At its heart, the ability to pray is a fruit of the Gospel. We are all sinners who have fallen short of God's glory, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. It is only through faith in Christ that we are justified and adopted into God's family, giving us the right to call Him "Father."

If you have not yet experienced this relationship with God, you can turn to Him now. Repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

To grow in your prayer life, I encourage you to begin by reading the Psalms, which provide a beautiful vocabulary for talking to God in every season of life. You can also follow the pattern Jesus gave His disciples, often called the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:9-13), focusing on God's glory, His kingdom, our daily needs, and our need for forgiveness. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can pray with other believers, as the prayer of a righteous person has great power (James 5:16).
Where was God on September 11th?

God was exactly where He has always been: on His throne as the sovereign Lord of all creation and present with every person who was suffering. The Bible teaches that God is omnipresent, meaning there is no place where His presence is absent. Psalm 139:7-10. Even in the darkest moments of human history, God is not distant or indifferent.

He was near to the brokenhearted and those crushed in spirit, as Psalm 34:18 promises. He was with the victims in their final moments, with the first responders who sacrificed their lives, and with the families who began a long journey of grief. While it is difficult to understand why God allows such profound evil to occur, we know from Scripture that we live in a world fractured by sin and human rebellion. Genesis 3:1-19; Romans 5:12. God hates injustice and the shedding of innocent blood. Proverbs 6:16-17.

The most profound answer to the question of where God is during suffering is found in the person of Jesus Christ. God did not remain far away from our pain. Instead, He entered into it. Jesus is truly God and truly man, and He became a man of sorrows, acquainted with deepest grief. Isaiah 53:3. On the cross, He took upon Himself the weight of human sin and the agony of death so that evil would not have the final word.

This is the heart of the Gospel: God is holy and just, yet He is also merciful and loving. Because of our sin, we are separated from God, but He provided a way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Through His resurrection, He offers the hope of a restored world where He will one day wipe away every tear and make all things new. Revelation 21:4-5.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

In the face of such immense tragedy, what is it about God's character that you find most difficult to reconcile?
Is Jesus Calling a good book? Are there any doctrinal problems with Jesus Calling?

The book Jesus Calling by Sarah Young has been a source of comfort for many Christians because of its gentle tone and its emphasis on experiencing the presence of God. However, many pastors and theologians have raised significant doctrinal concerns regarding its format and content. When evaluating any spiritual resource, we must always look to the Bible as our ultimate and final authority for faith and practice, as it is the inspired and sufficient Word of God (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Timothy 3:17).

The primary concern many believers have is that the book is written in the first person, as if Jesus Himself is speaking directly to the reader. While the author stated that these were her personal reflections during quiet times, presenting them as the direct words of Christ can blur the line between human thoughts and divine revelation. The Bible warns us not to add to or take away from the words of Scripture (Revelation 22:18; Revelation 22:19). Because the canon of Scripture is closed, we must be very cautious about any book that purports to provide new, direct messages from God that are not found in the Bible.

A second concern involves the sufficiency of Scripture. The Bible contains everything we need for life and godliness (2 Peter 1:3). When we seek a "fresh" word from God outside of the Bible, we may inadvertently suggest that the Scriptures are not enough to sustain us. We are called to be like the Bereans, who examined the Scriptures daily to see if what they were being taught was true (Acts 17:11). 

Thirdly, some critics note that the "Jesus" presented in the book often speaks in a way that focuses heavily on personal peace and emotional comfort, sometimes at the expense of other essential biblical truths such as God's holiness, the reality of sin, and the necessity of repentance. While God is indeed a God of comfort (2 Corinthians 1:3), His primary work in our lives is to conform us to the image of His Son through the truth of the Gospel.

The true source of peace and intimacy with God is not found in extra-biblical messages, but in the person and work of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him (Isaiah 59:2). Because of His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). He is now exalted as Lord over all. 

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

If you are looking for a devotional, it is often safer to choose one that explains and applies specific passages of Scripture rather than one that speaks in the first-person voice of God. This ensures that your heart and mind remain anchored in the infallible Word of God. What has been your experience with using devotionals alongside your Bible reading?
Does the Bible prophesy the coming of Muhammad?

From a biblical and historic Christian perspective, the Bible does not prophesy the coming of Muhammad. While some seek to find references to him in the scriptures, Christian theology maintains that the prophecies often cited actually point to Jesus Christ or the Holy Spirit.

One passage frequently discussed is Deuteronomy 18:15, where Moses tells the Israelites, "The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your brothers. It is to him you shall listen." In the New Testament, the apostles explicitly identify Jesus as the fulfillment of this prophecy. For example, in Acts 3:22, Peter quotes this verse to demonstrate that Jesus is the promised prophet. Similarly, Stephen refers to this promise in Acts 7:37 when speaking about Christ. The phrase "from among you, from your brothers" is understood in the context of the Israelite tribes.

Another set of verses often mentioned are found in the Gospel of John, where Jesus speaks of the "Helper" or "Counselor" (the Paraclete). In John 14:16, Jesus says, "And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper, to be with you forever." However, Jesus identifies this Helper later in the same conversation, saying in John 14:26, "But the Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things and bring to your remembrance all that I have said to you." Because the Holy Spirit is described as being invisible to the world and dwelling within the believers, Christians believe this refers to the third person of the Trinity rather than a human messenger.

The Bible teaches that Jesus Christ is the final and supreme revelation of God. Hebrews 1:1-2 states, "Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed the heir of all things, through whom also he created the world." Because Jesus is God in the flesh, His life, death, and resurrection completed the work of salvation.

The heart of the biblical message is that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not earned by our own works or religious devotion, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What has led you to explore the relationship between the Bible and these prophecies?
What is the definition of the term Gnostic?

The term Gnostic comes from the Greek word gnosis, which means knowledge. In the context of early church history, Gnosticism refers to a diverse group of religious movements that gained prominence in the second and third centuries. These groups claimed to possess a secret, superior knowledge that was necessary for salvation, a concept that stands in direct opposition to the clear and public message of the biblical Gospel.

A central tenet of Gnosticism was dualism, the belief that the spiritual realm is entirely good while the physical, material world is inherently evil. Because they viewed matter as corrupt, Gnostics struggled to accept that the holy God could create a physical world or that Jesus Christ could truly come in human flesh. This led to many errors, such as the idea that the Creator of the physical world was a lesser, perhaps even malevolent, being rather than the supreme God.

The Bible addresses these ideas by affirming that God created all things and declared them "very good" in Genesis 1:31. Furthermore, the New Testament emphasizes the reality of the incarnation, stating that "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" in John 1:14. The apostle John wrote specifically to counter early Gnostic-like leanings, teaching that "every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God" in 1 John 4:2.

True salvation is not found in uncovering hidden or esoteric secrets, but in the public and historical work of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that all people have sinned and are separated from a holy God, but God, in His great love and mercy, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. This victory over sin and death is the foundation of our hope.

We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by reaching a higher level of intellectual or spiritual "knowledge." As it says in Ephesians 2:8–9, "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast."

You can turn to God even now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is generalism?

Generalism is a broad term that describes an approach, philosophy, or skill set characterized by a wide scope rather than a narrow specialization. Depending on the context, it can refer to professional life, ethics, or theology.

In a professional or educational sense, a generalist is an individual who possesses a diverse range of knowledge and skills across many different areas. This is often contrasted with a specialist, who focuses deeply on one specific subject. A generalist often excels at seeing the big picture and connecting ideas from various fields.

In the study of ethics, generalism refers to the belief that there are general moral rules or principles that should guide human behavior. These rules are usually considered valid in most circumstances, though some generalists believe they can be set aside in extreme or conflicting situations. This position often sits between legalism, which follows rules strictly regardless of context, and situationalism, which argues that there are no fixed rules and that love or the specific situation should determine every action.

In theology, the concept of generalism most often arises in discussions regarding how God relates to and provides for humanity. Two key areas are General Revelation and General Atonement.

General Revelation is the truth about God that is available to all people, at all times, and in all places. The Bible teaches that God reveals His existence, power, and divine nature through the physical world He created and through the human conscience. Psalm 19:1-2; Romans 1:19-20; Romans 2:14-15. While this general knowledge is enough to show that God exists and that we are accountable to Him, it is not enough to save us from our sins, as it does not communicate the specific message of the Gospel.

General Atonement, often called unlimited atonement, is the theological view that Jesus Christ died for the sins of every person who has ever lived. Those who hold this view believe that Christ’s sacrifice was sufficient for all, even though its saving effects are only applied to those who believe. 1 John 2:2; 1 Timothy 2:4; 1 Timothy 4:10. This is often contrasted with particular redemption, which is the view that Christ’s death was intended specifically for the elect.

While we can see God’s hand generally in the beauty of a sunset or the complexity of the human body, the most important truth He has revealed is specific. The Bible teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. Romans 3:23. Our sin separates us from a holy God, but God, in His mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to bridge that gap. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Salvation is not found through general spiritual feelings or broad moral efforts, but through a specific person. Jesus said, I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. John 14:6. There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved. Acts 4:12.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting in Jesus Christ as your personal Savior today?
How should Christians view Men Going Their Own Way (MGTOW)?

The Men Going Their Own Way (MGTOW) movement often arises from a place of deep frustration, personal hurt, or a desire for self-preservation in the face of perceived risks in modern relationships and legal systems. When evaluating this movement, Christians should look to the Word of God as the ultimate authority for how we are to relate to others, manage our desires, and view the purpose of our lives.

From a biblical perspective, we must first acknowledge the reality of the pain that many men in this movement have experienced. We live in a fallen world where sin has corrupted every human institution, including marriage and the legal system (Genesis 3:16-19). However, the Christian response to a broken world is not to retreat into self-protection or isolation, but to look toward the restorative work of Jesus Christ.

Scripture teaches that God created humanity as male and female in His own image, and He declared that it was not good for man to be alone (Genesis 1:27; Genesis 2:18). While the Bible affirms that singleness is a legitimate and even gifted state when used to serve the Lord without distraction (1 Corinthians 7:32-35), it distinguishes this from a lifestyle motivated by resentment or a rejection of God's design for community. If a man chooses to remain single, the biblical motivation should be the advancement of the Gospel rather than the avoidance of risk or the pursuit of self-interest.

Furthermore, the movement sometimes fosters a generalized bitterness or negative view toward women. As followers of Christ, we are called to honor all people and to love our neighbors as ourselves (1 Peter 2:17; Matthew 22:39). Any philosophy that encourages a heart of hardness or distance from half of the human race is contrary to the command to put away all bitterness, wrath, and anger (Ephesians 4:31). Men are specifically called to treat women with dignity and respect, recognizing them as fellow image-bearers of God.

Ultimately, the desire for security and peace that many seek through MGTOW cannot be fully realized through self-reliance or social withdrawal. True security is found only in a right relationship with God. Our fundamental problem is not a biased society or difficult relationships, but our own sin which separates us from a holy and just God (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). God, in His great mercy and love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His sacrifice, Christ defeated sin and death, offering reconciliation to all who believe.

Salvation is not earned by our own efforts or by protecting ourselves from earthly trouble, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). When we trust in Him, we are given a new heart and a new purpose that moves us away from fear and toward sacrificial love.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

How does the idea of finding your security in Christ's love rather than in your own self-protection resonate with you?
What is corporate election?

Corporate election is the theological perspective that God's choice, or "election," primarily concerns a category or a community of people rather than a preselected list of specific individuals. In this view, God's elective purpose is focused on the corporate body of the Church "in Christ."

In this framework, Jesus Christ is understood as the primary "Elect" or "Chosen One" of God, as described in Isaiah 42:1 and 1 Peter 2:4-6. According to this perspective, God chose the Church as a corporate entity before the foundation of the world, which we see in Ephesians 1:4. Individuals become part of the elect not by being picked individually from eternity past, but by being united to Jesus Christ through faith. 

An illustration often used to explain this is that of a ship or an airplane. The "vessel" has a determined destination, and anyone who chooses to board the vessel will arrive at that destination. In the same way, the corporate view suggests that God has chosen the "vessel" of the Church, and all who enter into Christ by faith share in the corporate election and destiny of that body.

This view draws heavily on the biblical pattern of God choosing groups for His purposes. In the Old Testament, God chose the nation of Israel as a whole to be His people (Deuteronomy 7:6; Isaiah 41:8-9). In the New Testament, this corporate language is applied to the Church. For example, 1 Peter 2:9 describes believers as "a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession." The focus here is on the identity and mission of the group as it relates to God's redemptive plan.

While believers may hold different views on the specific mechanics of election, the core of our hope is the same: the person and work of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy and just, but every human being has sinned and fallen short of His standard (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment (Romans 6:23). 

However, God is also rich in mercy and love. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature, live a sinless life, and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the dead, proving His victory over sin and death and His identity as Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift given by grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Did Jesus speak in tongues?

The Bible does not record any instance of Jesus speaking in tongues. In the New Testament, the phenomenon of speaking in tongues is primarily associated with the outpouring of the Holy Spirit on the Church, beginning on the day of Pentecost (Acts 2:1–4).

While Jesus did not practice what we call the gift of tongues, His ministry was characterized by the full power and presence of the Holy Spirit. He spoke with divine authority, and He stated that the words He spoke were given to Him directly by the Father (John 12:49–50). In the Gospel of Mark, Jesus did mention that those who believe in Him would "speak in new tongues" (Mark 16:17), pointing toward the spiritual gifts that would be given to His followers after His ascension.

The gift of tongues, as described in the letters to the early churches, served as a sign of the Spirit's presence and a means of building up the body of Christ (1 Corinthians 14:2–5). Jesus, however, is the very source of the Spirit's power. His primary mission was not to exercise the spiritual gifts given to the Church, but to fulfill the Law and the Prophets through His life, death, and resurrection.

This leads us to the heart of why Jesus came. God is perfectly holy, but human beings are separated from Him by sin. Because of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our own works or spiritual experiences, but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What do you feel is the most significant thing you have learned about Jesus so far?
What does the Bible say about dealing with regrets?

It is deeply painful to look back at choices, words, or missed opportunities and feel the weight of "what if" or the sting of shame. The Bible acknowledges that regret is a part of the human experience, but it distinguishes between a regret that leads to despair and a regret that leads to life.

In 2 Corinthians 7:10, the apostle Paul writes, "For godly grief produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, whereas worldly grief produces death." Worldly regret often centers on the self, leading to a cycle of shame and hopelessness. Godly regret, however, is a realization that we have fallen short of God’s standards, which is intended to drive us toward His mercy.

The Bible provides several truths to help believers process and move past regret:

First, for those who are in Christ, God offers total and complete forgiveness. When we confess our sins and the ways we have failed, He does not hold them against us. Scripture says in Psalm 103:12, "as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us." He also promises in Micah 7:19 that He will "tread our iniquities underfoot" and "cast all our sins into the depths of the sea."

Second, God is a redeemer who can use even our past failures for His glory and our growth. Romans 8:28 promises, "And we know that for those who love God all things work together for good, for those who are called according to his purpose." While this does not mean that our past actions were good, it means that God is powerful enough to weave them into a story of grace and restoration.

Third, the Bible encourages us to focus on the present and the future rather than being paralyzed by the past. Paul, who had much to regret in his own life, having once persecuted the church, wrote in Philippians 3:13-14, "But one thing I do: forgetting what lies behind and straining forward to what lies ahead, I press on toward the goal for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus."

The ultimate answer to the burden of regret is the Gospel. Every person has sinned and fallen short of the holiness of God (Romans 3:23). This separation from God is the root of our deepest regrets. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on a cross as a substitute for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice, He paid the debt we could not pay and broke the power of sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our efforts or by "making up" for our past, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him with your past and your future today?

If you are struggling with heavy regrets, I encourage you to speak with a pastor or a mature believer in a local church who can walk alongside you. You can also pray directly to God, asking Him to help you believe His promises of forgiveness. Scripture tells us in Hebrews 4:16, "Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need."
Monergism vs. synergism - which view is correct?

The question of whether salvation is a work of God alone (monergism) or a cooperative effort between God and man (synergism) is one of the most significant discussions in Christian history. To understand which view is correct, we must look to the witness of Scripture regarding the condition of the human heart and the nature of God's grace.

Monergism comes from two Greek words meaning "one" and "work." This view maintains that God is the sole agent who acts to bring a person from spiritual death to spiritual life. The biblical foundation for this is the reality that, apart from Christ, we are spiritually "dead in the trespasses and sins" according to Ephesians 2:1. A person who is spiritually dead cannot choose to cooperate with God any more than a physical corpse can cooperate with a doctor. Therefore, God must first perform a miracle of regeneration, giving us a new heart and the gift of faith. As it says in Ezekiel 36:26-27, "And I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh. And I will put my Spirit within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes and be careful to obey my rules."

Synergism, meaning "working together," suggests that God offers grace to all people, but this grace is only effective if the individual chooses to cooperate with it. While this view rightly emphasizes human responsibility, it can inadvertently suggest that the deciding factor in salvation is something within the human will rather than the sovereign grace of God. However, the Bible teaches that our will is naturally resistant to God. Jesus said in John 6:44, "No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him. And I will raise him up on the last day." Furthermore, Romans 9:16 states, "So then it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God, who has mercy."

The monergistic view ensures that all the glory for our salvation goes to God alone. If we contributed even a small "yes" from our own natural ability, we would have a reason to boast. But Scripture says in Ephesians 2:8-9, "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast." In this view, God does not force the will; rather, He changes the heart so that the sinner now desires Christ and freely chooses Him.

At the heart of this discussion is the glorious Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him and deserving of His just judgment. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life of obedience that we could never live. He died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking upon Himself the punishment for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord of all. Salvation is not a reward for our efforts or our cooperation, but a free gift received through faith in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider these truths, I encourage you to reflect on your own standing before the Lord. What do you believe is the basis for your hope of eternal life?
Did Old Testament believers have eternal security?

Old Testament believers did have eternal security because their salvation, like ours, was founded on the unchanging character of God and His gracious promises. While they lived before the incarnation of Jesus, they were saved by looking forward to the promised Redeemer, just as we look back to His finished work on the cross.

The Bible teaches that justification has always been by grace alone through faith alone. In Genesis 15:6, it says of Abraham, "And he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness." The New Testament clarifies that Abraham was not justified by his own works or by keeping the law, but by faith in God (Romans 4:1–5; Galatians 3:6–9). Because their standing before God was based on His grace and their faith in His promise, their security rested in God’s power to keep them rather than their own ability to remain perfect.

God’s character is the ultimate guarantee of a believer's security. He says in Malachi 3:6, "For I the LORD do not change; therefore you, O children of Jacob, are not consumed." Because God is sovereign, faithful, and immutable, His purposes for those He calls cannot be frustrated. This is echoed in Psalm 37:28, which states, "For the LORD loves justice; he will not forsake his saints. They are preserved forever, but the children of the wicked shall be cut off."

Furthermore, the New Testament explains that the death of Jesus Christ provided redemption for the sins committed under the first covenant (Hebrews 9:15). The animal sacrifices of the Old Testament were "shadows" pointing to the "substance," which is Christ (Colossians 2:17; Hebrews 10:1–4). The Old Testament saints were kept by the power of God as they waited for the fulfillment of the promise, remaining "persuaded" of God's truth even when they did not see the final fulfillment in their lifetime (Hebrews 11:13–16).

This truth highlights the consistency of the Gospel throughout history. God is perfectly holy, yet all of us have sinned and are separated from Him. In His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is not earned by our efforts but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that the church is the bride of Christ?

The description of the church as the Bride of Christ is a beautiful biblical metaphor that illustrates the deep, intimate, and permanent relationship between Jesus and those who follow Him. It highlights God's sacrificial love, the commitment He has to His people, and the future hope of eternal union with Him.

In the Old Testament, God often described His relationship with Israel in terms of a marriage, identifying Himself as a husband to His people (Isaiah 54:5). In the New Testament, this imagery is fulfilled in Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul explains this mystery by teaching that human marriage is actually a picture of the greater reality of Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:31-32).

There are several key aspects to what it means for the church to be Christ's bride:

First, it signifies Christ’s sacrificial and protective love. Just as a husband is called to love his wife as his own body, Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her (Ephesians 5:25-28). This love is not based on the worthiness of the church, but on the grace and character of the Groom.

Second, it points to our purification and sanctification. The Bible describes Jesus as the one who cleanses His bride by the washing of water with the word, so that He might present her to Himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish (Ephesians 5:26-27). This process happens as the Holy Spirit works in the lives of believers to make them more like Jesus.

Third, it emphasizes a covenantal union. To be the bride of Christ means to be joined to Him in a legal and spiritual bond that cannot be broken. Believers are betrothed to one husband, Christ, and are called to live with pure devotion to Him (2 Corinthians 11:2).

Finally, it points to a future celebration. The story of the Bible concludes with the marriage supper of the Lamb, a great feast where the church is finally and fully united with Jesus in a renewed creation (Revelation 19:7-9). In that day, God will dwell with His people, and all sorrow and death will be done away with (Revelation 21:2-4).

The reason this metaphor is so powerful is that it is grounded in the Gospel. While we were yet sinners and unfaithful to God, the holy and perfect Son of God took on human nature to rescue us. Because of our sin, we were separated from God's holiness and deserved His judgment. However, Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers salvation by grace alone through faith alone to all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can I believe in the goodness of God when there is so much evil in the world?

It is deeply painful to look at the suffering and injustice in our world and wonder where God is. Your question is one that many of the most faithful people in the Bible also asked, such as Job or the writers of the Psalms. It is an honest struggle to reconcile a world full of brokenness with the belief in a God who is both all-powerful and all-good. It is okay to feel the weight of this tension, as God is not afraid of our hard questions.

To begin to understand this, we must look at how evil entered the world. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and good (Psalm 34:8; 1 John 1:5). He created a world that was very good, but evil entered through human rebellion and sin (Genesis 1:31; Genesis 3:1-19; Romans 5:12). Much of the evil we see today is the result of human choices and the reality of living in a world that is fractured by that initial fall.

However, God did not remain distant from our pain. The most powerful evidence of God's goodness is that He entered the world in the person of Jesus Christ to suffer alongside us. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and yet suffered the ultimate injustice of the cross (Philippians 2:5-8). He did not answer the problem of evil with a philosophical argument alone, but with His own body and blood.

The heart of the Christian faith, the Gospel, provides the clearest picture of God’s goodness in the face of evil. God is perfectly holy, yet every human being has sinned and is separated from Him (Romans 3:23). Because God is just, He must punish sin. Yet, because He is merciful and loving, He provided the way for our rescue. Jesus Christ died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross, taking the punishment we deserved. He was buried and then rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin, death, and evil (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now the exalted Lord who offers forgiveness and eternal life to all who turn to Him. This salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or effort of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Believing in God's goodness does not mean ignoring evil, but trusting that God is working through it to bring about a greater restoration. God promises that one day Christ will return to judge the living and the dead and make all things new (Revelation 21:1-5). In that day, He will wipe away every tear and put an end to all suffering. Until then, we can find comfort in the fact that He is a "man of sorrows and acquainted with grief" (Isaiah 53:3), and He promises to be near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18).

When you look at the suffering around you, what specific aspects of evil make it most difficult for you to trust in God's character?
What does "communion of the saints" mean in the ancient creeds?

The phrase "communion of the saints," as found in the ancient Apostles' Creed, refers to the spiritual union and fellowship that all believers share with one another because of their common union with Jesus Christ. This concept encompasses several layers of Christian reality, spanning both the physical and spiritual realms.

First, it signifies that every person who belongs to Christ is part of one body. Because believers are united to Christ, the Head, they are also united to one another. This union is created by the Holy Spirit and transcends denominational, cultural, and geographic boundaries. The Bible describes this as being many members of one body (1 Corinthians 12:12-13; Romans 12:4-5). In this communion, believers share in the same spiritual "goods," including the grace of God, the Word of God, the sacraments or ordinances, and mutual prayer and support.

Second, the communion of the saints includes the fellowship between the "church militant," those believers currently living on earth, and the "church triumphant," those who have died in the faith and are now in the presence of the Lord. This doctrine affirms that death does not sever the bond of the Spirit. Believers on earth are encouraged by the "great cloud of witnesses" who have gone before them (Hebrews 12:1). It highlights a continuity of worship and life among all who are redeemed by the blood of Christ, whether they are in heaven or on earth (Hebrews 12:22-24).

Third, this communion involves the practical duty of believers to love and serve one another. As members of the same family, Christians are called to bear one another's burdens and use their diverse spiritual gifts for the benefit of the whole community (Galatians 6:2; 1 Peter 4:10). It is a fellowship of mutual care, where the joys and sufferings of one are shared by all (1 Corinthians 12:26).

Being part of this "communion of the saints" is only possible through a right relationship with God. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), while all human beings have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from God and deserves His judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Jesus Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life.

What is your current understanding of your relationship with God?
How can I gain a passion for winning souls?

It is a beautiful thing to desire a heart that beats for what God cares about. A passion for sharing the Gospel is not something we manufacture through sheer willpower, but is a fruit of spending time with the Savior and seeing the world through His eyes.

To grow in this passion, begin by contemplating the immense love God has shown you. In 2 Corinthians 5:14, the Apostle Paul says that the love of Christ controls or compels us. When we truly grasp the depth of our own sin and the magnitude of the forgiveness we have received through Jesus, our hearts naturally overflow with a desire for others to know that same peace. If we find our zeal fading, it is often because we have lost sight of the glory of the Gospel in our own lives.

You might also pray for the same compassion that Jesus felt. In Matthew 9:36, it says that when Jesus saw the crowds, He had compassion for them because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd. Ask the Lord to give you eyes to see people not just as neighbors, coworkers, or strangers, but as eternal souls created by God who are currently separated from Him.

Understanding the urgency of the message we carry is also vital. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3) and that all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin creates a separation that we cannot bridge on our own. However, the good news is that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace, received through faith in Christ alone and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8–9).

If you have not done so, you can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that feels like the greatest challenge for you in sharing this hope with others?

Practically, you can ask the Holy Spirit for boldness and opportunities. In Acts 4:31, the early believers prayed for boldness, and the place where they were gathered was shaken as they were filled with the Spirit to speak the word of God. Start by praying for specific people by name, asking God to soften their hearts and to give you a natural "bridge" in conversation to speak of Christ. You do not have to be an expert in theology to share; you simply need to be a witness to what God has done. 

Consider finding a mature believer in your local church who has a heart for evangelism and ask if you can join them in their ministry or simply talk with them about their experiences. Growing in the spiritual disciplines of prayer and reading the Word will also keep your heart sensitive to the prompting of the Spirit. As you rest in God's grace and use the means He has provided, He will faithfully refine your desires and give you a growing love for the lost.
What is reprobation? What does it mean to be reprobate?

Reprobation is a sobering biblical concept that refers to God's sovereign decision to pass over certain individuals, leaving them to the consequences of their own sin and rebellion. To be reprobate means to be rejected or cast away, specifically in relation to God's saving grace. It describes a state where a person is abandoned by God to their own sinful desires because of their persistent rejection of His truth.

In the New Testament, the term often translates from the Greek word "adokimos," which means failing to pass a test, being disqualified, or being found worthless. For example, Romans 1:28 describes God giving people over to a "reprobate mind" (sometimes translated as a debased or depraved mind) because they did not see fit to acknowledge Him. This condition is characterized by a hardened heart and a loss of spiritual discernment, where a person becomes increasingly callous toward the things of God and follows after unrighteousness.

It is important to understand that God is perfectly holy and just. He does not force anyone to sin, but in reprobation, He justly withholds the grace that would otherwise restrain a person's sinful nature. Romans 9:22 speaks of God's desire to show His wrath and make His power known by enduring with much patience "vessels of wrath prepared for destruction." This highlights God's supreme authority to demonstrate His justice and holiness.

In 2 Timothy 3:8, those who oppose the truth are described as "men of corrupt minds, reprobate concerning the faith." This represents a state of active resistance to God and His Word. While this doctrine is difficult to process, it underscores the gravity of sin and the absolute necessity of God's intervening grace for salvation.

While the doctrine of reprobation reminds us of God's absolute holiness and our deserved judgment, the Gospel offers the only way of escape from this condition. God is the holy Creator who cannot ignore sin, and all humanity has fallen short of His glory through sin and rebellion, as noted in Romans 3:23. Because of this separation, we face His just wrath. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment that sinners deserved. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or merits. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What thoughts or questions do you have about God's mercy in light of His justice?
What does the Bible say about how to get baptized / how to baptize?

Baptism is a beautiful and significant act of obedience for a believer, serving as a public profession of faith in Jesus Christ and an outward symbol of the inward change God has wrought in the soul. The Bible provides several principles regarding who should be baptized and how the ordinance is to be carried out.

The New Testament pattern for baptism begins with a personal response to the Gospel. In the book of Acts, we see that those who heard the message, repented of their sins, and believed in Christ were the ones baptized. For example, after Peter preached at Pentecost, the people were told to repent and be baptized (Acts 2:38). Only those who received his word were baptized (Acts 2:41). This indicates that baptism is intended for those who have consciously placed their trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of their sins.

Regarding the method, the word used in the original Greek text of the New Testament is *baptizo*, which literally means to dip or immerse. The biblical accounts of baptism consistently point toward immersion in water. When Jesus was baptized, He came up out of the water (Matthew 3:16). Similarly, when Philip baptized the Ethiopian eunuch, they both went down into the water (Acts 8:38-39). 

This physical act of immersion beautifully illustrates the spiritual reality of what has happened to the believer. As the apostle Paul explains, being buried with Christ in baptism and being raised up out of the water represents our death to sin and our resurrection to walk in a new life through the power of the Holy Spirit (Romans 6:3-4; Colossians 2:12).

When performing a baptism, Jesus gave a specific command regarding the words to be spoken. He instructed His followers to baptize in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). This Trinitarian formula acknowledges the fullness of God’s character and His work in salvation.

If you are considering baptism, the first step is to ensure you have a personal relationship with Jesus Christ. The Gospel is the good news that although we are all sinners separated from a holy God, God loved us so much that He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8-9).

I would encourage you to speak with the leaders or elders of a local, Bible-believing church. Baptism is typically administered within the context of the local church community, as it marks your identification with the body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12:13). They can guide you through the process, hear your testimony of faith, and celebrate this milestone with you.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that might be holding you back from fully trusting Him today?
What is prevenient grace?

Prevenient grace refers to the grace of God that precedes any human action or decision. The word prevenient comes from a Latin root meaning to come before. In Christian theology, this concept describes the work of the Holy Spirit in a person's life that prepares their heart to hear and respond to the Gospel.

Because of the fall of man, every person is born with a sinful nature and is spiritually dead, as described in Ephesians 2:1 and Romans 3:10-12. This state of sin makes it impossible for a person to seek God or choose salvation on their own initiative. Prevenient grace is the divine influence that "comes before" a person's faith, acting to convict the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment, as seen in John 16:8.

There are different perspectives on how this grace operates among various Christian traditions:

In the Arminian and Wesleyan traditions, prevenient grace is believed to be extended to all people. It is thought to partially restore human free will, which was damaged by sin, enabling any individual to either accept or reject the offer of salvation in Christ. Proponents often point to Titus 2:11, which says the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation for all people.

In the Reformed or Calvinist tradition, while "common grace" is recognized as God’s kindness to all people (Matthew 5:45; Acts 14:17), the specific grace that leads to salvation is seen as "effectual" or "irresistible." This means that when God calls someone to Himself, His grace successfully and certainly brings that person to faith because He sovereignly regenerates their heart so they will freely and joyfully choose Him, as noted in John 6:37 and John 6:44.

Despite these differences in interpretation, both views agree on a fundamental truth: no one can come to God unless God first reaches out to them. Salvation is entirely a work of God's grace from beginning to end. It is not something earned by human merit or effort, for it is by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast, as stated in Ephesians 2:8-9.

The heart of this grace is found in the person and work of Jesus Christ. God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, as Romans 3:23 explains. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was raised bodily on the third day, conquering sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that Jesus died for our sins?

The message that Jesus died for our sins is the heart of the Christian faith, often called the Gospel or "good news." To understand what this means, we must first look at the character of God and the condition of humanity.

God is perfectly holy, righteous, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Habakkuk 1:13). Because He is just, He cannot simply ignore wrongdoing or leave sin unpunished. However, the Bible teaches that every human being has sinned and fallen short of God's glory (Romans 3:23). This sin creates a barrier between us and God, and the legal consequence of that sin is death and eternal separation from Him (Romans 6:23).

Because of His great love and mercy, God provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him without compromising His justice (John 3:16). Jesus Christ, who is eternally God the Son, took on human nature and lived the perfect, sinless life that we could never live (John 1:1; John 1:14; Hebrews 4:15).

When Jesus died on the cross, He acted as a substitute for sinners. This is often called penal substitutionary atonement. It means that He took our place, taking the guilt of our sins upon Himself and enduring the full weight of the judgment and wrath that we deserved. As 1 Peter 2:24 explains, "He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. By his wounds you have been healed." By bearing our punishment, Jesus satisfied the demands of God's justice so that we could be forgiven (Romans 5:8; 2 Corinthians 5:21).

The work of Christ did not end at the grave. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the dead, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming that His sacrifice was sufficient and accepted by God the Father (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4). Because of His finished work, salvation is offered to us as a free gift of grace. We are not saved by our own efforts, religious rituals, or good works, but through faith alone in Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Jesus is the only mediator between God and man, and there is no other name by which we can be saved (John 14:6; 1 Timothy 2:5; Acts 4:12).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive complete forgiveness and eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
What is Calvinism, and is it biblical?

Calvinism, often referred to as Reformed Theology, is a system of biblical interpretation that emphasizes the absolute sovereignty of God in all things, particularly in the salvation of humanity. Named after the French reformer John Calvin, this perspective seeks to ground every aspect of faith and life in the glory and will of God.

To understand Calvinism, many people look to an acronym known as TULIP, which summarizes the "five points" of Calvinism formulated at the Synod of Dort in response to different theological views at the time.

Total Depravity teaches that as a result of the fall of man, every part of human nature (reason, will, and emotions) has been corrupted by sin. This does not mean people are as evil as they could possibly be, but rather that no one is capable of choosing God or saving themselves apart from His divine intervention (Romans 3:10-12; Ephesians 2:1).

Unconditional Election suggests that before the foundation of the world, God chose to save a specific group of people not based on any foreseen merit or faith in them, but solely according to His sovereign good pleasure (Ephesians 1:4-5; Romans 9:11).

Limited Atonement, or Particular Redemption, is the belief that Christ’s death on the cross was intended to effectively secure the salvation of those whom the Father had chosen. While the value of Christ’s sacrifice is infinite and sufficient for all, Calvinists believe its specific design was to redeem His sheep (John 10:11; John 17:9).

Irresistible Grace describes the work of the Holy Spirit in which He draws the chosen sinner to Christ. This grace does not force someone against their will, but rather changes the heart so that the sinner now desires to come to Christ freely and gladly (John 6:44; Titus 3:5).

Perseverance of the Saints asserts that those whom God has truly called and regenerated will be kept by His power and will continue in faith until the end. Their security rests not in their own strength, but in the faithfulness of God to complete the work He began (Philippians 1:6; Romans 8:38-39).

Whether Calvinism is considered biblical is a subject of significant discussion among faithful Christians. Those who hold to these views believe they are the most consistent way to honor the biblical descriptions of God's power and the depth of human sin. Other sincere believers, such as those from the Arminian tradition, interpret many of these same passages differently, emphasizing human responsibility and the universal offer of the Gospel. Both groups typically agree on the "essentials" of the faith, such as the Trinity, the deity of Christ, and salvation by grace.

At the heart of any discussion about salvation is the reality of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we deserve separation from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, and not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8-9). Regardless of where one lands on the specific points of Calvinism, the Bible calls everyone to respond to this truth. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

What is your current understanding of how a person is made right with God?
Who was Ishtar, and is there any connection between Ishtar and Easter?

Ishtar was the ancient Mesopotamian goddess of love, war, and fertility. She was a central figure in the Akkadian, Assyrian, and Babylonian pantheons and was often associated with the planet Venus. In the Bible, there are references to the "Queen of Heaven," which many scholars identify as Ishtar or the closely related Canaanite goddess Astarte (Jeremiah 7:18; Jeremiah 44:17–19). 

The idea that the word "Easter" is derived from "Ishtar" is a common misconception, often based on the fact that the names sound similar. However, they come from entirely different linguistic and historical roots. The name Ishtar is Semitic in origin. In contrast, the English word "Easter" is Germanic. According to the eighth-century monk and historian Bede, the word comes from the Old English "Ēastre," which was the name of a Germanic goddess associated with the spring and the dawn. This root is related to the word "east," signifying the direction of the rising sun.

Furthermore, in almost every language other than English and German, the word for this holiday is a variation of the Hebrew word "Pesach," or Passover. For example, it is "Pâques" in French, "Pascua" in Spanish, and "Pascha" in Greek and Latin. This reflects the historical reality that Jesus was crucified and rose again during the Jewish Passover. The Christian celebration is rooted in the historical events of the life of Jesus Christ rather than pagan mythology.

The significance of this season is found in the bodily resurrection of Jesus, which is the foundation of the Christian faith. As the Apostle Paul explains, if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is useless and so is our faith (1 Corinthians 15:14). 

The Gospel message declares that because God is holy and supreme, and because all humans have sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are naturally separated from Him. In His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord. We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or merit.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What questions do you have about the historical evidence for the resurrection of Jesus?
Who are the Alawites, and what do they believe?

The Alawites are a minority religious group primarily located in Syria, with smaller populations in Lebanon and Turkey. They emerged in the 9th century as an offshoot of Shia Islam, founded by Muhammad ibn Nusayr. Because they have historically faced persecution, many of their core beliefs are kept secret, a practice known as "taqiyya," making them an esoteric or "closed" faith.

Their belief system is syncretistic, meaning it blends elements of Shia Islam, Gnosticism, and even some historical Christian traditions. Key aspects of Alawite doctrine include:

The Divinity of Ali: Unlike mainstream Muslims who revere Ali as the cousin and son in law of Muhammad, Alawites believe Ali was an incarnation of the Divine. They often speak of a triad consisting of the "Essence" (Ali), the "Name" (Muhammad), and the "Gate" (Salman al-Farisi).

Reincarnation: Alawites believe in metempsychosis, or the migration of the soul. They hold that souls are born multiple times, and their behavior in one life determines their status in the next. According to their tradition, a soul can eventually be purified and return to the stars from which they believe humans originated.

Christian Influences: Alawites observe several Christian holidays, including Christmas, Epiphany, and Palm Sunday. They also use bread and wine in a ritual that bears some outward resemblance to the Lord’s Supper, though its theological meaning differs from the Christian sacrament.

Rejection of Sharia: They do not generally follow the traditional Five Pillars of Islam in the same way Sunnis or Shias do. For instance, they do not have mosques and often do not practice the standard five daily prayers or the pilgrimage to Mecca, believing instead that these requirements have symbolic rather than literal meanings.

As we look at these beliefs through the lens of the Bible, we see significant differences from the historic Christian faith. While the Alawites incorporate elements of various religions, the Gospel of Jesus Christ stands unique in its claims and its promise of salvation.

The Bible teaches that there is only one God, eternally existing in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). Unlike the Alawite view of reincarnation, Scripture tells us that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment (Hebrews 9:27).

Furthermore, the central message of Christianity is that we are all separated from a holy God by our sin (Romans 3:23). We cannot save ourselves through multiple lifetimes or secret knowledge. Instead, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14). Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is not something earned or achieved over many lives, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why is sola fide important?

The doctrine of *sola fide*, or "faith alone," is vital because it protects the purity of the Gospel and ensures that all glory for salvation goes to God rather than to human effort. It is the teaching that we are declared righteous in God's sight only through faith in Jesus Christ, apart from any works of the law or personal merit. This truth is the foundation of the believer's peace, assurance, and relationship with God.

First, *sola fide* is important because it rightly recognizes the depth of human sin and the perfection of God's holiness. Scripture teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly just and holy, even our best works are stained by sin and cannot satisfy His righteous requirements (Isaiah 64:6). If salvation depended even partially on our own goodness, no one could ever be saved because no one can meet God's perfect standard.

Second, this doctrine upholds the sufficiency of Jesus Christ's work. On the cross, Jesus took the punishment for our sins and credited His perfect righteousness to those who believe. This is often called the "Great Exchange." The Bible says that for our sake God made Him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God (2 Corinthians 5:21). When we say salvation is by faith alone, we are saying that Jesus did everything necessary to reconcile us to God. Adding our own works to His finished work would imply that His sacrifice was not enough.

Third, *sola fide* provides the believer with true assurance and rest. If our standing with God were based on our performance, we would live in constant fear of whether we have done enough or if our latest sin has separated us from His love. However, since justification is by faith alone, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ (Romans 5:1). Our security rests on Christ's unchanging character and finished work rather than our fluctuating behavior.

Finally, this doctrine humbles us and leads us to worship. It removes all grounds for boasting, as salvation is a gift of grace that we receive with empty hands. Ephesians 2:8-9 states that for by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast. This leads to a life of "good works" that are not done to earn God's favor, but are done out of gratitude for the favor He has already freely given us in Christ.

The core of the Gospel is that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). God is holy and must judge sin, but in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not earned by being a good person or following religious rules; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
Does the Bible say anything about clairvoyance?

While the Bible does not use the specific word clairvoyance, it frequently addresses the practice of seeking hidden knowledge through supernatural means outside of God's revelation. In the Scriptures, these activities are categorized as divination, fortune telling, or consulting mediums.

The Bible consistently warns believers to avoid these practices. In the Old Testament, God provided clear instructions to His people regarding those who claimed to have such abilities. Deuteronomy 18:10-12 states that there shall not be found among you anyone who practices divination or tells fortunes or interprets omens, or a sorcerer or a charmer or a medium or a necromancer or one who inquires of the dead, for whoever does these things is an abomination to the Lord. Similarly, Leviticus 19:31 commands people not to turn to mediums or necromancers and not to seek them out, so as to be defiled by them.

The concern in Scripture is twofold. First, seeking knowledge through clairvoyance often involves spiritual forces that are not from God. In the New Testament, Acts 16:16-18 describes a slave girl who had a spirit of divination and brought her owners much gain by fortune telling. The Apostle Paul recognized that this ability came from a demonic source and commanded the spirit to come out of her in the name of Jesus Christ.

Second, seeking information through these means shows a lack of trust in God’s sovereignty and His Word. Isaiah 8:19 asks, when they say to you, "Inquire of the mediums and the necromancers who chirp and mutter," should not a people inquire of their God? The Bible teaches that God has already given us everything we need for life and godliness through His Word and the guidance of the Holy Spirit (2 Peter 1:3).

Often, the desire for clairvoyance or hidden knowledge stems from a deep human anxiety about the future or a desire for control. However, the Gospel provides a different kind of security. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from God, but because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. Jesus was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His lordship over all things.

Salvation and peace are not found in knowing the future, but in knowing the One who holds the future. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or by obtaining secret knowledge. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What thoughts or concerns about the future make you curious about these things?
What is the Shahada in Islam?

The Shahada, which is the Arabic word for "testimony," is the fundamental creed and the first of the Five Pillars of Islam. It is a short declaration of faith that consists of two distinct parts: "There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the messenger of Allah." In the Islamic tradition, reciting this statement with sincere belief in front of witnesses is the formal requirement for a person to convert to Islam.

As Christians, we also affirm the reality of one true God, a truth foundational to our faith as well. Deuteronomy 6:4 states, "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one." However, the biblical understanding of God differs significantly from the Islamic view expressed in the Shahada. Scripture reveals that the one true God exists eternally in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19).

While Islam honors Jesus as a prophet, the Bible teaches that He is much more. Jesus Christ is the eternal Word of God who became human (John 1:1; John 1:14). He is truly God and truly man. The core of the Christian message is not found in the teachings of a human messenger, but in the person and work of Jesus Christ Himself. 

The Gospel, or the "good news," addresses our greatest need. God is holy, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23).
