Are you currently connected with a local church where the Bible is preached and where you can experience this community?
What is the Moral argument for the existence of God?

The moral argument for the existence of God is a philosophical and theological reasoning that suggests the existence of objective moral values and duties points to a divine Lawgiver. This argument typically follows a simple logical structure: if God does not exist, objective moral values and duties do not exist. Since objective moral values and duties do exist, it follows that God exists.

At its core, the argument distinguishes between what is popular or cultural and what is truly right or wrong. If morality were merely a product of biological evolution or social convention, then "right" and "wrong" would be subjective and could change over time. However, most people recognize that certain actions, such as the intentional harming of an innocent person, are objectively evil regardless of whether a specific culture approves of them. This universal sense of "oughtness" suggests that there is a standard of goodness that exists outside of human opinion.

The Bible teaches that this standard is grounded in the character of God Himself. God is not just a being who follows a moral code; He is the very definition of goodness, holiness, and justice. Psalm 119:137 declares, "Righteous are you, O Lord, and right are your rules." Because we are created in the image of God (Genesis 1:27), we possess an inherent awareness of this moral reality. Romans 2:15 explains that the requirements of the law are "written on their hearts, while their conscience also bears witness."

This moral awareness serves a vital purpose: it shows us that we are accountable to our Creator. When we feel guilt or a sense of failure, it is often because we have violated the moral law that God has placed within us. The Bible confirms that every human being has failed to live up to God's perfect standard. Romans 3:23 says, "For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." Our moral failure, or sin, separates us from a holy God and leaves us in need of reconciliation.

The beauty of the Gospel is that God, in His mercy, did not leave us in our guilt. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to fulfill the moral law perfectly on our behalf. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was raised from the dead on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His sacrifice, justice was satisfied, and mercy was made available to all who believe.

Salvation is not earned by trying to be a "good person" or by following a moral checklist, but it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most compelling or challenging about the idea that God is the source of morality?
What is Ecclesiology?

Ecclesiology is the theological study of the Christian Church. The term comes from the Greek word ekklesia, which means an assembly or a gathering of people called out for a specific purpose. In the New Testament, this refers to the community of those who have been called out of the world and into a relationship with God through Jesus Christ.

The Bible presents the church in two primary ways. First, there is the universal church, which consists of all true believers in Jesus Christ across all of history and throughout the entire world (Ephesians 1:22-23; Hebrews 12:23). Second, there is the local church, which is a specific, visible gathering of believers in a particular location who commit to one another to worship God, grow in holiness, and carry out the Great Commission (1 Corinthians 1:2; Acts 14:23).

Several key themes define a biblical understanding of the church:

The Headship of Christ: Jesus Christ is the supreme authority and head of the church. The church exists by His power and for His glory (Colossians 1:18; Ephesians 5:23).

The Nature of the Church: The Scriptures describe the church using various metaphors, such as the Body of Christ, where every member has a unique and necessary function (1 Corinthians 12:12-27), the Household of God (1 Timothy 3:15), and the Temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 3:16).

The Ordinances: Most Protestant traditions recognize two primary ordinances commanded by Christ: Baptism and the Lord’s Supper. Baptism by immersion is an outward sign of an inward grace, symbolizing a believer's identification with the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus (Matthew 28:19; Romans 6:3-4). The Lord's Supper is a symbolic meal where believers remember Christ's sacrifice and experience His spiritual presence as they commune with Him and one another (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

Church Leadership and Governance: The New Testament outlines specific roles for the health of the local church, primarily elders (also called pastors or overseers) and deacons. Elders are called to shepherd the flock through teaching, prayer, and spiritual oversight, while deacons serve the physical and practical needs of the congregation (1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9; Acts 6:1-6). A healthy biblical model often includes elder-led congregational governance, where the leaders provide direction and the congregation affirms significant decisions.

The Mission of the Church: The church is called to make disciples of all nations, baptizing them and teaching them to obey everything Christ commanded (Matthew 28:19-20). This involves both the proclamation of the Gospel to the lost and the spiritual maturing of those who already believe.

At its heart, the church is a community of people who were once separated from God by sin but have been reconciled to Him. Because God is holy and we have all sinned, we deserve His judgment. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we perform (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What questions do you have about what it means to follow Jesus or find a local church?
What is the Teleological argument for the existence of God?

The Teleological argument, derived from the Greek word telos meaning end, purpose, or goal, is the argument for the existence of God based on the evidence of design and order in the universe. It suggests that the world exhibits such intricate complexity and functional beauty that it could not have happened by random chance, but must be the work of an intelligent Designer.

Historically, this argument is often associated with the watchmaker analogy presented by William Paley. He argued that if you found a watch in a field, you would immediately recognize that its gears and springs were intentionally arranged to keep time, implying the existence of a watchmaker. In a similar way, when we observe the complexity of the human eye, the "fine-tuning" of the laws of physics that allow for life, or the vast information stored in DNA, it points to a purposeful Creator.

The Bible affirms this reasoning, teaching that the natural world serves as a witness to its Maker. Psalm 19:1 states, "The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork." Furthermore, Romans 1:20 explains that "his invisible attributes, namely, his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made." These passages suggest that while the universe is not God, it clearly reflects His power, wisdom, and character.

While the Teleological argument helps us understand that a Designer exists, it is through the person of Jesus Christ that we truly come to know Him. This great Designer is also a holy and just God who created us to be in relationship with Him. However, our sin has created a separation between us and our Creator. Because God is merciful and loving, He took on human nature in Jesus Christ, living a perfect life and dying a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received not through our own efforts or good works, but through faith alone in Christ alone. I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What do you find most compelling when you look at the design and complexity of the world around us?
What is the difference between the universal and local church?

The Bible speaks of the church in two primary ways, often referred to as the universal church and the local church. Understanding the distinction between them helps us see both our identity in Christ and our responsibility to our brothers and sisters in faith.

The universal church consists of every person, across all of history and throughout the entire world, who has been saved by the grace of God through faith in Jesus Christ. It is not defined by a building or a single earthly organization, but by a shared union with Christ. When Jesus said, "I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it," He was referring to this global, spiritual body of believers in Matthew 16:18. This church is described as the body of Christ, where every member is joined together by the Holy Spirit regardless of their location or denomination, as seen in 1 Corinthians 12:12-13 and Ephesians 1:22-23.

The local church is a specific, visible gathering of believers in a particular geographical location who meet regularly to worship God, hear the preaching of His Word, pray, and care for one another. While the universal church is the spiritual reality of all believers, the local church is the physical expression of that reality. In the New Testament, we see this when Paul writes to the "church of God that is in Corinth" in 1 Corinthians 1:2 or to the "churches of Galatia" in Galatians 1:2. The local church is where we exercise spiritual gifts, practice the ordinances of baptism and the Lord's Supper, and submit to the leadership of elders for our spiritual growth and accountability, as encouraged in Hebrews 10:24-25 and Hebrews 13:17.

The relationship between the two is vital. A person becomes a member of the universal church the moment they are born again by the Spirit of God. However, the New Testament assumes that everyone who belongs to the universal church will also commit themselves to a local church. The local church is where the abstract love for "all the saints" becomes the practical, daily love for specific people.

At the heart of both the universal and local church is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We are all separated from a holy God by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we can do.

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. Once you trust in Him, you are part of His universal body, and finding a faithful local church is the natural next step to grow in that new life. What thoughts or questions do you have about joining a local fellowship of believers?
Why should I believe in God?

The question of why one should believe in God is perhaps the most significant question any person can ask, as the answer shapes how we understand our origin, our purpose, and our destiny. There are several ways to consider this, ranging from the world around us to the deep longings of the human heart, but the most profound reason is found in how God has revealed Himself to us.

First, we see evidence of God through the world He created. The complexity, beauty, and order of the universe suggest a Designer. The Bible tells us that God's eternal power and divine nature are clearly perceived through the things that have been made, so that people are without excuse (Romans 1:19-20). From the vastness of the stars to the intricate coding of DNA, creation speaks of a Creator (Psalm 19:1-2).

Second, we find evidence of God in our own moral conscience. We all have an inherent sense of right and wrong, justice and injustice. This moral law written on our hearts points to a moral Lawgiver (Romans 2:15). If we were merely the product of random biological processes, our deep sense of meaning and objective morality would be difficult to explain.

However, the most compelling reason to believe in God is found in the person of Jesus Christ. God did not remain distant or silent, but He entered into human history. Jesus is truly God and truly man, born of a virgin to reveal God's character to us perfectly (John 1:1; John 1:14; Colossians 1:15).

To understand why belief in God is necessary, we must look at the message of the Gospel. God is holy, supreme, and perfectly just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). He created us to know Him and reflect His glory, but we have all turned away from Him. This is what the Bible calls sin. Because of our sin, we are spiritually dead and separated from a holy God (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). No amount of good works or religious effort can bridge that gap.

Because of His great love and mercy, God provided the only way for us to be reconciled to Him. Jesus Christ lived the perfect life that we could not live. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking upon Himself the punishment that our sins deserved (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24). He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is not something we earn, but a gift of grace that we receive through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). To believe in God is to recognize your need for Him and to trust in what Jesus has done for you.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is it that makes you most curious or hesitant about believing in Him?
How is the church the Body of Christ?

The Bible uses the metaphor of a body to describe the relationship between Jesus Christ and those who believe in Him. This is not merely a poetic illustration but a spiritual reality that defines how Christians relate to God and to one another.

First, Jesus Christ is the Head of the body. Just as the human head directs the movements and functions of the physical body, Christ is the supreme authority and source of life for the church. The Scriptures teach that God put all things under His feet and gave Him as head over all things to the church, which is His body, the fullness of Him who fills all in all (Ephesians 1:22-23). Because He is the Head, the church finds its identity, purpose, and direction in Him alone (Colossians 1:18).

Second, the body is characterized by unity in diversity. A physical body has many parts, such as eyes, hands, and feet, each with a distinct and necessary function. Similarly, the church is composed of many individuals with different gifts, backgrounds, and roles, yet they are all part of one organism. The Apostle Paul explains that "For just as the body is one and has many members, and all the members of the body, though many, are one body, so it is with Christ" (1 Corinthians 12:12). This means that no member is unimportant and every believer has a God given role to play for the health of the whole (1 Corinthians 12:14-27).

Third, being the Body of Christ implies a profound interdependence and mutual care. When one part of a physical body suffers, the whole body feels the pain. In the same way, Christians are called to live in such a close community that "If one member suffers, all suffer together; if one member is honored, all rejoice together" (1 Corinthians 12:26). We are commanded to bear one another’s burdens and to use our spiritual gifts to build each other up in love (Romans 12:4-5; Ephesians 4:15-16).

Finally, the church is the Body of Christ because of our spiritual union with Him. This union is made possible only through the Gospel. We were all born separated from God by our sin, but Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice, He paid the debt for sin and defeated death. When a person turns from their sin and places their trust in Jesus Christ alone for salvation, they are joined to Him by the Holy Spirit and become a member of His body.

If you have not yet experienced this reconciliation with God, you can turn to Him now. Repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What is it that might be keeping you from placing your trust in Him today?
What are some undeniable examples of divine intervention?

When we consider divine intervention, we look to the Bible as our ultimate authority. Scripture records numerous instances where God stepped into human history to accomplish His will, demonstrate His power, and rescue His people. While skeptics may look for natural explanations, the believer sees the hand of a sovereign and loving God.

The most profound example of divine intervention is the incarnation, life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. God did not leave humanity to perish in sin but intervened by sending His Son. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life that we could not live (John 1:14; Hebrews 4:15). He died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (1 Peter 3:18). After being buried, He physically rose from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all (Philippians 2:9-11).

Another significant example is the Exodus of the Israelites from Egypt. When the people of God were enslaved and crying out for help, God intervened through a series of miraculous plagues and the parting of the Red Sea (Exodus 14:21-22). This was not a mere coincidence of weather but a targeted act of deliverance by the Lord to fulfill His covenant promises (Exodus 3:7-10).

We also see divine intervention in the miraculous conversion of individuals, such as Saul of Tarsus. Saul was a violent persecutor of the early church, yet God intervened directly on the road to Damascus to transform him into the Apostle Paul (Acts 9:1-6). This kind of intervention continues today whenever a person passes from spiritual death to life.

Every person is born separated from God because of sin (Romans 3:23). God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), and His wrath against sin is real. However, because of His great mercy, He offers salvation as a free gift. This salvation is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the institutional church?

The institutional church refers to the visible, organized expression of the Body of Christ on earth. While the "invisible church" consists of all true believers across all time and places, known only to God, the institutional church is composed of local congregations with specific structures, leadership, and practices established according to the patterns found in Scripture.

The Bible describes the church as the pillar and buttress of the truth in 1 Timothy 3:15. As an institution, it is characterized by several key elements:

First, it is defined by the gathering of believers for worship, the preaching of God’s Word, and the observation of the ordinances. These ordinances include believer’s baptism, which is an outward sign of inward grace, and the Lord’s Supper, where the church remembers the death of Christ and enjoys His spiritual presence (Acts 2:41-42; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

Second, the institutional church maintains a biblical leadership structure. Scripture provides qualifications for elders (also called pastors or overseers) who shepherd the flock through teaching and oversight, and deacons who serve the physical and practical needs of the community (1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9; Hebrews 13:17).

Third, it involves formal membership and accountability. Believers are called to submit to one another and to their leaders, practicing church discipline when necessary to maintain the purity of the body and to restore wandering brothers or sisters (Matthew 18:15-17; 1 Corinthians 5:1-5).

The ultimate purpose of the institutional church is to glorify God by making disciples of all nations. This mission is grounded in the Gospel, which is the heart of the church's message. The Gospel is the good news that although God is perfectly holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to save us. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any human works or religious merit (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:23-25; Romans 10:9-10). The institutional church serves as the primary place where this message is protected, proclaimed, and lived out in community.

You can turn to God right now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

Have you ever considered what it means to be part of a local body of believers in this way?
Is Christianity true?

Christianity rests upon the historical claim that Jesus of Nazareth is the Son of God who lived, died, and rose again from the dead. It is not merely a set of moral teachings or a personal philosophy, but a faith grounded in the truth of God’s intervention in human history.

The Bible teaches that God is holy, supreme, and the Creator of all things. He created us to know Him and reflect His glory. However, all of us have turned away from God, seeking our own way through sin. This rebellion separates us from God and leads to spiritual death (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23).

Because God is rich in mercy and love, He did not leave us in our lost state. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect, sinless life that we could not live and then died on a cross. In His death, He served as a substitute, taking the punishment for our sins upon Himself to reconcile us to God (John 3:16; 1 Peter 3:18). 

The truth of Christianity is confirmed by the bodily resurrection of Jesus. On the third day after His crucifixion, He rose from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–8). This was not a private vision but a historical event witnessed by hundreds of people. His resurrection ensures that those who trust in Him will also be raised to eternal life.

Salvation is a gift of grace that cannot be earned by our own efforts or good works. It is received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today? If you would like to explore this further, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible, which was written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name (John 20:31).
What is ministry?

Ministry is essentially service rendered to God and to others, motivated by the love of Christ and empowered by the Holy Spirit. The word itself comes from the Greek term diakonia, which means service or office. It encompasses both the internal building up of the church and the external mission of sharing the Gospel with the world (Matthew 28:18-20).

In the Bible, ministry is not reserved for a professional class of leaders. While God provides elders and deacons to lead and equip the local church, every believer has been given spiritual gifts to use for the common good (1 Corinthians 12:7; Ephesians 4:11-12). Ministry happens whenever a person uses their time, talents, or resources to honor God and help others, whether through teaching the Word, showing mercy to those in need, or working diligently in their daily job as if serving the Lord (Colossians 3:23-24).

Ultimately, all ministry points back to the person and work of Jesus Christ. Because God is perfectly holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), we are naturally separated from Him. Ministry is the outworking of the good news that Christ took our place, dying for our sins and rising from the dead to reconcile us to God (2 Corinthians 5:18-21). This salvation cannot be earned by our own works or ministry, but is received as a free gift by grace through faith in Jesus alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is classical apologetics?

Classical apologetics is a traditional method of defending the Christian faith that relies on a specific, two-step logical process. This approach seeks to show that Christianity is not only a matter of faith but is also grounded in sound reason and historical evidence.

The first step in classical apologetics is to establish theism, which is the belief that a supreme, personal God exists. This is typically done through natural theology, using logical arguments that do not require the Bible to be accepted as true beforehand. These arguments often include the cosmological argument, which suggests that the universe must have a cause, and the teleological argument, which points to the intricate design of the world as evidence of a Designer. This step aligns with the biblical principle that God's invisible attributes and eternal power are clearly perceived through the things He has made, as noted in Romans 1:20.

The second step moves from general belief in God to the specific truths of the Christian faith. Once the existence of God is established as a reasonable conclusion, the classical apologist uses historical evidence to prove that Jesus Christ is the Son of God and that the Bible is His inspired Word. A primary focus of this step is the historical reliability of the New Testament and the physical resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead. This follows the pattern of the Apostle Paul in Acts 17:22-31, where he first addressed the Athenians regarding the "Unknown God" who created the world before testifying about the resurrection of Jesus.

While other methods like evidentialism or presuppositionalism exist, the classical school emphasizes that human reason is a gift from God that can be used to remove intellectual obstacles to the Gospel. However, while logic can show that the Gospel is true, only the Holy Spirit can change a heart to believe it.

The ultimate goal of any defense of the faith is to point people toward the truth of who Jesus is. Scripture teaches that we are all separated from God by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross, and rise bodily on the third day to defeat sin and death, according to 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. This salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone.

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most interesting or perhaps most difficult about the idea of using logic to defend your faith?
Why does God allow birth defects?

This is a deeply personal and often painful question that many parents and families wrestle with, and it is important to approach it with great humility and compassion. To understand why birth defects occur, we must look to what the Bible teaches about the nature of our world, the character of God, and His ultimate purposes for humanity.

The Bible teaches that we live in a world that is "groaning" under the weight of sin and the Fall (Romans 8:22). When the first man, Adam, disobeyed God, sin entered the world, and with it came death, disease, and physical decay (Genesis 3:17-19). This means that all of creation, including our physical bodies, is subject to the effects of a broken world. Birth defects are a heartbreaking part of this broader reality where things are not as they were originally intended to be in the Garden of Eden.

However, Scripture also makes it clear that God is sovereign over all things, including the way we are formed. In Exodus 4:11, the Lord says to Moses, "Who has made man's mouth? Who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, the Lord?" This passage affirms that no person is an accident or a mistake in the eyes of God. Every individual, regardless of their physical or mental abilities, is created in the image of God and possesses inherent, infinite dignity (Genesis 1:27). Psalm 139:13-16 beautifully describes how God knits every person together in their mother's womb, seeing them and loving them before they are even born.

When Jesus was asked by His disciples why a man was born blind, they wanted to know if it was because of his sin or his parents' sin. Jesus responded in John 9:3, "It was not that this man sinned, or his parents, but that the works of God might be displayed in him." This suggests that while we may not always understand the specific "why," God can use disability and suffering to display His glory, His grace, and His power in ways we might never expect.

Ultimately, birth defects remind us of our need for a Savior and the hope of a world made new. God is holy and just, but because of our sin, we are all separated from Him. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not something we can earn through our own works, but it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

In the resurrection, God promises that those who trust in Christ will receive new, glorified bodies. Revelation 21:4 tells us that one day, "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore." While we face the sorrows of this fallen world now, we look forward to the day when all things will be restored and every broken thing will be made whole.

How does knowing that God is with you in the midst of suffering change the way you view these difficult questions?
Why doesn't God heal everyone?

This is one of the most difficult questions any of us can ask, and it is often born out of deep personal pain or the sight of others suffering. When we look at the ministry of Jesus, we see Him moved with compassion, healing the sick, and restoring the broken (Matthew 9:35; Matthew 14:14). Because God is all-powerful and perfectly good, it can be confusing when He chooses not to intervene in the way we hope.

While we cannot fully know the mind of God, the Bible provides several perspectives on why physical healing is not always granted in this life.

First, we live in a world that is fundamentally broken by sin. When humanity turned away from God, the consequence was the entrance of death, decay, and disease into the world (Genesis 3:17-19; Romans 8:20-22). Physical illness is a reminder that we are not yet in the world as God originally intended it to be, or as He will one day restore it to be.

Second, God often uses suffering for a purpose that we may not see or understand immediately. For the believer, God promises that He works all things together for good, specifically to conform us to the image of His Son (Romans 8:28-29). Sometimes, physical weakness is the very thing God uses to display His strength. The Apostle Paul pleaded with God three times to remove a "thorn in the flesh," but God responded by saying, "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness" (2 Corinthians 12:7-9). Through this, Paul learned to depend more fully on God’s grace rather than his own strength.

Third, God’s perspective is eternal while ours is often limited to the present. He is more concerned with our spiritual wholeness and eternal salvation than our temporary physical comfort. Even those whom Jesus healed during His earthly ministry eventually died of other causes. Physical healing in this life is a temporary mercy, but spiritual healing is an eternal necessity.

This leads us to the heart of the Gospel. While physical illness is a heavy burden, the greatest sickness we all face is sin, which separates us from a holy and perfect God (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). God, in His infinite love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His death and resurrection, Jesus defeated sin and death once and for all.

For everyone who trusts in Him, God promises a final, ultimate healing. In the new heavens and the new earth, there will be no more death, mourning, crying, or pain (Revelation 21:4). Our current "light and momentary troubles" are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all (2 Corinthians 4:17). Physical healing today is a "sign" of that coming Kingdom, but the full reality is yet to come.

God invites you to bring your pain, your questions, and your sickness to Him. He is "the Father of mercies and God of all comfort" (2 Corinthians 1:3). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and the hope of eternal life.

What is it about God's silence or the reality of suffering that feels most difficult for you right now?
What does it mean that the Bible is self-authenticating?

The concept that the Bible is self-authenticating means that the Word of God carries its own authority and does not depend on the testimony of any man or organization for its validity. Because the Bible is "theopneustos," or breathed out by God, its ultimate authority comes from the Author Himself rather than from human reason, historical proofs, or church tradition.

When we say the Scripture is self-authenticating, we recognize several key truths:

First, the Bible possesses objective characteristics that point to its divine origin. These include the majesty of its style, the purity of its moral teachings, the consistency of its various parts despite being written by many authors over centuries, and its power to convict the human heart. These qualities are inherent to the text because it is the Word of God. As 2 Timothy 3:16 states, "All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness."

Second, the self-authenticating nature of Scripture is confirmed to the believer through the internal testimony of the Holy Spirit. While the Bible is objectively true on its own, human beings are often blinded by sin and unable to see or submit to that truth. The Holy Spirit works within a person to open their eyes so they can recognize the Bible as the voice of their Creator. Jesus spoke of this recognition in John 10:27 when He said, "My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me."

Third, this means that the Bible is the final authority for faith and practice. If the Bible required an external proof to be considered true, then that proof would actually be a higher authority than the Bible. Instead, we believe the Bible is the supreme standard because God cannot swear by anyone greater than Himself, as mentioned in Hebrews 6:13.

This reality has a profound impact on how we understand our standing before God. The same Spirit who convinces us that the Bible is true also uses that Word to show us our need for a Savior. The Scriptures reveal that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, according to Romans 3:23.

The central message of this self-authenticating Word is the Gospel: that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. This was done to reconcile us to God, and salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works. As it says in Romans 10:9, "If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved."

You can turn to God even now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

How does the idea of God speaking directly through the Bible affect the way you think about seeking truth?
Is the priesthood of all believers biblical?

Yes, the priesthood of all believers is a deeply biblical concept that describes the identity and calling of every person who belongs to Jesus Christ. This doctrine teaches that every Christian has direct access to God through Christ and a personal responsibility to serve Him and others, without the need for an earthly human mediator.

The Apostle Peter explicitly describes the church in this way, writing that believers are being built into a spiritual house to be a holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ in 1 Peter 2:5. He continues in 1 Peter 2:9, saying, But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God’s special possession, that you may declare the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light. 

This New Testament reality fulfills the original purpose God expressed for His people in the Old Testament. In Exodus 19:6, God told the Israelites that they would be a kingdom of priests and a holy nation. While the Old Covenant had a specific class of priests from the tribe of Levi, the New Covenant in Christ opens this priestly access to every believer.

The foundation for our priesthood is Jesus Christ Himself, who is our Great High Priest as described in Hebrews 4:14-16. Because Jesus lived a perfect life, died as a sacrifice for our sins, and rose again, the veil that separated us from God has been torn. We can now approach the throne of grace with confidence. This means that every believer has the privilege of reading the Scriptures, praying directly to God, and ministering to fellow believers. It also means we are all called to represent God to the world by sharing the Gospel.

This direct access is only possible because of what Jesus has done. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious rituals.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Understanding your identity as a priest in God's kingdom changes how you view your daily life. Whether you are at work, at home, or in your community, you are a representative of Christ with the ability to intercede for others in prayer and to speak His truth. What questions do you have about how this role looks in your everyday life?
